  - Continuous SOTIF monitoring
  - Driver-in-loop remote fallback
  - Auto-driver reskilling program (Just Transition)
  - Quarterly algorithmic bias audits
- **Review trigger:** Re-assess every 6 months or after any incident.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** Discuss the **ethical implications of algorithmic bias** in pedestrian detection systems used in ADAS. Cite the **Gender Shades (2018)** study and propose **three engineering mitigations**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Evaluate the **lifecycle CO₂e emissions** of a conventional petrol car versus a battery-electric vehicle over **150,000 km** in the Indian grid context. Comment on whether EVs can be called "zero-emission vehicles" ethically. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

1. **Algorithmic Bias Definition (1 mark):** Systematic error in AI perception models that produces disparate performance across demographic groups (skin tone, gender, age).

2. **Gender Shades Study (1.5 marks):** Buolamwini & Gebru (2018) showed commercial face-recognition systems had **up to 34.7% error** for darker-skinned females vs. **0.8%** for lighter-skinned males. Pedestrian detection inherits similar risk due to non-diverse training datasets.

3. **Ethical Implications (1.5 marks):**
   - **Non-discrimination violation** — vulnerable road users disproportionately endangered.
   - **Moral hazard** — OEMs deploying undertested systems.
   - **Trust deficit** — public resistance to AVs.

4. **Three Engineering Mitigations (3 marks):**
   - **Diverse training datasets** including varied skin tones, body types, clothing, lighting.
   - **Independent third-party algorithmic audits** pre-deployment.
   - **Continuous monitoring** with demographic-stratified performance dashboards post-deployment.

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

1. **ICE Lifecycle (1.5 marks):**
   - Manufacturing: ~**7,000 kg CO₂e**
   - Use-phase (150,000 km @ ~120 g/km fuel-cycle): ~**18,000 kg CO₂e** (fuel) + ~**10,000 kg CO₂e** (well-to-tank) ≈ **28,000 kg CO₂e**
   - End-of-life: ~**1,000 kg CO₂e**
   - **Total ICE ≈ 36,000 kg CO₂e**

2. **EV Lifecycle (2 marks):**
   - Manufacturing (battery-heavy): ~**12,000 kg CO₂e**
   - Use-phase (India grid ~0.7 kg CO₂/kWh, 15 kWh/100 km): ~**18,000 kg CO₂e**
   - End-of-life: ~**2,000 kg CO₂e**
   - **Total EV ≈ 32,000 kg CO₂e**

3. **Comparison (1 mark):** EV saves **~4,000 kg CO₂e** (~11%) over 150,000 km in India grid context. Savings drop to ~**2,000 kg CO₂e** if grid is coal-heavy; rise to **>15,000 kg CO₂e** if grid is renewable-heavy.

4. **Ethical Verdict (2.5 marks):**
   - Calling EVs "zero-emission" is **ethically misleading** — it ignores embedded carbon and use-phase grid intensity.
   - **Ethically honest label:** "Tailpipe-zero" or "Low-Well-to-Wheel emission."
   - True ethical advance requires **simultaneous grid decarbonization**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on This Topic:**
> 1. **Conflating "tailpipe zero" with "lifecycle zero"** — Examiner will deduct 1–2 marks if no lifecycle distinction is made.
> 2. **Treating ethics as "philosophy" not "engineering"** — Always tie ethical points to a measurable engineering metric (cost, error rate, gCO₂/km).
> 3. **Ignoring Indian context** — Global examples are fine, but KTU 2024 explicitly values **India-specific** data (FAME-II, DPDP Act, MoRTH regulations).
> 4. **Single-framework thinking** — Using only utilitarianism OR only Kantian ethics loses marks. Always demonstrate **dual-framework analysis**.
> 5. **No mitigation proposed** — Identifying a problem without an engineering fix is a half-answer. Always propose **at least one mitigation per challenge**.
> 6. **Skipping stakeholders** — Any decision analysis without explicit stakeholder enumeration loses the process marks (1 mark minimum).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Rapid Revision Checklist

- **Four Pillars of AVT Challenges:** Technological, Environmental, Socio-Economic, Regulatory-Ethical.
- **Four Key Frameworks:** ALARP, SOTIF (ISO 21448), Privacy by Design, Triple Bottom Line.
- **Three Hard Constraints:** Non-discrimination, Lifecycle honesty, Accountability.
- **One Ethical Triangle:** V1 (Feasibility) × V2 (Viability) × V3 (Ethical Acceptability as GATE).
- **5-Step ERA:** Stakeholders → Utilities → WAU → Hard Constraints → ALARP.
- **Two Non-Negotiable Distinctions:**
  - Tailpipe-zero ≠ Lifecycle-zero
  - Utilitarian optimization ≠ Unconstrained action (deontological veto applies)
- **Numerical Benchmarks to Memorize:**
  - Embedded carbon: ~**150 kg CO₂e/kWh**
  - Battery recycling target: **>95%** by mass
  - Cold range loss: up to **40%**
  - EV/ICE price ratio: **1.3× to 1.8×**
  - V2X latency: <**10 ms**
- **Indian Regulatory Anchors:** FAME-II subsidy scheme, MoRTH ADAS standards, DPDP Act 2023 (data privacy), Battery Waste Management Rules 2022.
- **Cited Reference Studies:** Gender Shades (Buolamwini & Gebru, 2018), EU Battery Regulation 2023, UNECE WP.29.
- **Golden Sentence for Exams:** *"Advanced vehicle technology is not merely an engineering problem but a sociotechnical system whose acceptance hinges on integrating ethical acceptability as a gating constraint, not an afterthought."*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Tip:** In every 14-mark answer, anchor your reasoning to **at least one** of: a numerical metric, a named framework, an Indian regulation, and a stakeholder consideration. Answers missing all four are capped at **8 marks** even if verbose.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Machine Learning Algorithms for Automotive Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAUT865 - ADVANCED VEHICLE TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Machine Learning Algorithms for Automotive Systems # Module 1: Machine Learning Algorithms for Automotive Systems ## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU Definition (PEAUT865 / 2024 Scheme)** > *Machine Learning (ML) for Automotive Systems* refers to the computational paradigm in which algorithms build predictive or decisional mathematical models by extracting statistical patterns from multi-sensor vehicular data (camera, LiDAR, RADAR, CAN-bus telemetry, GNSS, IMU), **without being explicitly programmed for every contingency**. These models power perception, prediction, planning, and actuation loops in modern vehicles — from ADAS (Advanced Driver Assistance Systems) and autonomous driving stacks to predictive powertrain maintenance and intelligent in-cabin driver monitoring. ### 1.1.1 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: Teaching a Teenager to Drive** > Imagine teaching a teenager to drive. You don't write a 500-page rulebook covering every possible traffic scenario. Instead, you **let them observe thousands of driving hours**, correct them when they make mistakes, and reward good behavior. Over time, the brain "learns" patterns — "if a ball rolls onto the road, a child might follow," "if the car ahead brakes hard, brake too." > Machine Learning is exactly this, but for a **silicon brain running on ECUs (Electronic Control Units)**. The vehicle's cameras are its *eyes*, LiDAR is its *sense of touch at distance*, and the ML algorithm is the *hippocampus* that stores and generalizes driving experience. The bigger and cleaner the dataset, the better the "driver." ### 1.1.2 The Three Pillars of ML in Automotive Context | ML Paradigm | How it "Learns" | Canonical Automotive Use-Case | |---|---|---| | **Supervised Learning** | Learns from labelled input-output pairs (e.g., millions of images tagged "pedestrian"/"vehicle") | Lane detection, traffic sign classification, driver drowsiness detection | | **Unsupervised Learning** | Discovers hidden structure in unlabelled data | Anomaly detection in CAN-bus for **cybersecurity**, driver behaviour clustering | | **Reinforcement Learning (RL)** | An agent learns by trial-and-error using rewards/penalties in a simulated environment | Adaptive cruise control, autonomous lane-change decisions, **path planning** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme)** > Module 1 explicitly emphasizes the taxonomy of ML algorithms and their **automotive domain mapping**. Students must be able to *justify* which algorithm family suits a given vehicular sub-problem — this is a frequent **CO1 (Apply)** and **CO2 (Analyze)** exam target. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Operational Pipeline of an Automotive ML System An automotive ML system is rarely a single algorithm. It is an **end-to-end pipeline**. Understanding each stage is essential for KTU 14-mark design questions. ### Stage 1 — Data Acquisition (Sensors) * **Cameras**: 2D RGB images, high resolution, rich texture. Used for lane, sign, object recognition. * **LiDAR**: 3D point clouds via time-of-flight laser. Range ≈ **200 m**, accuracy ≈ **±2 cm**. * **RADAR**: Radio detection and ranging. Robust to weather. Used for adaptive cruise control (ACC) and blind-spot monitoring. * **Ultrasonic**: Short-range (<**5 m**) low-cost proximity sensing for parking assist. * **CAN-bus Telemetry**: Streams vehicle dynamics (speed, RPM, steering angle, brake pressure) at typically **500 kbps** for ML-driven predictive maintenance. ### Stage 2 — Data Pre-processing * **Normalization**: Scale features to $[0,1]$ or use Z-score $z = \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$. * **Noise Filtering**: Kalman filter, Gaussian smoothing, outlier rejection using **z-score threshold $\vert z \vert > 3$**. * **Data Augmentation** (image): rotation, flipping, brightness jitter — crucial because real crash data is rare. ### Stage 3 — Feature Extraction / Model Training * Classical ML: HOG (Histogram of Oriented Gradients), SIFT, LBP features → SVM/Random Forest. * Deep Learning: CNNs auto-extract hierarchical features from raw pixels. ### Stage 4 — Inference (Real-Time Deployment) * Inference time must typically be **< 50 ms** for Level 2+ ADAS. * Deployed on **embedded ECUs**: NVIDIA Jetson Orin, Qualcomm Snapdragon Ride, Mobileye EyeQ. ### Stage 5 — Actuation / Driver Feedback * Output drives steering, braking, throttle, HUD warning, or HMI alerts. --- ## 2.2 Mathematical Foundations (KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet) > [!NOTE] > **Exam Tip**: All formulas below are high-yield for Part B (14-mark) derivations and numerical problems. ### 2.2.1 Supervised Learning — Regression Foundations The simplest supervised model is **Linear Regression**, which assumes the target variable is a linear combination of features: $$\hat{y} = w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + \dots + w_n x_n + b = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b$$ The model parameters $\mathbf{w}$ and bias $b$ are learned by **minimizing a loss function**. The most common is **Mean Squared Error (MSE)**: $$L_{\text{MSE}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( y_i - \hat{y}_i \right)^2$$ The optimization is performed using **Gradient Descent**, an iterative first-order update: $$w_j \leftarrow w_j - \alpha \frac{\partial L}{\partial w_j}, \quad b \leftarrow b - \alpha \frac{\partial L}{\partial b}$$ where $\alpha$ is the **learning rate** (typically $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-2}$), and $\frac{\partial L}{\partial w_j}$ is the partial derivative (gradient) of the loss w.r.t. the parameter. ### 2.2.2 Classification Foundations — Logistic Regression & Sigmoid For binary classification (e.g., "Pedestrian" vs. "Non-Pedestrian"), a linear output is squashed through the **sigmoid (logistic) function**: $$\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}, \quad z = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b$$ The loss becomes the **Binary Cross-Entropy (BCE)**: $$L_{\text{BCE}} = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ y_i \log(\hat{y}_i) + (1 - y_i) \log(1 - \hat{y}_i) \right]$$ ### 2.2.3 Neural Network Building Block A single artificial neuron computes a weighted sum, adds a bias, and passes the result through a non-linear **activation function** $f(\cdot)$: $$a^{(l)} = f\!\left( \sum_{j} w_{ij}^{(l)} a_j^{(l-1)} + b_i^{(l)} \right)$$ Common activation functions: | Activation | Formula | Derivative | Automotive Use | |---|---|---|---| | ReLU | $f(z) = \max(0,z)$ | $f'(z) = 1$ if $z > 0$, else $0$ | Hidden layers of CNNs for object detection | | Sigmoid | $\sigma(z) = 1/(1+e^{-z})$ | $\sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))$ | Binary classification output | | Softmax | $\sigma_i(\mathbf{z}) = e^{z_i}/\sum_j e^{z_j}$ | — | Multi-class traffic-sign classifier | | Tanh | $\tanh(z)$ | $1 - \tanh^2(z)$ | RNN gates in driver behaviour modelling | ### 2.2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Master Table | # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Domain in Automotive | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Linear model | $\hat{y} = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b$ | Steering angle prediction | | 2 | Sigmoid | $\sigma(z) = 1/(1+e^{-z})$ | Pedestrian vs. background classifier | | 3 | Softmax | $\sigma_i = e^{z_i}/\sum_j e^{z_j}$ | Traffic sign recognition | | 4 | MSE Loss | $L = \frac{1}{N}\sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Velocity / SOC prediction | | 5 | Cross-Entropy | $L = -\sum y_i \log \hat{y}_i$ | Multi-class object classification | | 6 | Gradient Descent | $\theta \leftarrow \theta - \alpha \nabla_\theta L$ | Universal optimizer | | 7 | Backprop (chain rule) | $\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial w^{(l)}} = \dfrac{\partial L}{\partial a^{(l)}} \cdot \dfrac{\partial a^{(l)}}{\partial z^{(l)}} \cdot \dfrac{\partial z^{(l)}}{\partial w^{(l)}}$ | Training deep CNNs for perception | | 8 | Q-Learning Update | $Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha\!\left[ r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s',a') - Q(s,a) \right]$ | RL-based ACC / lane-change | | 9 | Discount Factor | $\gamma \in [0,1)$ | Balances immediate vs. future reward | | 10 | IOU (Intersection over Union) | $\text{IOU} = \dfrac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$ | Bounding-box accuracy in object detection | | 11 | Normalization (Z-score) | $z = (x - \mu)/\sigma$ | Sensor data pre-processing | | 12 | Softmax temperature | $p_i = e^{z_i/T}/\sum_j e^{z_j/T}$ | Confidence calibration in ADAS | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility**: Formula #10 (IoU) is the standard metric in the **KITTI, nuScenes, and Waymo Open** autonomous-driving benchmarks. Mastering it is essential for KTU CO3 (Design/Develop) questions on perception systems. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1 — Gradient Descent for Linear Regression (MSE) ### Step A: Define the Model and the Loss Given $N$ training samples $\{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$ with $\mathbf{x}_i \in \mathbb{R}^n$, the linear model and squared loss are: $$\hat{y}_i = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b, \qquad L_i = \frac{1}{2}\left( y_i - \hat{y}_i \right)^2$$ The total cost is the average: $$J(\mathbf{w}, b) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} L_i = \frac{1}{2N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\left( y_i - \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i - b \right)^2$$ ### Step B: Compute the Gradient with Respect to $w_j$ $$\frac{\partial J}{\partial w_j} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\left( \hat{y}_i - y_i \right) x_{ij}$$ *Explanation:* The derivative of the squared error $(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ is $2(y_i - \hat{y}_i)(-1) = -2(\hat{y}_i - y_i)$. Multiplying by the chain-rule factor $x_{ij}$ (from $\partial \hat{y}_i / \partial w_j = x_{ij}$) and dividing by $N$ gives the result above. ### Step C: Compute the Gradient with Respect to $b$ $$\frac{\partial J}{\partial b} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\left( \hat{y}_i - y_i \right)$$ *Explanation:* Since $\partial \hat{y}_i / \partial b = 1$, only the residual $(\hat{y}_i - y_i)$ remains after differentiation. ### Step D: Apply the Gradient Descent Update $$w_j^{\text{new}} = w_j^{\text{old}} - \alpha \frac{\partial J}{\partial w_j}, \qquad b^{\text{new}} = b^{\text{old}} - \alpha \frac{\partial J}{\partial b}$$ *Explanation:* We move the parameters in the **opposite direction** of the gradient (steepest ascent of loss) to descend toward a minimum. The scalar $\alpha$ controls step size. ### Step E: Convergence Check Iteration continues until either $\vert \nabla J \vert < \epsilon$ (gradient-norm tolerance, e.g., $\epsilon = 10^{-6}$) or a maximum epoch count is reached. For automotive real-time retraining pipelines, an early-stopping patience of $\mathbf{10}$ epochs on validation loss is typical. --- ## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2 — Backpropagation Chain Rule (1 Hidden Layer) > [!NOTE] > **Why Backprop Matters in ADAS**: Modern perception stacks (e.g., YOLOv8, EfficientDet) are deep CNNs trained via backpropagation. KTU examiners frequently test whether students can derive the chain-rule for a single layer. ### Step A: Network Architecture Consider a 2-layer fully-connected network: input $\mathbf{a}^{(0)} = \mathbf{x}$, hidden pre-activation $\mathbf{z}^{(1)} = \mathbf{W}^{(1)}\mathbf{a}^{(0)} + \mathbf{b}^{(1)}$, hidden activation $\mathbf{a}^{(1)} = f(\mathbf{z}^{(1)})$, output pre-activation $\mathbf{z}^{(2)} = \mathbf{W}^{(2)}\mathbf{a}^{(1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(2)}$, and output $\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \sigma(\mathbf{z}^{(2)})$. ### Step B: Define the Loss (Binary Cross-Entropy) $$L = -\left[ y \log \hat{y} + (1-y)\log(1-\hat{y}) \right]$$ ### Step C: Derivative of Loss w.r.t. Output Pre-activation $\mathbf{z}^{(2)}$ Using the well-known identity for BCE + sigmoid: $$\frac{\partial L}{\partial z^{(2)}} = \hat{y} - y$$ *Explanation:* This beautiful simplification (one of the reasons BCE is paired with sigmoid) comes from $\partial \sigma / \partial z = \sigma(1-\sigma)$ cancelling with the log-derivative. ### Step D: Derivative w.r.t. Hidden Weights $\mathbf{W}^{(2)}$ $$\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(2)}} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{z}^{(2)}} \cdot \frac{\partial \mathbf{z}^{(2)}}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(2)}} = \left(\hat{y} - y\right) \cdot \left(\mathbf{a}^{(1)}\right)^T$$ ### Step E: Derivative w.r.t. Hidden Pre-activation $\mathbf{z}^{(1)}$ (Chain Rule) $$\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{z}^{(1)}} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{z}^{(2)}} \cdot \frac{\partial \mathbf{z}^{(2)}}{\partial \mathbf{a}^{(1)}} \cdot \frac{\partial \mathbf{a}^{(1)}}{\partial \mathbf{z}^{(1)}}$$ $$\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{z}^{(1)}} = \left(\hat{y} - y\right) \cdot \mathbf{W}^{(2)} \odot f'\!\left(\mathbf{z}^{(1)}\right)$$ *Explanation:* This is the **backpropagation chain rule**. The error signal $(\hat{y}-y)$ is propagated *backwards* through the second weight matrix $\mathbf{W}^{(2)}$, and modulated element-wise ($\odot$) by the derivative of the activation $f'$. ### Step F: Derivative w.r.t. First-Layer Weights $\mathbf{W}^{(1)}$ $$\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(1)}} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{z}^{(1)}} \cdot \frac{\partial \mathbf{z}^{(1)}}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(1)}} = \left[\left(\hat{y} - y\right) \mathbf{W}^{(2)} \odot f'\!\left(\mathbf{z}^{(1)}\right)\right] \cdot \left(\mathbf{x}\right)^T$$ *Explanation:* This is the gradient used to update the *first* layer — exactly the same chain rule applied recursively. In a deep CNN, this recursion continues through dozens or hundreds of layers. --- ## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 3 — Q-Learning Update Rule (Reinforcement Learning) ### Step A: Markov Decision Process (MDP) Setup An autonomous-driving RL agent interacts with a simulator (e.g., CARLA, SUMO). At each step $t$: * $s_t$: state (vehicle speed, lane offset, gap to leader car). * $a_t$: action (accelerate, brake, lane-change-left, lane-change-right). * $r_t$: reward (e.g., +1 for safe distance, -10 for collision). * $s_{t+1}$: next state. ### Step B: Define the Action-Value Function $Q^\pi(s,a)$ The expected cumulative discounted reward when taking action $a$ in state $s$ and following policy $\pi$ thereafter: $$Q^\pi(s,a) = \mathbb{E}_\pi \!\left[ \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} r_{t+k} \,\Big\vert\, s_t=s,\ a_t=a \right]$$ where $\gamma \in [0,1)$ is the **discount factor** that prioritizes immediate rewards over distant ones. ### Step C: Bellman Optimality Equation For the optimal policy $\pi^*$, the value satisfies: $$Q^*(s,a) = \mathbb{E}\!\left[ r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^*(s',a') \,\Big\vert\, s,a \right]$$ *Explanation:* The value of doing $a$ in $s$ equals the immediate reward $r$ plus the discounted best future value from the next state $s'$. This recursion is what makes the equation **recursive** in expectation. ### Step D: Sample-Based Q-Learning Update (Off-Policy TD Control) Because the expectation is unknown, we use the observed sample $(s,a,r,s')$: $$Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha \left[ r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s',a') - Q(s,a) \right]$$ The bracketed term is the **TD-error** (Temporal Difference error): $$\delta_t = r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s',a') - Q(s,a)$$ ### Step E: Convergence Guarantee (Off-Policy, Tabular) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Theorem (Watkins & Dayan, 1992)**: > *If all state-action pairs are visited infinitely often, the learning rate $\alpha$ decays appropriately (e.g., $\alpha_t = 1/t$), and rewards are bounded, then $Q_t \rightarrow Q^*$ with probability 1.* --- ## 3.4 Analytical Algorithm-Selection Matrix The following table maps **automotive sub-problems** to the **most appropriate ML algorithm**, a frequent KTU 14-mark Part B sub-question type. | Automotive Sub-Problem | ML Algorithm | Reason for Selection | |---|---|---| | Lane detection (pixel classification) | **CNN (encoder-decoder like SegNet)** | Spatial feature extraction; handles curved lanes | | Traffic sign recognition (10–50 classes) | **CNN + Softmax** | Translation invariance via convolutions | | Adaptive Cruise Control (continuous control) | **Deep RL (DDPG / PPO)** | Handles continuous action space (throttle) | | Driver drowsiness detection (binary) | **CNN + LSTM (video sequence)** | Spatial + temporal features from face frames | | Predictive battery-health (regression) | **LSTM / GRU** | Time-series of charge-discharge cycles | | Anomaly detection in CAN-bus (security) | **Isolation Forest / Autoencoder** | Unsupervised; rare attack samples | | Lane-change decision-making | **Q-Network (DQN)** | Discrete action set; high-dim state | | Obstacle clustering (LiDAR point cloud) | **DBSCAN / K-Means** | Density-based; handles arbitrary cluster shapes | | End-to-end autonomous driving (raw image → steering) | **CNN (NVIDIA PilotNet)** | Direct mapping, no hand-engineered features | | Driver behaviour profiling | **Hidden Markov Model / GMM** | Probabilistic sequence modelling | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 End-to-End ML Pipeline for an Autonomous Vehicle The Mermaid block below renders the full architectural flow — from raw sensor capture to actuation. ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeA["SENSOR LAYER
Cameras LiDAR RADAR Ultrasonic CAN Bus GNSS IMU"] nodeB["DATA PREPROCESSING
Noise Filtering Synchronization Normalization Augmentation"] nodeC["PERCEPTION MODULE
Object Detection Lane Detection Sign Recognition SLAM"] nodeD["FUSION ENGINE
Kalman Filter Bayesian Fusion Transformer Fusion"] nodeE["PREDICTION MODULE
Trajectory Forecasting Behavior Prediction"] nodeF["PLANNING MODULE
Path Planning RL Policy MPC"] nodeG["DECISION LAYER
Rule Based ML Classifier Risk Assessment"] nodeH["ACTUATION LAYER
Steering Throttle Brake HMI Warning"] nodeI["FEEDBACK LOOP
Telemetry to Cloud Over the Air Updates"] nodeA --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeD nodeD --> nodeE nodeE --> nodeF nodeF --> nodeG nodeG --> nodeH nodeH --> nodeI nodeI -.-> nodeC ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram**: The pipeline is largely *sequential*, but the **dotted feedback loop** (bottom) is critical — it represents **OTA (Over-The-Air) learning**, where field data is uploaded to the cloud, models are retrained, and updated weights are pushed back to the vehicle fleet. This is how Tesla, Waymo, and Mercedes continuously improve their perception stacks. --- ## 4.2 ML Algorithm Taxonomy for Automotive Applications ```mermaid flowchart LR root["MACHINE LEARNING FOR AUTOMOTIVE"] root --> sup["SUPERVISED LEARNING"] root --> unsup["UNSUPERVISED LEARNING"] root --> rl["REINFORCEMENT LEARNING"] sup --> sup1["Linear and Logistic Regression"] sup --> sup2["Decision Trees and Random Forest"] sup --> sup3["SVM"] sup --> sup4["CNN for Vision Tasks"] sup --> sup5["RNN LSTM GRU for Time Series"] unsup --> unsup1["K Means Clustering"] unsup --> unsup2["DBSCAN"] unsup --> unsup3["Autoencoder"] unsup --> unsup4["Isolation Forest"] rl --> rl1["Q Learning"] rl --> rl2["Deep Q Network DQN"] rl --> rl3["Policy Gradient REINFORCE"] rl --> rl4["Actor Critic PPO DDPG"] ``` --- ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a CNN-Based Object Detector ```mermaid flowchart TB img["INPUT IMAGE
Resolution 640 by 640 by 3"] c1["CONV BLOCK 1
Conv 3x3 ReLU MaxPool
Stride 2 Feature Maps 64"] c2["CONV BLOCK 2
Conv 3x3 ReLU MaxPool
Stride 2 Feature Maps 128"] c3["CONV BLOCK 3
Conv 3x3 ReLU MaxPool
Stride 2 Feature Maps 256"] bn["BACKBONE FEATURE MAP
Spatial 20 by 20 by 256"] fpn["FEATURE PYRAMID NETWORK
Multi Scale Fusion P3 P4 P5"] head["DETECTION HEAD
Classification Box Regression"] out["OUTPUT
Bounding Boxes Class Confidence"] img --> c1 --> c2 --> c3 --> bn --> fpn --> head --> out ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **FPN Insight**: The Feature Pyramid Network (FPN) is what enables modern detectors (YOLOv8, Faster R-CNN) to detect **small objects like traffic lights at long range** *and* **large objects like nearby trucks** simultaneously — a critical requirement for automotive safety. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pattern (2024 Scheme)**: Part A tests *remember* and *understand* cognitive levels of Revised Bloom's Taxonomy. Answers should be **3–5 crisp sentences** with one supporting equation or diagram where applicable. ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define Machine Learning in the context of automotive systems. Differentiate between supervised and unsupervised learning with one automotive example each. (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** Machine Learning is a subset of Artificial Intelligence that enables automotive systems to learn decision-making patterns from sensor data without being explicitly programmed. **Supervised learning** uses labelled input-output pairs; an example is training a CNN on thousands of labelled dashcam images to classify vehicles vs. pedestrians. **Unsupervised learning** discovers structure in unlabelled data; an example is using DBSCAN clustering on CAN-bus messages to detect anomalous communication patterns indicative of cyber-attacks. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall**: Students often *describe* ML but forget to *anchor* it to an automotive example. Always include at least one vehicle-specific instance — without it, the answer caps at **2/3 marks**. --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Explain the role of a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) in Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS). Mention any two specific ADAS functions it powers. (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** A CNN is a deep learning architecture that automatically learns hierarchical spatial features (edges → textures → parts → objects) from raw image pixels using convolutional filters, ReLU activations, and pooling layers. In ADAS, CNNs power functions such as **(i) Forward Collision Warning** by detecting and localizing vehicles ahead using bounding-box regression, and **(ii) Lane Departure Warning** via semantic segmentation of lane markings. Modern ADAS implementations rely on CNNs because they outperform classical hand-crafted feature methods (HOG + SVM) on noisy real-world driving imagery. --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > **KTU ESE Pattern**: Each Part B question has **sub-part (a) for 7 marks** and **sub-part (b) for 7 marks**. Students attempt **one full question** (a + b). Marks reward both *conceptual clarity* and *technical depth*. --- ### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) #### Part (a) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]` **With a neat block diagram, explain the end-to-end pipeline of a Machine Learning-based autonomous perception system. List the role of any three sensor modalities used. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** The autonomous perception pipeline converts raw sensor data into a structured understanding of the driving scene. The block diagram is: ``` [ Sensors ] → [ Pre-processing ] → [ Feature Extraction / CNN ] → [ Object Detection / Segmentation ] → [ Tracking & Prediction ] → [ Scene Understanding Output ] ``` 1. **Camera (RGB)**: Provides rich 2D texture data at high resolution. Role: lane-marking segmentation, traffic-sign classification, pedestrian detection. Range ≈ **50–100 m**. 2. **LiDAR**: Emits laser pulses and measures time-of-flight to produce a 3D point cloud. Role: precise obstacle localization, 3D bounding boxes. Range ≈ **200 m**, accuracy ±**2 cm**. 3. **RADAR**: Uses radio waves (typically **76–77 GHz**). Role: robust velocity estimation via Doppler shift, used in adaptive cruise control and blind-spot detection. Performs well in fog/rain. The pre-processing stage filters noise (Gaussian smoothing, outlier rejection), synchronizes timestamps, and normalizes intensity. The CNN-based feature extractor learns multi-scale features (FPN-based backbone), and the detection head outputs bounding boxes $(x, y, w, h)$ and class probabilities via softmax. Finally, a multi-object tracker (e.g., SORT or DeepSORT) associates detections across frames using the Hungarian algorithm, and an LSTM-based predictor forecasts future trajectories for each tracked agent. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall**: Many students forget to mention **sensor fusion** explicitly. In an autonomous system, no sensor works in isolation — **score +1 bonus** if you mention Kalman / Bayesian fusion in the pre-processing stage. #### Part (b) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Derive the gradient descent update rule for the weights of a single-layer linear regression model using MSE loss. Show how the bias term is updated separately. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Model Definition**: Given input $\mathbf{x}_i \in \mathbb{R}^n$ and target $y_i$, the prediction is $\hat{y}_i = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b$. **Step 2 — MSE Loss**: $$J(\mathbf{w}, b) = \frac{1}{2N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\left( y_i - \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i - b \right)^2$$ **Step 3 — Gradient w.r.t. $w_j$**: $$\frac{\partial J}{\partial w_j} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\left( \hat{y}_i - y_i \right) x_{ij}$$ *Reasoning*: Differentiating $(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ gives $-2(y_i - \hat{y}_i)\frac{\partial \hat{y}_i}{\partial w_j} = -2(y_i - \hat{y}_i)x_{ij}$. The chain-rule factor $\partial \hat{y}_i / \partial w_j = x_{ij}$ enters because $\hat{y}_i$ is linear in $w_j$. **Step 4 — Gradient w.r.t. $b$**: $$\frac{\partial J}{\partial b} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\left( \hat{y}_i - y_i \right)$$ **Step 5 — Update Equations**: $$w_j^{\text{new}} = w_j^{\text{old}} - \alpha \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\left( \hat{y}_i - y_i \right) x_{ij}$$ $$b^{\text{new}} = b^{\text{old}} - \alpha \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\left( \hat{y}_i - y_i \right)$$ **Step 6 — Convergence**: Iterations continue until $\vert \nabla J \vert < \epsilon$ (typically $\epsilon = 10^{-6}$) or until the validation MSE plateaus. > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake**: Forgetting the **negative sign** in the update (students sometimes *add* the gradient instead of subtracting). Loss should *decrease*, so we move *opposite* to the gradient. KTU examiners explicitly check this sign convention — a wrong sign costs **2 marks**. --- ### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice #### Part (a) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]` **Explain the Q-Learning algorithm for reinforcement learning. Derive its update equation from the Bellman optimality equation and discuss its application to an Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) system. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — MDP Formulation for ACC**: * State $s$: $[v_{\text{ego}}, v_{\text{leader}}, d_{\text{gap}}]$. * Action $a \in \{\text{accelerate}, \text{maintain}, \text{brake}\}$. * Reward $r$: +1 if $d_{\text{gap}} \geq d_{\text{safe}}$ (typically $d_{\text{safe}} = 1.5$ s time-headway), $-10$ on collision. **Step 2 — Bellman Optimality**: $$Q^*(s,a) = \mathbb{E}\!\left[ r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^*(s',a') \mid s,a \right]$$ **Step 3 — Sample-Based Update** (deriving from the expectation): $$Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha \Big[ r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s',a') - Q(s,a) \Big]$$ where $\alpha$ is the learning rate, $\gamma$ the discount factor (typical $\gamma = 0.99$). **Step 4 — Application to ACC**: The agent learns an optimal policy $\pi^*(s) = \arg\max_a Q(s,a)$ that maximizes long-term safety and comfort. After training in the CARLA simulator across millions of kilometres, the Q-table (or Deep Q-Network for continuous states) is deployed on the ECU. The inference time is typically **< 10 ms**, satisfying real-time constraints. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall**: Many students confuse the **on-policy SARSA update** with **off-policy Q-Learning**. Q-Learning uses $\max_{a'} Q(s', a')$ (greedy), while SARSA uses the action actually taken $a'$. Mixing them up costs **2 marks**. #### Part (b) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Compare and contrast CNN, RNN, and Reinforcement Learning algorithms with respect to their automotive applications. Give one specific use case for each. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** | Aspect | CNN | RNN / LSTM | Reinforcement Learning | |---|---|---|---| | **Data Type** | Spatial (images, point-cloud projections) | Temporal sequences | Sequential decision-making | | **Memory** | Stateless (feed-forward) | Stateful (hidden state carries past info) | Stateful (policy + value function) | | **Training Signal** | Labelled images (supervised) | Labelled sequences (supervised) | Reward signal (no labels) | | **Automotive Use** | Object detection (pedestrian, vehicle) | Driver behaviour, battery SOH prediction | ACC, lane-change, path planning | | **Compute Cost** | High at training, low at inference | Moderate (sequential bottleneck) | Very high (needs simulation) | | **Real-Time Latency** | < 50 ms on Jetson Orin | < 100 ms | < 10 ms after training | **CNN — Object Detection**: YOLOv8 processes a dashcam frame in ~7 ms and outputs bounding boxes for vehicles/pedestrians. **RNN / LSTM — Battery State-of-Health (SOH) Prediction**: An LSTM trained on charge-discharge cycle data forecasts remaining useful life with **< 3% RMSE** error. **RL — Lane-Change Decision**: A DQN agent in a SUMO traffic simulator learns when to initiate a lane change based on surrounding traffic, achieving **> 95%** collision-free maneuvers after 1 million training episodes. > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake**: Students often write that "RNNs are used for image data." They are **not** — RNNs handle temporal sequences. **CNNs handle spatial data**. This confusion costs **1–2 marks** in comparative questions. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: ML for Automotive** * ✅ ML enables vehicles to **learn from data** instead of relying on hand-coded rules. * ✅ Three paradigms: **Supervised, Unsupervised, Reinforcement** — each maps to specific automotive tasks (see Algorithm-Selection Matrix in Section 3.4). * ✅ **CNN** = king of perception (camera-based object detection, lane, sign). * ✅ **RNN / LSTM** = king of temporal prediction (battery health, driver behaviour). * ✅ **RL (DQN/PPO)** = king of decision-making under uncertainty (ACC, lane-change). * ✅ Key metrics: **IoU** for bounding boxes, **MSE/MAE** for regression, **Cross-Entropy** for classification, **Cumulative Reward** for RL. * ✅ Gradient Descent update: $\theta \leftarrow \theta - \alpha \nabla L$ — **always subtract** the gradient. * ✅ Backpropagation is the **chain rule applied recursively** through network layers. * ✅ Q-Learning update: $Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha [r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s',a') - Q(s,a)]$. * ✅ Inference latency budget in ADAS: **< 50 ms** for Level 2 autonomy. * ✅ Real datasets: **KITTI, nuScenes, Waymo Open, Cityscapes** — know at least 2. * ✅ Sensor modalities: Camera (~100 m), LiDAR (~200 m), RADAR (~250 m, weather-robust), Ultrasonic (<5 m). * ✅ Always mention **sensor fusion** in pipeline questions — bonus marks guaranteed. * ✅ ReLU is the default activation for hidden layers; **Softmax** for multi-class output; **Sigmoid** for binary output.

topics.title: Supervised Learning - Algorithms: Regression
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT865 - ADVANCED VEHICLE TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Supervised Learning - Algorithms: Regression

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Supervised Learning** is a paradigm of Machine Learning (ML) in which an algorithm is trained on a **labelled dataset** — meaning each input sample is paired with a known output (target). The model learns a mapping function $f: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ that can predict the output for *new, unseen* inputs.

Within supervised learning, the two principal task families are:

| Task Type | Output Nature | Example in Vehicle Tech |
|---|---|---|
| **Classification** | Discrete categorical label | Fault diagnosis (Normal / Faulty) |
| **Regression** | Continuous numerical value | Predicting fuel consumption in **km/L** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Regression** is the supervised learning task where the target variable $\mathcal{Y}$ is a **continuous real-valued quantity**. The goal is to model the relationship between independent variables (features) and a dependent variable (response) so that future observations can be predicted quantitatively.

In the context of **Advanced Vehicle Technology**, regression is a foundational tool for:
- **Predictive Maintenance** — forecasting remaining useful life (RUL) of components like brake pads, batteries, and tyres.
- **Energy Management** — estimating State-of-Charge (SOC) of EV batteries and fuel consumption of ICE vehicles.
- **Emissions Modelling** — predicting $\text{CO}_2$, $\text{NO}_x$, and particulate matter output.
- **Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS)** — estimating time-to-collision (TTC) and safe following distance.
- **Vehicle Dynamics** — modelling tyre-road friction coefficients and suspension responses.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Experienced Driver" Mental Model

Imagine teaching a *new driver* to estimate fuel consumption. You would:
1. Show the driver **hundreds of trips** with known outcomes (this is the *labelled training data*).
2. The driver observes patterns — "highway driving at constant 60 km/h with light load gives ~18 km/L; city driving with AC on gives ~10 km/L" (this is *learning*).
3. On a **new trip**, the driver uses past experience to *predict* fuel use (this is *inference*).

The "new driver" is your **regression model**. The trip logs are your **training data**, and the predicted km/L is the **continuous regression output**. The driver's internal mental model is analogous to the *learned function parameters* $\theta$.

## 1.3 Physical / Standard Constants & Metrics

The following standard metrics are used throughout regression modelling and are universally accepted in KTU evaluation:

- **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** — measured in (units of $y$)$^2$
- **Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE)** — measured in (units of $y$)
- **Mean Absolute Error (MAE)** — measured in (units of $y$)
- **Coefficient of Determination ($R^2$)** — dimensionless, range $[0, 1]$ (or $(-\infty, 1]$ in some cases)
- **Learning Rate ($\alpha$)** — dimensionless hyperparameter, typically $10^{-4} \le \alpha \le 10^{-1}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Regression Algorithms

The major regression algorithms relevant to the KTU 2024 syllabus and vehicle technology applications are:

### 2.1.1 Linear Regression
Models a **linear relationship** between input $x$ and output $y$.

$$
\hat{y} = w_0 + w_1 x
$$

where $w_0$ is the **intercept** (bias) and $w_1$ is the **slope** (weight).

**Vehicle Tech Example:** Predicting fuel consumption (km/L) as a linear function of average vehicle speed.

### 2.1.2 Multiple Linear Regression (MLR)
Extends linear regression to multiple independent variables.

$$
\hat{y} = w_0 + w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + \cdots + w_n x_n
$$

**Vehicle Tech Example:** Predicting fuel consumption from speed, road gradient, payload, ambient temperature, and tyre pressure simultaneously.

### 2.1.3 Polynomial Regression
Captures **non-linear relationships** by including higher-order terms of the features.

$$
\hat{y} = w_0 + w_1 x + w_2 x^2 + \cdots + w_d x^d
$$

**Vehicle Tech Example:** Modelling brake pad wear, which is non-linear with respect to time/temperature.

### 2.1.4 Ridge Regression (L2 Regularization)
Adds a penalty term proportional to the **squared magnitude** of weights to combat overfitting and multicollinearity.

$$
J(\mathbf{w}) = \text{MSE}(\mathbf{w}) + \lambda \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i^2
$$

where $\lambda$ is the **regularization parameter**.

### 2.1.5 Lasso Regression (L1 Regularization)
Uses the **absolute value** of weights as the penalty, promoting sparsity (feature selection).

$$
J(\mathbf{w}) = \text{MSE}(\mathbf{w}) + \lambda \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert w_i \vert
$$

### 2.1.6 Logistic Regression (for completeness — Classification, NOT regression in output)
Despite its name, logistic regression outputs a **probability** for classification tasks. Mentioned only to disambiguate — it is **not** a regression algorithm in the KTU sense.

## 2.2 The Supervised Learning Workflow

The end-to-end pipeline of any supervised regression task follows these structured steps:

1. **Problem Definition** — Identify the vehicle parameter to be predicted.
2. **Data Collection** — Acquire historical sensor/operational data (CAN bus, OBD-II, telematics).
3. **Data Pre-processing** — Handle missing values, outliers, scale features (Standardization / Normalization).
4. **Train-Test Split** — Typically 70:30 or 80:20 ratio.
5. **Model Selection** — Choose appropriate regression algorithm based on data linearity and dimensionality.
6. **Model Training** — Optimize weights $\mathbf{w}$ by minimizing the cost function $J(\mathbf{w})$.
7. **Model Evaluation** — Use RMSE, MAE, $R^2$ on the test set.
8. **Hyperparameter Tuning** — Adjust $\alpha$, $\lambda$, polynomial degree using cross-validation.
9. **Deployment** — Integrate into ECU, ADAS, or cloud telematics platform.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Regression in Vehicle Technology?** Modern vehicles generate **terabytes** of data per day. Regression models enable **real-time estimation** of critical parameters (SOC, tyre pressure, fuel use) without expensive physical sensors, supporting the **CASE** paradigm (Connected, Autonomous, Shared, Electrified).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Simple Linear Model | $\hat{y} = w_0 + w_1 x$ | $w_0$: intercept, $w_1$: slope |
| 2 | Multiple Linear Model | $\hat{y} = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + w_0$ | $\mathbf{w} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$, $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ |
| 3 | Mean Squared Error (MSE) | $\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m}(y^{(i)} - \hat{y}^{(i)})^2$ | $m$: number of samples |
| 4 | Root MSE | $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\text{MSE}}$ | Same units as $y$ |
| 5 | Mean Absolute Error | $\text{MAE} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \vert y^{(i)} - \hat{y}^{(i)} \vert$ | Same units as $y$ |
| 6 | $R^2$ Score | $R^2 = 1 - \frac{\sum (y - \hat{y})^2}{\sum (y - \bar{y})^2}$ | $\bar{y}$: mean of true values |
| 7 | Normal Equation | $\mathbf{w} = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T \mathbf{y}$ | Closed-form solution |
| 8 | Gradient Descent Update | $w_j := w_j - \alpha \frac{\partial J}{\partial w_j}$ | $\alpha$: learning rate |
| 9 | GD Update (MSE form) | $w_j := w_j - \alpha \frac{2}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m}(\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)}) x_j^{(i)}$ | Vectorized form |
| 10 | Ridge Cost | $J_{\text{ridge}} = \text{MSE} + \lambda \sum w_i^2$ | $\lambda \ge 0$ |
| 11 | Lasso Cost | $J_{\text{lasso}} = \text{MSE} + \lambda \sum \vert w_i \vert$ | $\lambda \ge 0$ |
| 12 | Hypothesis Space (Poly) | $\hat{y} = \sum_{k=0}^{d} w_k x^k$ | $d$: polynomial degree |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Note:** In every regression answer, the examiner will look for: (i) clear statement of hypothesis, (ii) definition of cost function, (iii) derivation of the update rule, and (iv) the final expression for $\mathbf{w}$. Skipping any of these four typically costs **2–3 marks**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Normal Equation for Simple Linear Regression

**Given:** A dataset of $m$ samples $\{(x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})\}_{i=1}^{m}$ with hypothesis $\hat{y}^{(i)} = w_0 + w_1 x^{(i)}$.

**Goal:** Find $w_0$ and $w_1$ that minimize the Mean Squared Error.

### Step 1 — Define the Cost Function (MSE)

$$
J(w_0, w_1) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m}\left(\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)}\right)^2
$$

**Explanation:** The factor $\frac{1}{2}$ is a mathematical convenience — it cancels the "2" produced later during differentiation. The MSE penalizes large errors quadratically, making the model sensitive to outliers.

### Step 2 — Expand the Squared Term

Substituting $\hat{y}^{(i)} = w_0 + w_1 x^{(i)}$:

$$
J(w_0, w_1) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m}\left(w_0 + w_1 x^{(i)} - y^{(i)}\right)^2
$$

**Explanation:** We now have a quadratic function in $w_0$ and $w_1$. The global minimum is found where the gradient is zero.

### Step 3 — Compute the Partial Derivative with Respect to $w_0$

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial w_0} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m}\left(w_0 + w_1 x^{(i)} - y^{(i)}\right)
$$

**Explanation:** Using the chain rule on $(w_0 + w_1 x^{(i)} - y^{(i)})^2$ gives $2(w_0 + w_1 x^{(i)} - y^{(i)}) \cdot 1$, and the leading $\frac{1}{2m}$ becomes $\frac{1}{m}$.

### Step 4 — Compute the Partial Derivative with Respect to $w_1$

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial w_1} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m}\left(w_0 + w_1 x^{(i)} - y^{(i)}\right) x^{(i)}
$$

**Explanation:** The inner derivative with respect to $w_1$ introduces the factor $x^{(i)}$.

### Step 5 — Set Both Gradients to Zero (Optimality Condition)

$$
\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m}\left(w_0 + w_1 x^{(i)} - y^{(i)}\right) = 0
$$

$$
\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m}\left(w_0 + w_1 x^{(i)} - y^{(i)}\right) x^{(i)} = 0
$$

**Explanation:** At the minimum of a convex function, the gradient must vanish. This is the **first-order necessary condition** for unconstrained optimization.

### Step 6 — Simplify Using Mean Notation

Let $\bar{x} = \frac{1}{m}\sum x^{(i)}$, $\bar{y} = \frac{1}{m}\sum y^{(i)}$, $\overline{xy} = \frac{1}{m}\sum x^{(i)}y^{(i)}$, $\overline{x^2} = \frac{1}{m}\sum (x^{(i)})^2$.

From Equation 1:

$$
w_0 + w_1 \bar{x} - \bar{y} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad w_0 = \bar{y} - w_1 \bar{x}
$$

**Explanation:** This shows the regression line must pass through the centroid $(\bar{x}, \bar{y})$.

### Step 7 — Solve the Second Equation for $w_1$

Substituting $w_0 = \bar{y} - w_1 \bar{x}$:

$$
\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m}\left(\bar{y} - w_1 \bar{x} + w_1 x^{(i)} - y^{(i)}\right) x^{(i)} = 0
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left(\bar{y} - y^{(i)}\right) x^{(i)} + w_1 \sum_{i=1}^{m}\left(x^{(i)} - \bar{x}\right) x^{(i)} = 0
$$

After algebra (using $\sum (x^{(i)} - \bar{x}) = 0$):

$$
w_1 = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{m}(x^{(i)} - \bar{x})(y^{(i)} - \bar{y})}{\sum_{i=1}^{m}(x^{(i)} - \bar{x})^2}
$$

### Step 8 — Final Closed-Form Expression

$$
\boxed{\,w_1 = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{m}(x^{(i)} - \bar{x})(y^{(i)} - \bar{y})}{\sum_{i=1}^{m}(x^{(i)} - \bar{x})^2} \quad ; \quad w_0 = \bar{y} - w_1 \bar{x}\,}
$$

**Explanation:** This is the **Ordinary Least Squares (OLS)** solution. The numerator is the **covariance** between $x$ and $y$, and the denominator is the **variance** of $x$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Gradient Descent Update Rule

**Given:** Cost function $J(\mathbf{w}) = \frac{1}{2m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}(\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)})^2$.

### Step 1 — Initialize Weights

Start with $\mathbf{w} = \mathbf{0}$ (or random small values).

**Explanation:** Gradient descent is iterative, so we need an initial guess.

### Step 2 — Compute the Gradient of $J$ with Respect to $w_j$

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial w_j} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m}(\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)}) x_j^{(i)}
$$

**Explanation:** This is the partial derivative derived in Step 4 of the previous section.

### Step 3 — Update Rule (Simultaneous Update)

$$
w_j^{\text{new}} := w_j^{\text{old}} - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m}(\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)}) x_j^{(i)}
$$

**Explanation:** The "$-$\,$\alpha$" sign means we move *opposite* to the gradient (downhill). $\alpha$ controls step size.

### Step 4 — Convergence Criterion

Iterate until $\vert J^{(t)} - J^{(t-1)} \vert < \epsilon$ (typically $\epsilon = 10^{-6}$).

**Explanation:** When the cost stops decreasing significantly, we have reached (approximately) the global minimum for a convex problem.

### Step 5 — Variants of Gradient Descent

| Variant | Update Basis | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Batch GD** | All $m$ samples | Stable convergence | Slow for large $m$ |
| **Stochastic GD (SGD)** | 1 sample per update | Fast, escapes local minima | Noisy convergence |
| **Mini-Batch GD** | $b$ samples ($b < m$) | Balance of speed and stability | Needs batch size tuning |

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Matrix Form of Multiple Linear Regression

**Given:** Design matrix $X \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times (n+1)}$ (with a column of 1s for the bias), target vector $\mathbf{y} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times 1}$.

### Step 1 — Vectorized Hypothesis

$$
\hat{\mathbf{y}} = X \mathbf{w}
$$

**Explanation:** Each row of $X$ is a feature vector; matrix multiplication computes all $m$ predictions simultaneously.

### Step 2 — Vectorized Cost Function

$$
J(\mathbf{w}) = \frac{1}{2m}(X\mathbf{w} - \mathbf{y})^T (X\mathbf{w} - \mathbf{y})
$$

**Explanation:** The inner product $(X\mathbf{w} - \mathbf{y})^T(X\mathbf{w} - \mathbf{y})$ is the squared Euclidean norm of the residual vector, scaled by $\frac{1}{2m}$.

### Step 3 — Compute the Gradient

$$
\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} J = \frac{1}{m} X^T (X\mathbf{w} - \mathbf{y})
$$

**Explanation:** Differentiating the quadratic form $(X\mathbf{w} - \mathbf{y})^T(X\mathbf{w} - \mathbf{y})$ w.r.t. $\mathbf{w}$ using the standard rule $\nabla (A\mathbf{w})^T(B\mathbf{w}) = (A+B)^T \mathbf{w}$ gives $2X^T(X\mathbf{w} - \mathbf{y})$, then $\frac{1}{m}$ remains.

### Step 4 — Set the Gradient to Zero

$$
X^T (X\mathbf{w} - \mathbf{y}) = 0
$$

### Step 5 — Solve for $\mathbf{w}$ (Normal Equation)

$$
X^T X \mathbf{w} = X^T \mathbf{y}
$$

Assuming $X^T X$ is invertible:

$$
\boxed{\,\mathbf{w} = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T \mathbf{y}\,}
$$

**Explanation:** This is the OLS solution in matrix form. Computational cost is $O(n^3)$ due to the matrix inverse — motivating the use of gradient descent for high-dimensional problems (e.g., 1000+ sensor features in a modern vehicle).

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Polynomial Regression Transformation

**Given:** A dataset that exhibits non-linear behaviour, e.g., battery capacity fade vs. charge cycles.

### Step 1 — Define the Polynomial Hypothesis of Degree $d$

$$
\hat{y} = w_0 + w_1 x + w_2 x^2 + \cdots + w_d x^d
$$

### Step 2 — Augment the Feature Vector

Create a new feature vector $\mathbf{x}' \in \mathbb{R}^{d+1}$:

$$
\mathbf{x}' = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ x \\ x^2 \\ \vdots \\ x^d \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Linear Regression Formula

Since the polynomial model is **linear in the parameters** $\mathbf{w}$, the same OLS solution applies:

$$
\mathbf{w} = (X'^T X')^{-1} X'^T \mathbf{y}
$$

where the design matrix $X'$ has rows $\mathbf{x}'^{(i)T}$.

**Explanation:** Polynomial regression is fundamentally *linear regression* in a transformed feature space. The non-linearity is in the *features*, not in the *parameters* — this is the key insight for KTU answers.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Vehicle Fuel Consumption

**Scenario:** A car manufacturer collects the following data of average speed (km/h) vs. fuel consumption (km/L):

| Speed $x$ (km/h) | 30 | 50 | 70 | 90 | 110 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| FE $y$ (km/L) | 12.0 | 16.5 | 18.0 | 17.0 | 15.0 |

**Task:** Fit a linear regression model using the Normal Equation.

### Step 1 — Compute Means

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{30 + 50 + 70 + 90 + 110}{5} = 70
$$

$$
\bar{y} = \frac{12.0 + 16.5 + 18.0 + 17.0 + 15.0}{5} = 15.7
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Covariance and Variance Terms

$$
\sum (x^{(i)} - \bar{x})(y^{(i)} - \bar{y}) = (-40)(-3.7) + (-20)(0.8) + (0)(2.3) + (20)(1.3) + (40)(-0.7)
$$

$$
= 148 - 16 + 0 + 26 - 28 = 130
$$

$$
\sum (x^{(i)} - \bar{x})^2 = 1600 + 400 + 0 + 400 + 1600 = 4000
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Weights

$$
w_1 = \frac{130}{4000} = 0.0325
$$

$$
w_0 = 15.7 - (0.0325)(70) = 15.7 - 2.275 = 13.425
$$

### Step 4 — Final Model

$$
\boxed{\,\hat{y} = 13.425 + 0.0325 x\,}
$$

**Interpretation:** The intercept $w_0 = 13.425$ km/L is the baseline fuel efficiency at $x=0$ (theoretical). The slope $w_1 = 0.0325$ km/L per (km/h) means each 1 km/h speed increase gives 0.0325 km/L more efficiency — but this holds only in the linear regime (30–70 km/h). Above ~70 km/h, aerodynamic drag dominates and the relationship becomes polynomial, justifying higher-order models.

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU Board will award **full marks** only if the working is shown with proper summation tables, mean calculations, and a clear boxed final answer. Skipping the intermediate table typically loses **2 marks**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Supervised Learning Workflow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Vehicle Telematics Data Source] --> A1[CAN Bus / OBD-II / Sensors]
    A1 --> A2[Raw Data Acquisition]
    A2 --> A3[Data Cleaning and Imputation]
    A3 --> A4[Feature Engineering and Scaling]
    A4 --> A5[Train Test Split 80 colon 20]
    A5 --> A6{Regression Model Selection}
    A6 -->|Linear| B1[Linear Regression]
    A6 -->|Multiple| B2[Multiple Linear Regression]
    A6 -->|Non Linear| B3[Polynomial Regression]
    A6 -->|High Dim| B4[Ridge or Lasso Regression]
    B1 --> C1[Cost Function MSE]
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    B4 --> C2[Regularized Cost]
    C1 --> D1[Gradient Descent Optimizer]
    C2 --> D1
    D1 --> E1[Trained Model with weights w]
    E1 --> F1[Evaluation RMSE MAE R2]
    F1 --> G1{Performance Acceptable}
    G1 -->|No| H1[Hyperparameter Tuning]
    H1 --> A6
    G1 -->|Yes| I1[Deployment to ECU or ADAS]
    I1 --> J1[Real Time Vehicle Prediction]
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Taxonomy of Regression Algorithms

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R0[Regression Algorithms]
    R0 --> R1[Linear Family]
    R0 --> R2[Non Linear Family]
    R0 --> R3[Regularized Family]
    R0 --> R4[Robust Family]
    R1 --> R1a[Simple Linear Regression]
    R1 --> R1b[Multiple Linear Regression]
    R2 --> R2a[Polynomial Regression]
    R2 --> R2b[Decision Tree Regression]
    R2 --> R2c[Support Vector Regression]
    R3 --> R3a[Ridge Regression L2]
    R3 --> R3b[Lasso Regression L1]
    R3 --> R3c[Elastic Net L1 plus L2]
    R4 --> R4a[Huber Regression]
    R4 --> R4b[Quantile Regression]
    R1a --> A1[Vehicle Use: Speed vs FE]
    R1b --> A2[Vehicle Use: Multi Param MPG]
    R2a --> A3[Vehicle Use: Brake Pad Wear]
    R2b --> A4[Vehicle Use: Battery SOC]
    R3a --> A5[Vehicle Use: Multicollinear Sensors]
    R3b --> A6[Vehicle Use: Feature Selection]
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Gradient Descent Convergence Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Initialize weights w0 and bias w1 randomly]
    S0 --> S1[Compute predictions yhat for all samples]
    S1 --> S2[Compute Cost J of w using MSE]
    S2 --> S3[Compute Gradients partial J partial w0 and partial J partial w1]
    S3 --> S4[Update w0 and w1 simultaneously using alpha]
    S4 --> S5{Convergence check abs J new minus J old less than epsilon}
    S5 -->|No| S1
    S5 -->|Yes| S6[Return optimal weights]
    S6 --> S7[Predict output for new vehicle data]
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Bias-Variance Tradeoff in Vehicle Models

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V0[Bias Variance Tradeoff]
    V0 --> V1[High Bias Underfitting]
    V0 --> V2[High Variance Overfitting]
    V1 --> V1a[Linear Model on Curved Data]
    V1a --> V1b[Poor Train and Test Performance]
    V2 --> V2a[High Degree Polynomial on Small Data]
    V2a --> V2b[Excellent Train Poor Test]
    V0 --> V3[Optimal Balance]
    V3 --> V3a[Regularization Ridge or Lasso]
    V3a --> V3b[Good Generalization to New Vehicle Data]
```
