<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**"Differentiate between supervised and unsupervised learning with suitable examples from the automotive domain."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Supervised Learning | Unsupervised Learning |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Labels** | Uses labelled data (input-output pairs) | Uses unlabelled data (only inputs) |
| **Goal** | Learn a mapping $f: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ | Discover hidden patterns / groupings |
| **Automotive Example 1** | Predicting fuel consumption from speed and load (Regression) | Grouping drivers into aggressive / normal / eco clusters (Clustering) |
| **Automotive Example 2** | Classifying battery cells as healthy / faulty (Classification) | Detecting anomalies in CAN bus traffic (Anomaly Detection) |
| **Output** | Predicted label or value | Cluster ID, reduced dimensions, or density estimate |

(1 mark for clear definition of each, 1 mark for the comparison table, 1 mark for valid automotive examples.)

---

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**"Define the Mean Squared Error (MSE) cost function used in linear regression. Why is it preferred over MAE in gradient-based optimization?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** MSE is defined as the average of the squared differences between predicted and actual values:

$$
\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m}\left(\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)}\right)^2
$$

**Why preferred over MAE (1.5 marks):**
1. **Differentiability** — MSE is continuously differentiable everywhere, while MAE is not differentiable at zero, making gradient computation problematic.
2. **Smooth gradient** — MSE gives a well-defined gradient that scales with error magnitude, enabling faster convergence.
3. **Mathematical convenience** — The square term amplifies larger errors, which is desirable in safety-critical vehicle applications (e.g., braking distance prediction).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Choice-Based)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** For 14-mark regression questions, students commonly lose marks for:
> 1. **Skipping the hypothesis statement** $h_\theta(x) = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x$ before starting derivation.
> 2. **Not mentioning the cost function** $J(\theta_0, \theta_1)$ explicitly with the $\frac{1}{2m}$ factor.
> 3. **Confusing the symbols** $w$ and $\theta$ — use **$w$** consistently in KTU 2024.
> 4. **Forgetting the closed-form box** for the Normal Equation — must be boxed for full marks.
> 5. **Omitting the regularization term** derivation in Ridge/Lasso questions.
> 6. **Not connecting to a vehicle tech example** — earns at least 1–2 application marks.

---

### Question B-A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the Normal Equation for simple linear regression. Clearly state the hypothesis, cost function, and optimality conditions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable vehicle technology example, explain the multiple linear regression model. How does it extend simple linear regression? Discuss the matrix form. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 — Hypothesis (1 mark):**

$$
\hat{y}^{(i)} = w_0 + w_1 x^{(i)}
$$

**Step 2 — Cost Function (1 mark):**

$$
J(w_0, w_1) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m}\left(\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)}\right)^2
$$

**Step 3 — Partial Derivatives (2 marks):**

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial w_0} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m}\left(\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)}\right)
$$

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial w_1} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m}\left(\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)}\right) x^{(i)}
$$

**Step 4 — Setting to Zero and Solving (2 marks):**

Using mean notation and algebraic manipulation (as derived in Section 3.1), we obtain:

$$
w_1 = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{m}(x^{(i)} - \bar{x})(y^{(i)} - \bar{y})}{\sum_{i=1}^{m}(x^{(i)} - \bar{x})^2}, \quad w_0 = \bar{y} - w_1 \bar{x}
$$

**Step 5 — Final Boxed Expression (1 mark):**

$$
\boxed{\,\mathbf{w} = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T \mathbf{y}\,}
$$

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Multiple Linear Regression Definition (2 marks):** MLR models the relationship between **two or more** independent variables and a continuous dependent variable:

$$
\hat{y} = w_0 + w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + \cdots + w_n x_n
$$

**Vehicle Technology Example (2 marks):** Predicting the fuel consumption ($y$ in km/L) of a commercial truck using:
- $x_1$ = average speed (km/h)
- $x_2$ = road gradient (%)
- $x_3$ = payload (tonnes)
- $x_4$ = ambient temperature (°C)
- $x_5$ = tyre pressure (psi)

The fitted model might be:
$$
\hat{y} = 12.5 + 0.05 x_1 - 0.8 x_2 - 0.6 x_3 - 0.02 x_4 + 0.1 x_5
$$

Interpretation: Higher speed helps FE, while road gradient and payload hurt it (negative coefficients).

**Matrix Form (3 marks):** Stacking the data into the design matrix:

$$
X = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & x_1^{(1)} & x_2^{(1)} & \cdots & x_n^{(1)} \\ 1 & x_1^{(2)} & x_2^{(2)} & \cdots & x_n^{(2)} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ 1 & x_1^{(m)} & x_2^{(m)} & \cdots & x_n^{(m)} \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{w} = \begin{bmatrix} w_0 \\ w_1 \\ \vdots \\ w_n \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{y} = \begin{bmatrix} y^{(1)} \\ y^{(2)} \\ \vdots \\ y^{(m)} \end{bmatrix}
$$

The compact form is $\hat{\mathbf{y}} = X\mathbf{w}$, and the OLS solution is the same Normal Equation as derived in (a).

---

### Question B-B — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the Gradient Descent algorithm for linear regression. Derive the update rule for the weights. Discuss the role of the learning rate $\alpha$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With reference to advanced vehicle technology, explain the need for **Ridge** and **Lasso** regression. Derive their cost functions from the standard MSE and discuss when each is preferred. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Algorithm Explanation (2 marks):** Gradient Descent is an **iterative optimization** algorithm that updates the model parameters in the direction of steepest descent of the cost function. The general procedure is:

1. Initialize $\mathbf{w}$ randomly.
2. Compute gradient $\nabla J(\mathbf{w})$.
3. Update: $\mathbf{w} := \mathbf{w} - \alpha \nabla J(\mathbf{w})$.
4. Repeat until convergence.

**Update Rule Derivation (3 marks):** For $J = \frac{1}{2m}\sum (\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)})^2$ with $\hat{y}^{(i)} = w_0 + w_1 x^{(i)}$:

For $w_0$:

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial w_0} = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}(\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)})
$$

$$
w_0 := w_0 - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}(\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)})
$$

For $w_1$:

$$
w_1 := w_1 - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}(\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)}) x^{(i)}
$$

**Role of Learning Rate $\alpha$ (2 marks):**
- If $\alpha$ is **too small** → convergence is very slow, requiring many iterations.
- If $\alpha$ is **too large** → the algorithm may **diverge**, overshooting the minimum.
- The **optimal $\alpha$** is found empirically (typical range $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-1}$).
- For vehicle tech applications, $\alpha$ is often tuned using **learning rate scheduling** (decay) for stable convergence in real-time SOC estimation.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Need for Regularization in Vehicle Technology (2 marks):** Modern vehicles have **hundreds of sensors** generating highly correlated features (e.g., wheel speed sensors, multiple temperature probes, redundant IMU axes). This causes:
- **Multicollinearity** — unstable weight estimates.
- **Overfitting** — high training accuracy but poor test generalization.
- **High dimensionality** — too many features for limited data.

Regularization addresses these issues by penalizing large weights.

**Ridge Regression (L2) (2 marks):**

$$
J_{\text{ridge}}(\mathbf{w}) = \frac{1}{2m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left(\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)}\right)^2 + \frac{\lambda}{2m}\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j^2
$$

**Lasso Regression (L1) (2 marks):**

$$
J_{\text{lasso}}(\mathbf{w}) = \frac{1}{2m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left(\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)}\right)^2 + \frac{\lambda}{2m}\sum_{j=1}^{n} \vert w_j \vert
$$

**When to Prefer Each (1 mark):**
- **Ridge** — preferred when **all features are believed to contribute** (e.g., predicting emissions from all engine parameters). Shrinks weights but does not eliminate them.
- **Lasso** — preferred for **automatic feature selection** (e.g., identifying which of 50 CAN bus signals are most predictive of battery failure). Drives some weights to **exactly zero**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 — Module 1 Regression**

- [x] **Supervised learning** uses *labelled* data; **regression** predicts *continuous* outputs.
- [x] The hypothesis for simple linear regression is $\hat{y} = w_0 + w_1 x$; the cost is MSE.
- [x] The **Normal Equation** $\mathbf{w} = (X^TX)^{-1}X^T\mathbf{y}$ gives the closed-form OLS solution — cost $O(n^3)$.
- [x] **Gradient Descent** is iterative and scalable; update rule is $w_j := w_j - \alpha \frac{\partial J}{\partial w_j}$.
- [x] Learning rate $\alpha$ must be tuned: too small → slow; too large → divergence.
- [x] **Polynomial regression** is linear in parameters but non-linear in features — same OLS formula applies in transformed space.
- [x] **Ridge (L2)** uses $\lambda \sum w_i^2$ — shrinks weights, good for multicollinearity.
- [x] **Lasso (L1)** uses $\lambda \sum \vert w_i \vert$ — drives some weights to zero, useful for feature selection.
- [x] **MSE** penalizes large errors quadratically; **MAE** is more robust to outliers; **RMSE** has same units as $y$; **$R^2$** measures explained variance (closer to 1 is better).
- [x] **Vehicle tech applications** include fuel consumption prediction, battery SOC estimation, brake pad wear forecasting, emissions modelling, ADAS time-to-collision estimation.
- [x] **Common pitfall:** Always box the final answer (Normal Equation, weights, cost function). Examiners reward clear, structured derivations.
- [x] **Equation sheets in KTU 2024** are not provided — memorize the Normal Equation, Gradient Descent update, Ridge/Lasso cost functions, and evaluation metrics.
- [x] The regression line must pass through the centroid $(\bar{x}, \bar{y})$ — a frequently asked conceptual question.
- [x] **Variants of GD**: Batch (stable, slow), Stochastic (fast, noisy), Mini-Batch (best of both — used in modern vehicle ML pipelines).
- [x] **Feature scaling** (StandardScaler / MinMax) is **mandatory** before gradient descent to ensure fast convergence.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT865 - ADVANCED VEHICLE TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **Advanced Vehicle Technology**, **Classification** refers to the systematic, standardized categorization of automobiles and powered transportation systems into distinct groups based on predefined engineering, regulatory, and operational parameters. These parameters typically include the **prime mover (power source)**, **terrain of operation**, **intended application**, **load-carrying capacity**, **dimensional specifications**, and **energy storage configuration**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Directive (Module 1):**
> Classification forms the foundational taxonomy upon which subsequent modules (electric vehicles, hybrid powertrains, autonomous systems, and alternative fuels) are built. Mastery of this taxonomy is a prerequisite for every deriviation in the course.

The most widely accepted engineering classifications adhere to standards published by bodies such as the **SAE International (Society of Automotive Engineers)**, **ISO (International Organization for Standardization)**, the **MoRTH (Ministry of Road Transport and Highways, India)**, and the **AIS (Automotive Industry Standards)** under the CMVR (Central Motor Vehicles Rules).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **hospital**. Before a doctor prescribes medicine, they first classify the patient's condition — Is it viral or bacterial? Acute or chronic? Mild or severe? Only after this classification can the correct treatment protocol be applied.

Similarly, a **vehicle engineer** must first classify a vehicle before designing its drivetrain, choosing its battery, sizing its motor, or selecting its fuel system. A heavy commercial truck and a city scooter both belong to the broad "automobile" family, but their engineering solutions are *vastly* different — precisely because they fall into different classification buckets.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Takeaway:**
> Classification is essentially a **decision tree** that tells engineers, regulators, and manufacturers: "Given this vehicle's purpose and operating environment, here is the engineering playbook you should follow."

## 1.3 Key Classification Parameters (The "Big Five")

Every modern vehicle classification system pivots on these five parameters:

1. **Prime Mover / Power Source** — IC engine, electric motor, hybrid combination, fuel cell.
2. **Application / Purpose** — Passenger transport, goods carriage, special duty, agricultural, military.
3. **Terrain / Operating Environment** — On-road, off-road, amphibious, aerial, marine.
4. **Load / Capacity Metric** — Gross Vehicle Weight (GVW), payload, seating capacity.
5. **Regulatory Class** — Motor Vehicle classification under the Indian CMVR (L, M, N, O, T categories) or international equivalents.

## 1.4 Standard Indian CMVR Classification (AIS-052 Basis)

The Indian regulatory framework, governed by the **Motor Vehicles Act, 1988** and the **CMVR**, classifies road vehicles into the following broad categories:

| Category Code | Vehicle Class (Official Description) | Example |
|---------------|--------------------------------------|---------|
| **Category L** | Two-, three- and four-wheeled motor vehicles (Mopeds, Motorcycles, Quadricycles) | Hero Splendor, Bajaj Auto, TVS iQube |
| **Category M** | Motor vehicles with at least 4 wheels, used for carriage of passengers | Maruti Swift, Tata Tiago, Volvo Buses |
| **Category N** | Motor vehicles with at least 4 wheels, used for carriage of goods | Tata Ace, Ashok Leyland trucks, Eicher Pro |
| **Category O** | Trailers (including semi-trailers) designed to be towed by M/N category vehicles | Tanker trailers, container trailers |
| **Category T** | Agricultural and forestry tractors (non-road, off-road primarily) | Mahindra 575, John Deere 5310 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction for Exams:**
> - **Category M** = **M**otorized passenger vehicles
> - **Category N** = **N**on-passenger (goods) vehicles
> - The letter "**O**" stands for **O**ther (trailers) — not "Zero"
> - The letter "**T**" denotes agricultural **T**ractors
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Breakdown of Vehicle Classification

### 2.1.1 Classification by Prime Mover (Power Source)

This is the **most critical classification** for an Advanced Vehicle Technology course, as it directly dictates powertrain architecture.

**A. Internal Combustion Engine Vehicles (ICEV)**
- Use petroleum fuels (petrol, diesel) or alternative liquid/gaseous fuels.
- Operate on Otto cycle (petrol/CNG) or Diesel cycle (diesel).
- Subspecies: SI engine vehicles, CI engine vehicles.

**B. Battery Electric Vehicles (BEV)**
- Use electric motors powered exclusively by onboard rechargeable batteries.
- Zero tailpipe emissions.
- Examples: Tata Nexon EV, MG ZS EV, Tesla Model 3.

**C. Hybrid Electric Vehicles (HEV)**
- Combine ICE + Electric Motor + (usually) battery.
- Sub-classes: **Micro-hybrid**, **Mild-hybrid (48V)**, **Full-hybrid**, **Plug-in Hybrid (PHEV)**.

**D. Fuel Cell Electric Vehicles (FCEV)**
- Use hydrogen as fuel, generate electricity onboard via Proton Exchange Membrane (PEM) fuel cell.
- Examples: Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo.

**E. Solar / Wind / Other Renewable**
- Niche, experimental category. Use photovoltaic cells or other renewables.

### 2.1.2 Classification by Body Style (Passenger Vehicles)

| Segment | Common Name | Typical Length (m) | Example |
|---------|-------------|--------------------|---------|
| A | Micro / Hatch (Mini) | < 3.4 | Alto, Kwid |
| B | Compact / Premium Hatch | 3.4 – 3.9 | Swift, i20 |
| C | Sedan / Compact SUV | 4.0 – 4.5 | Honda City, Creta |
| D | Mid-size Sedan / SUV | 4.5 – 4.7 | Camry, Fortuner |
| E | Luxury / Full-size | > 4.7 | BMW 5 Series, Mercedes E |
| F | Ultra-luxury / Sports | Variable | Rolls Royce, Lamborghini |

### 2.1.3 Classification by Number of Wheels

| Wheels | Notation | Common Name |
|--------|----------|-------------|
| 2 | 2W | Motorcycles, scooters |
| 3 | 3W | Auto-rickshaws (passenger/cargo) |
| 4 | 4W | Cars, LCVs, HCVs |
| 6 | 6W | Medium commercial trucks |
| 8+ | Multi-axle | Heavy articulated trucks, buses |
| 18+ | Multi-axle trailers | B-double trucks, road trains |

### 2.1.4 Classification by Terrain / Operating Environment

- **On-Road (Type I)**: Designed for paved surfaces (e.g., hatchbacks, sedans).
- **Off-Road (Type II)**: Equipped with 4WD/4x4, high ground clearance, low-ratio gearbox (e.g., Mahindra Thar, Jeep Wrangler).
- **All-Terrain / Dual-Purpose**: A compromise (e.g., Tata Hexa, Toyota Fortuner).
- **Amphibious**: Can operate on both land and water (military applications).

### 2.1.5 Classification by Drive Configuration

- **2WD (Two-Wheel Drive)**: Front-wheel drive (FWD) or Rear-wheel drive (RWD).
- **4WD (Four-Wheel Drive)**: Driver-selectable, low-range capable.
- **AWD (All-Wheel Drive)**: Always-on, computer-managed torque distribution.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Classification Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is classification based on prime mover so critical?**
> Because every downstream design choice — from motor sizing to battery capacity, from exhaust treatment to thermal management — is governed by the energy storage and conversion technology in use.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does regulatory class (L, M, N, O, T) matter?**
> It determines licensing requirements, permit categories, safety standards (AIS ratings), homologation tests, and taxation (GST slabs in India).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula / Definition | Unit | Notes |
|-----------|----------------------|------|-------|
| **Gross Vehicle Weight (GVW)** | $GVW = \text{Curb Weight} + \text{Payload} + \text{Occupant Mass}$ | kg | Used for LCV/HCV classification |
| **Kerb Weight (Curb Weight)** | $W_{kerb} = W_{chassis} + W_{fluids} + W_{fuel(90\%)} + W_{accessories}$ | kg | Manufacturer-stated |
| **Payload Capacity** | $P = GVW - W_{kerb}$ | kg | Determines commercial class |
| **Power-to-Weight Ratio (PWR)** | $PWR = \dfrac{P_{max}}{W_{kerb}}$ | kW/kg or W/kg | Performance indicator |
| **Specific Energy (Battery)** | $E_{spec} = \dfrac{E_{stored}}{m_{battery}}$ | Wh/kg | Range determinant |
| **Energy Density (Battery Volumetric)** | $E_{vol} = \dfrac{E_{stored}}{V_{battery}}$ | Wh/L | Packaging metric |
| **Battery State of Charge (SoC)** | $SoC = \dfrac{Q_{remaining}}{Q_{rated}} \times 100$ | % | Range of 0–100 |
| **Range (EV)** | $R = E_{stored} \times \eta_{drivetrain} \times \dfrac{1}{E_{consumption}}$ | km | Where $E_{consumption}$ in kWh/km |
| **Regenerative Braking Recovery** | $E_{regen} = \tfrac{1}{2} \, m \, v^{2} \times \eta_{regen}$ | J | Converts KE to electrical |
| **Degree of Hybridization (DOH)** | $DOH = \dfrac{P_{electric}}{P_{electric} + P_{ICE}} \times 100$ | % | 0% = pure ICE, 100% = pure EV |
| **Wheel Torque** | $T_{wheel} = T_{motor} \times G_{ratio} \times \eta_{transmission}$ | Nm | Drivetrain sizing |
| **Maximum Climbing Grade** | $\theta = \arctan\left(\dfrac{T_{wheel} - F_{rolling}}{m \cdot g}\right)$ | degrees or % | Off-road capability |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Emissions Regulations**: ICE vehicles in India must comply with **BS-VI Stage II** norms; classification determines test cycles (IDC for M1, WMTC for L-category).
- **Insurance Premiums**: Vehicle class directly affects third-party insurance brackets.
- **Charging Infrastructure Design**: BEV classification determines connector type (AC Type 2, DC CCS2, Bharat DC-001).
- **Manufacturing Compliance**: Type-approval is impossible without correct classification.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Degree of Hybridization (DOH) Classification

The **Degree of Hybridization (DOH)** is a quantitative metric used to classify a hybrid powertrain along the spectrum from pure ICE to pure electric. It is derived from the power contribution of the electric machine relative to the total propulsion power.

### Step 1 — Define the Total Propulsion Power

The total propulsion power at the wheels is the sum of the ICE power contribution and the Electric Motor power contribution.

$$
P_{total} = P_{ICE} + P_{electric}
$$

**Explanation:** This assumes the ICE and electric machine can each independently (or jointly) deliver mechanical power to the drivetrain. In series hybrids, only $P_{electric}$ is mechanically connected to the wheels; in parallel or series-parallel configurations, both contribute.

### Step 2 — Define the Electric Power Contribution

The electric machine's contribution is the maximum sustained mechanical power it can deliver at the drivetrain input, factoring in the battery's discharge capability.

$$
P_{electric} = \min\left( P_{motor,max}, \; \dfrac{V_{batt} \cdot I_{discharge,max}}{1000} \right)
$$

**Explanation:** The effective electric power is **constrained** by the weaker of two limits: (a) the motor's mechanical rating, or (b) the battery's electrical discharge rate. The $\min()$ function captures this bottleneck.

### Step 3 — Express DOH as a Ratio

$$
DOH = \dfrac{P_{electric}}{P_{ICE} + P_{electric}} \times 100\%
$$

**Explanation:** The denominator is the **total available propulsion power**, and the numerator is the **electrical share**. A 0% DOH means a pure ICEV; 100% means a pure BEV.

### Step 4 — Classify Based on DOH Value

| DOH Range | Classification |
|-----------|----------------|
| $0\%$ | Conventional ICEV (no electrification) |
| $0\% < DOH \leq 10\%$ | Micro-hybrid (start-stop system only) |
| $10\% < DOH \leq 25\%$ | Mild hybrid (48V architecture) |
| $25\% < DOH \leq 50\%$ | Full hybrid (Toyota Hybrid System) |
| $50\% < DOH < 100\%$ | Plug-in hybrid (PHEV) |
| $DOH = 100\%$ | Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV) |

**Engineering Insight:** The TOYOTA PRIUS is a full hybrid with $DOH \approx 30\%$, the CHEVROLET VOLT is a PHEV with $DOH \approx 75\%$, and the TESLA MODEL 3 is a BEV with $DOH = 100\%$.

## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example: Classifying a Commercial Vehicle

**Problem Statement:**
A light commercial vehicle has the following specifications:
- Kerb Weight: $1{,}800$ kg
- Maximum Payload: $1{,}200$ kg
- Engine Power: $75$ kW (Diesel)
- Electric Machine: $20$ kW (Mild Hybrid Assist)
- Drive: Front-Wheel Drive (FWD)
- 4 wheels, monocoque body

**Step 1 — Compute GVW**
$$
GVW = W_{kerb} + P_{payload} = 1{,}800 + 1{,}200 = 3{,}000 \text{ kg}
$$

**Explanation:** GVW is the sum of empty vehicle weight plus maximum legal payload.

**Step 2 — Classify per AIS-052 / CMVR**
Since $GVW = 3{,}000$ kg (i.e., 3 tonnes) and it is used for carriage of goods, it falls under **Category N1** (Goods vehicles up to 3.5 tonnes).

**Step 3 — Compute DOH**
$$
DOH = \dfrac{20}{75 + 20} \times 100 = \dfrac{20}{95} \times 100 \approx 21.05\%
$$

**Step 4 — Classify Hybridization Tier**
Since $10\% < 21.05\% \leq 25\%$, this is a **Mild Hybrid Electric Vehicle (MHEV)**.

**Step 5 — Compute Power-to-Weight Ratio**
$$
PWR = \dfrac{P_{total}}{W_{kerb}} = \dfrac{75{,}000 + 20{,}000}{1{,}800} = \dfrac{95{,}000}{1{,}800} \approx 52.78 \text{ W/kg}
$$

**Step 6 — Classify Drive Type**
FWD (engine torque routed to front wheels) — classify as **2WD-FWD**.

**Final Classification Tag:**
> "**Category N1, Diesel Mild Hybrid (MHEV), 2WD-FWD, GVW 3,000 kg, PWR 52.78 W/kg**"

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Key Vehicle Class Examples

| Vehicle | Class (Reg.) | Body | Prime Mover | DOH | Drive |
|---------|--------------|------|-------------|-----|-------|
| Hero Splendor+ | L1 | 2W Commuter | ICE (Petrol) | 0% | RWD |
| Bajaj RE Auto | L5 | 3W | ICE (CNG) | 0% | RWD |
| Tata Nexon EV | M1 | Sub-4m SUV | BEV | 100% | FWD |
| Toyota Camry Hybrid | M1 | Sedan | HEV (Full) | 30% | FWD |
| MG Hector Plus | M1 | SUV | ICE (Petrol) | 0% | FWD |
| Mahindra Thar | M1 | Off-road SUV | ICE (Diesel) | 0% | 4WD |
| Tata Prima FL 5530.S | N3 | HCV Tractor | ICE (Diesel) | 0% | 6x4 |
| Tata Starbus EV | M3 | Bus | BEV | 100% | RWD |
| Tesla Semi | N3 | Class-8 Truck | BEV | 100% | 6x4 |
| Toyota Mirai | M1 | Sedan | FCEV | 100% | FWD |
| John Deere 6155M | Tractor-T | Ag. Tractor | ICE (Diesel) | 0% | 4WD |
| Ashok Leyland 2820 | N3 | Tipper | ICE (Diesel) | 0% | 6x4 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Master Classification Hierarchy of Road Vehicles

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Road Vehicle] --> B[Category L]
    A --> C[Category M]
    A --> D[Category N]
    A --> E[Category O]
    A --> F[Category T]

    B --> B1[L1 Moped less than 50cc 25kmh]
    B --> B2[L2 Moped greater than 50cc limited]
    B --> B3[L3 Motorcycle no sidecar]
    B --> B4[L4 Motorcycle with sidecar]
    B --> B5[L5 Three wheeled auto rickshaw]
    B --> B6[L6 L7 Quadricycle light and heavy]

    C --> C1[M1 Passenger up to 8 seats]
    C --> C2[M2 Passenger over 8 seats under 5 tonnes]
    C --> C3[M3 Passenger over 8 seats over 5 tonnes]

    D --> D1[N1 Goods under 3.5 tonnes]
    D --> D2[N2 Goods 3.5 to 12 tonnes]
    D --> D3[N3 Goods over 12 tonnes]

    E --> E1[O1 Trailer under 0.75 tonnes]
    E --> E2[O2 Trailer 0.75 to 3.5 tonnes]
    E --> E3[O3 Trailer 3.5 to 10 tonnes]
    E --> E4[O4 Trailer over 10 tonnes]

    F --> F1[T1 Agricultural compact]
    F --> F2[T2 Agricultural standard]
    F --> F3[T3 Agricultural heavy]
    F --> F4[T4 Forestry tractor]
    F --> F5[T5 Special tractor]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Powertrain Classification by Prime Mover

```mermaid
graph TD
    P[Powertrain Classification] --> Q1[Pure ICEV]
    P --> Q2[Battery Electric BEV]
    P --> Q3[Hybrid HEV]
    P --> Q4[Fuel Cell FCEV]
    P --> Q5[Solar PV]
    P --> Q6[Hydrogen ICE]

    Q3 --> R1[Mild Hybrid 10 to 25 percent DOH]
    Q3 --> R2[Full Hybrid 25 to 50 percent DOH]
    Q3 --> R3[Plug in Hybrid 50 to 99 percent DOH]
    Q3 --> R4[Series HEV]
    Q3 --> R5[Parallel HEV]
    Q3 --> R6[Series Parallel HEV]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Vehicle Architecture Block-Level Flow (BEV Example)

```mermaid
graph LR
    G[Grid AC Source] --> H[Onboard Charger]
    H --> I[Battery Pack BMS Controlled]
    I --> J[Power Inverter DC to AC]
    J --> K[Electric Motor PMSM or Induction]
    K --> L[Single Speed Reducer]
    L --> M[Wheels]
    I --> N[12V Auxiliary Battery]
    N --> O[Vehicle Lighting and ECU Bus]
    I --> P[Thermal Management Loop]
    K --> Q[Regenerative Brake Energy]
    Q --> I
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Decision Tree for HEV Tier Classification

```mermaid
graph TD
    S[Start Vehicle Classification] --> T{Drive Motor Present?}
    T -- No --> U[Pure ICEV DOH 0]
    T -- Yes --> V{External Charging Port?}
    V -- No --> W{Battery Size kWh?}
    W -- less than 1 --> X[Micro Hybrid]
    W -- 1 to 5 --> Y[Mild Hybrid 48V]
    W -- greater than 5 --> Z[Full Hybrid]
    V -- Yes --> AA[Plug in Hybrid PHEV]
    Z --> AB[Check Motor Power Share]
    AB -- 25 to 50 percent --> AC[Full Hybrid confirmed]
    AB -- 50 to 99 percent --> AD[Reclassify as PHEV]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Tip for Mermaid Block Diagrams:**
> Each node represents a classification bucket. Follow the arrows (edges) to trace the decision path. The terminal leaf nodes represent the final classified category.
