# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"Differentiate between BEV, HEV, and FCEV in terms of energy source and tailpipe emissions."**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | BEV | HEV | FCEV |
|-----------|-----|-----|------|
| **Primary Energy Source** | Grid electricity (stored in Li-ion battery) | Petrol/Diesel + Battery | Hydrogen gas |
| **Conversion Device** | Electric Motor | ICE + Electric Motor | PEM Fuel Cell + Motor |
| **Charging Method** | Plug-in (AC/DC) | Regenerative + ICE (no plug, except PHEV) | Hydrogen refueling station |
| **Tailpipe Emissions** | **Zero** | Reduced (CO2, NOx) | **Zero (only water vapor)** |
| **Refuel/Recharge Time** | 30 min (DC fast) – 8 h (AC) | 5 min (fuel) | 5–10 min (H2) |
| **Range** | 200–600 km typical | 800–1000 km | 500–700 km |

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**"Explain the AIS-052 classification of motor vehicles under CMVR. List all five categories with one example each."**

**Model Answer:**

The **Automotive Industry Standard 052 (AIS-052)** is a Code of Practice for Type Approval of Road Vehicles under the Central Motor Vehicles Rules (CMVR), 1989. It categorizes vehicles into:

- **Category L**: Powered two-, three-, and four-wheeled vehicles → *Example: Hero Splendor Plus (L1)*
- **Category M**: Motor vehicles with ≥4 wheels for passenger carriage → *Example: Maruti Suzuki Swift (M1)*
- **Category N**: Motor vehicles with ≥4 wheels for carriage of goods → *Example: Tata Ace Gold Diesel (N1)*
- **Category O**: Trailers (including semi-trailers) → *Example: Tanker trailer (O4)*
- **Category T**: Agricultural and forestry tractors → *Example: Mahindra 575 DI (T2)*

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**"With neat sketches, explain in detail the classification of electric and hybrid electric vehicles based on (a) degree of hybridization and (b) powertrain architecture. Also discuss the merits and demerits of each."**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Classification by Degree of Hybridization (DOH)

The Degree of Hybridization is defined as the ratio of electric machine power to the total propulsion power:

$$
DOH = \dfrac{P_{electric}}{P_{electric} + P_{ICE}} \times 100\%
$$

**Classification Tiers:**

1. **Micro-Hybrid (DOH ≤ 10%)**: Equipped with start-stop system. The ICE shuts off at traffic signals. Example: Maruti Suzuki Celerio S-CNG with idle start-stop. 48V architecture absent. Simple, low-cost, marginal fuel economy benefit (~5%).

2. **Mild Hybrid (10% < DOH ≤ 25%)**: 48V belt-driven starter-generator (BSG) provides torque assist during acceleration. Example: **Maruti Suzuki Dzire 48V SHVS** ($DOH \approx 18\%$). Limited electric-only drive capability.

3. **Full Hybrid (25% < DOH ≤ 50%)**: Powerful e-motor enables short electric-only propulsion. Example: **Toyota Camry Hybrid** ($DOH \approx 30\%$) using THS-II. Fuel economy benefit of 30–40%.

4. **Plug-in Hybrid (50% < DOH < 100%)**: Large battery (8–18 kWh) with external charging. Example: **Toyota Prius Prime** ($DOH \approx 70\%$). Pure EV mode for 25–50 km.

5. **Battery Electric Vehicle (DOH = 100%)**: ICE absent. Example: **Tata Nexon EV** ($DOH = 100\%$). Zero tailpipe emissions.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Classification by Powertrain Architecture

**1. Series Hybrid**: ICE drives a generator only; only the electric motor propels the wheels. ICE decoupled from wheels.
- Example: **Chevrolet Volt** (range-extender mode), **Nissan e-Power**.
- Merits: Smooth, simple transmission, ICE runs at constant optimum RPM.
- Demerits: Double energy conversion (mechanical → electrical → mechanical), ~10% efficiency loss.

**2. Parallel Hybrid**: ICE and electric motor both connected to wheels mechanically. Both can propel vehicle simultaneously.
- Example: **Honda Insight (1st gen)**, **Hyundai Ioniq Hybrid**.
- Merits: High efficiency at highway cruising.
- Demerits: Complex transmission coupling, control software intensive.

**3. Series-Parallel (Power-Split) Hybrid**: Combines both topologies using a planetary gearset.
- Example: **Toyota Prius**, **Toyota Camry Hybrid**.
- Merits: Best of both — efficient across all drive cycles.
- Demerits: Most complex, highest cost.

**Block Diagram (Series Hybrid):**
`ICE → Generator → Inverter → Battery ↔ Inverter → Motor → Wheels`

**Block Diagram (Parallel Hybrid):**
`ICE ↔ Clutch ↔ Transmission ↔ Wheels` and `Motor ↔ Transmission ↔ Wheels`

**Block Diagram (Series-Parallel):**
`ICE → Planetary Gear Set → [Path A: Motor-Generator 1] and [Path B: Motor 2] → Wheels`

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Many students incorrectly write that in a **Series Hybrid**, the ICE directly drives the wheels. **WRONG** — in series, the ICE is mechanically decoupled; it only charges the battery or feeds the motor-generator.
> - Students often confuse **4WD** with **AWD** and lose marks. **4WD** is driver-selectable, high-range/low-range; **AWD** is always on, no low range.
> - Do NOT confuse **MHEV** with **HEV** in your answer. MHEV cannot drive in pure electric mode.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2023]

**"Classify road vehicles as per AIS-052 / CMVR. With diagrams, explain the body-style classification of passenger cars and provide a comparative analysis between FWD, RWD, and AWD configurations."**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: AIS-052 Classification (Detailed)

Refer to **Section 1.4** of these notes for the master classification. The five top-level categories are:

$$
\text{Vehicle} \rightarrow
\begin{cases}
L & \text{(2W/3W/light 4W powered)} \\
M & \text{(Passenger, ≥4 wheels)} \\
N & \text{(Goods, ≥4 wheels)} \\
O & \text{(Trailers)} \\
T & \text{(Tractors)}
\end{cases}
$$

Each top-level category has sub-classes based on **GVW (Gross Vehicle Weight)**, **seating capacity**, or **specific use case**. For example:

- **M1**: ≤ 8 seats (including driver) AND ≤ 3.5 tonnes GVW
- **M2**: > 8 seats AND ≤ 5 tonnes GVW
- **M3**: > 8 seats AND > 5 tonnes GVW
- **N1**: ≤ 3.5 tonnes
- **N2**: 3.5–12 tonnes
- **N3**: > 12 tonnes

**Classification Flowchart (Mermaid):**

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0[Road Vehicle AIS 052] --> A1{Passenger or Goods?}
    A1 -- Passenger --> A2{Wheel Count 4 or more?}
    A1 -- Goods --> A3
    A2 -- Yes --> A4{Seating and Weight?}
    A4 -- under 8 seats under 3.5t --> A5[M1]
    A4 -- over 8 seats under 5t --> A6[M2]
    A4 -- over 8 seats over 5t --> A7[M3]
    A3 -- Goods --> A8{Weight Class?}
    A8 -- under 3.5t --> A9[N1]
    A8 -- 3.5 to 12t --> A10[N2]
    A8 -- over 12t --> A11[N3]
```

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Drive Configuration Comparison

| Feature | FWD | RWD | AWD |
|---------|-----|-----|-----|
| **Driven Axle** | Front | Rear | All wheels |
| **Traction (Dry)** | Good | Good | Excellent |
| **Traction (Wet/Snow)** | Very Good | Poor (without LSD) | Excellent |
| **Weight Distribution** | Front-heavy | Balanced (50:50 ideal) | Balanced |
| **Interior Space** | More (no propshaft hump) | Less (transmission tunnel) | Less |
| **Steering Feel** | Torque steer possible | Direct, communicative | Neutral |
| **Handling Limit** | Understeer | Oversteer (playful) | Highest grip |
| **Mechanical Complexity** | Low | Medium | High |
| **Fuel Economy** | Best (typical) | Moderate | Worst |
| **Vehicle Examples** | Honda City, Maruti Swift | BMW 3-Series (rear-bias) | Audi Quattro, Subaru Symmetrical AWD |
| **Common Applications** | Economy hatchbacks, sedans | Sports cars, luxury RWD | Off-road, performance SUVs, rally cars |

**Engineer's Selection Rule of Thumb:**

- FWD: Choose when **cost**, **packaging efficiency**, and **fuel economy** are priorities.
- RWD: Choose when **driving dynamics**, **balanced weight distribution**, and **performance** are priorities.
- AWD: Choose when **traction** is non-negotiable (off-road, snow, racetrack).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **FWD ≠ Front-engine, Front-wheel drive always.** FWD refers to driven wheels, not engine placement.
> - When asked for "examples," provide **at least TWO** per category or lose 1 mark.
> - In the comparison table, ensure you mention **understeer vs. oversteer** — this is a frequently tested concept and skipping it costs 2 marks.
> - AWD does NOT automatically mean off-road capability. Crossover AWDs (e.g., Toyota Hyryder) lack low-range gearing.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist**

- [ ] **Five AIS-052 categories**: **L, M, N, O, T** — remember the *M = Motorized passenger*, *N = Non-passenger (goods)* mnemonic.
- [ ] **DOH formula**: $\text{DOH} = P_{electric} / (P_{electric} + P_{ICE}) \times 100\%$
- [ ] **Hybrid tiers**: Micro (≤10%), Mild (10–25%), Full (25–50%), PHEV (50–99%), BEV (100%).
- [ ] **BEV powertrain flow**: Grid → OBC → Battery → Inverter → Motor → Reducer → Wheels.
- [ ] **Series vs. Parallel vs. Series-Parallel**: Series (ICE decoupled from wheels), Parallel (both coupled), Power-Split (planetary gearset, e.g., Toyota THS).
- [ ] **FCEV** = Hydrogen + Fuel Cell + Electric Motor; emits only water vapor.
- [ ] **GVW** = Kerb Weight + Payload + Occupant Mass. Used to differentiate N1, N2, N3.
- [ ] **M1 = ≤8 seats + ≤3.5t**; **M2 = >8 seats + ≤5t**; **M3 = >8 seats + >5t**.
- [ ] **4WD vs. AWD**: 4WD is selectable with low-range; AWD is always-on, no low range.
- [ ] **Power-to-Weight Ratio (PWR)** is a key performance classifier for high-performance vehicles.
- [ ] **Indian context**: BS-VI Stage II emissions, AIS-052 type approval, FAME-II subsidy eligibility for BEV/PHEV.
- [ ] **Body style segments**: A (Micro), B (Premium Hatch), C (Sedan/Compact SUV), D (Mid-size SUV), E (Luxury), F (Ultra-luxury).
- [ ] **Three-wheelers** (L5) are classified separately from 4-wheelers despite some quadricycles (L6, L7) being very compact.
- [ ] **CMVR stands for** Central Motor Vehicles Rules, the regulatory backbone of Indian automotive classification.
- [ ] **Common exam trap**: Confusing the **O category** (trailers) with the digit "0" (zero). It is the **letter O**, standing for "**Other trailers**."

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip from KTU Valuation Desk:**
> Always support classification answers with **at least one sketch, table, or flowchart**. Pure text answers typically score 60–70% of allotted marks, while structured answers with visuals routinely score 85–95%. Use the Mermaid diagrams from Section 4 as inspiration.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Neural Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAUT865 - ADVANCED VEHICLE TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Neural Networks ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!NOTE] > **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:** > An **Artificial Neural Network (ANN)** is a massively parallel, distributed information-processing system inspired by the biological neural structure of the human brain. It consists of interconnected processing elements called **neurons** (or nodes) organized into **input, hidden, and output layers**, which learn complex, non-linear input–output mappings through iterative weight adjustment using training data. In the context of **Advanced Vehicle Technology**, neural networks form the computational backbone of modern **Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS)**, autonomous driving perception stacks, engine control mapping, battery state estimation in EVs, and predictive fault diagnostics. The course code **PEAUT865** specifically frames neural networks as an *enabling soft-computing technique* for solving vehicle engineering problems that classical analytical models cannot easily handle (e.g., recognizing a pedestrian in a camera frame). ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of a neural network like a **team of trainee automotive quality inspectors** learning to identify defective engine parts on an assembly line: - Each **inspector (neuron)** looks at one or two specific features of the part (weight, surface roughness, dimension). - They each give a small **opinion (weighted score)**. - A **senior inspector (activation function)** combines these opinions and decides whether the part is defective. - When their final verdict is wrong, they are told *how much* they contributed to the error, and they **adjust their individual emphasis (weights)** accordingly. After thousands of parts, they become extremely accurate. This iterative self-correction is exactly what happens during **training a neural network using backpropagation**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Syllabus Highlights for PEAUT865:** > - Understand **biological inspiration** of artificial neurons. > - Comprehend the **architecture** (single-layer, multi-layer feedforward, recurrent). > - Apply **activation functions** (Sigmoid, ReLU, Tanh, Softmax). > - Grasp **learning paradigms**: Supervised, Unsupervised, Reinforcement. > - Implement **backpropagation algorithm** for weight updates. > - Apply neural networks to **automotive perception, control, and diagnostics**. ### Physical Constants and Standard Metrics - **Learning Rate ($\eta$)**: Typically **$0.001$ to $0.1$** — controls step size of weight updates. - **Universal Approximation Constant**: A single hidden-layer network with a finite number of neurons can approximate any continuous function on a compact domain to arbitrary accuracy (Cybenko, 1989). - **Standard batch size** in automotive deep learning: **$32$ to $256$ samples** for training perception models. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Biological Neuron vs Artificial Neuron | Component | Biological Neuron | Artificial Neuron | |---|---|---| | Input signal | Dendrites (chemical synapses) | Inputs $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n$ | | Processing | Soma (cell body) | Weighted summation $\sum w_i x_i + b$ | | Output channel | Axon | Activation output $y = f(\cdot)$ | | Synaptic strength | Neurotransmitter concentration | Weight $w_i$ (learnable parameter) | | Threshold | Firing threshold ($\approx -55$ mV) | Bias $b$ + activation function | ### 2.2 McCulloch-Pitts (MCP) Neuron Model — The Foundation The simplest formal neuron proposed in **1943** by Warren McCulloch and Walter Pitts: $$ y = f\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i - \theta\right) $$ where: - $x_i$ = $i$-th binary input ($0$ or $1$) - $w_i$ = synaptic weight - $\theta$ = threshold (bias) - $f(\cdot)$ = step activation function - $y$ = binary output ### 2.3 Perceptron Learning Rule For each input sample, weights are updated as: $$ w_i^{new} = w_i^{old} + \eta \, (t - y) \, x_i $$ $$ b^{new} = b^{old} + \eta \, (t - y) $$ where $t$ is the target (desired) output and $y$ is the actual output. ### 2.4 Generalized Multi-Layer Network Forward Pass For a neuron $j$ in layer $l$: $$ z_j^{(l)} = \sum_{i} w_{ij}^{(l)} a_i^{(l-1)} + b_j^{(l)} $$ $$ a_j^{(l)} = f\!\left(z_j^{(l)}\right) $$ ### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Equation | Description | Used In | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $y = f\left(\sum w_i x_i + b\right)$ | Generic neuron output | All ANN problems | | 2 | $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ | Sigmoid activation | Output probability | | 3 | $\tanh(z) = \dfrac{e^{z}-e^{-z}}{e^{z}+e^{-z}}$ | Hyperbolic tangent activation | Hidden layers (zero-centered) | | 4 | $\text{ReLU}(z) = \max(0, z)$ | Rectified Linear Unit | Deep hidden layers | | 5 | $\text{Softmax}(z_i) = \dfrac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_k e^{z_k}}$ | Multi-class probability distribution | Classification output | | 6 | $w_{ij}^{(new)} = w_{ij}^{(old)} - \eta \dfrac{\partial E}{\partial w_{ij}}$ | Gradient descent weight update | Backpropagation | | 7 | $\delta^{(L)} = (a^{(L)} - y) \odot f'(z^{(L)})$ | Output-layer error term | Backpropagation | | 8 | $\delta^{(l)} = \left((W^{(l+1)})^{T} \delta^{(l+1)}\right) \odot f'(z^{(l)})$ | Hidden-layer error backpropagation | Backpropagation | | 9 | $E = \dfrac{1}{2}\sum_k (t_k - y_k)^2$ | Mean Squared Error (MSE) loss | Regression tasks | | 10 | $E = -\sum_k t_k \log(y_k)$ | Cross-Entropy Loss | Classification tasks | | 11 | $w_i^{new} = w_i + \eta(t-y)x_i$ | Perceptron rule (no derivative) | Single-layer learning | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Automotive Utility of These Equations:** > - **Equation 1** powers every sensor-fusion node in an autonomous vehicle (camera + LiDAR + radar inputs). > - **Equation 4 (ReLU)** is the de-facto standard in modern CNN-based pedestrian detection used in Tesla Autopilot and Mobileye EyeQ chips. > - **Equation 6–8 (Backpropagation)** enables training of neural networks used for battery State-of-Charge (SOC) estimation in EVs — a topic frequently tested in PEAUT865. ### 2.6 Activation Function Comparison (Engineering Perspective) | Activation | Range | Derivative | Pros | Cons | Vehicle Tech Use | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Step | $\{0,1\}$ | Undefined | Binary decisions | Not differentiable, blocks gradient | Legacy logic gating | | Sigmoid | $(0,1)$ | $\sigma(1-\sigma)$ | Smooth, probabilistic | Vanishing gradient | Brake/throttle probability | | Tanh | $(-1,1)$ | $1-\tanh^2$ | Zero-centered | Vanishing gradient | Steering angle prediction | | ReLU | $[0,\infty)$ | $0$ or $1$ | No vanishing gradient (positive side), fast | Dead neurons | Lane detection CNNs | | Softmax | $(0,1)$ sum=$1$ | $y_i(1-y_i)$ | Multi-class output | Expensive | Traffic sign classification | --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Perceptron Convergence for Linear Separability Consider a **2-input perceptron** trained to classify points belonging to two linearly separable classes. We must prove the perceptron learning rule converges to a separating hyperplane. **Step 1 — Setup:** Let input vectors be $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^2$ and weights be $\mathbf{w} \in \mathbb{R}^2$. The decision boundary is the line: $$ \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x} + b = 0 $$ **Step 2 — Misclassification Condition:** A point is misclassified if $y(t - y_{\text{pred}}) \le 0$, where $t \in \{-1, +1\}$. **Step 3 — Weight Update Step:** For each misclassified sample, perform: $$ \mathbf{w}^{new} = \mathbf{w}^{old} + \eta \, t \, \mathbf{x} $$ **Step 4 — Margin Definition:** Let $\gamma > 0$ be the geometric margin of the optimal hyperplane $\mathbf{w}^*$ with $\lVert \mathbf{w}^*\rVert = 1$. Then for all correctly classified samples: $$ t \, (\mathbf{w}^{*T} \mathbf{x}) \ge \gamma $$ **Step 5 — Bounding the Dot Product:** Take the dot product of $\mathbf{w}^{new}$ with $\mathbf{w}^*$: $$ \begin{aligned} \mathbf{w}^{*T} \mathbf{w}^{new} &= \mathbf{w}^{*T}\mathbf{w}^{old} + \eta \, t \, (\mathbf{w}^{*T}\mathbf{x}) \\ &\ge \mathbf{w}^{*T}\mathbf{w}^{old} + \eta\gamma \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 — Iterative Lower Bound (after $k$ updates):** $$ \mathbf{w}^{*T} \mathbf{w}^{(k)} \ge k \, \eta \, \gamma $$ **Step 7 — Bounding the Norm of $\mathbf{w}^{(k)}$:** Using $\lVert \mathbf{x}\rVert \le R$ (bounded input) and Cauchy-Schwarz: $$ \begin{aligned} \lVert \mathbf{w}^{(k)}\rVert^2 &= \lVert \mathbf{w}^{(k-1)} + \eta \, t \, \mathbf{x}\rVert^2 \\ &= \lVert \mathbf{w}^{(k-1)}\rVert^2 + 2\eta \, t \, (\mathbf{w}^{(k-1)T}\mathbf{x}) + \eta^2 \lVert\mathbf{x}\rVert^2 \end{aligned} $$ Since the sample is misclassified, $t(\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}) \le 0$, so: $$ \lVert \mathbf{w}^{(k)}\rVert^2 \le \lVert \mathbf{w}^{(k-1)}\rVert^2 + \eta^2 R^2 $$ **Step 8 — Iterative Upper Bound:** $$ \lVert \mathbf{w}^{(k)}\rVert^2 \le k \, \eta^2 R^2 $$ **Step 9 — Combining the Bounds (Cauchy-Schwarz):** $$ k \, \eta \, \gamma \le \mathbf{w}^{*T} \mathbf{w}^{(k)} \le \lVert \mathbf{w}^{*}\rVert \cdot \lVert \mathbf{w}^{(k)}\rVert = \lVert \mathbf{w}^{(k)}\rVert \le \sqrt{k}\,\eta R $$ **Step 10 — Solving for $k$:** $$ k \, \eta \, \gamma \le \sqrt{k}\,\eta R \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; k \le \frac{R^2}{\gamma^2} $$ **Conclusion:** The perceptron **converges in a finite number of updates bounded by $R^2 / \gamma^2$**, where $R$ is the maximum input norm and $\gamma$ is the margin. This is the famous **Perceptron Convergence Theorem (Rosenblatt, 1958; Novikoff, 1962)**. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Backpropagation for a 2-Layer Network Consider a 2-layer feedforward network with: - Input $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ - Hidden layer activation $\mathbf{a}^{(1)} \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ with weights $W^{(1)}$ and bias $b^{(1)}$ - Output $\mathbf{a}^{(2)} \in \mathbb{R}^{K}$ with weights $W^{(2)}$ and bias $b^{(2)}$ - Loss function $E = \dfrac{1}{2}\sum_{k}(t_k - a_k^{(2)})^2$ **Step 1 — Forward Pass Equations:** $$ z_i^{(1)} = \sum_j W_{ij}^{(1)} x_j + b_i^{(1)} $$ $$ a_i^{(1)} = f(z_i^{(1)}) $$ $$ z_k^{(2)} = \sum_i W_{ki}^{(2)} a_i^{(1)} + b_k^{(2)} $$ $$ a_k^{(2)} = f(z_k^{(2)}) $$ **Step 2 — Output-Layer Error Gradient:** Using chain rule, the gradient of $E$ w.r.t. $W_{ki}^{(2)}$: $$ \frac{\partial E}{\partial W_{ki}^{(2)}} = \frac{\partial E}{\partial a_k^{(2)}} \cdot \frac{\partial a_k^{(2)}}{\partial z_k^{(2)}} \cdot \frac{\partial z_k^{(2)}}{\partial W_{ki}^{(2)}} $$ Computing each factor: $$ \frac{\partial E}{\partial a_k^{(2)}} = -(t_k - a_k^{(2)}), \quad \frac{\partial a_k^{(2)}}{\partial z_k^{(2)}} = f'(z_k^{(2)}), \quad \frac{\partial z_k^{(2)}}{\partial W_{ki}^{(2)}} = a_i^{(1)} $$ Therefore: $$ \frac{\partial E}{\partial W_{ki}^{(2)}} = -(t_k - a_k^{(2)}) \, f'(z_k^{(2)}) \, a_i^{(1)} $$ **Step 3 — Output Error Signal $\delta_k^{(2)}$:** Define the local error signal: $$ \delta_k^{(2)} = (t_k - a_k^{(2)}) \, f'(z_k^{(2)}) $$ So the weight update becomes: $$ W_{ki}^{(2)} \leftarrow W_{ki}^{(2)} + \eta \, \delta_k^{(2)} \, a_i^{(1)} $$ **Step 4 — Hidden-Layer Error Signal:** The hidden layer has no direct "target", so error must be back-propagated: $$ \frac{\partial E}{\partial a_i^{(1)}} = \sum_k \frac{\partial E}{\partial a_k^{(2)}} \cdot \frac{\partial a_k^{(2)}}{\partial z_k^{(2)}} \cdot \frac{\partial z_k^{(2)}}{\partial a_i^{(1)}} = -\sum_k (t_k - a_k^{(2)}) \, f'(z_k^{(2)}) \, W_{ki}^{(2)} $$ Substituting $\delta_k^{(2)}$: $$ \frac{\partial E}{\partial a_i^{(1)}} = -\sum_k \delta_k^{(2)} \, W_{ki}^{(2)} $$ **Step 5 — Hidden Layer Local Error:** $$ \delta_i^{(1)} = \left(\sum_k W_{ki}^{(2)} \, \delta_k^{(2)}\right) f'(z_i^{(1)}) $$ **Step 6 — Hidden Layer Weight Gradient:** $$ \frac{\partial E}{\partial W_{ij}^{(1)}} = \delta_i^{(1)} \, x_j $$ **Step 7 — Compact Update Rule (all layers):** $$ W^{(l)} \leftarrow W^{(l)} - \eta \, \delta^{(l)} \, (a^{(l-1)})^{T} $$ $$ b^{(l)} \leftarrow b^{(l)} - \eta \, \delta^{(l)} $$ This compact form is exactly what a programming framework (e.g., TensorFlow) implements under the hood. For automotive SOC estimators, this same chain rule allows the network to learn non-linear battery discharge curves with **R$^2 > 0.98$** accuracy on test data. --- ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Vehicle Engine Fault Classification A neural network must classify engine vibration signatures into two states: **Normal ($t=0$)** and **Faulty Bearing ($t=1$)**. Inputs are two spectral features $x_1 = 0.6$, $x_2 = 0.8$. Initial weights $w_1 = 0.2$, $w_2 = -0.1$, $b = 0.0$, learning rate $\eta = 0.5$, activation = step function with threshold $0$. **Step 1 — Forward Pass:** $$ z = w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + b = (0.2)(0.6) + (-0.1)(0.8) + 0 = 0.12 - 0.08 = 0.04 $$ $$ y = f(0.04) = 1 \quad \text{(step output)} $$ **Step 2 — Compute Error:** $$ \text{Error} = t - y = 0 - 1 = -1 $$ **Step 3 — Apply Perceptron Update Rule:** $$ w_1^{new} = w_1 + \eta(t-y)x_1 = 0.2 + 0.5 \times (-1) \times 0.6 = 0.2 - 0.3 = -0.10 $$ $$ w_2^{new} = w_2 + \eta(t-y)x_2 = -0.1 + 0.5 \times (-1) \times 0.8 = -0.1 - 0.4 = -0.50 $$ $$ b^{new} = b + \eta(t-y) = 0 + 0.5 \times (-1) = -0.50 $$ **Step 4 — Verification on Same Sample:** $$ z^{new} = (-0.10)(0.6) + (-0.50)(0.8) + (-0.50) = -0.06 - 0.40 - 0.50 = -0.96 $$ $$ y^{new} = f(-0.96) = 0 \;\; \checkmark \;\; \text{(matches target } t=0) $$ This is a single epoch of training. After several epochs over the full dataset, the network converges to correctly classify the vibration signature — illustrating the **practical use of neural networks in engine health monitoring**. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Multi-Layer Perceptron for Automotive Application ```mermaid flowchart LR inp1[x1: Vibration] --> h1n1 inp2[x2: Temperature] --> h1n1 inp3[x3: Pressure] --> h1n1 inp1 --> h1n2 inp2 --> h1n2 inp3 --> h1n2 inp1 --> h1n3 inp2 --> h1n3 inp3 --> h1n3 h1n1["Hidden Neuron 1
Sum + ReLU"] --> on1["Output: Healthy
Softmax"] h1n2["Hidden Neuron 2
Sum + ReLU"] --> on1 h1n3["Hidden Neuron 3
Sum + ReLU"] --> on1 h1n1 --> on2["Output: Bearing Fault
Softmax"] h1n2 --> on2 h1n3 --> on2 h1n1 --> on3["Output: Overheat
Softmax"] h1n2 --> on3 h1n3 --> on3 ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Backpropagation Data Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Input Layer x] --> B[Forward Pass: Compute z and a for each layer] B --> C[Output Layer Prediction y_pred] C --> D{Compare with Target t} D -- Compute Error E --> E[Output Error delta L] E --> F[Backpropagate delta through layers] F --> G[Update Weights W and Bias b using gradient descent] G --> H{Convergence Reached?} H -- No --> B H -- Yes --> I[Trained Network for Vehicle Diagnosis] ``` ### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Neural Network in ADAS ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SensorInput["Sensor Input Stage"] cam["Camera Frames"] lidar["LiDAR Point Cloud"] radar["Radar Returns"] canbus["CAN Bus Telemetry"] end subgraph PreProc["Preprocessing Stage"] norm["Normalization Layer"] fus["Sensor Fusion Block"] end subgraph DeepNN["Neural Network Core"] conv["Convolutional Feature Extractor"] rnn["Recurrent Temporal Memory"] fc["Fully Connected Decision Layer"] end subgraph OutputStage["Vehicle Decision Stage"] brake["Brake Actuator Signal"] steer["Steering Angle Command"] warn["Driver Warning HUD"] end cam --> norm lidar --> norm radar --> norm canbus --> norm norm --> fus fus --> conv conv --> rnn rnn --> fc fc --> brake fc --> steer fc --> warn ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation:** > - The **Sensor Input Stage** collects raw signals analogous to dendrites. > - The **Neural Network Core** performs feature extraction (convolution), temporal modelling (RNN), and decision-making (fully connected layer). > - The **Vehicle Decision Stage** mirrors the axon output that triggers downstream mechanical action. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** Define an Artificial Neural Network. List any **four** characteristics that make it suitable for automotive applications. **Model Answer:** An Artificial Neural Network (ANN) is a computational model inspired by the structure of biological neurons, consisting of interconnected processing units (neurons) organized in layers that learn input–output mappings by adjusting connection weights. Four characteristics suited for automotive applications: 1. **Non-linearity** — captures complex relationships like tire-road friction. 2. **Learning from data** — adapts to driver behaviour over time. 3. **Parallel processing** — enables real-time sensor fusion. 4. **Fault tolerance** — degrades gracefully when a sensor fails. *(Examiner's note: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for characteristics with valid automotive examples.)* --- **Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** Compare the **Sigmoid** and **ReLU** activation functions in terms of gradient behaviour. Which one is preferred in deep networks used for autonomous driving perception? **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Sigmoid | ReLU | |---|---|---| | Range | $(0,1)$ | $[0,\infty)$ | | Gradient for large input | Vanishes ($\approx 0$) | Constant ($=1$) | | Computational cost | Exponential | Max operation | **ReLU** is preferred in deep perception networks because it **avoids the vanishing-gradient problem** during backpropagation, allowing faster convergence on large image datasets (e.g., Cityscapes, KITTI used for self-driving). --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice > **Question Selection Rule:** Students must attempt **either Question A or Question B in full**. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** **(a)** With a neat labelled diagram, explain the **architecture of a Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP)**. Differentiate between **feedforward** and **feedback (recurrent)** networks. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Architecture of MLP:** A Multi-Layer Perceptron consists of: 1. **Input Layer** — receives raw feature vector $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)$. No computation occurs here. 2. **One or more Hidden Layers** — each neuron computes a weighted sum followed by a non-linear activation: $$ a_j^{(l)} = f\!\left(\sum_i w_{ij}^{(l)} a_i^{(l-1)} + b_j^{(l)}\right) $$ 3. **Output Layer** — produces final prediction (regression value or class probability). Information propagates strictly in one direction — from input to output — through fully connected (dense) edges. **Feedforward vs Feedback Networks:** | Feature | Feedforward | Feedback (Recurrent) | |---|---|---| | Signal flow | Unidirectional (input → output) | Loops / self-connections allowed | | Memory | Stateless | Has internal state (memory) | | Use case | Static pattern classification | Time-series forecasting | | Vehicle example | Image-based sign recognition | Predicting future vehicle trajectory | | Training algorithm | Backpropagation | Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT) | **Diagram (textual representation):** $$\text{Input}(x_1,\dots,x_n) \;\to\; \text{Hidden Layer 1} \;\to\; \text{Hidden Layer 2} \;\to\; \text{Output}(y_1,\dots,y_k)$$ --- **(b)** Derive the **backpropagation weight update rule** for the hidden layer of a 2-layer feedforward network using the Mean Squared Error loss function. Show all intermediate steps. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Define Network Equations:** - Hidden layer pre-activation: $z_j^{(1)} = \sum_i w_{ij}^{(1)} x_i + b_j^{(1)}$ - Hidden activation: $a_j^{(1)} = f(z_j^{(1)})$ - Output pre-activation: $z_k^{(2)} = \sum_j w_{kj}^{(2)} a_j^{(1)} + b_k^{(2)}$ - Output activation: $a_k^{(2)} = f(z_k^{(2)})$ **Step 2 — Loss Function:** $$ E = \frac{1}{2}\sum_k (t_k - a_k^{(2)})^2 $$ **Step 3 — Compute Output-Layer Local Error:** $$ \delta_k^{(2)} = (a_k^{(2)} - t_k) \, f'(z_k^{(2)}) $$ **Step 4 — Propagate Error to Hidden Layer:** $$ \delta_j^{(1)} = \left(\sum_k w_{kj}^{(2)} \, \delta_k^{(2)}\right) \, f'(z_j^{(1)}) $$ **Step 5 — Compute Hidden-Layer Weight Gradient:** $$ \frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{ij}^{(1)}} = \delta_j^{(1)} \, x_i $$ **Step 6 — Update Rule:** $$ w_{ij}^{(1)} \leftarrow w_{ij}^{(1)} - \eta \, \delta_j^{(1)} \, x_i $$ $$ b_j^{(1)} \leftarrow b_j^{(1)} - \eta \, \delta_j^{(1)} $$ **Step 7 — Engineering Interpretation:** Each hidden weight is updated in proportion to (a) the learning rate, (b) the input that flows through it, and (c) the back-propagated error indicating how much that neuron contributed to the final prediction error. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** **(a)** Explain **biological inspiration** of an artificial neuron. Draw the block diagram of a **McCulloch-Pitts neuron** and derive its mathematical model. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Biological Inspiration:** The human brain contains approximately **86 billion neurons**. Each biological neuron consists of: - **Dendrites** — receive electrochemical signals from other neurons. - **Soma (cell body)** — sums the incoming signals. - **Axon** — transmits the output pulse when the sum exceeds a threshold. - **Synapses** — connection points where learning occurs by varying the synaptic strength. **Block Diagram of McCulloch-Pitts Neuron:** ``` x1 ----[w1]----+ x2 ----[w2]----+--> [Σ] --> [Threshold f] --> y ... | xn ----[wn]----+ (-θ) ``` **Mathematical Model:** $$ y = f\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i - \theta\right) $$ where $f(\cdot)$ is a binary step function: $$ y = \begin{cases} 1, & \text{if } \sum w_i x_i \ge \theta \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ This model can realize basic logical functions (AND, OR) but cannot solve the XOR problem — a limitation that motivated multi-layer networks. --- **(b)** A 3-input perceptron is used to classify whether a vehicle's **anti-lock braking system (ABS)** should activate. Inputs are wheel speed sensors: $x_1=1, x_2=1, x_3=0$. Initial weights: $w_1=0.5, w_2=-0.3, w_3=0.8$, bias $b=0.1$, target $t=1$. Using sigmoid activation and learning rate $\eta=0.2$, perform **one epoch of training** showing the forward pass, error, and weight update. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Forward Pass (Weighted Sum):** $$ z = w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + w_3 x_3 + b = (0.5)(1) + (-0.3)(1) + (0.8)(0) + 0.1 = 0.3 $$ **Step 2 — Sigmoid Activation:** $$ y = \sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-0.3}} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.7408} = \frac{1}{1.7408} = 0.5744 $$ **Step 3 — Compute Error:** $$ \text{Error} = t - y = 1 - 0.5744 = 0.4256 $$ **Step 4 — Compute Gradient using MSE Loss with Sigmoid:** For sigmoid + MSE, the local error simplifies to: $$ \delta = (t - y) \cdot y \cdot (1 - y) = 0.4256 \times 0.5744 \times 0.4256 $$ Computing each factor: $$ y(1-y) = 0.5744 \times 0.4256 = 0.2445 $$ $$ \delta = 0.4256 \times 0.2445 = 0.1041 $$ **Step 5 — Update Weights:** $$ w_1^{new} = w_1 + \eta \delta x_1 = 0.5 + 0.2 \times 0.1041 \times 1 = 0.5208 $$ $$ w_2^{new} = w_2 + \eta \delta x_2 = -0.3 + 0.2 \times 0.1041 \times 1 = -0.2792 $$ $$ w_3^{new} = w_3 + \eta \delta x_3 = 0.8 + 0.2 \times 0.1041 \times 0 = 0.8000 $$ $$ b^{new} = b + \eta \delta = 0.1 + 0.2 \times 0.1041 = 0.1208 $$ **Step 6 — Verification of Improved Output:** $$ z^{new} = 0.5208 - 0.2792 + 0 + 0.1208 = 0.3624 $$ $$ y^{new} = \sigma(0.3624) = 0.5896 $$ The output increased from $0.5744$ to $0.5896$, moving toward target $t=1$ — confirming the network is learning. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting the bias term $b$** in the weighted sum. Marks deducted if bias is omitted in both forward and update steps. **(−2 marks)** > 2. **Using the wrong activation derivative.** With sigmoid + MSE, the gradient is $(t-y)\,y(1-y)$; with sigmoid + cross-entropy, it simplifies to $(t-y)\,y$ only. Mixing these costs marks. **(−2 marks)** > 3. **Sign error in update rule.** KTU expects $w^{new} = w^{old} + \eta \delta x$ for MSE form, or $w^{new} = w^{old} - \eta \frac{\partial E}{\partial w}$ for general gradient descent. Getting the sign wrong is a frequent pitfall. **(−1 to −2 marks)** > 4. **Not stating the assumption of activation function explicitly.** Always declare whether you are using step, sigmoid, or ReLU before solving. **(−1 mark)** > 5. **Skipping the chain-rule expansion in backpropagation derivation.** Examiners expect the explicit $\frac{\partial E}{\partial a} \cdot \frac{\partial a}{\partial z} \cdot \frac{\partial z}{\partial w}$ breakdown. **(−3 marks if skipped)** --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Biological neuron** inspiration: dendrites → weights; soma → summation; axon → activation output. - **McCulloch-Pitts model** is the foundational binary neuron; cannot solve XOR — led to MLP. - **Perceptron learning rule**: $w^{new} = w^{old} + \eta(t-y)x$ — converges in finite steps for linearly separable data (Perceptron Convergence Theorem). - **Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP)** adds hidden layers with non-linear activations to model complex, non-linear vehicle systems. - **Activation functions** matter critically: **ReLU** for deep perception, **Sigmoid/Softmax** for output probability, **Tanh** for zero-centred hidden activations. - **Backpropagation** = chain rule applied layer-by-layer from output to input to compute gradients. - **Local error signal** $\delta^{(l)}$ at any layer = back-propagated weighted error $\odot$ activation derivative. - **Loss functions**: MSE for regression (e.g., SOC estimation), Cross-Entropy for classification (e.g., traffic sign). - **Learning paradigms**: Supervised (labelled vehicle data), Unsupervised (anomaly detection), Reinforcement (autonomous driving policy). - **Automotive applications**: ADAS perception, ABS/ESC control, EV battery SOC/SOH estimation, engine fault diagnosis, autonomous driving trajectory prediction. - **Vanishing gradient** is a major issue with Sigmoid/Tanh in deep networks — mitigated by ReLU and Batch Normalization. - **Universal Approximation Theorem**: A single hidden-layer MLP with sufficient neurons can approximate any continuous function — the mathematical justification for using neural networks in complex vehicle modelling. ---

topics.title: Applications in Vehicle Systems: Traffic Sign Recognition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAUT865 - ADVANCED VEHICLE TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Applications in Vehicle Systems: Traffic Sign Recognition # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Traffic Sign Recognition (TSR)** is a computer-vision-based **Advanced Driver Assistance System (ADAS)** sub-function that automatically detects, classifies, and interprets roadside traffic signs (regulatory, warning, and informational) from a moving vehicle's forward-facing camera, and conveys the recognized information to the driver via Human-Machine Interface (HMI) or feeds it to higher-level autonomous-driving decision modules. In formal ISO/SAE-aligned terms, TSR operates as a **perception-class subsystem** within the SAE J3016 Level 1–3 automation hierarchy, sitting functionally between the *Sensing* layer and the *Planning* layer of the autonomous-driving stack. | Aspect | Specification | |---|---| | **Input modality** | Monocular RGB camera (typically 1–2 MP, 30–60 fps) | | **Output** | Sign class ID + confidence score + 3-D position estimate | | **Latency budget** | **≤ 100 ms** end-to-end (ISO 26262 ASIL-B timing constraint) | | **Operating range** | 0 m – 250 m line-of-sight | | **Recognition accuracy (state-of-art)** | **> 99 %** on GTSRB benchmark | | **Functional safety** | ISO 26262 ASIL-B (driver warning), ASIL-D capable (autonomous actuation) | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Co-Pilot Reader" Imagine a vigilant human **co-pilot** sitting beside you in the car. His job is to constantly scan the road ahead through the windshield. The moment he spots a speed-limit sign, his brain performs three near-instant tasks: 1. **Spot** the sign (ignoring billboards, shop signs, reflections). 2. **Read** it ("60 km/h" — a red circle with a number). 3. **Whisper** to you, *"Speed limit 60 ahead"* — well before you reach it. A TSR system is the **electronic co-pilot reader**. The *camera* is the eye, the *image-processing algorithm* is the visual cortex, the *classifier* is the language center that decodes the symbol, and the *HMI beep/display* is the whisper. Unlike a human, it never blinks, never gets distracted, and reacts in milliseconds — but it can be fooled by a faded sign, heavy rain, or a sticker-plastered lamp-post in ways a human would not be. > [!NOTE] > **Why TSR matters in 2024+ vehicles:** With **UN Regulation No. 157 (ALKS)** and **Euro NCAP 2025** protocols mandating intelligent speed assistance and sign-aware cruise control, TSR is no longer a luxury feature — it is a *regulated* ADAS building block in the European Union, Japan, and increasingly India (AIS-184 draft). ## 1.3 Position in the ADAS Stack TSR is typically a **feed-forward perception module** that hands structured metadata to: - **Intelligent Speed Assist (ISA)** — enforces or warns about speed limits. - **Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC)** — adjusts set-speed to local limit. - **Autonomous Emergency Braking (AEB)** — interprets "STOP" / "YIELD". - **Lane-Keep Assist (LKA)** — uses warning signs as contextual cues. It is therefore classified as a **primary perception function** — failure of TSR propagates downstream into potentially safety-critical actuation commands, hence the strict **ASIL-B/D** classification. ## 1.4 Salient Characteristics & KTU High-Yield Highlights > [!IMPORTANT] > **Five properties a KTU examiner loves to test:** > 1. **Real-time constraint** — must run at ≥ 25 fps on embedded ECU (e.g., NVIDIA Jetson Orin, Mobileye EyeQ). > 2. **Robustness to photometric variation** — day/night/rain/glare invariance. > 3. **Multi-class capability** — typically 40–200 sign classes (GTSRB: 43, TT100K: 221). > 4. **Multi-scale detection** — signs appear from ~10×10 px to 200×200 px. > 5. **Tracking & temporal consistency** — a sign shouldn't flicker between "60" and "80" frame-to-frame. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Functional Architecture of a TSR Pipeline A modern TSR system follows the canonical **five-stage pipeline**: 1. **Image Acquisition** — CMOS/CCD camera, global shutter preferred (avoids rolling-shutter artifacts at highway speeds). 2. **Pre-processing** — de-noising (Gaussian / bilateral filter), colour-space conversion (RGB → HSV or LUV), histogram equalisation, ROI cropping (sky / hood exclusion). 3. **Detection (Localisation)** — "Where is the sign?" — outputs bounding boxes. Common methods: Colour-thresholding, HOG + SVM, sliding-window CNN, YOLO/SSD/Faster-R-CNN. 4. **Classification (Recognition)** — "What sign is it?" — outputs class label + confidence. Common methods: LeNet-5, AlexNet, custom CNNs, Vision Transformers (ViT-S). 5. **Post-processing / Tracking** — Kalman filter, optical-flow, or IoU-based tracker across frames; HMI output. ## 2.2 Detection Stage — Mathematical Foundations ### 2.2.1 Colour-Based Segmentation Traffic signs have **standardised chromaticity** defined by the *Vienna Convention on Road Signs and Signals (1968)*. In HSV space: $$H \in \begin{cases} [0°, 10°] \cup [340°, 360°] & \text{(RED — Stop, Yield, No-Entry)} \\ [40°, 70°] & \text{(YELLOW — Warning)} \\ [200°, 250°] & \text{(BLUE — Mandatory)} \end{cases}$$ A pixel is classified as a *sign candidate* if its **HSV hue, saturation, and value** fall inside the predefined bounding box: $$C_{sign}(x,y) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } H(x,y) \in [H_{lo}, H_{hi}] \text{ AND } S(x,y) > S_{th} \text{ AND } V(x,y) > V_{th} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ Connected-component analysis then groups contiguous candidate pixels into blobs, and shape filters (circularity $\psi$, rectangularity $\rho$) reject non-sign blobs. ### 2.2.2 Shape Features **Circularity** $\psi$ for a contour of area $A$ and perimeter $P$: $$\psi = \frac{4\pi A}{P^{2}}, \quad \psi \in [0,1] \text{ (1 = perfect circle)}$$ **Rectangularity** $\rho$ as ratio of contour area to bounding-box area: $$\rho = \frac{A}{A_{bbox}}, \quad \rho \in [0,1]$$ **Triangularity** $\tau$ — Hu moment-based discriminant: $$\tau = \frac{I_{1} + I_{2}}{2 \cdot I_{3}}, \quad \text{(Hu moments } I_{1}, I_{2}, I_{3}\text{)}$$ ### 2.2.3 Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) Formulation A CNN layer applies a learnable 2-D filter $W$ of size $k \times k$ with bias $b$, followed by a non-linear activation $\sigma$ (typically ReLU): $$Y_{i,j}^{(l)} = \sigma\!\left(\sum_{m=0}^{k-1}\sum_{n=0}^{k-1} W_{m,n}^{(l)} \cdot X_{i+m, j+n}^{(l-1)} + b^{(l)}\right)$$ **Max-pooling** downsamples by retaining the maximum in each $p \times p$ window: $$Z_{i,j}^{(l)} = \max_{0 \le u,v < p} \, Y_{p \cdot i + u, \; p \cdot j + v}^{(l)}$$ **Softmax classifier** at the output converts logits $z_k$ into class probabilities $p_k$: $$p_k = \frac{e^{z_k}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}}, \quad \sum_{k=1}^{K} p_k = 1$$ **Cross-entropy loss** (the function CNNs minimise during training): $$\mathcal{L}_{CE} = -\sum_{k=1}^{K} y_k \log(p_k)$$ where $y_k$ is the one-hot ground-truth label. The network weights are updated via **stochastic gradient descent** with back-propagation: $$W^{(l)} \leftarrow W^{(l)} - \eta \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{CE}}{\partial W^{(l)}}$$ where $\eta$ is the **learning rate** (typical range $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-2}$). ## 2.3 Tracking & Temporal Filtering To stabilise the HMI output, detection hypotheses are tracked across frames. A simple **$\alpha$-$\beta$ filter** (constant-velocity kinematic model) fuses position $x_k$ and velocity $\dot{x}_k$: $$\hat{x}_{k|k} = \hat{x}_{k|k-1} + \alpha \cdot (z_k - \hat{x}_{k|k-1})$$ $$\hat{\dot{x}}_{k|k} = \hat{\dot{x}}_{k|k-1} + \frac{\beta}{T} \cdot (z_k - \hat{x}_{k|k-1})$$ where: - $z_k$ = measured bounding-box centre at frame $k$ - $\hat{x}_{k|k-1}$ = predicted centre - $T$ = frame interval (s) - $\alpha, \beta$ = filter gains, with $\alpha \in [0.3, 0.6]$, $\beta \in [0.05, 0.15]$ for road-sign tracking A sign is **committed to the HMI** only when it has been observed in $N \ge 3$ consecutive frames with class label $c$ having mean confidence $\bar{p}_c \ge 0.9$. ## 2.4 Performance Metrics > [!NOTE] > **KTU examiners repeatedly test these four metrics** — memorise the table. **Accuracy** $$Acc = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$$ **Precision (positive predictive value)** $$Prec = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$$ **Recall (sensitivity, true-positive rate)** $$Rec = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$$ **F1-score (harmonic mean)** $$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot Prec \cdot Rec}{Prec + Rec}$$ **Intersection over Union (IoU)** for bounding-box quality: $$IoU = \frac{\vert B_{pred} \cap B_{gt} \vert}{\vert B_{pred} \cup B_{gt} \vert}, \quad \text{typical threshold} \ge 0.5$$ **Frames Per Second (FPS)** — real-time viability on target ECU: $$FPS = \frac{N_{frames}}{T_{total}} \quad \text{with} \quad T_{total} = T_{acq} + T_{pre} + T_{det} + T_{cls} + T_{post}$$ ## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet (Single-Block Reference) | # | Formula | Description | Typical Units / Range | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\psi = 4\pi A / P^{2}$ | Circularity of contour | $[0,1]$, $\ge 0.85$ for circles | | 2 | $\rho = A / A_{bbox}$ | Rectangularity | $[0,1]$, $\ge 0.85$ for rectangles | | 3 | $p_k = e^{z_k} / \sum_j e^{z_j}$ | Softmax probability | $[0,1]$, $\sum p_k = 1$ | | 4 | $\mathcal{L}_{CE} = -\sum_k y_k \log p_k$ | Cross-entropy loss | nats or bits | | 5 | $W \leftarrow W - \eta \, \partial \mathcal{L}/\partial W$ | SGD weight update | $\eta \in [10^{-4}, 10^{-2}]$ | | 6 | $Acc = (TP+TN)/(TP+TN+FP+FN)$ | Classification accuracy | $[0,1]$ | | 7 | $Prec = TP/(TP+FP)$ | Precision | $[0,1]$ | | 8 | $Rec = TP/(TP+FN)$ | Recall / sensitivity | $[0,1]$ | | 9 | $F_1 = 2PR/(P+R)$ | F1-score | $[0,1]$ | | 10 | $IoU = \vert B_{p} \cap B_{g} \vert / \vert B_{p} \cup B_{g} \vert$ | Detection box overlap | $\ge 0.5$ | | 11 | $\hat{x}_{k} = \hat{x}_{k}^{-} + \alpha(z_k - \hat{x}_{k}^{-})$ | Tracker correction | $\alpha \in [0,1]$ | | 12 | $FPS = N_{frames}/T_{total}$ | Real-time metric | $\ge 25$ fps for ADAS | | 13 | $d_{real} = f \cdot H_{px} / H_{sign}$ | Monocular distance estimate | metres | The last formula (13) is the **pinhole-camera depth estimate**: a sign of *known physical height* $H_{sign}$ (e.g., 0.6 m for EU speed limit) subtending $H_{px}$ pixels in an image of focal length $f$ is at distance $d_{real}$ from the camera. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility | Industry use-case | TSR contribution | |---|---| | **Intelligent Speed Assist (ISA)** | EU GSR-2 mandates ISA from 2024; TSR is the primary sensor. | | **Autonomous Driving (Waymo, Cruise, Tesla FSD)** | Sign compliance is a hard-coded legal requirement. | | **Fleet telematics & insurance** | Driver-score cards based on speed-limit compliance events. | | **HD-map validation** | Detected signs update outdated map databases. | | **Driver-monitoring research** | Compares driver behaviour vs. posted limits (DTW distance). | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Softmax Gradient (used in CNN back-prop) We need $\partial \mathcal{L}_{CE} / \partial z_i$ because the chain rule of back-propagation requires the gradient of the loss w.r.t. the *pre-activation* logits. **Step 1 — Write the loss explicitly for a single sample:** $$ \begin{aligned} \mathcal{L}_{CE} &= -\sum_{k=1}^{K} y_k \log p_k \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute the softmax definition $p_k = e^{z_k} / \sum_j e^{z_j}$:** $$ \begin{aligned} \mathcal{L}_{CE} &= -\sum_{k=1}^{K} y_k \log\!\left(\frac{e^{z_k}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}}\right) \\ &= -\sum_{k=1}^{K} y_k \left[ z_k - \log\!\left(\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}\right) \right] \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Differentiate w.r.t. $z_i$. Note that the second term is independent of $k$ once summed, but does depend on $z_i$ through the partition function $Z = \sum_j e^{z_j}$:** $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{CE}}{\partial z_i} &= -\left[ y_i - \log Z \cdot \frac{\partial \log Z}{\partial z_i} \right] \cdot \text{(for } k=i\text{)} - \sum_{k \ne i} \left[ -y_k \cdot \frac{\partial \log Z}{\partial z_i} \right] \\ &= -y_i + \log Z \cdot \frac{\partial \log Z}{\partial z_i} + \sum_{k \ne i} y_k \cdot \frac{\partial \log Z}{\partial z_i} \\ &= -y_i + \frac{\partial \log Z}{\partial z_i} \underbrace{\left(\log Z \cdot \mathbf{1}_{k=i} + \sum_{k \ne i} y_k \right)}_{\text{but cleaner to use } \frac{\partial \log Z}{\partial z_i} = \frac{e^{z_i}}{Z} = p_i} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Recognise that $\frac{\partial \log Z}{\partial z_i} = \frac{1}{Z}\frac{\partial Z}{\partial z_i} = \frac{e^{z_i}}{Z} = p_i$, and simplify the algebra by noting that $\sum_k y_k = 1$ (one-hot encoding):** $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{CE}}{\partial z_i} &= -y_i + p_i \sum_{k=1}^{K} y_k \\ &= p_i - y_i \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key result — memorise for KTU derivations:** > $$\boxed{\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{CE}}{\partial z_i} = p_i - y_i}$$ > This is one of the cleanest gradients in deep learning, and the reason softmax + cross-entropy are always used together. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of Monocular Distance Estimate Given a pinhole camera of focal length $f$ (in pixels), the projection of an object of physical height $H_{real}$ placed at perpendicular distance $d$ from the camera occupies $H_{px}$ pixels in the image: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{By similar triangles: } \quad \frac{H_{px}}{f} &= \frac{H_{real}}{d} \\ \therefore \quad d &= \frac{f \cdot H_{real}}{H_{px}} \end{aligned} $$ **Numerical example (typical TSR scenario):** - $f = 1200$ px (camera with 6 mm lens on 1/3″ sensor) - $H_{real} = 0.6$ m (EU standard speed-limit sign) - $H_{px} = 48$ px (sign at ~15 m ahead) $$ d = \frac{1200 \times 0.6}{48} = 15 \text{ m} \quad \checkmark $$ This distance feeds the **time-to-collision (TTC)** calculation used by the downstream planner: $$ \begin{aligned} TTC = \frac{d - d_{safe}}{v_{ego}} \end{aligned} $$ where $d_{safe}$ is the stopping distance, $v_{ego}$ is the host-vehicle speed (m/s). --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Algorithm Comparison for TSR > [!NOTE] > **This comparative table is a 14-mark favourite in KTU ESE.** Learn the trade-offs. | Algorithm | Detection accuracy | Speed (FPS on GPU) | Robustness to lighting | Training data required | Typical use | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Colour-thresholding + template match | 70–80 % | 60+ | **Poor** (fails at night) | None | Legacy low-cost systems | | HOG + linear SVM | 85–92 % | 30–45 | Medium | 10–20 k images | Mid-tier 2015-era TSR | | LeNet-5 CNN | 93–96 % | 40–60 | Good | 30 k+ images | Entry-level ADAS (Mobileye EyeQ3) | | Custom 8-layer CNN | 96–98 % | 25–40 | Very good | 50 k+ images | Mainstream 2020 production TSR | | YOLOv5 / YOLOv8 (one-stage) | 98–99 % | 60–120 | Excellent | 100 k+ images | Modern multi-task ADAS | | Faster-R-CNN (two-stage) | 99 %+ | 10–20 | Excellent | 100 k+ images | Off-line HD-map harvesting | | Vision Transformer (ViT-S) | 99.2 % | 30–60 | Excellent | 200 k+ images | Research / next-gen OEM | --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Confusion-Matrix Metrics A TSR classifier is evaluated on a 10 000-frame test set containing 1 200 true speed-limit signs. The model produces: - True Positives $TP = 1\,140$ - False Positives $FP = 30$ - False Negatives $FN = 60$ - True Negatives $TN = 8\,770$ **Verify the total:** $TP + FP + FN + TN = 1140 + 30 + 60 + 8770 = 10\,000$ ✓ **Compute Accuracy:** $$ \begin{aligned} Acc &= \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} = \frac{1140 + 8770}{10000} = \frac{9910}{10000} = 0.991 = 99.1\% \end{aligned} $$ **Compute Precision:** $$ \begin{aligned} Prec &= \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{1140}{1140 + 30} = \frac{1140}{1170} = 0.974 = 97.4\% \end{aligned} $$ **Compute Recall (Sensitivity):** $$ \begin{aligned} Rec &= \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{1140}{1140 + 60} = \frac{1140}{1200} = 0.950 = 95.0\% \end{aligned} $$ **Compute F1-score:** $$ \begin{aligned} F_1 &= \frac{2 \cdot Prec \cdot Rec}{Prec + Rec} = \frac{2 \times 0.974 \times 0.950}{0.974 + 0.950} = \frac{1.8506}{1.924} = 0.962 = 96.2\% \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering interpretation:** The model is *over-confident* in its positive predictions (precision > recall). To improve recall, the decision threshold should be lowered from 0.5 to ~0.3, accepting more false positives but catching more true signs. This trade-off is captured by the **Precision-Recall curve** and the **Average Precision (AP)** metric, defined as: > $$AP = \int_{0}^{1} P(R) \, dR$$ --- ## 3.5 Worked Example — IoU Calculation for Bounding-Box Evaluation Predicted box $B_p = (x_p, y_p, w_p, h_p) = (100, 200, 80, 60)$ Ground-truth box $B_g = (x_g, y_g, w_g, h_g) = (120, 210, 70, 55)$ **Step 1 — Compute corners:** - $B_p$: top-left $(100,200)$, bottom-right $(180,260)$ - $B_g$: top-left $(120,210)$, bottom-right $(190,265)$ **Step 2 — Compute intersection rectangle:** $$ \begin{aligned} x_{inter}^{left} &= \max(100,120) = 120 \\ y_{inter}^{top} &= \max(200,210) = 210 \\ x_{inter}^{right} &= \min(180,190) = 180 \\ y_{inter}^{bot} &= \min(260,265) = 260 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} A_{inter} &= (180-120) \times (260-210) = 60 \times 50 = 3\,000 \text{ px}^{2} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Compute union area:** $$ \begin{aligned} A_p &= 80 \times 60 = 4\,800 \\ A_g &= 70 \times 55 = 3\,850 \\ A_{union} &= A_p + A_g - A_{inter} = 4800 + 3850 - 3000 = 5\,650 \text{ px}^{2} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — IoU:** $$ \begin{aligned} IoU &= \frac{A_{inter}}{A_{union}} = \frac{3000}{5650} = 0.531 = 53.1\% \end{aligned} $$ Since $IoU \ge 0.5$, this prediction is a **True Positive** under the PASCAL-VOC criterion. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 End-to-End TSR Functional Block Diagram (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Camera Sensor
