<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: Define Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS). List any four examples with their functional domain.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** ADAS are electronic systems that assist the human driver by automating, adapting, or enhancing vehicle systems to improve safety and comfort. They operate at **SAE Level 1 (single-domain assistance)** or **SAE Level 2 (combined lateral and longitudinal assistance)**.

**Four Examples:**

| # | System | Domain |
|---|--------|--------|
| 1 | Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) | Longitudinal |
| 2 | Lane Keeping Assist (LKA) | Lateral |
| 3 | Blind Spot Monitoring (BSM) | Surround / Warning |
| 4 | Autonomous Emergency Braking (AEB) | Longitudinal / Safety |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write generic descriptions of "self-driving" without distinguishing **ADAS** from **autonomous driving**. Remember: at ADAS levels, the **human driver is always legally responsible** and must monitor the environment. Autonomous driving begins at **SAE Level 3**, where the system — not the driver — becomes the fallback performer under defined conditions.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: What is "time-headway" in ACC? Why is it preferred over a fixed-distance gap policy?**

**Model Answer:**

**Time-headway** is defined as the ratio of inter-vehicle gap to ego vehicle speed:

$$t_{hw} = \frac{d}{v_\text{ego}}$$

**Why preferred over fixed-distance policy:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> 1. **Speed-proportional safety:** As speed increases, a larger absolute gap is automatically enforced.
> 2. **String stability:** A constant time-headway policy ensures that disturbances do not amplify downstream in a platoon.
> 3. **Natural human driving model:** Empirical studies show humans naturally maintain roughly constant $t_{hw}$, not constant $d$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Examiners specifically look for the **string-stability** argument. Merely stating "it is more comfortable" will earn only partial marks; you **must** mention that constant-distance policies are *string-unstable* and amplify disturbances, which is why **constant time-headway** is the industry default.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Part B Module-1 questions carry **14 marks** with an internal choice (i.e., the student answers **one of two** full 14-mark questions). Each 14-mark question is split into two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Adapted]

**Q: (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the generic architecture of an ADAS function. Discuss the role of each block with examples of sensors and actuators used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the radar range equation for a 77 GHz automotive radar. State two reasons why mmWave frequencies are preferred for automotive radar. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

The generic ADAS architecture consists of **four functional blocks** connected in a closed loop:

1. **Sensing Block** — Acquires raw environmental and ego-state data.
   - *Sensors:* 77 GHz radar, mono/stereo camera, lidar, ultrasonic, GNSS/IMU.

2. **Perception Block** — Converts raw data into a structured environment model.
   - *Tasks:* object detection, lane detection, multi-object tracking, sensor fusion.
   - *Output:* a list of tracked objects with state vectors $(x, y, v_x, v_y, \text{class})$.

3. **Decision / Planning Block** — Translates perception into driving intent.
   - *Examples:* ACC gap controller, LKA steering planner, AEB trigger logic.
   - *Outputs:* desired acceleration $a_\text{des}$, desired steering angle $\delta_\text{des}$.

4. **Actuation Block** — Executes the decision through vehicle subsystems.
   - *Longitudinal:* electronic throttle, ESC hydraulic brake, electric parking brake.
   - *Lateral:* Electric Power Steering (EPS) torque overlay, rear-wheel steering.

> [!TIP]
> **Marks Allocation Guide:** Block diagram — 2 marks; explanation of each block — 3 marks; sensor/actuator examples — 2 marks.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

Starting from the isotropic radiator power density at range $R$:

$$S_t = \frac{P_t G_t}{4 \pi R^2}$$

Power intercepted by target with RCS $\sigma$:

$$P_\text{scat} = S_t \cdot \sigma = \frac{P_t G_t \sigma}{4 \pi R^2}$$

Power density at receiver (also at range $R$):

$$S_r = \frac{P_\text{scat}}{4 \pi R^2} = \frac{P_t G_t \sigma}{(4 \pi)^2 R^4}$$

Power collected by receive antenna of effective aperture $A_e = G_r \lambda^2 / (4 \pi)$:

$$P_r = \frac{P_t \, G_t \, G_r \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4 \pi)^3 \, R^4}$$

**Two reasons mmWave (76–77 GHz) is preferred:**

1. **Shorter wavelength** ($\lambda \approx 4$ mm) directly increases received power via the $\lambda^2$ term in the numerator, improving SNR for long-range detection.
2. **Physically small antennas** can produce narrow, high-gain beams — enabling compact bumper-mounted units with fine angular resolution.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often confuse the *monostatic* (single-antenna) and *bistatic* (separate Tx/Rx) forms. KTU questions typically expect the **monostatic** form with $G_t = G_r = G$. Writing $G_t G_r$ as $G^2$ saves a half-mark and demonstrates fluency.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Adapted]

**Q: (a)** Derive the minimum safe following distance formula for an AEB system. Comment on how a reduced reaction time influences the achievable collision-avoidance speed. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is Lane Keeping Assist (LKA)? With a suitable control law, explain how lateral error and heading error are combined to generate a steering correction. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Derivation (re-stated in exam-ready format):**

Given ego velocity $v_e$, lead velocity $v_l$, gap $d$, reaction time $t_r$, max deceleration $a_\text{max}$:

Stopping distance of ego: $d_\text{stop,e} = v_e t_r + v_e^2 / (2 a_\text{max})$
Stopping distance of lead: $d_\text{stop,l} = v_l^2 / (2 a_\text{lead})$
Required gap to avoid collision: $d_\text{min} = d_\text{stop,e} - d_\text{stop,l}$

Assuming $a_\text{lead} = a_\text{max}$:

$$d_\text{min} = (v_e - v_l) \cdot t_r + \frac{(v_e - v_l)^2}{2 a_\text{max}}$$

**Influence of reduced reaction time:**

The reaction-time term is **linear** in $t_r$, while the kinetic-energy term is **quadratic** in relative velocity. At low speeds, reducing $t_r$ from 1.5 s (human) to 0.2 s (ADAS) saves approximately:

$$\Delta d = (v_e - v_l) \cdot (1.5 - 0.2) = 1.3 (v_e - v_l)$$

At $v_\text{rel} = 20$ m/s, this is **26 m of saved distance** — directly raising the maximum avoidable collision speed by a substantial margin.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner expects:** Quantitative comparison between human and ADAS reaction times and the corresponding increase in avoidable-speed envelope.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Definition:** LKA is a lateral-domain ADAS function that actively corrects the steering of a vehicle to keep it within its lane, particularly during unintentional lane drift.

**Control Law:**

$$\delta_\text{LKA} = -K_{LP} \cdot e_y - K_{LD} \cdot \dot{e}_y - K_H \cdot \Delta\psi$$

| Term | Physical Meaning |
|------|------------------|
| $e_y$ | Lateral offset (perpendicular distance to lane center) |
| $\dot{e}_y$ | Rate of change of lateral offset (lateral velocity) |
| $\Delta\psi$ | Heading error (angle between vehicle axis and lane tangent) |
| $K_{LP}$, $K_{LD}$, $K_H$ | Tuned feedback gains |

**Interpretation:**

- The **$-K_{LP} e_y$** term pulls the vehicle back toward the lane center (proportional correction).
- The **$-K_{LD} \dot{e}_y$** term provides damping, preventing overshoot and oscillation.
- The **$-K_H \Delta\psi$** term provides anticipatory correction, steering in the direction of the lane's heading.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Many students write only the **lateral-offset** term and forget the heading term. KTU examiners specifically look for **all three** terms with **physical meaning** for each. Drawing the control-loop block diagram and labeling the signals earns the full 7 marks.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Loss Points on Driver Assistance Questions:**
> 1. **Failing to draw diagrams** — A block diagram is worth 1–2 marks and is the most overlooked element.
> 2. **Confusing ADAS levels (L1 vs L2)** with autonomous levels (L3–L5).
> 3. **Skipping units** in derivations — always include $\text{m/s}^2$, $\text{m/s}$, or $\text{Nm}$.
> 4. **Writing the radar equation with $G^2$ but writing $G_t$ and $G_r$ as if independent** — the monostatic assumption must be stated.
> 5. **Forgetting to define all variables** in the LKA control law.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Driver Assistance**

- **Definition:** ADAS = electronic/mechatronic systems assisting (not replacing) the human driver; operates at **SAE Level 1 or Level 2**.
- **SAE Levels:** Level 0 = none; Level 1 = single domain; Level 2 = combined lateral + longitudinal; **Levels 3+ are NOT ADAS** (they are automation).
- **Three driving domains:** Longitudinal, Lateral, Surround/Informational.
- **Generic ADAS pipeline:** **Sense → Perceive → Plan → Actuate** (closed loop).
- **Key longitudinal functions:** ACC, AEB, FCW.
- **Key lateral functions:** LKA, LDW.
- **Key surround functions:** BSM, Parking Assist, DMS.
- **Time-headway:** $t_{hw} = d / v_\text{ego}$, typical range **1.4–2.2 s**.
- **Stopping distance:** $d_\text{stop} = v t_r + v^2 / (2 a_\text{max})$ — memorize this in both forms.
- **Minimum safe gap (AEB):** $d_\text{min} = v_\text{rel} t_r + v_\text{rel}^2 / (2 a_\text{max})$.
- **TTC:** $\text{TTC} = d / v_\text{rel}$; warning threshold $\sim$ 2.5 s, braking threshold $\sim$ 1.4 s.
- **Radar equation (monostatic):** $P_r = P_t G_t G_r \lambda^2 \sigma / (4\pi)^3 R^4$.
- **Radar band:** **76–77 GHz** mmWave; preferred for compact antennas and high $P_r$ ($\lambda^2$ term).
- **LKA control law:** $\delta_\text{LKA} = -K_{LP} e_y - K_{LD} \dot{e}_y - K_H \Delta\psi$ — three-term structure is essential.
- **Lane model:** $y(x) = a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 x^2$ (parabolic/quadratic fit).
- **Sensor fusion:** Industry standard combines radar (range/velocity) + camera (classification) + lidar (3D shape) + ultrasonic (parking) — no single sensor satisfies all functions.
- **Why ADAS matters:** ~90% of accidents trace to human error; reducing $t_r$ from 1.5 s to 0.2 s is the single largest safety gain.
- **String stability:** CTH policy is string-stable if $k_1 t_{hw}^2 + 2 k_2 t_{hw} > 1$.

> [!TIP]
> **One-line recall mantra for the exam hall:** *"ADAS is Sense–Perceive–Plan–Act at SAE L1/L2, dominated by ACC (longitudinal), LKA (lateral), and AEB (safety)."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Unsupervised Learning - Algorithms: Clustering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAUT865 - ADVANCED VEHICLE TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Unsupervised Learning - Algorithms: Clustering # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Unsupervised Learning** is a paradigm of Machine Learning (ML) in which an algorithm is trained on a dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{ \mathbf{x}_1, \mathbf{x}_2, \dots, \mathbf{x}_n \}$ that contains **input features only**, with no associated target labels $y$. The objective is to discover latent structure, groupings, density distributions, or low-dimensional manifolds inherent to the data. **Clustering**, within this paradigm, is defined as the task of partitioning an unlabeled dataset into $k$ homogeneous subgroups (clusters) such that: $$ \mathcal{C} = \{ C_1, C_2, \dots, C_k \}, \quad \text{where} \quad \bigcup_{i=1}^{k} C_i = \mathcal{D} \quad \text{and} \quad C_i \cap C_j = \emptyset \;\; \forall \; i \neq j $$ The assignment is governed by an **internal similarity criterion** (intra-cluster compactness) and an **external separation criterion** (inter-cluster isolation). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In Module 1 of *Advanced Vehicle Technology*, clustering is positioned as the foundational data-mining tool for **Connected, Autonomous, Shared, and Electric (CASE)** vehicles — used for driver-behavior profiling, fault diagnostics from CAN-bus telemetry, and traffic-pattern recognition. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition Imagine a busy **multi-level parking lot** during a college festival. There are no signboards, no painted lane markings (i.e., no "labels"), and hundreds of cars have parked themselves seemingly at random. Now picture a security guard standing on the top floor looking down. Without being told *which car belongs to whom*, the guard's brain instinctively groups them: - **Group 1 (top-left):** All red hatchbacks parked neatly in a row → *compact cluster (low intra-distance)*. - **Group 2 (center):** A scatter of two-wheelers scattered loosely → *sparse, low-density cluster*. - **Group 3 (basement):** A single black SUV parked far away from every other vehicle → *outlier / noise point*. The guard did this using only **proximity, color similarity, and density cues**. A clustering algorithm does *exactly* this — but mathematically, deterministically, and at scale across millions of telemetry records streaming from a fleet of vehicles. ## 1.3 Why Clustering Matters in Advanced Vehicle Technology | Automotive Application | Role of Clustering | |---|---| | **Driver Behavior Profiling** | Segments drivers into *Aggressive / Normal / Eco* classes from accelerometer & throttle-position data | | **Predictive Maintenance** | Groups similar engine-vibration signatures to detect early-stage bearing faults | | **Traffic Flow Analysis** | Identifies congestion hotspots and free-flow corridors from V2X data | | **Autonomous Driving (LiDAR)** | Separates foreground obstacles from background in 3-D point clouds | | **EV Battery Analytics** | Clusters cell-voltage curves to identify aging modules | | **Insurance Telematics** | Customer segmentation for usage-based premiums | ## 1.4 Core Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold Emphasis) - **Silhouette Coefficient range:** $-1 \le s_i \le +1$ (closer to $+1$ is better) - **Davies–Bouldin Index:** $\text{DBI} \ge 0$ (lower is better) - **Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS):** measured in the squared units of the feature space - **Euclidean norm $L_2$** is the **default distance metric** unless stated otherwise. > [!NOTE] > **Bird's-Eye View of the Clustering Family:** > > 1. **Partition-based** → K-Means, K-Medoids > 2. **Hierarchical** → Agglomerative, Divisive > 3. **Density-based** → DBSCAN, OPTICS > 4. **Model-based** → Gaussian Mixture Models (GMM) > 5. **Grid-based** → STING, CLIQUE # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Taxonomy of Clustering Algorithms — Operational Logic ### A. Partition-Based: K-Means (Lloyd's Algorithm) **The "Why":** K-Means assumes clusters are *spherical, equally sized, and isotropic* in feature space. It seeks to minimize the **Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS)**, also called the *inertia*. **The "How" — Step-by-Step Operational Logic:** 1. The user specifies the number of clusters $k$ (a *hyperparameter*). 2. Initialize $k$ centroids $\{\mu_1, \mu_2, \dots, \mu_k\}$ — by **Forgy** (random sample), **Random Partition**, or **K-Means++**. 3. **Assignment Step:** Each data point $\mathbf{x}_j$ is assigned to the nearest centroid using squared Euclidean distance. 4. **Update Step:** Each centroid is recomputed as the arithmetic mean of all points currently assigned to its cluster. 5. Steps 3–4 are repeated until **convergence** (centroids stop moving, or a maximum iteration count is reached). ### B. Hierarchical: Agglomerative (Bottom-Up) **The "Why":** Does *not* require pre-specifying $k$. Produces a **dendrogram** — a tree-like visualization showing the merging hierarchy. **The "How":** 1. Start with $n$ singleton clusters, each containing one data point. 2. Compute an $n \times n$ **proximity (distance) matrix** between every pair of points. 3. Repeatedly merge the two *closest* clusters based on a **linkage criterion** (Single, Complete, Average, Ward). 4. Stop when a single cluster containing all points remains. ### C. Density-Based: DBSCAN **The "Why":** Detects **arbitrarily shaped** clusters and explicitly labels noise. Requires two parameters: $\epsilon$ (neighborhood radius) and *MinPts* (minimum points to form a dense region). **The "How" — Three Point Categories:** - **Core Point:** Has at least *MinPts* neighbors within radius $\epsilon$. - **Border Point:** Lies within $\epsilon$ of a core point but has fewer than *MinPts* neighbors. - **Noise Point:** Neither core nor border — flagged as an outlier. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Key Variable Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Squared Euclidean Distance** | $d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \sum_{d=1}^{D}(x_d - y_d)^2$ | $\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y} \in \mathbb{R}^D$ | | 2 | **Manhattan Distance ($L_1$)** | $d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \sum_{d=1}^{D} \vert x_d - y_d \vert$ | Use `\vert` not `\vert` | | 3 | **Minkowski Distance ($L_p$)** | $d_p(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \left(\sum_d \vert x_d - y_d \vert^p\right)^{1/p}$ | $p \ge 1$; $p=2$ → Euclidean | | 4 | **WCSS Objective (K-Means)** | $J = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{\mathbf{x} \in C_i} \Vert \mathbf{x} - \mu_i \Vert_2^2$ | Minimize via Lloyd iteration | | 5 | **Centroid Update Rule** | $\mu_i^{(t+1)} = \dfrac{1}{\vert C_i^{(t)} \vert} \sum_{\mathbf{x} \in C_i^{(t)}} \mathbf{x}$ | Mean of assigned points | | 6 | **Silhouette Score for point $i$** | $s_i = \dfrac{b_i - a_i}{\max(a_i, b_i)}$ | $a_i$ = mean intra-cluster dist; $b_i$ = mean nearest-cluster dist | | 7 | **Davies–Bouldin Index** | $\text{DBI} = \dfrac{1}{k} \sum_{i=1}^{k} \max_{j \neq i} \left( \dfrac{S_i + S_j}{M_{ij}} \right)$ | $S_i$ = cluster scatter; $M_{ij}$ = centroid separation | | 8 | **DBSCAN Reachability** | $N_\epsilon(\mathbf{x}) = \{ \mathbf{y} : d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) \le \epsilon \}$ | Neighborhood definition | | 9 | **Ward Linkage Cost** | $\Delta(A,B) = \dfrac{\vert A \vert \cdot \vert B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert} \, \Vert \mu_A - \mu_B \Vert^2$ | Minimizes WCSS increase on merge | | 10 | **Elbow Method (Optimal $k$)** | Plot $J(k)$ vs $k$; pick the "elbow" kink | Heuristic only | ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility In **automotive production lines**, K-Means is used to cluster **weld-spot quality data** from inline sensors — operators can instantly identify a malfunctioning welding robot whose cluster drifts away from the centroid of healthy units. In **fleet telematics**, DBSCAN excels at detecting **anomalous driving events** (harsh braking, swerving) because these appear as sparse, isolated clusters against a dense background of normal trips. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked to *compare* algorithms, always present your answer using three axes — **(i) cluster shape assumption**, **(ii) scalability**, and **(iii) noise handling**. This is the exact rubric examiners reward. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation I: Optimal Centroid for K-Means (Closed-Form Proof) We must prove that the cluster mean $\mu_i$ minimizes the sum of squared Euclidean distances from the points in $C_i$ to itself. **Step 1 —** Define the cost contributed by cluster $C_i$: $$ J_i(\mu_i) = \sum_{\mathbf{x}_j \in C_i} \Vert \mathbf{x}_j - \mu_i \Vert_2^2 $$ **Step 2 —** Expand the squared norm (each $\mathbf{x}_j \in \mathbb{R}^D$): $$ J_i(\mu_i) = \sum_{j \in C_i} \left( \mathbf{x}_j - \mu_i \right)^\top \left( \mathbf{x}_j - \mu_i \right) = \sum_{j \in C_i} \left( \mathbf{x}_j^\top \mathbf{x}_j - 2\mu_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j + \mu_i^\top \mu_i \right) $$ **Step 3 —** Take the gradient with respect to $\mu_i$ and set it to zero (first-order optimality condition): $$ \nabla_{\mu_i} J_i = \sum_{j \in C_i} \left( -2\mathbf{x}_j + 2\mu_i \right) = 0 $$ **Step 4 —** Factor the constant $2$ out and solve: $$ \sum_{j \in C_i} \mathbf{x}_j - \sum_{j \in C_i} \mu_i = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sum_{j \in C_i} \mu_i = \sum_{j \in C_i} \mathbf{x}_j $$ **Step 5 —** Note that $\mu_i$ is constant with respect to the summation index $j$, so $\sum_{j \in C_i} \mu_i = \vert C_i \vert \cdot \mu_i$. Hence: $$ \mu_i^{\star} = \frac{1}{\vert C_i \vert} \sum_{j \in C_i} \mathbf{x}_j $$ > [!NOTE] > **Conclusion:** The optimal centroid is the **arithmetic mean** of its assigned points. This is why K-Means can only produce *convex, isotropic* clusters — the mean is a linear operator that preserves spherical geometry. ## 3.2 Derivation II: Silhouette Score for a Single Point **Step 1 —** For point $i$ in cluster $C_I$, define its **mean intra-cluster distance**: $$ a_i = \frac{1}{\vert C_I \vert - 1} \sum_{j \in C_I, \, j \neq i} d(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) $$ **Step 2 —** For every *other* cluster $C_J$, compute the mean distance from $i$ to all points in $C_J$: $$ d(i, C_J) = \frac{1}{\vert C_J \vert} \sum_{j \in C_J} d(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) $$ **Step 3 —** Define $b_i$ as the **smallest** of these means — the distance to the *nearest neighboring cluster*: $$ b_i = \min_{J \neq I} d(i, C_J) $$ **Step 4 —** Combine using the standardized form: $$ s_i = \frac{b_i - a_i}{\max(a_i, b_i)} $$ **Step 5 — Interpretation of bounds:** - If $a_i \ll b_i$, then $s_i \to +1$ → point is **well-clustered**. - If $a_i \gg b_i$, then $s_i \to -1$ → point may have been **misassigned**. - If $a_i \approx b_i$, then $s_i \approx 0$ → point lies **on the cluster boundary**. ## 3.3 Derivation III: Ward Linkage Cost from Variance Perspective **Step 1 —** For a cluster $A$ with $n_A$ points, its within-cluster variance is: $$ S_A = \sum_{\mathbf{x} \in A} \Vert \mathbf{x} - \mu_A \Vert^2 $$ **Step 2 —** If we merge $A$ and $B$ into $A \cup B$, the new variance becomes: $$ S_{A \cup B} = \sum_{\mathbf{x} \in A \cup B} \Vert \mathbf{x} - \mu_{A \cup B} \Vert^2 $$ **Step 3 —** Using the parallel-axis theorem (a.k.a. Huygens' identity), the **merge cost** equals: $$ \Delta(A, B) = S_{A \cup B} - S_A - S_B $$ **Step 4 —** Expanding $\mu_{A \cup B} = \dfrac{n_A \mu_A + n_B \mu_B}{n_A + n_B}$ and simplifying: $$ \Delta(A, B) = \frac{n_A \, n_B}{n_A + n_B} \, \Vert \mu_A - \mu_B \Vert^2 $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** Ward linkage **minimizes information loss** at every merge. In automotive applications (e.g., grouping similar crash-test profiles), Ward is the most defensible hierarchical method because it preserves *within-group homogeneity*. ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Property | K-Means | Hierarchical (Aggl.) | DBSCAN | |---|---|---|---| | **Cluster Shape** | Spherical only | Any (depends on linkage) | Arbitrary | | **Specify $k$ in advance?** | Yes | No (cut dendrogram) | No | | **Handles Noise?** | No (forces assignment) | No | **Yes (explicit)** | | **Scalability** | $O(nkt)$ | $O(n^2)$ to $O(n^3)$ | $O(n \log n)$ with index | | **Deterministic?** | No (random init) | Yes | Yes | | **Sensitivity to Scaling** | High | High | Moderate | | **Best Automotive Use** | Driver segmentation | Crash-test grouping | Anomaly detection | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Clustering Pipeline for Vehicle Telematics ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Telematics Stream
CAN-bus, GPS, IMU] --> B[Data Preprocessing] B --> B1[Missing Value Imputation] B --> B2[Outlier Removal via IQR] B --> B3[Feature Scaling
StandardScaler / MinMax] B1 --> C[Feature Engineering] B2 --> C B3 --> C C --> C1[Jerk, Throttle Gradient,
Steering Entropy] C1 --> D[Algorithm Selection Module] D --> D1{k known?} D1 -- Yes --> E1[K-Means Engine] D1 -- No and small n --> E2[Agglomerative Engine] D1 -- No and noise suspected --> E3[DBSCAN Engine] E1 --> F[Cluster Validation Layer] E2 --> F E3 --> F F --> F1[Silhouette Score] F --> F2[Davies-Bouldin Index] F --> F3[Elbow Method] F1 --> G[Driver Profile Output] F2 --> G F3 --> G G --> G1[Aggressive Cluster] G --> G2[Normal Cluster] G --> G3[Eco Cluster] ``` ## 4.2 Algorithmic State Diagram — K-Means Iteration Cycle ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> InitCentroid InitCentroid: Initialize k Centroids InitCentroid --> AssignPoints AssignPoints: Assign Each x_j to Nearest Centroid AssignPoints --> UpdateCentroids UpdateCentroids: Recompute mu_i as Cluster Mean UpdateCentroids --> CheckConvergence CheckConvergence: Converged or Max Iter? CheckConvergence -- No --> AssignPoints CheckConvergence -- Yes --> OutputClusters OutputClusters: Final Partition C OutputClusters --> [*] ``` ## 4.3 Conceptual Flow — Agglomerative Clustering (Dendrogram Construction) ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Point P1] --> M1 P2[Point P2] --> M1 P3[Point P3] --> M2 P4[Point P4] --> M2 P5[Point P5] --> M3 P6[Point P6] --> M3 M1[Merge: Closest Pair 1
distance = d1] --> M4 M2[Merge: Closest Pair 2
distance = d2] --> M4 M3[Merge: Closest Pair 3
distance = d3] --> M5 M4[Cluster A+B] --> M5 M5[Final Single Cluster
at root of dendrogram] --> Cut[Cut Dendrogram
at threshold tau] Cut --> K1[Cluster 1] Cut --> K2[Cluster 2] Cut --> K3[Cluster 3] ``` ## 4.4 DBSCAN Neighborhood Classification Schematic ```mermaid flowchart TB Q[Query Point Q] --> R[Draw epsilon-ball
around Q] R --> S{Neighbor Count
geq MinPts?} S -- Yes --> CP[Core Point
Q is dense] S -- No --> T{Is Q within
epsilon of a Core Point?} T -- Yes --> BP[Border Point] T -- No --> NP[Noise Point
Outlier] CP --> U[Start/Extend
Density-Reachable Cluster] BP --> U NP --> V[Flagged as Anomaly] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation Note:** In the K-Means cycle, the convergence guarantee is *local* — Lloyd's algorithm converges to a **local minimum** of the WCSS objective, not a global one. This is why **K-Means++** initialization is critical in engineering deployments. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]** > Differentiate between **supervised** and **unsupervised learning**. State two real-world automotive applications where unsupervised clustering is preferred over supervised classification. **(3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Supervised Learning | Unsupervised Learning | |---|---|---| | **Label availability** | Requires labeled data $(x, y)$ pairs | Uses unlabeled data $x$ only | | **Goal** | Learn mapping $f: X \to Y$ | Discover latent structure in $X$ | | **Evaluation** | Accuracy, Precision, Recall | Silhouette, DBI, WCSS | | **Examples** | SVM, Random Forest | K-Means, DBSCAN, PCA | **Automotive applications where unsupervised is preferred:** 1. **Anomaly detection in CAN-bus frames** — labels for "attack patterns" are scarce and constantly evolving; DBSCAN can flag novel anomalies without prior training. 2. **Driver-style discovery** — natural groupings (Eco / Normal / Aggressive) emerge without needing a human to pre-label every driver. > [!WARNING] > **Pitfall:** Students often write *"unsupervised learning is more accurate"* — this is wrong. Unsupervised is preferred when **labels are unavailable, expensive, or unknown a priori**, not because it is inherently more accurate. --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 1]** > Explain the **Elbow Method** for determining the optimal number of clusters $k$ in K-Means. State one limitation. **(3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** The Elbow Method plots the **WCSS** $J(k)$ against $k$ for $k = 1, 2, \dots, K_{\max}$. As $k$ increases, $J(k)$ monotonically decreases because each cluster becomes tighter. The optimal $k$ is identified at the **"elbow point"** — where the marginal reduction in WCSS sharply drops (analogous to an elbow joint in a plot). Mathematically, one seeks the $k$ that maximizes the second-difference curvature: $$ \Delta^2 J(k) = J(k-1) - 2J(k) + J(k+1) $$ **Limitation:** The elbow is often *visually ambiguous* — there may be no sharp kink, especially in high-dimensional or overlapping automotive datasets. In such cases, the **Silhouette Method** is recommended as a complementary diagnostic. --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks) — K-Means Deep Dive** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper Module 1]` #### Part (a) — 7 Marks > State and explain the **K-Means clustering algorithm** with its objective function. Derive the update rule for the centroid. Apply the **first two iterations** of K-Means (with $k=2$) on the following 2-D vehicle dataset and show the cluster assignments: > > Points: $P_1=(2,10), \; P_2=(2,5), \; P_3=(8,4), \; P_4=(5,8), \; P_5=(7,5), \; P_6=(6,4), \; P_7=(1,2), \; P_8=(4,9)$ > > Initial centroids: $\mu_1 = (2,10)$ and $\mu_2 = (5,8)$. **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Objective function of K-Means:** $$ J = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{\mathbf{x} \in C_i} \Vert \mathbf{x} - \mu_i \Vert_2^2 $$ **Step 2 — Centroid update rule (derived in Section 3.1):** $$ \mu_i^{\star} = \frac{1}{\vert C_i \vert} \sum_{\mathbf{x} \in C_i} \mathbf{x} $$ **Step 3 — Iteration 1: Distance computation (squared Euclidean):** For each point, compute $d^2(P_j, \mu_1)$ and $d^2(P_j, \mu_2)$, then assign to the closer centroid. $$ d^2(P_1, \mu_1) = 0, \quad d^2(P_1, \mu_2) = (2-5)^2 + (10-8)^2 = 13 $$ Assign $P_1 \to C_1$. $$ d^2(P_2, \mu_1) = (2-2)^2 + (5-10)^2 = 25, \quad d^2(P_2, \mu_2) = (2-5)^2 + (5-8)^2 = 18 $$ Assign $P_2 \to C_2$. $$ d^2(P_3, \mu_1) = (8-2)^2 + (4-10)^2 = 72, \quad d^2(P_3, \mu_2) = (8-5)^2 + (4-8)^2 = 25 $$ Assign $P_3 \to C_2$. $$ d^2(P_4, \mu_1) = (5-2)^2 + (8-10)^2 = 13, \quad d^2(P_4, \mu_2) = 0 $$ Assign $P_4 \to C_2$. $$ d^2(P_5, \mu_1) = (7-2)^2 + (5-10)^2 = 50, \quad d^2(P_5, \mu_2) = (7-5)^2 + (5-8)^2 = 13 $$ Assign $P_5 \to C_2$. $$ d^2(P_6, \mu_1) = (6-2)^2 + (4-10)^2 = 52, \quad d^2(P_6, \mu_2) = (6-5)^2 + (4-8)^2 = 17 $$ Assign $P_6 \to C_2$. $$ d^2(P_7, \mu_1) = (1-2)^2 + (2-10)^2 = 65, \quad d^2(P_7, \mu_2) = (1-5)^2 + (2-8)^2 = 52 $$ Assign $P_7 \to C_2$. $$ d^2(P_8, \mu_1) = (4-2)^2 + (9-10)^2 = 5, \quad d^2(P_8, \mu_2) = (4-5)^2 + (9-8)^2 = 2 $$ Assign $P_8 \to C_2$. **After Iteration 1:** $$ C_1 = \{P_1\}, \qquad C_2 = \{P_2, P_3, P_4, P_5, P_6, P_7, P_8\} $$ **Centroid updates:** $$ \mu_1^{(1)} = (2, 10) $$ $$ \mu_2^{(1)} = \frac{1}{7}\left[(2+8+5+7+6+1+4),\;(5+4+8+5+4+2+9)\right] = \left(\frac{33}{7}, \frac{37}{7}\right) \approx (4.71, 5.29) $$ **Step 4 — Iteration 2: Re-assignment with updated centroids.** Recompute distances against $\mu_1^{(1)} = (2,10)$ and $\mu_2^{(1)} \approx (4.71, 5.29)$. $$ d^2(P_2, \mu_2^{(1)}) \approx (2-4.71)^2 + (5-5.29)^2 \approx 7.40, \quad d^2(P_2, \mu_1) = 25 $$ Assign $P_2 \to C_2$ (still). Continue similarly; after iteration 2, the typical stable partition converges to: $$ C_1 = \{P_1, P_4, P_8\}, \qquad C_2 = \{P_2, P_3, P_5, P_6, P_7\} $$ with $\mu_1 \approx (3.67, 9.0)$ and $\mu_2 \approx (4.8, 4.0)$. --- #### Part (b) — 7 Marks > Explain the **K-Means++ initialization** scheme. Why does it outperform random initialization? Mention its time complexity. **Model Solution:** **Algorithm Steps of K-Means++:** 1. Choose the first centroid $\mu_1$ uniformly at random from the dataset. 2. For each remaining point $\mathbf{x}_j$, compute its squared distance $D(\mathbf{x}_j)^2 = \min_{i} \Vert \mathbf{x}_j - \mu_i \Vert^2$ to the *nearest already-chosen* centroid. 3. Select the next centroid $\mu_{\text{new}}$ from the dataset with probability proportional to $D(\mathbf{x}_j)^2$ — i.e., points far from existing centroids have a higher chance of being chosen. 4. Repeat steps 2–3 until $k$ centroids are chosen. 5. Proceed with standard Lloyd's iteration. **Why it outperforms random initialization:** K-Means++ provides a **probabilistic guarantee** that the initial seeding is $O(\log k)$ competitive with the *optimal* WCSS solution (Arthur & Vassilvitskii, 2007). Random seeding can catastrophically place both centroids in the same dense region, leaving another cluster unrepresented. K-Means++ *spreads* the seeds across the feature space, drastically reducing the number of iterations needed for convergence. **Time Complexity:** - Per-iteration of K-Means: $O(nkd)$ where $n$ = points, $k$ = clusters, $d$ = dimensions. - K-Means++ initialization overhead: $O(nkd)$. - Overall: $O(nkdi)$ where $i$ is the number of iterations to convergence. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Hierarchical & Density-Based Alternative** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 1]` #### Part (a) — 7 Marks > Describe the **Agglomerative Hierarchical Clustering** algorithm. Explain the four common linkage criteria with their formulas. Which linkage is most suitable for **clustering crash-test dummy biomechanical data**, and why? **Model Solution:** **Algorithm:** 1. Initialize each point as its own cluster → $n$ clusters. 2. Compute the $n \times n$ pairwise distance matrix. 3. Repeat: - Find the pair of clusters $(C_a, C_b)$ with the smallest inter-cluster distance per the chosen linkage. - Merge them. 4. Stop when one cluster remains; cut the dendrogram at the desired height to obtain $k$ clusters. **Four Linkage Criteria (formulas):** $$ \text{Single: } \; d_{\text{single}}(C_a, C_b) = \min_{\mathbf{x} \in C_a, \mathbf{y} \in C_b} d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) $$ $$ \text{Complete: } \; d_{\text{complete}}(C_a, C_b) = \max_{\mathbf{x} \in C_a, \mathbf{y} \in C_b} d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) $$ $$ \text{Average: } \; d_{\text{avg}}(C_a, C_b) = \frac{1}{n_a n_b} \sum_{\mathbf{x} \in C_a, \mathbf{y} \in C_b} d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) $$ $$ \text{Ward: } \; d_{\text{Ward}}(C_a, C_b) = \frac{n_a n_b}{n_a + n_b} \, \Vert \mu_a - \mu_b \Vert^2 $$ **Best Choice for Crash-Test Data: Ward Linkage.** *Reason:* Biomechanical injury metrics (HIC, chest deflection, femur load) must remain **internally homogeneous** within regulatory groupings (e.g., Euro NCAP star ratings). Ward linkage directly minimizes the *increase* in within-cluster variance at every merge, preserving intra-group compactness — critical for safety-class certification. --- #### Part (b) — 7 Marks > Explain the **DBSCAN** algorithm in detail. How does it classify a point as **core**, **border**, or **noise**? Justify why DBSCAN is preferred over K-Means for **detecting anomalous vehicle sensor patterns** from an EV battery management system (BMS). **Model Solution:** **DBSCAN Definition:** Given radius $\epsilon$ and minimum points *MinPts*, the $\epsilon$-neighborhood of point $\mathbf{p}$ is: $$ N_\epsilon(\mathbf{p}) = \{ \mathbf{q} \in \mathcal{D} : d(\mathbf{p}, \mathbf{q}) \le \epsilon \} $$ **Point Classification Rules:** 1. **Core Point:** $\vert N_\epsilon(\mathbf{p}) \vert \ge \text{MinPts}$. 2. **Border Point:** $\vert N_\epsilon(\mathbf{p}) \vert < \text{MinPts}$ **AND** $\mathbf{p} \in N_\epsilon(\mathbf{q})$ for some core point $\mathbf{q}$. 3. **Noise Point:** Neither core nor border. **Algorithm Steps:** 1. Label all points as unvisited. 2. Pick an unvisited point $\mathbf{p}$ and mark it visited. 3. If $\vert N_\epsilon(\mathbf{p}) \vert \ge \text{MinPts}$, start a new cluster; recursively add all density-reachable points. 4. Else, temporarily label $\mathbf{p}$ as noise. 5. Repeat until all points are visited. **Why DBSCAN is Preferred for BMS Anomaly Detection:** - **Cluster shape is arbitrary:** Normal BMS readings form a dense, non-spherical manifold in feature space; K-Means would fragment them incorrectly. - **Explicit noise handling:** A faulty cell whose voltage curve deviates from the pack will appear as a **noise point**, enabling immediate fault isolation. - **No need to pre-specify $k$:** The number of fault modes is not known in advance — DBSCAN discovers it adaptively. - **Robust to outliers:** In a real EV fleet, only ~2% of trips are anomalous; DBSCAN does not force-fit these rare events into spurious clusters the way K-Means would. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students confuse **density-reachable** with **density-connected**. Density-reachable is a *chained* transitive relation (via core points); density-connected requires a *mutual* core point. Mis-stating this costs 2 marks in long answers. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Clustering = unsupervised grouping** based on intrinsic similarity; no labels are used. - **K-Means** minimizes WCSS $J = \sum_i \sum_{\mathbf{x} \in C_i} \Vert \mathbf{x} - \mu_i \Vert^2$; the optimal centroid is the arithmetic mean. - **Lloyd's algorithm** is iterative (Assignment → Update → Convergence) but only guarantees **local minima** — hence the importance of **K-Means++**. - **Agglomerative Hierarchical Clustering** builds a **dendrogram**; **Ward linkage** is preferred when within-group variance must be minimized (e.g., crash-test data). - **DBSCAN** uses $\epsilon$ and *MinPts*; it is the gold standard for **arbitrary-shaped clusters** and **explicit noise/outlier detection**. - **Evaluation metrics:** Silhouette ($+1$ ideal, $-1$ misassigned), Davies–Bouldin (lower is better), Elbow Method (visual heuristic). - **Automotive use cases to memorize:** 1. Driver behavior profiling → K-Means 2. Anomaly detection in CAN-bus / BMS → DBSCAN 3. Crash-test severity grouping → Ward hierarchical 4. Traffic congestion hotspot identification → K-Means or DBSCAN - **Feature scaling** (StandardScaler / MinMax) is **non-negotiable** before applying distance-based clustering. - **Curse of Dimensionality:** For high-dimensional automotive telemetry, consider **PCA pre-processing** before clustering.

topics.title: Dimensionality Reduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT865 - ADVANCED VEHICLE TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Dimensionality Reduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Dimensionality Reduction (DR)** is a mathematical and statistical process of transforming high-dimensional vehicle data (collected from multiple onboard sensors, ECUs, and ADAS subsystems) into a lower-dimensional representation that preserves the most informative characteristics of the original dataset. In the context of **Advanced Vehicle Technology**, it is applied to engine telemetry streams, LiDAR/camera point clouds, battery State-of-Health (SoH) records, and Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) communication logs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Dimensionality Reduction is defined as the mapping of a dataset $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$ residing in a $d$-dimensional ambient space onto a reduced representation $Y \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times k}$, where $k \ll d$, such that the intrinsic geometric, statistical, or semantic structure of $X$ is maximally retained in $Y$.*

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Diagnostic Dashboard" Concept

Imagine a modern **Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV)** transmitting **200 parameters every 100 milliseconds** to a cloud-based fleet management server (cell voltage, current, pack temperature, State-of-Charge, State-of-Health, motor RPM, torque, GPS coordinates, accelerometer data, wheel-slip ratios, regenerative braking intensity, tyre pressure, ambient humidity, etc.).

A human engineer cannot intuitively interpret a 200-dimensional live feed. **Dimensionality Reduction is analogous to condensing a 200-page factory service manual into a 5-page executive summary** — the summary must capture every *critical* engineering insight (range, thermal runway risk, driver aggression index) while discarding redundant noise.

In simple words:
> It is the art of **squashing** high-dimensional data into a low-dimensional form **without losing the story**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Vehicle-Tech Metrics Used with DR:**
> - **Explained Variance Ratio (EVR):** $\eta \in [0,1]$, target $\eta \geq 0.95$ for diagnostic compression.
> - **Reconstruction Error (RE):** typically $\text{RE} < 0.05$ in production-grade vehicle telemetry pipelines.
> - **Computational Latency:** Critical real-time constraint, $\tau_{\text{DR}} \leq 10\text{ ms}$ per inference frame in ADAS.

### 1.3 Necessity of DR in Advanced Vehicle Technology

| Engineering Motivation | Quantitative Impact |
|---|---|
| **Curse of Dimensionality** | In autonomous driving perception, raw point clouds exceed **$\mathbf{10^6}$ points/sec**; DR prunes this to $\sim \mathbf{10^3}$ features. |
| **Storage & Bandwidth Optimization** | V2X data compression of up to **$\mathbf{60\%}$** using PCA-based feature compression. |
| **Real-Time Inference Speed** | Reducing feature count from $d = 1024 \rightarrow k = 32$ yields **$\sim 30\times$** speed-up in ML inference. |
| **Noise Suppression** | Sensor fusion benefits — DR filters Gaussian noise inherent in MEMS accelerometers and gyroscopes. |
| **Visualization for Diagnostics** | 2D/3D embedding of fault clusters allows service technicians to visually triage vehicles. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Dimensionality Reduction Techniques

Dimensionality Reduction methods are broadly classified into two philosophical schools:

**A. Feature Selection (Subspace Selection)**
- Selects a subset $S \subset \{1, 2, \dots, d\}$ of existing original features.
- Retains *interpretable* raw sensor channels.
- Methods: Filter (Chi-Square, Mutual Information), Wrapper (Recursive Feature Elimination), Embedded (L1 regularization — **Lasso**).

**B. Feature Extraction (Projection / Manifold Learning)**
- Constructs **new synthetic features** as linear/nonlinear combinations of originals.
- Most widely applied in vehicle tech: PCA, LDA, t-SNE, UMAP, Autoencoders.

### 2.2 Why It Works — The Mathematical Intuition

If we model the vehicle dataset $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$ with mean vector $\mu \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ and covariance matrix $\Sigma \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d}$, real engineering systems exhibit a strong structure:

- **Low Intrinsic Dimensionality:** Although $d$ may be large, data lies near a low-dimensional **manifold** $\mathcal{M}$ of dimension $k$.
- **Covariance Concentration:** Most eigenvalues $\lambda_i$ of $\Sigma$ are very small (noise directions); only the top-$k$ carry signal.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **exam-ladder essential** for KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions.

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Sigma = \frac{1}{n-1}(X - \bar{X})^{\top}(X - \bar{X})$ | Sample covariance matrix | PCA foundation |
| 2 | $\Sigma v_i = \lambda_i v_i$ | Eigenvalue equation | Principal axes |
| 3 | $\text{EVR}_k = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{k}\lambda_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{d}\lambda_i}$ | Explained variance ratio | Choosing $k$ |
| 4 | $X_{\text{std}} = \dfrac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$ | Z-score standardization | Pre-PCA scaling |
| 5 | $Y = X_{\text{std}} W_k$ | PCA projection ($W_k \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times k}$) | Dimensionality compression |
| 6 | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{recon}} = \lVert X - \hat{X} \rVert_{F}^{2}$ | Frobenius reconstruction loss | Autoencoder / PCA reconstruction |
| 7 | $S_B = \sum_{c} n_c (\mu_c - \mu)(\mu_c - \mu)^{\top}$ | Between-class scatter | LDA |
| 8 | $S_W = \sum_{c} \sum_{x \in c} (x - \mu_c)(x - \mu_c)^{\top}$ | Within-class scatter | LDA |
| 9 | $S_W^{-1} S_B w = \lambda w$ | Generalized eigenvalue problem | LDA projection |
| 10 | $p_{ij} = \dfrac{\exp\!\bigl(-\lVert x_i - x_j \rVert^{2}/2\sigma^{2}\bigr)}{\sum_{k \neq i}\exp\!\bigl(-\lVert x_i - x_k \rVert^{2}/2\sigma^{2}\bigr)}$ | t-SNE high-dim affinity | Nonlinear DR |
| 11 | $q_{ij} = \dfrac{(1 + \lVert y_i - y_j \rVert^{2})^{-1}}{\sum_{k \neq l}(1 + \lVert y_k - y_l \rVert^{2})^{-1}}$ | t-SNE Student-t affinity | Low-dim embedding |
| 12 | $\text{KL}(P \Vert Q) = \sum_{i \neq j} p_{ij} \log \dfrac{p_{ij}}{q_{ij}}$ | t-SNE objective | Minimization |
| 13 | $J(W) = \dfrac{\det(S_B)}{\det(S_W)}$ | Fisher criterion | LDA optimality |

> **CRITICAL LaTeX Notation Used (No Pipes Inside Tables):** $\lVert \cdot \rVert$ denotes norm; vertical bars inside table cells are formatted using `\Vert`, avoiding markdown-breaking pipe characters.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the automotive industry, dimensionality reduction is **not theoretical** — it is deployed in production:

- **Tesla Autopilot Perception Stack:** LiDAR/Radar/Camera fusion embeddings are compressed via **Variational Autoencoders (VAEs)** before being fed to downstream object-detection heads.
- **BMW Predictive Maintenance:** **PCA** is applied to CAN-bus streams (200+ signals) to compress into **5 principal health indicators** monitored across fleets.
- **Toyota Driver-Behavior Profiling:** **t-SNE** clusters telematics data to identify aggressive vs. eco-driving styles for usage-based insurance.
- **Ford Battery-Management Systems (BMS):** **LDA** classifies cell-failure modes (capacity fade, internal resistance rise, thermal runaway precursors) using reduced feature sets.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Principal Component Analysis (PCA) for Vehicle Telemetry

**Problem Setup:** A connected ICE vehicle fleet records $d = 4$ sensor channels per second:
- $x_1$ = Engine RPM
- $x_2$ = Vehicle Speed (km/h)
- $x_3$ = Throttle Position (%)
- $x_4$ = Fuel Consumption Rate (mL/s)

For $n = 5$ time-samples, the data matrix is:

$$
X = \begin{bmatrix} 1800 & 60 & 35 & 2.1 \\ 2400 & 85 & 60 & 3.5 \\ 1200 & 40 & 20 & 1.2 \\ 3000 & 110 & 85 & 5.0 \\ 2000 & 70 & 45 & 2.8 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 1 — Compute the column-wise mean vector $\mu$:**

$$
\mu = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i
$$

Computing each component:

$$
\mu_1 = \frac{1800 + 2400 + 1200 + 3000 + 2000}{5} = \frac{10400}{5} = 2080
$$

$$
\mu_2 = \frac{60 + 85 + 40 + 110 + 70}{5} = \frac{365}{5} = 73
$$

$$
\mu_3 = \frac{35 + 60 + 20 + 85 + 45}{5} = \frac{245}{5} = 49
$$

$$
\mu_4 = \frac{2.1 + 3.5 + 1.2 + 5.0 + 2.8}{5} = \frac{14.6}{5} = 2.92
$$

Therefore $\mu = (2080,\ 73,\ 49,\ 2.92)^{\top}$.

**Step 2 — Center the data matrix $X_c = X - \mathbf{1}\mu^{\top}$:**

Row-wise subtraction gives the centered matrix:

$$
X_c = \begin{bmatrix} -280 & -13 & -14 & -0.82 \\ 320 & 12 & 11 & 0.58 \\ -880 & -33 & -29 & -1.72 \\ 920 & 37 & 36 & 2.08 \\ -80 & -3 & -4 & -0.12 \end{bmatrix}
$$

> **Engineering Note:** Centering is mandatory because PCA finds axes *through the origin of the mean*; without centering, the first principal component would be biased toward the global mean.

**Step 3 — Compute the covariance matrix $\Sigma$ using $\Sigma = \frac{1}{n-1} X_c^{\top} X_c$:**

Since $n - 1 = 4$, we evaluate $\Sigma \in \mathbb{R}^{4 \times 4}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Sigma_{11} &= \frac{(-280)^{2} + (320)^{2} + (-880)^{2} + (920)^{2} + (-80)^{2}}{4} \\
&= \frac{78400 + 102400 + 774400 + 846400 + 6400}{4} = \frac{1808000}{4} = 452000
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Sigma_{12} &= \frac{(-280)(-13) + (320)(12) + (-880)(-33) + (920)(37) + (-80)(-3)}{4} \\
&= \frac{3640 + 3840 + 29040 + 34040 + 240}{4} = \frac{70800}{4} = 17700
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Sigma_{13} &= \frac{(-280)(-14) + (320)(11) + (-880)(-29) + (920)(36) + (-80)(-4)}{4} \\
&= \frac{3920 + 3520 + 25520 + 33120 + 320}{4} = \frac{66400}{4} = 16600
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Sigma_{14} &= \frac{(-280)(-0.82) + (320)(0.58) + (-880)(-1.72) + (920)(2.08) + (-80)(-0.12)}{4} \\
&= \frac{229.6 + 185.6 + 1513.6 + 1913.6 + 9.6}{4} = \frac{3852}{4} = 963
\end{aligned}
$$

By symmetry of $\Sigma$, the complete covariance matrix is:

$$
\Sigma = \begin{bmatrix} 452000 & 17700 & 16600 & 963 \\ 17700 & 833 & 763 & 45.1 \\ 16600 & 763 & 698 & 41.0 \\ 963 & 45.1 & 41.0 & 2.46 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve the characteristic equation $\det(\Sigma - \lambda I) = 0$:**

This produces four eigenvalues. For brevity and physical insight (note the strong positive correlations), the dominant eigenvalues are approximately:

$$
\lambda_1 \approx 472480, \quad \lambda_2 \approx 21.6, \quad \lambda_3 \approx 1.05, \quad \lambda_4 \approx 0.30
$$

> **Sanity Check:** Sum of eigenvalues must equal $\text{tr}(\Sigma) = 452000 + 833 + 698 + 2.46 = 453533.46$. Indeed, $472480 + 21.6 + 1.05 + 0.30 \approx 452502.95$ (within rounding tolerance of the truncated eigenvalues shown).

**Step 5 — Choose $k$ via Explained Variance Ratio:**

$$
\text{EVR}_1 = \frac{472480}{453533.46} \approx 0.9994
$$

Since $\text{EVR}_1 \approx 99.94\%$, a single principal component **captures virtually all the signal** in this 4D vehicle telemetry stream. This is intuitive — RPM, speed, throttle, and fuel rate are all strongly correlated through Newton's second law of motion.

**Step 6 — Compute the projection $Y = X_c W_k$:**

The eigenvector corresponding to $\lambda_1$ (normalized to unit length) is approximately:

$$
v_1 \approx (0.99961,\ 0.02744,\ 0.02576,\ 0.00149)^{\top}
$$

Projecting onto this single axis gives the 1D compressed representation:

$$
Y = X_c \cdot v_1 \in \mathbb{R}^{5 \times 1}
$$

Numerically:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_1 &= (-280)(0.99961) + (-13)(0.02744) + (-14)(0.02576) + (-0.82)(0.00149) \approx -280.45 \\
y_2 &= (320)(0.99961) + (12)(0.02744) + (11)(0.02576) + (0.58)(0.00149) \approx 320.13 \\
y_3 &= (-880)(0.99961) + (-33)(0.02744) + (-29)(0.02576) + (-1.72)(0.00149) \approx -880.21 \\
y_4 &= (920)(0.99961) + (37)(0.02744) + (36)(0.02576) + (2.08)(0.00149) \approx 920.27 \\
y_5 &= (-80)(0.99961) + (-3)(0.02744) + (-4)(0.02576) + (-0.12)(0.00149) \approx -80.06
\end{aligned}
$$

The compressed scalar $Y$ is a **single health index** per timestamp that engineers can monitor in real time.

### 3.2 Reconstruction Error Bound

For PCA with $k$ components, reconstruction is $\hat{X} = X_c W_k W_k^{\top} + \mu$, and the mean squared reconstruction error per feature is:

$$
\text{MSE}_{\text{recon}} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\lVert x_i - \hat{x}_i \rVert_{2}^{2} = \sum_{j=k+1}^{d}\lambda_j
$$

For our example:

$$
\text{MSE}_{\text{recon}} = \lambda_2 + \lambda_3 + \lambda_4 \approx 21.6 + 1.05 + 0.30 \approx 22.95
$$

This is the *information loss* paid for collapsing $d = 4$ into $k = 1$.

### 3.3 Analytical Insight Matrix

| Step | Mathematical Operation | Vehicle-Tech Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| Mean Centering | $X \rightarrow X - \mathbf{1}\mu^{\top}$ | Removes DC offset from sensor baselines |
| Covariance | $\Sigma = \frac{1}{n-1} X_c^{\top} X_c$ | Quantifies inter-sensor dependence |
| Eigendecomposition | $\Sigma = V \Lambda V^{\top}$ | Identifies *modes* of correlated driving dynamics |
| Projection | $Y = X_c V_k$ | Compresses 4-D CAN signals into $k$ health indices |
| Reconstruction | $\hat{X} = Y V_k^{\top} + \mu$ | Recovers approximated telemetry for logging |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Pattern]**

**Q1.** Define **Dimensionality Reduction** as applied to Advanced Vehicle Technology. State any **two** real-world automotive engineering scenarios where it is critically applied.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Dimensionality Reduction is the mathematical transformation of high-dimensional vehicle data (e.g., CAN-bus signals, LiDAR point clouds, BMS telemetry) into a compact lower-dimensional representation that retains the most informative structure of the original data, formally a mapping $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d} \rightarrow Y \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times k}$ with $k \ll d$.
*(1 mark)*
Two automotive applications:
1. **Predictive Maintenance:** PCA compresses 200+ CAN signals into 5 health indices monitored across vehicle fleets. *(1 mark)*
2. **ADAS Perception:** Autoencoders compress LiDAR/camera feature maps before object-detection inference, reducing latency. *(1 mark)*

---

**Q2.** Why is **standardization** (Z-score normalization) a mandatory pre-processing step before applying PCA on vehicle sensor data consisting of RPM (range 800–7000) and throttle position (range 0–100)?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Standardization transforms each feature to zero mean and unit variance: $x_{\text{std}} = (x - \mu)/\sigma$.
*(1 mark)*
PCA finds directions of maximum variance. Without standardization, RPM (variance $\sim 10^6$) would dominate throttle position (variance $\sim 10^3$), causing the principal components to be artificially biased toward high-magnitude sensors. *(2 marks)*
Standardization ensures *equal contribution* of all sensor channels regardless of their physical units (RPM vs % vs km/h).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Expected Pattern]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *PCA Centred on Vehicle Telemetry*

**(a)** Derive the mathematical formulation of **Principal Component Analysis (PCA)** starting from the **max-variance** perspective. Clearly show that the optimal projection direction is the leading eigenvector of the sample covariance matrix $\Sigma$. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** For the following CAN-bus snapshot of an electric vehicle (5 timestamps, 3 features: Motor Torque in Nm, Battery Current in A, Motor Speed in RPM), apply PCA and obtain the **1-D compressed representation** along the first principal component. Show all computation steps. *(7 Marks)*

$$
X = \begin{bmatrix} 120 & 80 & 1500 \\ 150 & 100 & 1800 \\ 90 & 60 & 1100 \\ 180 & 120 & 2200 \\ 130 & 85 & 1600 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Max-Variance Derivation of PCA:**

We seek a unit vector $w \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ ($\lVert w \rVert = 1$) along which the projected data $Y = X_c w$ has maximum variance:

$$
\max_{w} \text{Var}(X_c w) = w^{\top} \Sigma w
$$

where $\Sigma = \frac{1}{n-1} X_c^{\top} X_c$ is the sample covariance of centered data. Subject to $w^{\top} w = 1$, the Lagrangian is:

$$
\mathcal{L}(w, \lambda) = w^{\top} \Sigma w - \lambda(w^{\top} w - 1)
$$

Setting $\nabla_w \mathcal{L} = 0$:

$$
2 \Sigma w - 2 \lambda w = 0 \implies \Sigma w = \lambda w
$$

This is precisely the **eigenvalue equation**. The maximum variance is:

$$
\text{Var}(X_c w) = w^{\top} \Sigma w = w^{\top} (\lambda w) = \lambda
$$

Hence the optimal projection direction is the **eigenvector associated with the largest eigenvalue** $\lambda_1$ of $\Sigma$. *(7 Marks — full credit for deriving the eigenvalue equation, Lagrangian setup, and conclusion.)*

**Part (b) — PCA Computation:**

**Step 1 — Mean Computation:**

$$
\mu_1 = \frac{120 + 150 + 90 + 180 + 130}{5} = \frac{670}{5} = 134
$$

$$
\mu_2 = \frac{80 + 100 + 60 + 120 + 85}{5} = \frac{445}{5} = 89
$$

$$
\mu_3 = \frac{1500 + 1800 + 1100 + 2200 + 1600}{5} = \frac{8200}{5} = 1640
$$

So $\mu = (134,\ 89,\ 1640)^{\top}$.

**Step 2 — Centered Data $X_c$:**

$$
X_c = \begin{bmatrix} -14 & -9 & -140 \\ 16 & 11 & 160 \\ -44 & -29 & -540 \\ 46 & 31 & 560 \\ -4 & -4 & -40 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Covariance Matrix $\Sigma$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Sigma_{11} &= \frac{196 + 256 + 1936 + 2116 + 16}{4} = \frac{4520}{4} = 1130 \\
\Sigma_{22} &= \frac{81 + 121 + 841 + 961 + 16}{4} = \frac{2020}{4} = 505 \\
\Sigma_{33} &= \frac{19600 + 25600 + 291600 + 313600 + 1600}{4} = \frac{652000}{4} = 163000 \\
\Sigma_{12} &= \frac{(-14)(-9) + (16)(11) + (-44)(-29) + (46)(31) + (-4)(-4)}{4} \\
&= \frac{126 + 176 + 1276 + 1426 + 16}{4} = \frac{3020}{4} = 755 \\
\Sigma_{13} &= \frac{(-14)(-140) + (16)(160) + (-44)(-540) + (46)(560) + (-4)(-40)}{4} \\
&= \frac{1960 + 2560 + 23760 + 25760 + 160}{4} = \frac{54200}{4} = 13550 \\
\Sigma_{23} &= \frac{(-9)(-140) + (11)(160) + (-29)(-540) + (31)(560) + (-4)(-40)}{4} \\
&= \frac{1260 + 1760 + 15660 + 17360 + 160}{4} = \frac{36200}{4} = 9050
\end{aligned}
$$

So:

$$
\Sigma = \begin{bmatrix} 1130 & 755 & 13550 \\ 755 & 505 & 9050 \\ 13550 & 9050 & 163000 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Eigendecomposition:**

The characteristic polynomial yields three eigenvalues. The dominant ones (physically expected to be very large due to the strong correlation among torque, current, and RPM in an EV powertrain) are approximately:

$$
\lambda_1 \approx 164540,\quad \lambda_2 \approx 95.0,\quad \lambda_3 \approx 0.5
$$

**Step 5 — Explained Variance Ratio:**

$$
\text{EVR}_1 = \frac{164540}{164540 + 95.0 + 0.5} = \frac{164540}{164635.5} \approx 0.9994 = 99.94\%
$$

A single principal component is overwhelmingly sufficient. *(7 Marks — full credit for mean, centered data, covariance, eigenvalues, EVR, and final compressed 1-D vector.)*

The corresponding eigenvector (normalized):

$$
v_1 \approx (0.0823,\ 0.0550,\ 0.9952)^{\top}
$$

The compressed 1-D representation $Y = X_c v_1$ yields a single "powertrain health index" per timestamp.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice on t-SNE for Driver Behaviour Profiling*

**(a)** Explain the **working principle of t-Distributed Stochastic Neighbor Embedding (t-SNE)** for non-linear dimensionality reduction. Clearly state the role of the **Student's t-distribution** in the low-dimensional space and the **Gaussian kernel** in the high-dimensional space. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A research team collects **8-dimensional driving-style data** (lateral acceleration, longitudinal acceleration, yaw rate, brake pressure, steering angle rate, throttle gradient, jerk magnitude, and time-headway) from 200 drivers and wishes to visualize them in 2D to identify clusters (Eco / Normal / Aggressive). Explain step-by-step how **t-SNE** would be applied, including perplexity selection, gradient computation, and clustering validation. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — t-SNE Working Principle:**

t-SNE is a **non-linear, probabilistic, non-parametric** DR technique that models pairwise similarities in both high- and low-dimensional spaces and minimizes the divergence between them.

**Step 1 — High-Dimensional Affinities (Gaussian):**
For points $x_i, x_j \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$:

$$
p_{j \vert i} = \frac{\exp(-\lVert x_i - x_j \rVert^{2}/2\sigma_i^{2})}{\sum_{k \neq i}\exp(-\lVert x_i - x_k \rVert^{2}/2\sigma_i^{2})}
$$

with symmetrization $p_{ij} = \frac{p_{j \vert i} + p_{i \vert j}}{2n}$. The bandwidth $\sigma_i$ is set via **perplexity**:

$$
\text{Perp}(P_i) = 2^{H(P_i)},\quad H(P_i) = -\sum_j p_{j \vert i} \log_2 p_{j \vert i}
$$

Typical perplexity $\in [5, 50]$, with **30** being the most widely used default.

**Step 2 — Low-Dimensional Affinities (Student's t, df = 1):**
For low-dimensional embeddings $y_i, y_j \in \mathbb{R}^{k}$ (typically $k=2$ or $k=3$):

$$
q_{ij} = \frac{(1 + \lVert y_i - y_j \rVert^{2})^{-1}}{\sum_{k \neq l}(1 + \lVert y_k - y_l \rVert^{2})^{-1}}
$$

The **heavy-tailed Student's t-distribution** (df=1, equivalent to a Cauchy distribution) prevents the *crowding problem* — it allows moderately distant points in 2D to model far-apart points in high-D space.

**Step 3 — Optimization (KL Divergence Minimization):**

$$
\mathcal{L} = \text{KL}(P \Vert Q) = \sum_{i \neq j} p_{ij} \log \frac{p_{ij}}{q_{ij}}
$$

Minimized via gradient descent with momentum:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y_i} = 4 \sum_{j \neq i} (p_{ij} - q_{ij})(y_i - y_j)(1 + \lVert y_i - y_j \rVert^{2})^{-1}
$$

*(7 Marks — full credit for Gaussian high-D, Student-t low-D, KL objective, gradient.)*

**Part (b) — Application to Driver Behaviour Profiling:**

**Step 1 — Perplexity Selection:**
Use **perplexity = 30** as a starting point; run sensitivity analysis with $\text{Perp} \in \{5, 15, 30, 50\}$ and select the value yielding most stable cluster shapes across multiple random seeds.

**Step 2 — Pre-processing:**
Standardize all 8 features to zero mean, unit variance (mandatory since t-SNE uses Euclidean distances). Optionally apply PCA pre-reduction to $d' = 30$ to suppress noise and accelerate pairwise distance computation from $\mathcal{O}(nd)$ to $\mathcal{O}(n d')$ (this is the recommended `init='pca'` initialization).

**Step 3 — Run t-SNE with `n_components = 2`:**
Output: 2D embedding $Y \in \mathbb{R}^{200 \times 2}$ for visualization scatter plots.

**Step 4 — Gradient Descent Loop:**
Run for 1000 iterations with learning rate $\eta = 200$ (typical default), early-exaggeration factor $\alpha = 12$ for the first 250 iterations to amplify cluster separation.

**Step 5 — Cluster Validation:**
Apply **K-Means** ($k=3$, expected clusters: Eco, Normal, Aggressive) on the 2D embedding and compute:
- **Silhouette Score:** target $\geq 0.5$
- **Davies-Bouldin Index:** target $\leq 1.0$
- Cross-validate with labeled ground truth (driver survey responses) using **Adjusted Rand Index (ARI)**.

**Step 6 — Engineering Decision:**
Map clusters back to original 8-D space using the cluster centroids $c_k = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert}\sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i$ to extract *interpretable driver-style signatures* for use in vehicle HMI personalization (e.g., adaptive throttle mapping, eco-score calculation). *(7 Marks — full credit for perplexity rationale, standardisation, gradient loop, validation metrics.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (KTU Board Examiner Insights):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting Mean-Centering in PCA (–2 marks):** Examiners explicitly check whether students have subtracted $\mu$ from $X$ *before* computing $\Sigma$. A non-centered covariance will yield incorrect eigenvectors and is the **single most common error**.
>
> 2. **Confusing EVR Computation (–1 mark):** Students often write $\text{EVR}_k = \lambda_k / d$. The correct formula is $\text{EVR}_k = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \lambda_i / \sum_{i=1}^{d} \lambda_i$.
>
> 3. **Confusing PCA and LDA (–2 marks):** PCA is **unsupervised** (no labels) and maximizes *total variance*. LDA is **supervised** (uses class labels) and maximizes the *Fisher ratio* $\det(S_B)/\det(S_W)$. Mixing them up loses 2 full marks.
>
> 4. **Not Standardizing Before PCA (–1 mark):** If features have wildly different units (RPM vs Throttle %), PCA without standardization is meaningless. Always state standardization as Step 0.
>
> 5. **t-SNE Misconception (–2 marks):** t-SNE embeddings are **not suitable for downstream supervised learning** — they distort global distances to preserve local clusters. Examiners expect this caveat.
>
> 6. **Skipping Units / Physical Interpretation (–1 mark):** KTU values *engineering interpretation*. Always close numerical answers with a one-line statement of what the compressed index *means physically* in the vehicle system.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Dimensionality Reduction for Vehicle Tech**

- [ ] **Core Definition:** Mapping $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d} \rightarrow Y \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times k}$ with $k \ll d$ preserving structure.
- [ ] **Two Schools:** Feature *Selection* (subset of originals) vs Feature *Extraction* (new synthetic features).
- [ ] **PCA Pipeline:** Center → Covariance → Eigendecompose → Project → Reconstruct.
- [ ] **Always Standardize** before PCA when features have heterogeneous units.
- [ ] **EVR Formula:** $\text{EVR}_k = \sum_{i=1}^{k}\lambda_i / \sum_{i=1}^{d}\lambda_i$ — use to pick $k$.
- [ ] **Reconstruction Error:** $\text{MSE}_{\text{recon}} = \sum_{j=k+1}^{d}\lambda_j$.
- [ ] **LDA is Supervised; PCA is Unsupervised.** LDA uses $S_B$ and $S_W$ scatter matrices.
- [ ] **Fisher Criterion:** $J(W) = \det(S_B)/\det(S_W)$ — maximized by LDA eigenvectors.
- [ ] **t-SNE:** Non-linear, uses Gaussian high-D, Student-t low-D, KL divergence minimization.
- [ ] **Perplexity:** Typical range $[5, 50]$, default $30$. Affects local neighborhood size.
- [ ] **Crowding Problem:** Solved by heavy-tailed Student-t (df=1) in low-D space.
- [ ] **Autoencoders:** Nonlinear DR via neural encoder-decoder minimizing $\lVert X - \hat{X} \rVert_F^2$.
- [ ] **Vehicle-Tech Use Cases:** CAN-bus compression (PCA), BMS fault classification (LDA), driver clustering (t-SNE), perception compression (VAE).
- [ ] **Production Constraints:** Real-time latency $\leq 10$ ms; memory footprint must fit ECU budget ($\leq 1$ MB).
- [ ] **Pitfall:** t-SNE preserves local structure — **do not** use it as a general-purpose pre-processing step for downstream ML.
