# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]
**Define State of Charge (SOC) and State of Health (SOH) of an EV battery. Why is accurate SOC estimation challenging for Li-ion cells?**

**Model Answer:**

**State of Charge (SOC)** is the ratio of the **remaining capacity** in a battery to its **rated (nominal) capacity**, expressed as a percentage:

$$
SOC(t) = \frac{Q_{remaining}(t)}{Q_{rated}} \times 100\%
$$

It is the **electrical equivalent of a fuel gauge** — a value of 100% means fully charged, and 0% means fully discharged (and should never actually be reached in Li-ion to avoid damage).

**State of Health (SOH)** is the ratio of the **current maximum capacity** of an aged battery to its **original rated capacity** when new:

$$
SOH(t) = \frac{Q_{max, current}(t)}{Q_{max, new}} \times 100\%
$$

A new battery has SOH = 100%. An EV battery is considered **end-of-life** when SOH drops to **80%** (typical industry threshold), at which point it is repurposed for stationary storage.

**Why SOC is Challenging to Estimate:**

1. **Flat OCV-SOC curve** — between 30–80% SOC, the open-circuit voltage changes by only ~50 mV per 10% SOC, making voltage-based estimation imprecise.
2. **Temperature dependence** — the OCV-SOC relationship shifts by up to 20 mV between -10 °C and +40 °C.
3. **Aging** — as the cell degrades, internal resistance grows, distorting the OCV curve further.
4. **Hysteresis** — the OCV differs slightly between charge and discharge at the same SOC, by ~5–15 mV.
5. **Sensor drift** — coulomb counting accumulates error over time due to current-sensor offset.

AI techniques (LSTM, GRU, Fuzzy Logic) address these by **fusing multiple data sources** and **learning nonlinear patterns** that classical models cannot capture.

**[3 Marks Breakdown: 1 mark for SOC definition, 1 mark for SOH definition, 1 mark for any 2 challenges.]**

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**What is Regenerative Braking, and how does AI optimize the energy recovered during braking?**

**Model Answer:**

**Regenerative Braking** is a mechanism in EVs and HEVs where the **electric traction motor acts as a generator** during deceleration, converting the vehicle's **kinetic energy** into **electrical energy** that is fed back into the battery. The mechanical power recovered is:

$$
P_{regen} = \tau_{motor} \cdot \omega_{motor} \cdot \eta_{motor}
$$

where $\tau_{motor}$ is the motor torque, $\omega_{motor}$ is the angular speed, and $\eta_{motor}$ is the motor-generator efficiency (typically 85–95%).

**AI Optimization of Regenerative Braking:**

A **Neural Network or Reinforcement Learning agent** decides, in real time, the **split ratio** between:
- **Regenerative braking force** (recovers energy but limited by battery SOC, temperature, and max charge rate).
- **Mechanical (friction) braking force** (necessary at very low speeds, when SOC is near 100%, or in emergencies).

**Inputs to the AI model:**
- Vehicle speed, brake pedal position, accelerator release rate
- Battery SOC, temperature, and state of health
- Road gradient (from GPS/elevation data)
- Traffic flow (from V2X or camera)

**Outputs:**
- Optimal regenerative torque request
- Optimal brake-blend ratio

For example, an LSTM network trained on driving data can predict that the driver is **approaching a stop** and **pre-charge the battery gradually** instead of waiting for hard braking, thus reducing peak currents and improving energy recovery by 8–15% over rule-based systems.

**[3 Marks Breakdown: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for the energy equation, 1 mark for AI optimization approach.]**

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Internal Choice Pattern:** Each Part B question offers **Module Internal Choice** — i.e., the student answers **ONE** of two alternatives, both of which are equally weighted at 14 marks with two sub-parts (a) of 7 marks and (b) of 7 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Sample Paper Model]
**(a)** [7 Marks] With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of an AI-enabled Battery Management System** for an electric vehicle. Discuss the role of the **State Estimator**, the **Energy Management Controller**, and the **Thermal Management Module**.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Derive the **Coulomb Counting equation** for SOC estimation. Discuss its **two main limitations** and explain how a **LSTM neural network** overcomes them.

---

**Model Solution to Part (a):**

**Architecture of AI-Enabled BMS:**

An AI-enabled BMS is organized as a **hierarchical, multi-layer system** with the following blocks:

**1. Physical Layer (Battery Pack):**
A Li-ion battery pack consists of cells connected in **series (for voltage)** and **parallel (for capacity)**. A typical EV pack might be 96s2p configuration producing ~400 V and 100 Ah.

**2. Sensor Layer:**
Each module contains sensors for:
- **Cell voltage** (16-bit ADC, ±5 mV accuracy)
- **Current** (Hall-effect sensor, ±0.5% accuracy)
- **Temperature** (NTC thermistors, ±1 °C)
- **Cell balancing circuits** (passive or active)

**3. Data Acquisition and Preprocessing Layer:**
- Analog signals are digitized, filtered (low-pass to remove noise), and normalized.
- Features such as **moving averages**, **deltas (dV/dt, dI/dt)**, and **cumulative throughput** are computed.

**4. AI State Estimator Layer:**
This is the **brain** of the system. It typically contains:
- **LSTM/GRU networks** for dynamic SOC
- **Convolutional Neural Networks** for SOH from charging curves
- **Gaussian Process Regression** for SOH with confidence bounds
- **Particle Filter** as a backup Bayesian estimator

**5. Energy Management Controller:**
Implements the optimal **power split** between motor, battery, and (in HEVs) engine. Uses **reinforcement learning (DQN or DDPG)** to learn the long-term cost-optimal policy.

**6. Thermal Management Module:**
Uses **fuzzy logic** or **PID + AI correction** to control coolant pump speed, fan speed, and (in some vehicles) refrigeration cycles.

**7. Communication Layer:**
- **CAN bus** between BMS and VCU at 500 kbps.
- **Ethernet** for high-bandwidth AI sensor data.
- **Cellular (4G/5G)** for cloud telemetry and OTA updates.

**Block Diagram (textual representation):**

$$
[\text{Battery Pack}] \to [\text{Sensor + ADC}] \to [\text{Filter/Feature Extract}] \to [\text{AI State Estimator: SOC, SOH, SOE}] \to [\text{Energy Mgmt AI}] \to [\text{Actuator: Charger, Motor Inverter, Thermal Loop}]
$$

**Why this architecture is "AI-enabled":** The classical BMS would have a hard-coded state estimator (coulomb counting + lookup tables). The AI-enabled version **replaces** this with a trainable neural network that improves continuously via **OTA (Over-The-Air) updates** as more data is collected from the fleet.

**[7 Marks Breakdown: 2 marks for block diagram, 2 marks for sensor/preprocessing, 2 marks for AI state estimator, 1 mark for energy and thermal management.]**

---

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

**Derivation of Coulomb Counting Equation:**

Refer to the derivation in **Section 3.1** above for the full step-by-step.

Final equation:

$$
SOC(t) = SOC_0 - \frac{1}{Q_{rated}} \int_{0}^{t} \eta_c \cdot I(\tau) \, d\tau
$$

**Limitation 1 — Sensor Offset Drift:**

If the current sensor has an offset $\Delta I$, the integrated error over time $T$ is:

$$
\Delta Q = \Delta I \cdot T
$$

For $\Delta I = 10$ mA and $T = 1$ hour:
- $\Delta Q = 0.01 \times 3600 = 36$ mAh
- On a 50 Ah battery, this is a 0.072% error per hour
- Over 1000 hours of operation, error = 72% — **catastrophic**

**Limitation 2 — Unknown Initial SOC:**

If the vehicle has been parked for weeks, self-discharge has occurred. The true $SOC_0$ is unknown, and any integration error compounds from the start.

**How LSTM Overcomes These Limitations:**

An **LSTM** network takes a sliding window of inputs (typically last 60–120 seconds of V, I, T data) and predicts the current SOC. The LSTM:

1. **Learns to recognize voltage signatures** at known SOC points (e.g., the "knee" at 20% and 80%), allowing it to **reset the integrator** periodically.
2. **Is trained on labeled data** (V, I, T, T_actual) so it learns the **non-linear mapping** without needing the true initial SOC — it auto-calibrates from patterns.
3. **Generalizes across temperatures and aging** if the training set is diverse, since the same V-I-T signatures appear regardless of conditions.
4. **Reaches < 2% SOC error** in published benchmarks, versus 5–10% for pure coulomb counting.

**Mathematical LSTM Update (forward pass):**

$$
f_t = \sigma(W_f \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f)
$$

$$
i_t = \sigma(W_i \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_i)
$$

$$
\tilde{C}_t = \tanh(W_C \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_C)
$$

$$
C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t
$$

$$
h_t = \sigma(W_o \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_o) \odot \tanh(C_t)
$$

$$
\hat{SOC}_t = W_{out} h_t + b_{out}
$$

**Training Objective:**

$$
\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{t=1}^{N} (SOC_t^{true} - \hat{SOC}_t)^2 \to \text{minimized via Adam optimizer}
$$

**[7 Marks Breakdown: 2 marks for derivation, 1 mark for limitation 1, 1 mark for limitation 2, 3 marks for LSTM explanation and equations.]**

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Sample Paper Model — Alternative Choice]
**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **Multi-Stage Constant Current (MCC) charging protocol**. How does an **AI algorithm** optimize the stage currents to **minimize battery degradation** while maintaining fast charging time?

**(b)** [7 Marks] With the **Q-Learning update equation**, explain how a **Reinforcement Learning agent** learns an optimal **overnight EV charging schedule** in a Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G) scenario.

---

**Model Solution to Part (a):**

**Multi-Stage Constant Current (MCC) Charging Protocol:**

The **MCC protocol** charges a Li-ion battery using a **decreasing staircase of constant current levels**. A typical profile for a 50 Ah cell might be:

| Stage | C-Rate | SOC Range | Rationale |
|---|---|---|---|
| Stage 1 | 1.5C | 0% → 50% | Cell can accept high current at low SOC |
| Stage 2 | 1.0C | 50% → 70% | Moderate current to limit heating |
| Stage 3 | 0.5C | 70% → 85% | Reduced current to avoid Li-plating |
| Stage 4 | 0.2C | 85% → 100% | Trickle charge near full |

**Why decreasing current?** Because as SOC increases, the cell's **overpotential decreases**, and the risk of **lithium plating** at the anode increases dramatically. The **anode potential** must stay above **0 V vs. Li/Li⁺** to avoid plating, and high currents at high SOC can drive it negative.

**AI Optimization of MCC Stage Currents:**

An AI optimizer (e.g., a **Genetic Algorithm** or **Deep RL agent**) searches for the optimal current setpoints $\{I_1, I_2, I_3, I_4\}$ and **switch SOC points** $\{SOC_1^*, SOC_2^*, SOC_3^*\}$ to minimize a composite cost:

$$
\text{Cost} = w_1 \cdot t_{charge} + w_2 \cdot \Delta SOH + w_3 \cdot T_{max} + w_4 \cdot E_{loss}
$$

where:
- $t_{charge}$ = total charging time
- $\Delta SOH$ = capacity fade per cycle
- $T_{max}$ = peak battery temperature
- $E_{loss}$ = energy lost as heat
- $w_i$ = user-defined weights

**Inputs to the AI:**
- Cell chemistry (NMC, LFP, NCA)
- Initial SOC and temperature
- Target final SOC
- Aging state (current SOH)

**Outputs:**
- Optimal $I_1, I_2, I_3, I_4$ and switch SOCs

**Result:** AI-optimized profiles can deliver the **same SOC in 20% less time** with **30% less capacity fade** compared to manufacturer-default CC-CV profiles.

**Connection to Smart Charging:** Modern EVs (e.g., Porsche Taycan, Hyundai Ioniq 5) use **AI-thermal-aware charging** that pre-conditions the battery to ~25 °C before fast charging, then uses a **dynamically adjusted MCC profile** learned from millions of miles of fleet data.

**[7 Marks Breakdown: 2 marks for MCC stages table, 2 marks for Li-plating physics, 2 marks for AI cost function, 1 mark for real-world example.]**

---

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

**Q-Learning for Overnight EV Charging in V2G:**

**Scenario:** The EV is plugged in at 10 PM with SOC = 30%. Departure is at 7 AM (target SOC = 90%). Electricity prices vary hourly: cheap at night, expensive at peak (6 PM–9 PM the next day). The owner may also **sell** energy back to the grid (V2G) during peak hours, but this **degrades the battery**.

**Step 1: Define the State Space $S$:**

The state at time $t$ is a tuple:

$$
s_t = (SOC_t, t_{hour}, price_t, T_{battery}, t_{remaining})
$$

A continuous state space is discretized for tabular Q-learning, or kept continuous for **Deep Q-Network (DQN)**.

**Step 2: Define the Action Space $A$:**

$$
a_t \in \{-2C, -1C, -0.5C, 0, 0.5C, 1C, 1.5C\}
$$

where **negative C-rates represent V2G discharge** and **positive C-rates represent grid-to-vehicle charging**.

**Step 3: Define the Reward Function $R$:**

$$
r_{t+1}(s_t, a_t) = -\alpha \cdot price_t \cdot P_t - \beta \cdot C_{rate}^2 + \gamma \cdot \mathbb{1}_{\{SOC \geq 0.9 \, \text{at} \, t = 7\text{AM}\}}
$$

- First term: cost of purchased energy (penalty for charging during expensive hours).
- Second term: degradation penalty proportional to the **square** of C-rate.
- Third term: large bonus for meeting departure SOC target.

**Step 4: Q-Value Update Rule (Bellman Equation):**

$$
Q(s_t, a_t) \leftarrow Q(s_t, a_t) + \alpha_{lr} \left[ r_{t+1} + \gamma_{df} \max_{a'} Q(s_{t+1}, a') - Q(s_t, a_t) \right]
$$

where:
- $\alpha_{lr}$ = learning rate (typically 0.1–0.5)
- $\gamma_{df}$ = discount factor (typically 0.9–0.99)

**Step 5: The Agent Learns the Policy:**

After thousands of simulated nights, the Q-table converges. The optimal policy is:

$$
\pi^*(s) = \arg\max_{a} Q(s, a)
$$

**Resulting Behavior (Example):**
- At **10 PM (low price)**: Charge at 1.0C until SOC = 70%.
- At **11 PM–5 AM (cheapest prices)**: Charge at 0.5C to top up to 80%, then idle.
- At **5 AM**: Charge gently to 90% just before departure.
- The agent **does NOT** discharge to grid at 6 PM the next day because the degradation penalty outweighs the price arbitrage profit, given the battery's current SOH.

**Convergence Guarantee:** Q-Learning converges to the **optimal Q-function** as long as every state-action pair is visited infinitely often (a condition met by $\epsilon$-greedy exploration with $\epsilon$ decaying from 0.1 to 0.01).

**Practical Extension:** In production, **Deep Q-Networks (DQN)** replace the Q-table with a neural network, and **LSTM-DQN** adds memory to handle partial observability (e.g., when the EV cannot see future prices perfectly).

**[7 Marks Breakdown: 1 mark for state, 1 mark for action, 2 marks for reward function, 2 marks for Q-update and policy, 1 mark for practical extension.]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not confuse SOC with SOH** — SOC is an *instantaneous* energy level (like a fuel gauge), while SOH is a *long-term* health metric (like engine wear). Many students lose 1–2 marks by mixing them up.
> 2. **Always state units** when writing equations. Writing "$SOC = \frac{Q}{Q_{rated}}$" without showing it's a percentage costs 0.5 marks.
> 3. **In LSTM explanations**, do NOT just write "an LSTM is used." You must mention the **gates (forget, input, output)**, the **cell state**, and the **hidden state**. Examiners specifically test gate equations.
> 4. **For Q-Learning**, the equation must be written in full — the **Bellman form with the max operator inside**. Partial credit is given if you only mention "Q-table updates" without the equation.
> 5. **Negative C-rates for V2G** — clearly label that **discharge is negative current**; many students draw arrows in the wrong direction on V2G diagrams.
> 6. **Coulomb Counting limit** — always quote the **drift equation** $\Delta Q = \Delta I \cdot T$ to score full marks on the "limitations" sub-question.
> 7. **Do not skip the Li-plating physics** when discussing MCC charging — the **anode potential staying above 0 V vs. Li/Li⁺** is the key concept examiners want to see.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **🚀 Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 — AI Energy Management**

- [ ] **SOC** = remaining/rated capacity (0–100%); **SOH** = current max/original max; **SOE** = energy-based version of SOC; **RUL** = cycles/years until SOH = 80%.
- [ ] **Coulomb counting** is the baseline SOC method but suffers from **sensor drift** and **unknown initial SOC**.
- [ ] **Thevenin equivalent circuit** has one **OCV source**, one **ohmic resistor $R_0$**, and one **RC branch ($R_{diff}, C_{diff}$)**.
- [ ] **LSTM** is the dominant AI for dynamic SOC; it has **forget gate, input gate, cell state, output gate**.
- [ ] **LSTM forward pass equations** — memorize $f_t, i_t, \tilde{C}_t, C_t, h_t$ — they are tested directly.
- [ ] **Reinforcement Learning (Q-Learning)** uses the **Bellman update**: $Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha [r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s',a') - Q(s,a)]$.
- [ ] **Reward function** in V2G should penalize **price × power** and **C-rate²** (degradation), and reward **target SOC achievement**.
- [ ] **Multi-Stage Constant Current (MCC)** charging uses **decreasing C-rates** at increasing SOC to avoid **Li-plating** at the anode.
- [ ] **C-rate** is current divided by rated capacity (in 1/hr); **1C** for a 50 Ah battery = 50 A.
- [ ] **Regenerative braking** recovers kinetic energy; AI optimizes the **brake blend ratio** between regen and friction brakes.
- [ ] **V2G** allows bidirectional energy flow; AI schedules charge/discharge based on **time-of-use pricing** and **battery degradation cost**.
- [ ] **Cycle life** $L = L_0 \cdot (DOD/DOD_{ref})^{-k_1} \cdot e^{k_2 (T - T_{ref})}$ — higher DOD and higher temperature accelerate aging.
- [ ] **Arrhenius factor** $AF = e^{(E_a/R)(1/T_{ref} - 1/T)}$ — higher temperature = more aging.
- [ ] **AI techniques comparison**: LSTM for time-series, CNN for feature extraction, Fuzzy for interpretability, RL for sequential decisions, GPR for uncertainty, GA for offline optimization.
- [ ] **Block diagram layers** of AI-BMS: Sensor → Preprocessing → State Estimator → Energy Mgmt → Actuator → Cloud.
- [ ] **Tesla, BYD, CATL** use AI-driven BMS; **Nissan Leaf** historically struggled without it.
- [ ] **Thermal management** is critical — every 10 °C rise in temperature roughly **doubles** the aging rate.
- [ ] **Fast charging limit** is set by **anode potential vs. Li/Li⁺** staying above 0 V — not by battery size alone.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: AI in EV Powertrain Optimization: Motor Control - AI for Enhancing Electric Motor Efficiency and Performance
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT865 - ADVANCED VEHICLE TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** AI in EV Powertrain Optimization: Motor Control - AI for Enhancing Electric Motor Efficiency and Performance

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**AI-Enhanced Electric Motor Control** refers to the integration of artificial intelligence paradigms—such as **Artificial Neural Networks (ANN)**, **Fuzzy Logic Inference Systems (FIS)**, **Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL)**, and **Genetic Algorithms (GA)**—into the closed-loop control architecture of Electric Vehicle (EV) traction motors to dynamically optimize electromagnetic torque production, minimize copper and iron losses, suppress torque ripple, predict rotor position, and adaptively manage thermal envelopes under stochastic real-world driving cycles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> AI is **not** replacing the physical motor—it is augmenting the *control brain* (the inverter + ECU) that drives the Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor (PMSM), Induction Motor (IM), or Switched Reluctance Motor (SRM). The learning happens offline (training) and online (adaptation), producing a *non-linear, self-tuning* controller.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / The "Driving Instructor" Intuition

Imagine you are learning to drive a manual-gear car (the **motor**) with the help of a driving instructor (the **AI controller**).

| Component | Real-World Analogy | Engineering Equivalent |
|-----------|--------------------|-----------------------|
| Student driver | The car's brain trying to control the clutch | Classical PI controller (fixed-gain, rule-based) |
| Driving instructor whispering advice | Real-time correction of throttle & gear | AI-driven adaptive controller |
| Road conditions (rain, uphill, traffic) | Variable disturbances | Load torque variations, battery SoC fluctuations |
| Student's intuition after practice | Learned optimal responses | Trained Neural Network weights & biases |

A **classical controller** is like the student driving with a *fixed checklist* (rules). It works fine in predictable traffic, but when an unexpected hill (non-linearity, saturation, temperature drift) appears, the controller needs *re-tuning*. An **AI controller**, on the other hand, *learns from thousands of prior driving sessions* and adjusts its throttle and gear selections on the fly—achieving higher **efficiency (η)**, smoother **torque (T)**, and longer **battery range (km)**.

---

## 1.3 Why AI in Motor Control? The Three Engineering Pain Points

> [!NOTE]
> **Fundamental Motors Are Non-Linear by Nature.**

1. **Magnetic Saturation** → Inductance $L_d, L_q$ is *not* constant; it varies with current $i_d, i_q$.
2. **Temperature Coupling** → Winding resistance $R_s$ rises with temperature $T_{coil}$ (≈ +0.4 %/°C for copper).
3. **Cross-Coupling (d-q axis)** → $d$-axis current affects $q$-axis flux and vice-versa.

These non-linearities make **fixed-gain PI controllers** insufficient at high speeds, high torques, and field-weakening regions—*exactly* where AI excels because it can approximate *any arbitrary non-linear function* (Universal Approximation Theorem).

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (KTU High-Yield Constants)

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Value/Unit |
|--------|----------|---------------------|
| $\eta_{motor}$ | Peak motor efficiency | **96–98 %** (PMSM), 92–95 % (IM) |
| $T_{ripple}$ | Torque ripple (acceptable) | **< 5 %** for premium EVs |
| $\rho_{cu}$ | Copper resistivity at 20 °C | **1.68 × 10⁻⁸ Ω·m** |
| $\alpha_{cu}$ | Copper temperature coefficient | **3.93 × 10⁻³ /°C** |
| $f_{sw}$ | Inverter switching frequency | **8–20 kHz** (typical) |
| $\omega_{max}$ | Max mechanical speed | **15 000–20 000 rpm** (Tesla Model 3) |
| $\omega_{base}$ | Base speed (constant torque region) | **4 000–6 000 rpm** |

---

## 1.5 Section 1 Recap

> [!TIP]
> **In one sentence:** AI in EV motor control = *non-linear adaptive learning* that overcomes the rigidity of classical PI controllers to deliver superior efficiency, lower torque ripple, robust sensorless operation, and predictive thermal safety.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Classical vs AI Control Architecture

### 2.1.1 Block Diagram of a PMSM Drive

The EV powertrain consists of:

$$\text{Battery} \rightarrow \text{Inverter} \rightarrow \text{Motor} \rightarrow \text{Wheels}$$

The *control loop* closes through current/voltage sensors feeding back to an **ECU** running either:

- **Field-Oriented Control (FOC)** — classical approach
- **Direct Torque Control (DTC)** — classical approach
- **AI-Augmented FOC/DTC** — modern approach

### 2.1.2 Operational Logic Steps (Classical FOC → AI-Augmented FOC)

1. **Measure** three-phase stator currents $i_a, i_b, i_c$ via Hall-effect or shunt resistors.
2. **Transform** to rotating $d$-$q$ reference frame via Park & Clarke transforms → $i_d, i_q$.
3. **Compare** $i_d, i_q$ with reference values $i_d^*, i_q^*$ (error = $e_d, e_q$).
4. **Classical PI Control:** Apply $v_d = K_p e_d + K_i \int e_d\,dt$ (fixed gains).
5. **AI Control:** Apply $v_d = f_{NN}(i_d, i_q, \omega, T_{coil}, \theta)$ where $f_{NN}$ is a trained neural network.
6. **Inverse Park transform** → $v_\alpha, v_\beta$ → **SVPWM** → gate signals → inverter drives motor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 'Why':** AI steps in at stage (5), where the inverse mapping from *desired torque-speed* to *optimal voltage* is inherently non-linear and load-dependent. The AI learns this mapping from data, freeing the engineer from manually tuning PI gains for every operating point.

---

## 2.2 Mathematical Model of PMSM in d-q Frame (KTU Foundation)

The electromagnetic torque of a PMSM (surface-mounted) is governed by:

$$T_e = \frac{3}{2} p \left[ \psi_m i_q + (L_d - L_q) i_d i_q \right]$$

where:

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|--------|---------|--------------|
| $T_e$ | Electromagnetic torque | N·m |
| $p$ | Number of pole pairs | dimensionless |
| $\psi_m$ | Permanent magnet flux linkage | Wb |
| $i_d, i_q$ | $d$-axis and $q$-axis currents | A |
| $L_d, L_q$ | $d$-axis and $q$-axis inductances | H |

For surface-mounted PMSM, $L_d = L_q$, simplifying to:

$$T_e = \frac{3}{2} p \, \psi_m \, i_q$$

The **stator voltage equations** in the rotating frame are:

$$v_d = R_s i_d + L_d \frac{di_d}{dt} - \omega_e L_q i_q$$

$$v_q = R_s i_q + L_q \frac{di_q}{dt} + \omega_e (L_d i_d + \psi_m)$$

---

## 2.3 Loss Model — The Optimization Target

Total motor losses $P_{loss}$ consist of:

$$P_{loss} = P_{cu} + P_{iron} + P_{stray} + P_{mech}$$

**Copper loss (dominant):**

$$P_{cu} = \frac{3}{2} R_s \left( i_d^2 + i_q^2 \right)$$

**Iron (core) loss — Steinmetz equation:**

$$P_{iron} = k_h \omega_e B^{\alpha} + k_e \omega_e^2 B^{2}$$

where $k_h$ = hysteresis coefficient, $k_e$ = eddy current coefficient.

> [!NOTE]
> **AI Optimization Goal:** Find the trajectory $(i_d^*(t), i_q^*(t))$ that **minimizes** $P_{loss}$ subject to the constraint $T_e^{ref}(t) = T_e^{demand}(t)$, across the entire WLTP/UDDS drive cycle.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $T_e = \tfrac{3}{2} p \psi_m i_q$ | PMSM torque (SPM) | N·m |
| 2 | $v_d = R_s i_d + L_d \tfrac{di_d}{dt} - \omega_e L_q i_q$ | d-axis voltage | V |
| 3 | $v_q = R_s i_q + L_q \tfrac{di_q}{dt} + \omega_e (L_d i_d + \psi_m)$ | q-axis voltage | V |
| 4 | $P_{cu} = \tfrac{3}{2} R_s (i_d^2 + i_q^2)$ | Copper loss | W |
| 5 | $R_s(T) = R_{s,20}\left[1 + \alpha_{cu}(T - 20)\right]$ | Resistance temperature dependence | Ω |
| 6 | $\omega_e = p \cdot \omega_m$ | Electrical to mechanical speed | rad/s |
| 7 | $\eta = \dfrac{T_e \omega_m}{T_e \omega_m + P_{loss}}$ | Motor efficiency | dimensionless |
| 8 | $T_{ripple} = \dfrac{T_{max} - T_{min}}{T_{avg}} \times 100\%$ | Torque ripple | % |
| 9 | $\dot{x} = f(x, u)$ | State-space model (used for MPC + AI) | — |
| 10 | $J(\theta) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N}(y_k - \hat{y}_k)^2$ | ANN training loss (MSE) | — |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Range Extension:** Tesla, Lucid, and Rivian report **4–8 % range gains** using AI-tuned torque vectoring and loss-minimizing current trajectories.
- **Sensorless Operation:** AI estimators (Back-EMF + ANN observer) eliminate physical position sensors, reducing cost by **\$50–100/motor**.
- **Predictive Maintenance:** Deep learning on vibration/current signatures detects bearing faults **30–60 days** before failure.
- **Thermal Safety:** LSTM networks predict hotspot temperatures, preventing magnet demagnetization (NdFeB magnets lose flux above **150–180 °C**).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Loss-Minimizing Current Reference (MTPA)

The **Maximum Torque Per Ampere (MTPA)** strategy finds $(i_d^*, i_q^*)$ that minimize current magnitude for a given torque.

### Step 1 — Define the Optimization Problem

We minimize the stator current vector magnitude:

$$\min_{i_d, i_q} \; I_s^2 = i_d^2 + i_q^2$$

subject to the torque constraint (for IPM, interior PMSM where $L_d \neq L_q$):

$$T_e = \frac{3}{2} p \left[ \psi_m i_q + (L_d - L_q) i_d i_q \right] = T_e^{ref}$$

### Step 2 — Form the Lagrangian

$$\mathcal{L} = i_d^2 + i_q^2 + \lambda \left( T_e^{ref} - \frac{3}{2} p \left[ \psi_m i_q + (L_d - L_q) i_d i_q \right] \right)$$

### Step 3 — Take Partial Derivatives

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial i_d} = 2 i_d - \lambda \cdot \frac{3}{2} p (L_d - L_q) i_q = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial i_q} = 2 i_q - \lambda \cdot \frac{3}{2} p \left[ \psi_m + (L_d - L_q) i_d \right] = 0$$

### Step 4 — Solve the System

From the first equation:

$$\lambda = \frac{4 i_d}{3 p (L_d - L_q) i_q}$$

Substituting into the second and simplifying yields the **MTPA trajectory**:

$$i_d^{MTPA} = \frac{\psi_m - \sqrt{\psi_m^2 + 8 (L_d - L_q)^2 \left(\frac{T_e^{ref}}{3p}\right)^2}}{4 (L_d - L_q)}$$

$$i_q^{MTPA} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{T_e^{ref}}{\tfrac{3}{2} p}\right)^2 - \psi_m i_d} \cdot \frac{1}{L_d - L_q}$$

### Step 5 — Why Use AI Here?

The closed-form MTPA requires **known $L_d, L_q, \psi_m$**, which *change with temperature and saturation*. Re-deriving every 100 ms is computationally impossible.

**AI Solution:** Train an ANN $(i_d^{ref}, i_q^{ref}) = f_{NN}(T_e^{ref}, \omega, T_{coil})$ once offline using motor map data, then deploy as a *single matrix multiply + bias* in the ECU (latency **< 100 µs**).

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: AI Techniques vs Motor Control Tasks

| Control Task | Classical Approach | AI Technique Used | Performance Gain |
|--------------|--------------------|-------------------|------------------|
| **MTPA Optimization** | Lookup Table (LUT) | ANN / SVR | +2–3 % efficiency, smaller LUT |
| **Sensorless Position** | Sliding-mode observer | LSTM / GRU Neural Net | ±0.5° accuracy at low speed |
| **Torque Ripple Reduction** | Harmonic injection | Repetitive NN + Notch Filter | Ripple 6 % → 1.5 % |
| **Parameter Identification** | Recursive Least Squares | Deep Belief Network | Real-time $R_s, L_d$ tracking |
| **Thermal Prediction** | Lumped RC model | LSTM / Physics-Informed NN | ±2 °C accuracy, 30 s ahead |
| **Fault Diagnosis** | Threshold logic | CNN on current FFT | 98 % fault-classification accuracy |
| **Energy Management** | Rule-based | Deep Reinforcement Learning | +6–12 % battery range |

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Efficiency Map Construction via AI

Motor efficiency $\eta(\omega, T)$ is defined as:

$$\eta(\omega, T) = \frac{T \cdot \omega}{T \cdot \omega + P_{cu}(\omega, T) + P_{iron}(\omega, T)}$$

The classical approach measures $\eta$ at thousands of $(\omega, T)$ points on a dynamometer (cost: **3–5 days, ~\$15 000**).

### AI-Powered Surrogate Model

1. Measure $\eta$ at a sparse grid (e.g., 100 points).
2. Train a **Gaussian Process Regressor (GPR)** or **Neural Network**:

$$\hat{\eta}(\omega, T) = f_{NN}(\omega, T; \theta^*)$$

3. Validate using k-fold cross-validation.
4. Deploy: full efficiency map generated in **< 1 second** with RMSE < 0.3 %.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to compare MTPA vs AI-MTPA, emphasize that AI handles **time-varying parameters** ($R_s, L_d, \psi_m$) that classical MTPA assumes constant. This is the single highest-weightage differentiator in KTU 2024 board exams.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 AI-Augmented FOC Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Driver Demand Pedal] --> B[Torque Demand Calculator]
    B --> C[MTPA AI Module]
    C --> D[Current Reference id_ref iq_ref]
    D --> E[AI Based PI Controller]
    E --> F[Inverse Park Transform]
    F --> G[SVPWM Generator]
    G --> H[3 Phase Inverter]
    H --> I[PMSM Motor]
    I --> J[Mechanical Output to Wheels]
    I --> K[Current Sensors ABC]
    I --> L[Position Speed Sensor]
    K --> M[Clarke Park Transform]
    L --> M
    M --> E
    I --> N[Temperature Sensor]
    N --> C
    C --> O[Efficiency Optimizer Loss Minimizer]
    O --> C
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style C fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style E fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style H fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style I fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style O fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

**Description:** The dashed green node C represents the AI inference engine (Neural Network) computing optimal current references. It receives feedback from temperature sensor N and feeds the loss-minimizer O. The blue node E represents the AI-tuned PI controller replacing fixed-gain controllers.

---

## 4.2 AI Training vs Deployment Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Motor Dynamometer Data] --> B[Data Preprocessing]
    B --> C[Feature Engineering]
    C --> D[ANN Model Selection]
    D --> E[Offline Training GPU]
    E --> F[Validation Test Set]
    F -->|Pass| G[Model Quantization INT8]
    F -->|Fail| D
    G --> H[ECU Deployment]
    H --> I[Real Time Inference Microseconds]
    I --> J[Continuous Online Learning]
    J --> D
    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style E fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style H fill:#87CEFA,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style I fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

---

## 4.3 Hierarchical AI Architecture for Motor Control

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Layer 1 Real Time Control Layer 100 microseconds] --> L2[Layer 2 Adaptive Tuning Layer 10 milliseconds]
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3 Optimization Layer 100 milliseconds]
    L3 --> L4[Layer 4 Fleet Learning Cloud 1 day]
    L1 --> M1[PI Controllers SVPWM]
    L2 --> M2[Neural Network Gain Scheduling]
    L3 --> M3[RL Agent Energy Optimization]
    L4 --> M4[Global Model Aggregation Federated Learning]
    style L1 fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,color:#FFF
    style L2 fill:#FFD93D,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style L3 fill:#6BCB77,stroke:#333,color:#FFF
    style L4 fill:#4D96FF,stroke:#333,color:#FFF
```

---

## 4.4 Sensorless AI Observer Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Measured Va Vb Vc] --> B[Clarke Transform]
    B --> C[Moving Horizon Estimator]
    C --> D[LSTM Neural Network]
    D --> E[Estimated Rotor Position theta_hat]
    D --> F[Estimated Speed omega_hat]
    D --> G[Estimated Load Torque TL_hat]
    E --> H[FOC Controller]
    F --> H
    style C fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style D fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Drawing Note:** Always label AI nodes clearly with "Neural Network," "Fuzzy Logic," or "RL Agent." Boards reward students who explicitly call out the AI technique inside the block.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEAUT865]`

**Q: Define Artificial Neural Network (ANN) and explain how it can be used for MTPA control of a PMSM motor.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **Artificial Neural Network (ANN)** is a computational model inspired by biological neurons, consisting of interconnected layers of nodes (input, hidden, output) with weighted connections $\mathbf{w}$ and biases $\mathbf{b}$, trained via backpropagation to minimize a loss function (commonly Mean Squared Error).

In **MTPA control of PMSM**, an ANN takes as inputs:

- $T_e^{ref}$ — reference torque
- $\omega_m$ — mechanical speed
- $T_{coil}$ — winding temperature

and outputs the optimal current references $i_d^*, i_q^*$ that minimize stator current magnitude (and thus copper loss) for a given torque. The network is trained offline on motor-map data covering saturation and temperature variations, then deployed in the ECU for real-time inference in **< 100 µs**, replacing bulky lookup tables and providing adaptive, parameter-robust current trajectories.

*(Word count: ~120, KTU markscheme rewards: definition + 2 key application points)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEAUT865]`

**Q: List any three advantages of using AI-based control over classical PI control in EV motor drives.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Adaptation to Non-Linearities:** AI controllers (ANN, Fuzzy) inherently handle magnetic saturation, cross-coupling, and temperature drift without manual gain retuning, unlike fixed-gain PI controllers which lose performance outside their tuning envelope.

2. **Multi-Objective Optimization:** AI can simultaneously optimize efficiency, torque ripple, and acoustic noise—achieving Pareto-optimal trade-offs impossible with classical SISO PI loops.

3. **Sensorless Operation:** AI observers (LSTM, CNN) estimate rotor position, speed, and load torque from voltage/current alone, eliminating physical encoders/resolvers and reducing cost by **\$50–100** per motor.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> In Module 2 questions, students commonly lose **3–4 marks** by:
> - Writing *only* the AI algorithm name without **architecture diagram + numerical example**
> - Skipping the **loss model derivation** ($P_{cu}, P_{iron}$)
> - Not linking AI outputs to **measurable EV metrics** (range, torque ripple, efficiency %)
> - Confusing MTPA (torque-axis optimization) with Field Weakening (speed-axis optimization)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of an **AI-based Field Oriented Control (FOC)** system for a PMSM motor in an EV. Highlight the role of each AI module. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **MTPA trajectory equations** for an Interior PMSM (IPM) and explain how an Artificial Neural Network can be used to implement MTPA in real-time, replacing the lookup-table approach. Discuss the training data requirements and inference latency. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Block Diagram:** (Refer Section 4.1 above; expected: 3 marks)

**Explanation of AI Modules:**

1. **MTPA AI Module (Input: $T_e^{ref}, \omega_m, T_{coil}$; Output: $i_d^*, i_q^*$)** — Computes loss-minimizing current references in real time. *(1 mark)*

2. **AI-Tuned PI Controller** — Replaces fixed-gain PI with a neural-network-based controller that adjusts gains dynamically based on operating point. *(1 mark)*

3. **Efficiency Optimizer** — A reinforcement-learning agent that learns the optimal $(i_d^*, i_q^*)$ trajectory across entire WLTP drive cycles to maximize km/kWh. *(1 mark)*

4. **Sensorless Observer (LSTM)** — Estimates rotor position $\hat{\theta}$ and speed $\hat{\omega}$ from voltage/current alone, enabling operation without an encoder. *(1 mark)*

**Conclusion:** The AI-augmented FOC delivers **2–4 % efficiency gain**, **> 50 % torque ripple reduction**, and **robustness to parameter variation**. *(0 marks, optional)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**MTPA Derivation:** (Refer Section 3.1 — Steps 1 to 4 above; expected: 3 marks)

**ANN Implementation:**

- **Architecture:** 3 inputs → 8 hidden neurons (tanh activation) → 2 outputs $(i_d^*, i_q^*)$. *(1 mark)*
- **Training Data:** Collected from finite-element (FEA) simulation + dynamometer sweep across $T_e \in [0, T_{max}]$ and $T_{coil} \in [20°C, 150°C]$. Dataset size: ~50 000 samples. *(1 mark)*
- **Loss Function:** MSE $= \tfrac{1}{N}\sum(y - \hat{y})^2$ minimized via Adam optimizer with learning rate $10^{-3}$ over 500 epochs. *(1 mark)*
- **Inference Latency:** Quantized INT8 ANN on NXP S32K3 MCU executes in **< 50 µs**, compared to 2-D LUT lookup of 800 µs. *(1 mark)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the **loss minimization problem** in EV traction motors. Derive the expression for copper loss and iron loss, and show how AI can minimize total losses online. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **sensorless control of PMSM using AI observers**. Compare the AI approach with classical Back-EMF observers and Sliding-Mode observers. Include a brief note on training methodology. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Loss Model:** (Refer Section 2.3 formulas; expected: 3 marks)

**Copper Loss:** $P_{cu} = \tfrac{3}{2} R_s (i_d^2 + i_q^2)$

**Iron Loss (Steinmetz):** $P_{iron} = k_h f B^{\alpha} + k_e f^2 B^{2}$

**Total:** $P_{loss} = P_{cu} + P_{iron} + P_{stray} + P_{mech}$

**Online Loss Minimization via AI:** *(4 marks)*

A **Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) agent** is trained with the reward function:

$$r_t = -\left[ P_{loss}(i_d, i_q, \omega, T) \cdot \Delta t \right] + \beta \cdot \frac{\Delta \text{SOC}}{\Delta t}$$

The agent learns the optimal action $a_t = (i_d^*, i_q^*)$ via the **Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (DDPG)** algorithm. After 10⁶ training episodes on a virtual WLTP cycle, the agent converges to a policy that achieves **3–6 % lower losses** than MTPA, especially in the field-weakening region.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Sensorless AI Observer Working Principle:** *(3 marks)*

- **Inputs:** $v_a, v_b, v_c$ (3-phase voltages), $i_a, i_b, i_c$ (currents)
- **Processing:** Clarke transform → $(v_\alpha, v_\beta, i_\alpha, i_\beta)$
- **AI Core:** LSTM with 2 layers, 64 hidden units
- **Outputs:** $\hat{\theta}_r$ (rotor angle), $\hat{\omega}_r$ (rotor speed), $\hat{T}_L$ (load torque)
- **Update Rate:** 20 kHz (same as PWM)

**Comparison Table:** *(4 marks)*

| Feature | Back-EMF Observer | Sliding-Mode Observer | AI Observer (LSTM) |
|---------|-------------------|----------------------|--------------------|
| Accuracy at low speed | Poor (< 5 % speed) | Moderate (chattering) | Excellent (< 0.5° error) |
| Robustness to parameter variation | Low | Moderate | High |
| Computational Cost | Low | Low | Moderate (INT8: < 30 µs) |
| Training Required | No | No | Yes (offline) |
| Adaptability | None | Limited | Online learning possible |

**Training Methodology:** Dataset generated via FEA + dynamic motor model at 1000 operating points; LSTM trained with Adam optimizer for 200 epochs; final model pruned and INT8-quantized for ECU deployment.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam**

- **Core Definition:** AI in motor control = *adaptive, non-linear, data-driven* control replacing fixed-gain PI.
- **Most-used AI techniques:** ANN (for function approximation), Fuzzy Logic (for rule-based reasoning), DRL (for sequential decision-making), LSTM (for time-series prediction).
- **MTPA Equation (must memorize):** $T_e = \tfrac{3}{2} p \psi_m i_q$ for SPM, with $L_d \neq L_q$ correction for IPM.
- **Loss Formula:** $P_{cu} = \tfrac{3}{2} R_s (i_d^2 + i_q^2)$ is the dominant loss term.
- **Efficiency Range:** PMSM peaks at **96–98 %**, IM at **92–95 %**.
- **Torque Ripple Target:** **< 5 %** for premium EVs.
- **AI Inference Latency:** Must be **< 100 µs** for real-time deployment.
- **Sensorless Accuracy:** AI achieves **±0.5°** rotor position error; classical methods **±3–5°**.
- **Range Gain from AI:** Tesla/Lucid claim **+4–8 %** via AI optimization.
- **Thermal Limit:** NdFeB magnets demagnetize above **150–180 °C** — AI thermal predictors are essential.
- **Mathematical Tool to remember:** Universal Approximation Theorem — ANN can approximate any continuous function to arbitrary precision → justifies AI for any non-linear motor dynamics.
- **Exam phrase that scores high marks:** *"AI overcomes the rigidity of classical PI controllers by providing adaptive, parameter-robust, real-time optimal control across the entire torque-speed envelope."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Regenerative Braking: AI for Maximizing Energy Recovery
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT865 - ADVANCED VEHICLE TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Regenerative Braking: AI for Maximizing Energy Recovery

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Regenerative Braking (RB)** is an energy recovery mechanism that decelerates a vehicle by converting its **kinetic energy (KE)** and **potential energy** into a storable form—typically electrical energy in a high-voltage battery or supercapacitor—via the traction motor operating as a generator. In the AI-augmented paradigm, **Artificial Intelligence (AI)** dynamically orchestrates the *brake force split*, *regenerative torque scheduling*, *State-of-Charge (SOC)* management, and *thermal constraints* in real time to maximize the recovered energy per braking event.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Exam Ready):**
> *Regenerative braking is a closed-loop electromechanical deceleration strategy wherein the propulsion machine is commanded to operate in generator mode during a braking demand. When fused with AI, the system shifts from a static lookup-table torque map to an adaptive, learning-based policy that learns the optimal regenerative torque profile from heterogeneous driving data, road gradients, battery health, and driver behavior.*

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Smart Elevator"**
> Imagine a high-rise elevator: when it descends with passengers, a counterweight pulls it down. Instead of letting gravity waste the descent energy as heat (brake pads), the elevator drives a generator on the motor shaft, recapturing electricity that *feeds back into the building's grid*. Now, add a **smart AI controller**: it knows the elevator's load, the floor it started from, and how many people are waiting. It decides *exactly* how much energy to recover versus how much to dissipate as heat to ensure a smooth, safe stop. **This is precisely what AI does for a modern EV's brakes.**

A simpler analogy is the **bicycle dynamo**: pedaling spins a small generator that lights a bulb. In an EV, instead of *you* pedaling, the vehicle's own momentum spins the motor, charging the battery. The AI acts as the *smart regulator* deciding *how hard* the dynamo should engage without stalling the "rider" (i.e., destabilizing the vehicle).

## 1.3 Why AI? The Limitation of Conventional RB

| Conventional RB | AI-Augmented RB |
|---|---|
| Uses **static lookup tables** (speed → torque map) | Uses **learned policies** (Reinforcement Learning) |
| Fixed brake-blend ratio (e.g., 60:40 regen:friction) | **Adaptive blend** based on SOC, temperature, gradient |
| Ignores driver intent variability | Predicts driver braking **intensity & duration** |
| One-size-fits-all calibration | **Personalized** to individual driving style |
| Reacts to surface changes slowly | Anticipates via V2X / camera / radar fusion |

## 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (Highlighted for Examiners)

- **Regenerative Efficiency ($\eta_{reg}$)** = $\dfrac{E_{recovered}}{E_{deceleration\_required}}$ — **typically 60%–70%** in production EVs.
- **Energy Recovery Rate (kW)** — peak regen power of modern EVs: **150 kW – 350 kW**.
- **State of Charge (SOC)** operating window for safe regen: **~10% – 90%**.
- **Brake Blending Ratio** — the proportional split between regenerative and friction braking torque.
- **Round-Trip Efficiency** of the recovered energy (storage + discharge): **~70%–85%**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

When the driver depresses the brake pedal, the following chain of events occurs in an AI-managed RB system:

1. **Brake Pedal Travel Sensor (BPTS)** measures pedal position and rate ($\dot{x}_{pedal}$). This signal is fed to the **Vehicle Control Unit (VCU)**.
2. **AI Inference Engine** (running on the VCU/ECU) fuses this with:
   - Vehicle speed $v$
   - Battery SOC and temperature $T_{batt}$
   - Motor temperature $T_{mot}$
   - Road gradient $\theta$ (from GPS + IMU)
   - Predicted traffic state (from ADAS sensors)
3. **Optimal Torque Allocation**: The AI solves (or approximates) a constrained optimization problem to compute the regenerative torque $T_{reg}$ that:
   - Maximizes recovered energy $E_{rec}$
   - Respects SOC upper bound to prevent overcharge
   - Respects motor thermal limits
   - Maintains vehicle longitudinal stability
4. **Friction Blending**: Any shortfall $(T_{demand} - T_{reg})$ is commanded to the hydraulic friction brakes via the **Electronic Stability Control (ESC)** module.
5. **Battery Management System (BMS)** accepts the regen current $I_{reg}$ within its $C$-rate and SOC constraints.
6. **Learning Phase**: The AI agent updates its policy weights using **Reward = $E_{recovered} - \lambda \cdot (T_{demand}-T_{reg})^2$** (penalizing deviation from driver demand).

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the AI Layer

- **Non-linearity**: Battery internal resistance, motor back-EMF, and brake-pad friction are all temperature- and SOC-dependent. A *linear* controller cannot track optimal operation.
- **Stochasticity**: Real-world traffic involves unpredictable cut-ins and pedestrian events. AI predicts a *probability distribution* over future braking demands.
- **Multi-objective optimization**: Recovering maximum energy while preserving brake feel, pedal firmness, NVH (noise-vibration-harshness), and pad life is a Pareto problem—AI handles this elegantly.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description / Variable Definitions | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{kinetic} = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2$ | Recoverable kinetic energy of the vehicle | Joules (J) |
| 2 | $E_{recovered} = \displaystyle\int_{t_0}^{t_f} \tau_{reg}(t)\cdot\omega(t)\,dt$ | Total electrical energy regenerated | Joules (J) |
| 3 | $P_{reg} = \tau_{reg} \cdot \omega = V_{batt} \cdot I_{reg}$ | Instantaneous regenerative power | Watts (W) |
| 4 | $\eta_{reg} = \dfrac{E_{recovered}}{E_{deceleration\_required}}$ | Regenerative efficiency | Dimensionless (0–1) |
| 5 | $F_{brake,total} = F_{reg} + F_{fric}$ | Brake force blending (parallel) | Newtons (N) |
| 6 | $T_{reg,max}(v) = \dfrac{P_{reg,max}}{\omega(v)} = \dfrac{P_{reg,max}\cdot r}{v}$ | Maximum regen torque at speed $v$ | N·m |
| 7 | $V_{batt} = E_{emf} - I_{reg}\cdot R_{int}(SOC,T)$ | Battery terminal voltage during regen | Volts (V) |
| 8 | $\tau_{emf} = k\cdot\Phi\cdot I_{a}$ | Motor torque (acting as generator) | N·m |
| 9 | $E_{emf} = k\cdot\Phi\cdot\omega$ | Back-EMF induced in motor | Volts (V) |
| 10 | $SOC(t) = SOC(t_0) + \dfrac{1}{C_{batt}}\displaystyle\int_{t_0}^{t} I_{reg}(\tau)\,d\tau$ | Battery state of charge update | % or fraction |
| 11 | $J_{AI} = -E_{recovered} + \lambda_{1}(SOC-SOC_{max})^2 + \lambda_{2}(T_{mot}-T_{limit})^2$ | AI cost function (negative reward) | J + penalty |
| 12 | $P_{aux} = P_{HVAC} + P_{electronics} + P_{lighting}$ | Auxiliary load (must be subtracted) | Watts (W) |
| 13 | $E_{net} = \eta_{reg}\cdot E_{kinetic} - E_{aux,t}$ | Net energy benefit per braking event | Joules (J) |
| 14 | $\eta_{round-trip} = \eta_{charge}\cdot\eta_{discharge}\cdot\eta_{motor-gen}$ | End-to-end efficiency | Dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always express angles in **radians**, $v$ in **m/s**, mass in **kg**, and time in **seconds** to maintain SI unit consistency in derivations.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Range Extension**: AI-optimized RB adds **5%–20%** to EV range in urban drive cycles (WLTP, FTP-75).
- **Brake Pad Longevity**: Regenerative blending can extend friction pad life to **>300,000 km** in Teslas.
- **Thermal Headroom**: Smart AI derates regen current to keep the motor and battery within **40°C–60°C** optimal window.
- **Grid Services (V2G)**: The same AI infrastructure enables **Vehicle-to-Grid** bidirectional charging.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Maximum Recoverable Energy During a Braking Event

**Problem Setup:** A vehicle of mass $m$ traveling at initial speed $v_0$ decelerates uniformly to rest over a distance $s$. Compute the maximum theoretical recoverable energy if regen efficiency is $\eta_{reg}$.

**Step 1 — Initial Kinetic Energy:**
By definition, the kinetic energy of the moving vehicle is given by the standard Newtonian formulation.

$$E_{kinetic,initial} = \tfrac{1}{2} m v_0^2$$

**Step 2 — Final Kinetic Energy:**
At the end of the braking event, the vehicle comes to rest, hence $v_f = 0$.

$$E_{kinetic,final} = \tfrac{1}{2} m v_f^2 = 0$$

**Step 3 — Total Energy Dissipated (Work–Energy Theorem):**
By the work–energy theorem, the work done by the brake system equals the change in kinetic energy.

$$W_{brake} = \Delta E_{kinetic} = E_{kinetic,final} - E_{kinetic,initial} = -\tfrac{1}{2} m v_0^2$$

The negative sign indicates energy leaves the system. The magnitude of braking work is therefore $\tfrac{1}{2} m v_0^2$.

**Step 4 — Energy Recovered by Regen:**
Only a fraction $\eta_{reg}$ of this braking work is converted to electrical energy; the rest is dissipated as heat in friction brakes, motor copper losses, and battery internal resistance.

$$E_{recovered,max} = \eta_{reg} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} m v_0^2$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Illustration (Board Style):**
For a $m = 1800$ kg vehicle braking from $v_0 = 27.78$ m/s (100 km/h) with $\eta_{reg} = 0.70$:

$$E_{kinetic} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 1800 \cdot (27.78)^2 = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 1800 \cdot 771.73 \approx 6.945 \times 10^5 \text{ J}$$

$$E_{recovered} = 0.70 \cdot 6.945 \times 10^5 \approx 4.86 \times 10^5 \text{ J} \approx 0.135 \text{ kWh}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Braking from 100 km/h to 0 in an 1800 kg EV recovers approximately **0.135 kWh**—enough to drive an additional **~1–1.5 km** in typical urban conditions.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: AI Cost Function for Optimal Regenerative Torque Scheduling

**Problem Setup:** During a braking event lasting $T$ seconds, find the regenerative torque profile $T_{reg}(t)$ that maximizes recovered energy subject to battery SOC and motor thermal constraints.

**Step 1 — Define the Maximization Objective:**
The total energy recovered over the braking interval $[0, T]$ is the time-integral of mechanical power delivered to the generator.

$$E_{recovered} = \displaystyle\int_{0}^{T} T_{reg}(t) \cdot \omega(t) \cdot \eta_{gen}\,dt$$

**Step 2 — Constraints Encoding:**
The AI must respect the following hard constraints:
- **SOC upper limit:** $SOC(t) \le SOC_{max}$ (typically 0.90)
- **Motor torque limit:** $T_{reg}(t) \le T_{reg,max}(v)$
- **Power limit:** $T_{reg}(t) \cdot \omega(t) \le P_{reg,max}$
- **Thermal limit:** $T_{mot}(t) \le T_{mot,max}$

**Step 3 — Convert to a Minimization (Cost Function):**
Reinforcement learning agents minimize a *cost* $J$. We negate the recovered energy and add quadratic penalties for constraint violations.

$$J_{AI} = - \displaystyle\int_{0}^{T} T_{reg}(t) \cdot \omega(t) \cdot \eta_{gen}\,dt + \lambda_1 \int_{0}^{T} \max(0, SOC(t)-SOC_{max})^2\,dt + \lambda_2 \int_{0}^{T} \max(0, T_{mot}(t)-T_{mot,max})^2\,dt$$

**Step 4 — Discretization for Real-Time Deployment:**
The VCU operates at $\Delta t = 10$ ms. The continuous integral is approximated using a Riemann sum.

$$J_{AI}^{disc} = -\sum_{k=0}^{N} T_{reg}[k] \cdot \omega[k] \cdot \eta_{gen} \cdot \Delta t + \lambda_1 \sum_{k=0}^{N} \left[\max(0,\, SOC[k]-SOC_{max})\right]^2 + \lambda_2 \sum_{k=0}^{N} \left[\max(0,\, T_{mot}[k]-T_{mot,max})\right]^2$$

**Step 5 — Policy Update (Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient — DDPG):**
The actor network $\pi_\theta$ outputs an action $a_k = T_{reg}[k]$. The critic network $Q_\phi(s_k, a_k)$ estimates future cumulative cost. The policy is updated via gradient descent.

$$\theta \leftarrow \theta - \alpha \cdot \nabla_\theta Q_\phi(s_k, \pi_\theta(s_k))$$

The AI thus learns to output the *maximum permissible* $T_{reg}[k]$ at every instant without violating SOC, thermal, or power constraints.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Brake Force Blending for Stable Deceleration

**Problem Setup:** The driver demands a total deceleration $a_{demand}$. The regenerative system provides $a_{reg}$. Compute the required friction deceleration $a_{fric}$.

**Step 1 — Newton's Second Law (Longitudinal Dynamics):**
The total deceleration equals the sum of regenerative and friction contributions, in parallel braking architecture.

$$m \cdot a_{demand} = m \cdot a_{reg} + m \cdot a_{fric}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for Friction Component:**
Rearranging the equation, the friction brake must supply the residual demand.

$$a_{fric} = a_{demand} - a_{reg}$$

**Step 3 — Express in Torque Form:**
The regenerative torque $T_{reg}$ produces a deceleration at the wheel of radius $r_{wh}$ and gear ratio $G$.

$$a_{reg} = \dfrac{T_{reg} \cdot G \cdot \eta_{drivetrain}}{m \cdot r_{wh}}$$

**Step 4 — Stability Constraint (No Wheel Lock on Regen):**
To prevent wheel lock during pure regenerative braking on low-$\mu$ surfaces, the AI enforces a slip-ratio constraint $\lambda \le \lambda_{max}$.

$$\lambda = \dfrac{v_{wheel} - v_{vehicle}}{v_{vehicle}} \le 0.10 \text{ (typical)}$$

The AI reduces $T_{reg}$ *before* the friction ECU activates ABS intervention.

**Step 5 — Numerical Illustration:**
Given $m = 1500$ kg, $a_{demand} = 3.0$ m/s², $T_{reg} = 800$ N·m, $G = 9$, $\eta_{drivetrain} = 0.95$, $r_{wh} = 0.33$ m:

$$a_{reg} = \dfrac{800 \cdot 9 \cdot 0.95}{1500 \cdot 0.33} = \dfrac{6840}{495} \approx 13.8 \text{ m/s}^2$$

Since $a_{reg} > a_{demand}$, the AI *automatically clamps* $T_{reg}$ to the limit:

$$T_{reg,clamped} = \dfrac{a_{demand} \cdot m \cdot r_{wh}}{G \cdot \eta_{drivetrain}} = \dfrac{3.0 \cdot 1500 \cdot 0.33}{9 \cdot 0.95} \approx 173.7 \text{ N·m}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Strategy | Energy Recovery | Computational Load | Implementation Cost | KTU Exam Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Rule-based (lookup table)** | Low–Medium | Negligible | Low | **Very High** |
| **Fuzzy logic controller** | Medium | Low | Low | **High** |
| **Equivalent Consumption Minimization (ECMS)** | Medium–High | Moderate | Medium | **High** |
| **Dynamic Programming (offline)** | High (theoretical max) | Very High (offline) | High | **Medium** |
| **Reinforcement Learning (DDPG/PPO)** | **Highest** (adaptive) | High (training) / Low (inference) | Medium–High | **Emerging** |
| **Neural Network MPC** | Highest + constraint satisfaction | Very High | High | **Emerging** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System-Level Block Diagram — AI-Controlled Regenerative Braking Architecture

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Pattern]**

**Q: Define regenerative braking in electric vehicles. List any two factors that limit the maximum regenerative energy recovery.**

**Model Answer:**

Regenerative braking is an energy recovery mechanism in which the electric traction motor of an EV is operated in **generator mode** during deceleration. The motor's back-EMF is used to charge the high-voltage battery, thereby converting the vehicle's kinetic energy into storable electrical energy.

**Two limiting factors:**
1. **State of Charge (SOC) of the battery:** When SOC approaches 100%, the battery cannot accept further charge, forcing the friction brakes to absorb all braking energy.
2. **Motor speed and power limits:** Below a threshold speed (typically 5–10 km/h), the back-EMF becomes too small to generate useful charging current; also, the motor's peak power rating caps instantaneous regen.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Many students write only "battery is full" without mentioning *motor speed limits* or *thermal limits*. Always state at least **two distinct physical constraints** for full 3-mark credit.

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Pattern]**

**Q: What is the role of Artificial Intelligence in maximizing energy recovery during regenerative braking?**

**Model Answer:**

AI enhances regenerative braking by replacing static, lookup-table-based control with **adaptive, learning-based algorithms**. Specifically, AI:

1. **Predicts driver braking intent** using neural networks trained on pedal-pressure history, enabling pre-emptive regenerative torque scheduling.
2. **Optimizes brake force blending** between regen and friction in real time, maximizing the regen fraction while preserving pedal feel and ABS function.
3. **Learns from fleet data** (cloud-based) to improve policy across millions of miles, adapting to diverse road gradients, traffic patterns, and battery aging conditions.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – ESE Pattern, Module 2 Choice]**

**Q: (a)** With the aid of a block diagram, explain the architecture of an AI-controlled regenerative braking system in a Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV). Describe the roles of the VCU, BMS, and AI inference engine. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 1600 kg electric vehicle traveling at 90 km/h applies the brakes and decelerates uniformly to 30 km/h. If the regenerative braking efficiency is 65% and auxiliary loads consume 200 W throughout the 8-second braking event, compute: (i) the kinetic energy dissipated, (ii) the electrical energy recovered, and (iii) the net energy stored in the battery. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

The AI-controlled regenerative braking system comprises the following subsystems:

1. **Driver Input Layer:** Brake pedal position sensor and accelerator release detection feed raw demand signals to the VCU.
2. **Sensor Fusion Layer:** ADAS camera/radar, GPS, and IMU provide context—gradient, traffic density, and predicted braking events ahead.
3. **VCU (Vehicle Control Unit):** Acts as the central orchestrator. It runs the AI inference engine, arbitrates between subsystems, and issues torque commands at 100 Hz.
4. **AI Inference Engine:** A trained neural network (typically DDPG or PPO actor) that maps the *state vector* (speed, SOC, temperatures, gradient, traffic) to the *optimal regenerative torque command*.
5. **BMS (Battery Management System):** Enforces hard constraints—SOC ≤ 90%, cell temperature 15°C–45°C, C-rate ≤ 2C—and reports available regen headroom to the VCU.
6. **Motor Inverter and Traction Motor:** Execute the torque command by commutating the PMSM in generator mode.
7. **ESC (Electronic Stability Control):** Activates friction brakes for any shortfall, and intervenes via ABS/TCS if wheel slip exceeds thresholds.

**Communication:** All modules communicate over **CAN-FD or Automotive Ethernet** at 1–10 Mbit/s with sub-10 ms latency.

**Role of AI:** The AI is the *decision-making brain* that converts a high-dimensional state into a single optimal action—*how much regenerative torque right now*—learning from both real-time telemetry and historical fleet data.

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:**
- $m = 1600$ kg
- $v_0 = 90$ km/h $= 25$ m/s
- $v_f = 30$ km/h $= 8.33$ m/s
- $\eta_{reg} = 0.65$
- $P_{aux} = 200$ W
- $t_{brake} = 8$ s

**(i) Kinetic Energy Dissipated:**

The change in kinetic energy is given by the difference of the final and initial kinetic energies.

$$\Delta E_{kinetic} = \tfrac{1}{2} m v_f^2 - \tfrac{1}{2} m v_0^2$$

Substituting the values:

$$\Delta E_{kinetic} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 1600 \cdot (8.33)^2 - \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 1600 \cdot (25)^2$$

$$\Delta E_{kinetic} = 800 \cdot 69.39 - 800 \cdot 625$$

$$\Delta E_{kinetic} = 55{,}512 - 500{,}000 = -444{,}488 \text{ J}$$

The magnitude of kinetic energy dissipated is $\approx \mathbf{4.44 \times 10^5 \text{ J}}$.

**(ii) Electrical Energy Recovered:**

The recovered energy is the product of regenerative efficiency and the dissipated kinetic energy.

$$E_{recovered} = \eta_{reg} \cdot \vert \Delta E_{kinetic} \vert = 0.65 \cdot 444{,}488 \approx 288{,}917 \text{ J}$$

$$\boxed{E_{recovered} \approx 2.89 \times 10^5 \text{ J} \approx 0.0803 \text{ kWh}}$$

**(iii) Net Energy Stored in Battery:**

The auxiliary load continuously consumes power during the 8-second event, so we subtract this parasitic loss.

$$E_{aux} = P_{aux} \cdot t = 200 \cdot 8 = 1600 \text{ J}$$

$$E_{net} = E_{recovered} - E_{aux} = 288{,}917 - 1600 \approx 287{,}317 \text{ J}$$

$$\boxed{E_{net} \approx 2.87 \times 10^5 \text{ J} \approx 0.0798 \text{ kWh}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [ALTERNATIVE — KTU Internal Choice Pattern]**

**Q: (a)** Explain the Reinforcement Learning (RL) framework for AI-driven regenerative braking. Define state, action, reward, and policy. How does the AI handle the multi-objective trade-off between energy recovery and brake-pedal feel? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the maximum regenerative torque allowable at a given vehicle speed such that wheel lock is avoided on a road with friction coefficient $\mu$. Assume a rear-wheel-drive EV with weight distribution 45:55 (front:rear). **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

A Reinforcement Learning (RL) agent learns the optimal regenerative braking policy through interaction with the vehicle's environment.

**State ($s_t$):** A vector describing the vehicle's current operating point:
$$s_t = \begin{bmatrix} v, & SOC, & T_{batt}, & T_{mot}, & \theta, & \dot{x}_{pedal}, & a_{prev} \end{bmatrix}^T$$

where $v$ is vehicle speed, $SOC$ is battery state of charge, $T_{batt}$ and $T_{mot}$ are battery and motor temperatures, $\theta$ is road gradient, $\dot{x}_{pedal}$ is pedal velocity, and $a_{prev}$ is previous timestep deceleration.

**Action ($a_t$):** The continuous-valued regenerative torque command:
$$a_t = T_{reg}(t) \in [0,\, T_{reg,max}(v)]$$

**Reward ($r_t$):** A scalar feedback signal combining energy recovery with constraint penalties:
$$r_t = \eta_{gen} \cdot T_{reg}(t) \cdot \omega(t) \cdot \Delta t - \lambda_1 \cdot \max(0,\, SOC-SOC_{max})^2 - \lambda_2 \cdot \max(0,\, T_{mot}-T_{limit})^2 - \lambda_3 \cdot (a_{demand}-a_{actual})^2$$

**Policy ($\pi_\theta$):** A neural network (actor) mapping states to actions:
$$a_t = \pi_\theta(s_t)$$

**Multi-Objective Trade-off Handling:**

The third penalty term $\lambda_3 \cdot (a_{demand} - a_{actual})^2$ penalizes deviation from the driver's demanded deceleration. This preserves **brake-pedal feel**—a key KTU exam point. The $\lambda$ hyperparameters are tuned via Pareto analysis to balance:
- **Objective 1:** Maximize $E_{recovered}$
- **Objective 2:** Match driver-intended deceleration
- **Objective 3:** Protect battery health (SOC and temperature)

The agent thus learns a *Pareto-optimal* policy that recovers maximum energy **subject to driver acceptance and hardware safety**.

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Vehicle Dynamics on an Inclined Road:**
The total braking force required for deceleration $a$ on a gradient $\theta$ is:
$$F_{brake} = m \cdot a + m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta$$

For level road ($\theta = 0$): $F_{brake} = m \cdot a$.

**Step 2 — Maximum Tire Friction Force:**
The maximum force that a tire can transmit to the road without slipping is governed by the friction coefficient:
$$F_{tire,max} = \mu \cdot N$$

where $N$ is the normal reaction force on the driven wheels.

**Step 3 — Normal Force on Rear Wheels (Static Load Distribution):**
For a vehicle with static weight distribution 45:55 (front:rear), the rear axle carries $0.55 \cdot m \cdot g$ of normal force on level ground. With dynamic weight transfer during braking (deceleration $a$):
$$N_{rear} = 0.55 \cdot m \cdot g - m \cdot a \cdot \dfrac{h_{cg}}{L}$$

where $h_{cg}$ is the center-of-gravity height and $L$ is the wheelbase.

**Step 4 — Maximum Allowable Regen Torque:**
The regenerative torque at the rear wheels is $T_{reg} \cdot G / r_{wh}$, and this must not exceed the available friction force:
$$\dfrac{T_{reg} \cdot G \cdot \eta_{drivetrain}}{r_{wh}} \le \mu \cdot N_{rear}$$

Solving for the maximum regenerative torque:
$$T_{reg,max} = \dfrac{\mu \cdot r_{wh} \cdot N_{rear}}{G \cdot \eta_{drivetrain}}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Illustration:**
Given $m = 1500$ kg, $\mu = 0.7$, $r_{wh} = 0.32$ m, $G = 9$, $\eta_{drivetrain} = 0.95$, $L = 2.6$ m, $h_{cg} = 0.55$ m, and $a = 3$ m/s² (level ground):

$$N_{rear} = 0.55 \cdot 1500 \cdot 9.81 - 1500 \cdot 3 \cdot \dfrac{0.55}{2.6}$$

$$N_{rear} = 8093.25 - 952.04 = 7141.21 \text{ N}$$

$$T_{reg,max} = \dfrac{0.7 \cdot 0.32 \cdot 7141.21}{9 \cdot 0.95} = \dfrac{1599.63}{8.55} \approx 187.1 \text{ N·m}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the weight-transfer term** when the question says "deceleration is high." Many students use only the static $0.55\,mg$ and lose 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting drivetrain efficiency $\eta_{drivetrain}$** in the denominator of the torque equation.
> 3. **Confusing front and rear axle** ratios (45:55 front:rear means the *rear* carries 55%).
> 4. **Not stating the limiting assumption** that the friction coefficient is for dry asphalt (~0.7–0.9); on wet ice ($\mu \approx 0.1$) the regen torque must be drastically reduced.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 2**

- ✅ **Regenerative braking = motor as generator**, not a separate device. The same PMSM/induction motor that propels the car becomes a generator under negative torque.
- ✅ **Energy recoverable** is bounded by $\tfrac{1}{2}mv^2 \cdot \eta_{reg}$. Always compute this first in numerical problems.
- ✅ **Regen cannot operate near 0% or 100% SOC** — the AI must derate or disable regen outside the 10%–90% window.
- ✅ **AI replaces lookup tables** with learned policies (RL, neural networks, MPC). State includes SOC, temperatures, gradient, and driver intent.
- ✅ **Brake blending** is split into **series** (regen-first, friction residual) and **parallel** (both active simultaneously). Modern EVs use parallel.
- ✅ **Key constraint equations** (memorize):
  - $E_{rec} = \eta_{reg} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2$
  - $P_{reg} = T_{reg} \cdot \omega = V_{batt} \cdot I_{reg}$
  - $T_{reg,max} = \dfrac{\mu \cdot r_{wh} \cdot N_{rear}}{G \cdot \eta_{drivetrain}}$
- ✅ **Round-trip efficiency** of recovered energy is **~70%–85%** (regen charge + later discharge).
- ✅ **AI cost function** includes energy term, SOC penalty, thermal penalty, and driver-pedal-feel penalty.
- ✅ **Auxiliary loads** ($P_{HVAC}$, lighting, electronics) must be **subtracted** from the gross recovered energy to obtain net energy benefit.
- ✅ **Typical exam marks allocation:** Part A conceptual (3 marks) + Part B analytical + diagram (14 marks). Always include a labeled block diagram for full credit.
- ✅ **Units discipline:** SI units throughout — kg, m/s, J, W, N·m, V, A. **NEVER mix km/h with m/s** in the same equation without conversion.
- ✅ **Realistic numerical values to remember:**
  - EV mass: 1200–2500 kg
  - Regen peak power: 100–350 kW
  - $\eta_{reg}$: 0.6–0.75
  - Battery pack: 400V or 800V architecture
  - SOC operating window: 10%–90%
- ✅ **Examiner loves** asking "list factors limiting regen" — answer must include **SOC limits, motor speed, thermal limits, brake-pedal feel, and wheel-slip stability.**
