4.4 ODD Dimensional Decomposition ```mermaid flowchart TB ODD[ODD OPERATIONAL DESIGN DOMAIN]:::root ODD --> D1[Weather Conditions] ODD --> D2[Roadway Type] ODD --> D3[Speed Range] ODD --> D4[Geographic Region] ODD --> D5[Time of Day] ODD --> D6[Traffic Density] D1 --> S1["Clear / Rain / Snow / Fog"] D2 --> S2["Urban / Suburban / Highway"] D3 --> S3["0 to 130 km/h etc"] D4 --> S4["Geofenced Coordinates"] D5 --> S5["Day / Night / Twilight"] D6 --> S6["Free Flow to Congested"] classDef root fill:#0f3460,stroke:#e94560,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation:** The ODD is a **multi-dimensional constraint space**. Level 4 systems operate within a *subset* of this space, while Level 5 systems must operate on the *full Cartesian product* of all dimensions. The exponential growth of the ODD space ($N_{total} = \prod_i \vert D_i \vert$) is why Level 5 remains an open research challenge. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define the term "Dynamic Driving Task" (DDT) as per SAE J3016. List its sub-tasks.** **Model Answer:** The **Dynamic Driving Task (DDT)** is defined by SAE J3016 as *"all of the real-time operational and tactical functions required to operate a vehicle in on-road traffic, excluding the strategic functions such as trip scheduling and selection of destinations."* It consists of the following sub-tasks: 1. **Lateral Vehicle Motion Control** (steering control) 2. **Longitudinal Vehicle Motion Control** (acceleration and braking) 3. **Object and Event Detection and Response (OEDR)** 4. **Maneuver Planning** 5. **Enhancement of conspicuity** (lighting, signaling, horn) --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Differentiate between SAE Level 3 and Level 4 automation in terms of fallback responsibility and ODD.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Level 3 (Conditional) | Level 4 (High) | |-----------|----------------------|----------------| | **Fallback** | System issues takeover request; **human is fallback** | **System handles fallback** automatically | | **ODD** | Restricted; system requests handover at ODD exit | System performs safe-stop (MRM) at ODD exit | | **Human Role** | Fallback-ready user (must be available) | Passenger (not required) | | **Example** | Honda Legend (Japan, 2021) | Waymo One robotaxi | --- ## PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]` #### **(a) Explain in detail the SAE J3016 classification of driving automation. With suitable block diagrams, differentiate between Level 2 and Level 3 automation. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** The SAE J3016 standard classifies driving automation into **six levels** (0 to 5) based on: - Who performs the DDT (human or system) - Who performs the fallback - The Operational Design Domain (ODD) | Level | Name | DDT by System | Fallback by | ODD | |-------|------|---------------|-------------|-----| | 0 | No Automation | None (warnings only) | Human | Unlimited (driven) | | 1 | Driver Assistance | Lateral OR Longitudinal | Human | Unlimited (driven) | | 2 | Partial Automation | Both Lateral + Longitudinal | Human | Unlimited (driven) | | 3 | Conditional Automation | Full DDT | Human (takeover) | Limited ODD | | 4 | High Automation | Full DDT + Fallback | System | Limited ODD | | 5 | Full Automation | Full DDT + Fallback | System | Unlimited ODD | **Level 2 vs. Level 3 — Key Differences:** 1. **Driver Engagement:** In Level 2, the driver must **continuously monitor** the environment; in Level 3, the driver becomes a **fallback-ready user** and may divert attention. 2. **Legal Responsibility:** At Level 2, the **human is the driver**; at Level 3, the **automated system is the driver** when engaged. 3. **OEDR:** Level 2 system performs partial OEDR with human supervision; Level 3 system performs full OEDR independently. 4. **Fallback Mechanism:** Level 2 → immediate human intervention required; Level 3 → system issues a **takeover request** with a lead time (≥ 10 s). ``` Block Diagram Comparison: LEVEL 2: LEVEL 3: ┌─────────────────┐ ┌─────────────────┐ │ HUMAN (DRIVER) │ │ ADS │ │ • Monitors │ │ • Full DDT │ │ • Fallback │ │ • Full OEDR │ │ • Supervises │ │ • Issues TKR │ └────────┬────────┘ └────────┬────────┘ │ controls fallback │ TKR when ODD exits ▼ ▼ ┌─────────────────┐ ┌─────────────────┐ │ SYSTEM │ │ HUMAN │ │ • Lateral+Long │ │ (Fallback-Ready)│ │ • Partial OEDR │ │ Takes over on TKR│ └─────────────────┘ └─────────────────┘ ``` #### **(b) With a neat diagram, explain the concept of Operational Design Domain (ODD). Why is ODD critical for distinguishing Level 4 from Level 5? (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** The **Operational Design Domain (ODD)** defines the **specific operating conditions** under which an automated driving system is designed to function. It includes parameters such as: - **Weather:** Clear, rain, snow, fog - **Roadway type:** Highway, urban, rural - **Speed range:** e.g., 0–60 km/h - **Geographic region:** Geofenced coordinates - **Time of day:** Day, night - **Traffic conditions:** Free flow, congested **Diagram:** ``` ┌─────────────────── FULL OPERATIONAL SPACE ───────────────────┐ │ │ │ ┌── ODD: Urban, Day, Clear, < 60 km/h ──┐ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ █████████████████████████████████ │ │ │ │ ██ ADS OPERATING ZONE (Level 4) ██ │ │ │ │ █████████████████████████████████ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ └─────────────────────────────────────────┘ │ │ │ │ [Out-of-ODD regions require Level 5 capability] │ └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ ``` **Criticality of ODD in Distinguishing Level 4 from Level 5:** 1. **Level 4 ODD is Restricted:** A Level 4 system operates *only* within a defined subset of conditions. Outside the ODD, the system must execute a **Minimum Risk Maneuver (MRM)** — typically a safe stop. 2. **Level 5 ODD is Unlimited:** A Level 5 system must operate in *any* condition a human driver can handle, with no ODD restrictions. 3. **Practical Implication:** Current robotaxis (Waymo, Cruise) are Level 4 because they are geofenced. To achieve Level 5, the ADS must demonstrate competence across the entire Cartesian product of ODD parameters — a **combinatorially explosive** validation problem. 4. **Mathematical Formulation:** $$ \text{Level 4} \iff \kappa_{ODD} < 1 $$ $$ \text{Level 5} \iff \kappa_{ODD} = 1 $$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]` #### **(a) Explain the three primary functions used by SAE J3016 to differentiate automation levels. Discuss why the transition from Level 2 to Level 3 is considered the most critical. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** The SAE J3016 standard uses three primary functional criteria to differentiate automation levels: **1. Sustained Lateral and Longitudinal Vehicle Motion Control:** - Defines whether the system continuously controls **steering** (lateral) and **acceleration/braking** (longitudinal). - Level 0: None; Level 1: One of the two; Level 2+: Both. **2. Object and Event Detection and Response (OEDR):** - The ability of the system to detect driving-relevant objects/events and respond appropriately. - Level 1–2: Partial (human supplements); Level 3+: Full (system independent). **3. Fallback Performance of Dynamic Driving Task:** - The system's ability to handle situations where the DDT cannot be continued (e.g., system failure, ODD exit). - Level 0–2: Human; Level 3: Human (via takeover request); Level 4–5: System (via MRM). **Why Level 2 → Level 3 is the Most Critical Transition:** 1. **Legal/Regulatory Shift:** At Level 2, the human is legally the driver; at Level 3, the manufacturer/ADS assumes legal driving responsibility when engaged. This triggers massive regulatory, insurance, and liability realignments. 2. **Psychological Shift:** The driver transitions from **active operator** to **passive fallback user**, fundamentally changing human factors requirements (drowsiness detection, takeover time, etc.). 3. **Technical Shift:** The ADS must independently perform OEDR — this requires a complete sensor stack (LiDAR, radar, cameras, HD maps), AI-based perception, and predictive planning. 4. **Economic Shift:** Sensor and compute costs increase by approximately **2.5× per level** (as shown in Section 3.4), making the Level 2→3 transition a major capital investment threshold. 5. **Validation Burden:** Level 3 systems must demonstrate reliability equivalent to human drivers (~10⁻⁸ crashes/km), requiring billions of simulation miles. #### **(b) Describe the role of the human driver, the ADS, and the dispatcher/remote operator across SAE Levels 0 to 5. Use a comparative table. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** | Level | Human Driver Role | ADS Role | Dispatcher/Remote Operator Role | |-------|-------------------|----------|----------------------------------| | **0** | Performs **100% of DDT**; monitors environment | Issues warnings (FCW, LDW) or momentary intervention (AEB) | Not applicable | | **1** | Performs remaining DDT; monitors environment continuously | Sustained control in **one domain** (lateral OR longitudinal) | Not applicable | | **2** | Continuously **supervises** the system; fallback responsibility | Sustained control in **both** domains; partial OEDR | Not applicable | | **3** | **Fallback-ready user**; must respond to takeover request (≥ 10 s lead time) | **Performs full DDT** including OEDR; issues TKR on ODD exit | Not required but may assist in remote monitoring | | **4** | **Not required** as a fallback; may sit as passenger | Performs full DDT **including fallback** (MRM/safe stop) | May activate ADS, request disengagement, or tele-operate in edge cases | | **5** | **No driver concept**; occupant is passenger | Performs full DDT + fallback in **unlimited ODD** | May manage fleet logistics, destination input, or emergency override | **Key Observations:** - The **human driver's responsibility monotonically decreases** from Level 0 to Level 5. - The **ADS responsibility monotonically increases** across the same range. - The **dispatcher/remote operator** becomes relevant only at Level 4+, where they may assist with fleet operations, ODD selection, or remote guidance during system uncertainty. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:** > 1. **Common Mistake 1 (–2 marks):** Students often confuse *Driver Assistance* (Level 1) with *Partial Automation* (Level 2) because both use the word "assistance." **Correction:** Level 1 = system does *one* of lateral/longitudinal; Level 2 = system does *both*. > 2. **Common Mistake 2 (–3 marks):** Writing that "Level 3 is fully autonomous" — incorrect! Level 3 is *conditional* and still requires a human fallback. The word "conditional" must appear in any valid definition. > 3. **Common Mistake 3 (–2 marks):** Confusing ODD with OEDR. **OEDR** = Object & Event Detection Response (a *function*). **ODD** = Operational Design Domain (an *operating envelope*). These are different concepts. > 4. **Common Mistake 4 (–1 mark):** Not mentioning "fallback" when describing Level 4 and 5 — fallback is the **decisive differentiator** between Level 3 and Level 4. --- ## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER ### 🚗 Rapid Revision Checklist - **SAE J3016** is the **official international standard** for classifying driving automation (Levels 0–5). - **Three pillars of classification:** (1) Lateral + Longitudinal Control, (2) OEDR, (3) Fallback Performance. - **Level 0:** No sustained automation; warnings/momentary interventions only. - **Level 1:** Single-domain automation (steering OR speed, NOT both). - **Level 2:** Dual-domain automation (steering AND speed), but human supervises. - **Level 3:** ADS performs **full DDT**; human is fallback-ready (≥ 10 s takeover time). - **Level 4:** ADS performs full DDT **including fallback**; restricted ODD. - **Level 5:** ADS performs full DDT including fallback; **unlimited ODD**. - **Level 2 → 3 transition** = the most critical (legal, technical, economic shift). - **DDT** = Dynamic Driving Task = all real-time operational/tactical driving functions. - **OEDR** = Object and Event Detection and Response (perceptual function). - **ODD** = Operational Design Domain (operating envelope, multidimensional). - **MRM** = Minimum Risk Maneuver (safe stop) — required for Level 3+ on ODD exit. - **Sensor cost scales ~2.5× per level** (exponential growth). - **Functional Safety:** ISO 26262 ASIL levels escalate from A (Level 0–1) to D (Level 3–5). - **No commercially available Level 5 vehicle exists as of 2024.** - **Current production examples:** Tesla Autopilot (L2), Honda Legend (L3 in Japan), Waymo One (L4 in geofenced zones). - **Examiner's favorite keywords:** "fallback-ready user," "Operational Design Domain," "Minimal Risk Maneuver," "OEDR," "DDT." > [!NOTE] > **Final Study Tip:** Always describe SAE levels in terms of **WHO does WHAT** (human vs. system) and **WHEN** (which ODD). A 14-mark answer that explicitly maps each level to the three primary functions (lateral/longitudinal control, OEDR, fallback) will score maximum marks.

topics.title: Data Processing and Decision Making
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT865 - ADVANCED VEHICLE TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Data Processing and Decision Making

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Data Processing and Decision Making** in advanced vehicle technology refers to the end-to-end computational pipeline by which raw sensory, vehicular, and environmental data is acquired, conditioned, fused, and transformed into actionable control commands for vehicle subsystems. This process forms the **cognitive layer** of modern vehicles, enabling functions ranging from engine management and battery optimization to Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS) and autonomous driving.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, the data processing chain is formally decomposed into four sequential stages:

1. **Data Acquisition**: Sampling of signals from on-board sensors, controllers, and V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything) communication channels.
2. **Data Pre-processing & Conditioning**: Noise filtering, calibration, time-stamping, and normalization.
3. **Data Fusion & Feature Extraction**: Multi-sensor integration using techniques such as Kalman filtering, Bayesian inference, and neural network-based feature extraction.
4. **Decision Making & Actuation**: Application of rule-based, probabilistic, or AI-driven logic to generate final actuator commands.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**: This topic is the *integrative core* of Module 3. It links the perception layer (sensors) to the actuation layer (drive-by-wire, motor controllers, braking). Examiners frequently test the *closed-loop relationship* between data quality and decision reliability.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Driver

Imagine you are driving a car at **80 km/h** on a highway. Your **eyes, ears, and skin** act as sensors. The **visual cortex** of your brain performs *data fusion* (combining the speedometer reading, the road curvature, and the distance to the vehicle ahead). The **prefrontal cortex** then performs *decision making* (apply brakes, accelerate, or steer), and your **limbs** actuate. 

In a modern vehicle, the **ECU/MCU/domain controller** plays the role of the brain, the **sensor suite** acts as sensory organs, and the **drive-by-wire actuators** are the limbs. Data processing and decision making is essentially *automating the brain's role*.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Standard CAN Bus Data Rate**: **500 kbps** (Classical CAN) or **up to 5 Mbps** (CAN-FD).
- **Typical LiDAR Sampling Frequency**: **10 Hz to 20 Hz** with point cloud sizes in millions per second.
- **Vehicle Real-Time Control Loop Latency Budget**: **< 10 ms** for safety-critical functions.
- **ASIL-D (ISO 26262) Maximum Fault Tolerant Time Interval**: **< 100 ms**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Vocabulary**: Familiarize yourself with terms *ECU (Electronic Control Unit)*, *SoC (System on Chip)*, *ROS (Robot Operating System)*, *ISO 26262*, *ASIL (Automotive Safety Integrity Level)*, *E/E Architecture*, and *Domain Controller*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Data Processing Pipeline — Stepwise Logic

### Stage 1: Data Acquisition
- **Inputs**: Radar, LiDAR, camera, IMU, GPS, wheel speed sensors, battery management system (BMS), V2X messages.
- **Sampling Rate $f_s$** must satisfy the **Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem**:

$$f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$$

where $f_{max}$ is the highest frequency component in the physical signal. Failure to satisfy this introduces **aliasing**, which corrupts decision-making.

### Stage 2: Pre-processing
- **Noise filtering** via low-pass filters (e.g., moving average):

$$y[n] = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} x[n-k]$$

- **Outlier rejection** using Z-score thresholding.
- **Time synchronization** across heterogeneous sensors using PTP (Precision Time Protocol).

### Stage 3: Sensor Fusion
- **Kalman Filter** (linear, Gaussian) and **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** for nonlinear systems.
- **Bayesian Inference** for probabilistic reasoning under uncertainty.
- **Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN)** for camera-based object detection.

### Stage 4: Decision Making Algorithms

There are three dominant paradigms in KTU syllabus context:

| Paradigm | Principle | Use Case |
|----------|-----------|----------|
| **Rule-based** | IF-THEN-ELSE logic with threshold conditions | AEB, Lane Departure Warning |
| **Probabilistic (Bayesian)** | Posterior inference with priors | Obstacle classification, weather detection |
| **AI/Deep Learning** | End-to-end learning from labeled data | Lane keeping, traffic sign recognition |

## 2.2 Why Decision Making is Hard in Vehicles

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Uncertainties**: Vehicles operate under (1) *sensor uncertainty* (noise, occlusion), (2) *environmental uncertainty* (weather, road variability), and (3) *actuator uncertainty* (tire-road friction variability). A robust decision-making system must minimize the **expected cost of error** under all three.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation | Variables / Units | Engineering Utility |
|---------|----------|-------------------|---------------------|
| Sampling Theorem | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | $f_s$: Hz, $f_{max}$: Hz | Anti-aliasing design |
| Moving Average Filter | $y[n] = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1}x[n-k]$ | $N$: window size | Noise suppression |
| Kalman Prediction | $\hat{x}_{k\vert k-1} = A\hat{x}_{k-1\vert k-1} + Bu_{k-1}$ | $A$: state transition, $B$: control matrix | State estimation |
| Kalman Update | $K_k = P_{k\vert k-1}H^T(HP_{k\vert k-1}H^T + R)^{-1}$ | $K_k$: Kalman gain, $R$: measurement noise cov. | Sensor fusion |
| Bayes' Theorem | $P(A\vert B) = \frac{P(B\vert A)P(A)}{P(B)}$ | $P$: probability | Obstacle classification |
| Latency Budget | $T_{total} = T_{sense} + T_{process} + T_{actuate}$ | Seconds | Real-time system design |
| Mean Squared Error (Decision accuracy) | $MSE = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Dimensionless | ML model evaluation |
| Signal-to-Noise Ratio | $SNR = 10 \log_{10}\frac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}$ | Decibels (dB) | Sensor quality assessment |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Substitution Rule**: In all Kalman-related derivations, the **measurement noise covariance $R$** and **process noise covariance $Q$** are tuning parameters. If $R$ is large, the filter trusts predictions more; if $Q$ is large, it trusts measurements more. This trade-off appears in **14-mark problems** frequently.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **ADAS Systems**: Forward collision warning uses radar + camera fusion processed at 20 Hz with sub-50 ms latency.
- **Battery Management Systems (BMS)**: Cell voltage/temperature data is processed to estimate State of Charge (SoC) and State of Health (SoH).
- **Drive-by-Wire Systems**: Steering, braking, and throttle decisions are made electronically with redundant fail-operational logic.
- **Autonomous Driving (Level 3+)**: Uses high-performance SoCs (e.g., NVIDIA Orin, Qualcomm Snapdragon Ride) capable of hundreds of TOPS (Tera Operations Per Second).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: The Discrete-Time Kalman Filter

The Kalman filter is the **mathematical backbone** of automotive sensor fusion. It provides optimal state estimates for linear dynamic systems under Gaussian noise. Below is the complete derivation that KTU examiners expect.

### 3.1.1 System Model

Consider a discrete-time linear system described by:

$$x_k = A x_{k-1} + B u_{k-1} + w_{k-1}$$

The measurement equation is:

$$z_k = H x_k + v_k$$

**Where:**
- $x_k \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is the state vector at time step $k$.
- $u_{k-1} \in \mathbb{R}^m$ is the control input.
- $z_k \in \mathbb{R}^p$ is the measurement vector.
- $A$ is the state transition matrix, $B$ is the control input matrix, $H$ is the measurement matrix.
- $w_{k-1} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q)$ is the process noise with covariance $Q$.
- $v_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R)$ is the measurement noise with covariance $R$.

### 3.1.2 Prediction Step Derivation

The *a priori* state estimate is computed by propagating the previous estimate:

$$\hat{x}_{k\vert k-1} = A \hat{x}_{k-1\vert k-1} + B u_{k-1}$$

This equation says: the predicted state at time $k$ equals the system dynamics applied to the previous best estimate plus the known control input. The *a priori* error covariance is:

$$P_{k\vert k-1} = A P_{k-1\vert k-1} A^T + Q$$

This expresses that uncertainty grows due to both the propagation of previous uncertainty and the addition of process noise.

### 3.1.3 Update Step Derivation — Kalman Gain

The measurement residual (innovation) is defined as:

$$\tilde{y}_k = z_k - H \hat{x}_{k\vert k-1}$$

The Kalman gain is derived by *minimizing the a posteriori error covariance* $P_{k\vert k}$. Setting the partial derivative $\frac{\partial P_{k\vert k}}{\partial K_k} = 0$ yields:

$$K_k = P_{k\vert k-1} H^T (H P_{k\vert k-1} H^T + R)^{-1}$$

**Step-by-step logic**: $K_k$ balances the trust between the prediction and the measurement. If $R \to 0$, then $K_k \to H^{-1}$ (full trust in measurement). If $P_{k\vert k-1} \to 0$, then $K_k \to 0$ (full trust in prediction).

### 3.1.4 State and Covariance Update

The optimal *a posteriori* state estimate is:

$$\hat{x}_{k\vert k} = \hat{x}_{k\vert k-1} + K_k (z_k - H \hat{x}_{k\vert k-1})$$

The *a posteriori* error covariance is:

$$P_{k\vert k} = (I - K_k H) P_{k\vert k-1}$$

### 3.1.5 The Complete Recursive Cycle

The Kalman filter alternates between the **Predict** and **Update** stages:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Predict:}\\
&\hat{x}_{k\vert k-1} = A \hat{x}_{k-1\vert k-1} + B u_{k-1} \\
&P_{k\vert k-1} = A P_{k-1\vert k-1} A^T + Q \\
&\textbf{Update:}\\
&K_k = P_{k\vert k-1} H^T (H P_{k\vert k-1} H^T + R)^{-1} \\
&\hat{x}_{k\vert k} = \hat{x}_{k\vert k-1} + K_k (z_k - H \hat{x}_{k\vert k-1}) \\
&P_{k\vert k} = (I - K_k H) P_{k\vert k-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Bayesian Decision Making for Obstacle Classification

Suppose a vehicle's radar reports an obstacle detection event. The decision "Is the obstacle a pedestrian?" is a **binary hypothesis test**.

**Hypotheses:**
- $H_0$: Obstacle is *not* a pedestrian (background).
- $H_1$: Obstacle *is* a pedestrian.

By Bayes' theorem:

$$P(H_1 \vert z) = \frac{P(z \vert H_1) P(H_1)}{P(z)}$$

Where the **evidence** is computed using the law of total probability:

$$P(z) = P(z \vert H_0) P(H_0) + P(z \vert H_1) P(H_1)$$

The decision rule (minimum error) is:

$$
\text{Decide } H_1 \text{ if } \frac{P(z \vert H_1)}{P(z \vert H_0)} > \frac{P(H_0)}{P(H_1)} = \eta
$$

The right-hand side $\eta$ is the **decision threshold**. Modifying $\eta$ allows the engineer to tune the *precision-recall trade-off* of the classifier.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix for Vehicle Functions

| Vehicle Function | Primary Sensors | Fusion Method | Decision Algorithm | Latency Target |
|------------------|-----------------|---------------|-------------------|----------------|
| **Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC)** | Radar, Camera | Kalman Filter | PID + State Machine | **< 100 ms** |
| **Automatic Emergency Braking (AEB)** | Radar, LiDAR, Camera | EKF | Threshold + Bayes | **< 50 ms** |
| **Lane Keeping Assist (LKA)** | Camera | CNN | End-to-End Learning | **< 30 ms** |
| **Blind Spot Detection** | Ultrasonic, Radar | Weighted Average | Rule-based | **< 200 ms** |
| **Battery SoC Estimation** | Voltage, Current, Temp | EKF / UKF | Recursive Bayesian | **< 1 s** |
| **Electronic Stability Control (ESC)** | IMU, Wheel Speed | Kalman Filter | PID + Gain Scheduling | **< 10 ms** |
| **Traffic Sign Recognition** | Camera | CNN | Classification + Rule | **< 200 ms** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pattern Recognition for Exams**: Notice the inverse correlation between *safety criticality* and *latency tolerance*. AEB and ESC — the most safety-critical functions — must have the lowest latencies. This is a frequently asked 7-mark question.

## 3.4 Worked Example: Kalman Filter for Vehicle Position Estimation

**Problem Setup**: A vehicle's position is estimated using GPS and IMU. The state vector is $x_k = [p_k; v_k]^T$ (position and velocity). The system is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_k &= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & \Delta t \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} x_{k-1} + w_{k-1} \\
z_k &= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} x_k + v_k
\end{aligned}
$$

With $\Delta t = 0.1$ s, $Q = \text{diag}(0.01, 0.1)$, $R = 1.0$, and initial $P_0 = I$.

**Initial state:** $\hat{x}_{0\vert 0} = [0; 0]^T$.

**Iteration 1 — Prediction:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{x}_{1\vert 0} &= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0.1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} \\
P_{1\vert 0} &= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0.1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0.1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.01 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.1 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} 1.11 & 0.1 \\ 0.1 & 1.1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration 1 — Update (with $z_1 = 0.5$ m):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_1 &= H P_{1\vert 0} H^T + R = 1.11 + 1 = 2.11 \\
K_1 &= P_{1\vert 0} H^T S_1^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.11 \\ 0.1 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \frac{1}{2.11} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.526 \\ 0.0474 \end{bmatrix} \\
\hat{x}_{1\vert 1} &= \hat{x}_{1\vert 0} + K_1 (z_1 - H \hat{x}_{1\vert 0}) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.526 \\ 0.0474 \end{bmatrix} \cdot 0.5 \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} 0.263 \\ 0.0237 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

This iterative cycle continues, converging the position estimate toward the true value while the velocity estimate stabilizes as more measurements arrive.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Vehicle Data Processing & Decision-Making Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Physical Environment] --> B[Sensor Layer]
    B --> B1[Radar]
    B --> B2[LiDAR]
    B --> B3[Camera]
    B --> B4[IMU and GPS]
    B --> B5[CAN Bus and Vehicle Network]
    B --> C[Data Acquisition Unit DAQ]
    C --> D[Pre-processing Engine]
    D --> D1[Noise Filtering]
    D --> D2[Time Synchronization PTP]
    D --> D3[Calibration and Normalization]
    D3 --> E[Sensor Fusion Layer]
    E --> E1[Kalman Filter]
    E --> E2[Bayesian Inference]
    E --> E3[Deep CNN Feature Extractor]
    E3 --> F[Decision Making Layer]
    F --> F1[Rule Based Logic]
    F --> F2[Probabilistic Reasoning]
    F --> F3[AI Decision Network]
    F3 --> G[Actuation Layer]
    G --> G1[Drive by Wire Steering]
    G --> G2[Brake-by-Wire]
    G --> G3[Motor Controller for EV]
    G --> G4[HMI and Display]
    G4 --> H[Vehicle Dynamics Response]
    H -. feedback .-> B
```

## 4.2 Decision-Making State Machine for Adaptive Cruise Control

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> ACC_Off
    ACC_Off --> ACC_Standby : Driver Activates
    ACC_Standby --> Target_Lock : Lead Vehicle Detected
    Target_Lock --> Following : Distance Greater Than Set Gap
    Following --> Closing : Lead Vehicle Slows
    Closing --> Emergency_Brake : TTC Less Than Threshold
    Emergency_Brake --> Following : Safe Distance Restored
    Closing --> Following : Gap Restored
    Following --> ACC_Standby : Lead Vehicle Lost
    Emergency_Brake --> Driver_Override : Brake Pedal Pressed
    Driver_Override --> ACC_Off : System Disengaged
```

## 4.3 Sensor Fusion Data Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Perception_Layer [Perception Layer]
        S1[Radar Data] --> F1
        S2[Camera Images] --> F2
        S3[LiDAR Point Cloud] --> F3
        S4[GPS and IMU] --> F4
    end
    F1 --> K[Kalman Filter Core]
    F2 --> C[CNN Object Detector]
    F3 --> C
    C --> M1[Object List Tracker]
    F4 --> K
    K --> M1
    M1 --> DEC[Central Decision Engine]
    DEC --> ACT[Actuator Commands]
    ACT --> FB[Feedback to Sensors]
    FB -.-> S1
    FB -.-> S2
    FB -.-> S3
    FB -.-> S4
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture: ISO 26262 Compliant Decision Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Input Stage] --> B[ASIL-D Compliant Sensor Input]
    B --> C[Redundant Microcontroller Channel A]
    B --> D[Redundant Microcontroller Channel B]
    C --> E[Cross Comparison Checker]
    D --> E
    E --> F{Voting Logic}
    F --> G[Safe State if Disagreement]
    F --> H[Proceed to Actuation]
    H --> I[Drive by Wire Command]
    I --> J[Vehicle Response]
    J --> K[Feedback Loop]
    K --> A
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Block Diagram Reading for KTU**: In 14-mark drawing questions, examiners typically expect (1) a clearly labeled input stage, (2) a fusion stage with *named algorithms*, (3) a decision stage with *threshold or rule logic*, and (4) an actuation stage with *feedback*. Always include arrows for data flow direction.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *Define sensor fusion in the context of advanced vehicle technology. List any two sensor fusion techniques.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks)**: Sensor fusion is the process of combining data from multiple sensors to produce a more accurate, reliable, and complete estimate of the vehicle's state and its environment than any single sensor could provide alone. It mitigates individual sensor limitations such as noise, occlusion, and limited field of view.
- **Two Techniques (1 mark)**: (1) Kalman Filter (for linear Gaussian systems), (2) Bayesian Inference (for probabilistic multi-hypothesis reasoning). Other valid answers: Extended Kalman Filter, Particle Filter, Convolutional Neural Network fusion.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *What is the Nyquist sampling theorem, and why is it important for vehicle data acquisition systems?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Statement (1.5 marks)**: The Nyquist-Shannon sampling theorem states that to reconstruct a band-limited signal without aliasing, the sampling frequency $f_s$ must be at least twice the highest frequency $f_{max}$ present in the signal: $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$.
- **Importance (1.5 marks)**: Vehicle sensors (e.g., wheel speed at high RPM, vibration sensors) can contain high-frequency components. Failure to sample adequately leads to aliasing, which produces *false* low-frequency signals that can cause erroneous safety decisions. For example, undersampled wheel speed data could mislead the ABS controller.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]** 

(a) *Explain the architecture of a typical vehicle data processing and decision-making system with a neat block diagram. Describe the function of each block in detail.* **(7 marks)**

(b) *Derive the complete set of equations for the discrete-time Kalman filter used in vehicle state estimation. Clearly explain the prediction and update steps.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Architecture (7 marks):**
The architecture consists of four interconnected layers:

1. **Sensor Layer (1.5 marks)**: Comprises radar, LiDAR, camera, IMU, GPS, and wheel speed sensors. Each provides complementary information. Radar measures relative velocity; LiDAR gives 3D geometry; camera provides semantic understanding; IMU tracks orientation.

2. **Data Acquisition and Pre-processing (1.5 marks)**: Samples at rates satisfying Nyquist criterion, performs analog-to-digital conversion, removes noise via filters, and synchronizes timestamps across sensors using PTP.

3. **Sensor Fusion and Feature Extraction Layer (2 marks)**: Combines sensor streams using algorithms like Kalman filter, EKF, and CNN-based object detectors. Output is a unified representation of the vehicle's environment (e.g., tracked object list with position, velocity, and class).

4. **Decision Making and Actuation Layer (2 marks)**: Uses rule-based, probabilistic, or AI-driven logic to compute control commands for steering, braking, and throttle actuators. A feedback loop re-monitors the system's response.

*(Insert the architecture diagram from Section 4.1 here for full marks.)*

**Part (b) — Kalman Filter Derivation (7 marks):**

System model:
$$x_k = A x_{k-1} + B u_{k-1} + w_{k-1}, \quad w_{k-1} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q)$$
$$z_k = H x_k + v_k, \quad v_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R)$$

**Prediction Step (3 marks):**
The a priori state estimate:
$$\hat{x}_{k\vert k-1} = A \hat{x}_{k-1\vert k-1} + B u_{k-1}$$
The a priori error covariance:
$$P_{k\vert k-1} = A P_{k-1\vert k-1} A^T + Q$$

**Update Step (3 marks):**
The Kalman gain is derived by minimizing the trace of $P_{k\vert k}$:
$$K_k = P_{k\vert k-1} H^T (H P_{k\vert k-1} H^T + R)^{-1}$$
Updated state:
$$\hat{x}_{k\vert k} = \hat{x}_{k\vert k-1} + K_k (z_k - H \hat{x}_{k\vert k-1})$$
Updated covariance:
$$P_{k\vert k} = (I - K_k H) P_{k\vert k-1}$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The Kalman filter recursively fuses predictions and measurements to produce optimal state estimates under Gaussian noise assumptions. For non-linear vehicle dynamics, the Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) or Unscented Kalman Filter (UKF) is used.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

(a) *With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) system as a data processing and decision-making application. Discuss the role of sensor fusion.* **(7 marks)**

(b) *Explain Bayesian decision making for obstacle classification in autonomous vehicles. Define the likelihood ratio test and explain how the decision threshold affects system behavior.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — ACC System (7 marks):**

1. **System Overview (2 marks)**: ACC is a Level 2 ADAS feature that automatically adjusts vehicle speed to maintain a safe distance from a leading vehicle. The driver sets a desired speed and a desired time-gap.

2. **Sensor Suite (1.5 marks)**: 
   - **Long-range radar** (77 GHz) measures the relative distance and velocity of the lead vehicle.
   - **Forward camera** classifies the lead vehicle (e.g., distinguishes a car from a truck).
   - **Wheel speed sensors** provide ego-vehicle speed feedback.

3. **Sensor Fusion and Decision Logic (2 marks)**: Radar and camera outputs are fused using a Kalman filter to produce a robust estimate of the lead vehicle's relative position and velocity. The decision engine computes **Time-To-Collision (TTC)**:

   $$TTC = \frac{d_{rel}}{v_{rel}}$$

   where $d_{rel}$ is relative distance and $v_{rel}$ is relative velocity. If TTC falls below threshold (typically 2-3 seconds), the system reduces throttle or applies mild braking via a longitudinal controller (usually PID).

4. **State Transition (1.5 marks)**: The system has discrete states — *Standby, Following, Closing, Emergency* — managed by a state machine (see Section 4.2). Transitions depend on fused sensor inputs.

**Part (b) — Bayesian Decision Making (7 marks):**

1. **Problem Formulation (2 marks)**: For each detected object, we test two hypotheses: $H_0$ (not a pedestrian) and $H_1$ (pedestrian). Using Bayes' theorem, the posterior probability is:
   $$P(H_1 \vert z) = \frac{P(z \vert H_1) P(H_1)}{P(z \vert H_0) P(H_0) + P(z \vert H_1) P(H_1)}$$

2. **Likelihood Ratio Test (3 marks)**: Define the likelihood ratio:
   $$\Lambda(z) = \frac{P(z \vert H_1)}{P(z \vert H_0)}$$
   The decision rule becomes:
   $$\text{Decide } H_1 \text{ if } \Lambda(z) > \eta = \frac{P(H_0)}{P(H_1)}$$
   This is the **Neyman-Pearson criterion** for minimum error probability.

3. **Threshold Effects (2 marks)**: 
   - A *high* threshold $\eta$ reduces false positives (fewer incorrect pedestrian alarms) but may miss real pedestrians (low recall).
   - A *low* threshold catches more pedestrians (high recall) but increases false alarms.
   - Engineers tune $\eta$ to balance precision and recall based on ISO 26262 risk assessment.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Confusing prior with posterior**: $P(H_1)$ is the *prior* (before seeing data); $P(H_1 \vert z)$ is the *posterior* (after). Examiners deduct 1 mark for this confusion.
> 2. **Forgetting units in latency budget**: The total latency equation $T_{total} = T_{sense} + T_{process} + T_{actuate}$ must specify units (seconds or milliseconds). Always include units.
> 3. **Skipping the matrix inversion in Kalman gain**: The term $(HP_{k\vert k-1}H^T + R)^{-1}$ is often mistyped or omitted. This is a 2-mark deduction hotspot.
> 4. **Not defining matrices in Kalman derivation**: $A, B, H, Q, R$ must be defined explicitly. Examiners deduct up to 1.5 marks for undefined symbols.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024:**

- [ ] **Data Processing Pipeline**: 4 stages — *Acquisition → Pre-processing → Fusion → Decision*.
- [ ] **Nyquist Criterion**: $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ — anti-aliasing is non-negotiable in safety systems.
- [ ] **Kalman Filter**: Recursive predictor-corrector. Tuning $Q$ and $R$ balances prediction vs. measurement trust.
- [ ] **Bayesian Decision**: Posterior = (Likelihood × Prior) / Evidence. Tune threshold $\eta$ for precision-recall trade-off.
- [ ] **Latency Budget**: $T_{total} = T_{sense} + T_{process} + T_{actuate} < 10$ ms for ASIL-D functions.
- [ ] **Decision Algorithms**: Rule-based, Probabilistic (Bayes), AI/Deep Learning.
- [ ] **Sensor Fusion Techniques**: Kalman, EKF, UKF, Particle Filter, CNN.
- [ ] **Functional Safety**: ISO 26262, ASIL classification (A to D), redundancy via dual-channel architectures.
- [ ] **Real-Time Constraints**: Hard real-time = deadline violation causes system failure (e.g., AEB).
- [ ] **Vehicle Communication**: CAN (500 kbps), CAN-FD (5 Mbps), Automotive Ethernet (1 Gbps), FlexRay.
- [ ] **AI in Vehicles**: End-to-end learning works for perception but is hard to certify for safety. Hybrid AI+rule-based systems dominate current production.
- [ ] **Actuation**: Drive-by-wire systems (steering, brake, throttle) require *redundant* and *fail-operational* design.
- [ ] **Higher-Level Concepts to remember for 14-mark questions**: 
  - The relationship between sensor noise covariance $R$ and Kalman gain $K_k$.
  - The structure of an ACC system as a concrete example of the data processing pipeline.
  - Why decision-making latency is critical in safety-critical functions.
  - The trade-off between false positives and false negatives in Bayesian classification.
- [ ] **Common numerical units to memorize**: ms (latency), Hz (sampling), dB (SNR), TOPS (AI compute), Mbps (bus speed).

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Data Processing and Decision Making**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Software and Hardware Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT865 - ADVANCED VEHICLE TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Software and Hardware Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Automotive Software and Hardware Architecture** refers to the structured, layered framework of electronic control units (ECUs), sensors, actuators, communication buses, and the embedded software stack (operating system, middleware, and application algorithms) that collectively sense vehicle state, execute control logic, and actuate mechanical subsystems in modern road vehicles.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** context, this topic is treated under *PEAUT865 – Advanced Vehicle Technology* and covers the **Electrical/Electronic (E/E) architecture** that underpins Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS), electrified powertrains, connected-car features, and autonomous driving functions.

### Key Subsystems

| Subsystem | Hardware Element | Software Element |
|---|---|---|
| Sensing | LiDAR, Radar, Camera, IMU, Wheel-speed sensor | Sensor-fusion algorithms, Kalman filters |
| Processing | ECU, MCU, SoC, GPU, FPGA | Real-time OS (RTOS), AUTOSAR BSW |
| Actuation | Solenoids, Motors, Injectors, Brake-by-wire | Closed-loop control code (PID, MPC) |
| Communication | CAN, LIN, FlexRay, Automotive Ethernet | Network management, diagnostics (UDS) |
| HMI | Display, HUD, Speakers | UI frameworks, voice recognition |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a modern car as a human body.**
> - The **Hardware Architecture** is the *nervous system + brain*: ECUs are the "mini-brains," sensors are the "eyes and ears," and the wiring harness + bus (CAN/Ethernet) is the "spinal cord" carrying signals.
> - The **Software Architecture** is the *thought process*: the rules your brain follows to decide "if the brake pedal is pressed, then apply hydraulic pressure to the front disc brakes within 50 ms."
>
> Just as a reflex arc bypasses conscious thought (real-time, hard-deadline control), a vehicle's **hard real-time ECUs** must respond within strict time bounds, whereas the infotainment system is more like daydreaming — soft real-time, less critical.

### Why this matters in KTU 2024

- **Functional Safety (ISO 26262)** assigns *Automotive Safety Integrity Levels* (ASIL A–D) based on the risk of software/hardware failure.
- A **typical modern vehicle contains 70–150 ECUs** running over **100 million lines of code** (compared to ~10 million in a Boeing 787).
- Standardised architecture (**AUTOSAR**) reduces development cost and enables **hardware-independent software** portability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard metric to remember:** Bit-rate of **CAN = 500 kbps** (Classical CAN) or **2 Mbps** (CAN-FD); **Automotive Ethernet = 100 Mbps – 1 Gbps**; **FlexRay = 10 Mbps** with deterministic time-triggered scheduling.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Vehicle E/E (Electrical/Electronic) Hardware Architecture

### 2.1.1 Evolution of Architectures

- **Distributed Architecture (Legacy):** Each function has its own dedicated ECU (e.g., one ECU for ABS, another for airbag). Result: 80+ ECUs, kilometres of wiring, high mass (~50 kg harness).
- **Domain-Centralised Architecture:** ECUs grouped into *domains* — Powertrain, Chassis, Body, ADAS, Infotainment. Each domain has a **Domain Controller (DCU)**.
- **Zonal Architecture (Modern/Tesla-style):** Physical *zones* (front-left, front-right, rear, central) replace functional domains. High-performance central compute (HPC) running Linux/QNX.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners often ask to *compare* these three architectures. The trend in 2024+ is clearly **zonal + central compute**.

### 2.1.2 Core Hardware Building Blocks

1. **Electronic Control Unit (ECU)** — A microcontroller-based module with:
   - **MCU/SoC** (e.g., Infineon Aurix TC3xx, NXP S32K, Renesas RH850)
   - **Memory**: Flash (program), SRAM (runtime), EEPROM (calibration)
   - **Power supply**: typically 12 V (ICE) or 12 V + 48 V (mild hybrid)
   - **Communication transceivers** (CAN, LIN, FlexRay, Ethernet PHY)

2. **Sensors (Input layer)**
   - *Position/Speed:* Hall-effect, resolver, wheel-speed (variable reluctance)
   - *Inertial:* 6-axis IMU (3-axis accel + 3-axis gyro), used in ESP/ESC
   - *Environment:* Camera (mono/stereo), Radar (24/77 GHz), LiDAR, Ultrasonic
   - *Vehicle-state:* Steering-angle, pedal-position, yaw-rate, yaw-angle

3. **Actuators (Output layer)**
   - *Hydraulic:* Brake calipers, ABS modulator (active pressure)
   - *Electric:* EPS motor, throttle body, e-machine (EV), e-compressor
   - *Thermal:* PTC heater, coolant pump

4. **Communication Buses**

| Bus | Speed | Topology | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **LIN** | 20 kbps | Single-wire, master-slave | Window lift, mirror, seat |
| **CAN 2.0** | 500 kbps | Differential 2-wire, CSMA/CR | Powertrain, Body |
| **CAN-FD** | 2 Mbps | Same as CAN | Modern gateways |
| **FlexRay** | 10 Mbps | Dual-channel, TDMA | Chassis, x-by-wire |
| **Automotive Ethernet (100BASE-T1)** | 100 Mbps | Single-pair unshielded | ADAS, Diagnostics |
| **1000BASE-T1** | 1 Gbps | Single-pair | Central compute backbone |

## 2.2 Software Architecture — AUTOSAR Framework

**AUTOSAR (AUTomotive Open System ARchitecture)** is the global standard for automotive software, jointly developed by OEMs and Tier-1 suppliers.

### 2.2.1 Layered Architecture (from top to bottom)

1. **Application Software Layer (ASW):** Vehicle functions, written as *Software Components (SWC)* communicating via *Sender-Receiver* or *Client-Server* ports.
2. **Runtime Environment (RTE):** The "middleware" — abstracts the underlying hardware from the application; routes signals between SWCs and the Basic Software.
3. **Basic Software (BSW):** Includes:
   - **Services Layer:** OS, Diagnostics (DCM/DEM), Communication, Memory
   - **ECU Abstraction Layer (ECUAL):** Drivers for μC peripherals (ADC, PWM, GPIO)
   - **Microcontroller Abstraction Layer (MCAL):** Lowest level — direct register access
4. **Hardware:** The actual MCU, sensors, actuators.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Classic Platform AUTOSAR (CP)** is for hard real-time, deeply embedded ECUs.
> **Adaptive Platform AUTOSAR (AP)** runs on POSIX-OS (e.g., AUTOSAR Adaptive on QNX, Linux) for high-performance processors (ADAS, autonomous driving).

### 2.2.2 Real-Time Operating System (RTOS)

Automotive RTOS uses **preemptive, priority-based scheduling** with bounded interrupt latency.

- **OSEK/VDX OS** — historical European standard, predecessor of AUTOSAR OS.
- **FreeRTOS / SafeRTOS / QNX Neutrino** — common in ADAS domain controllers.
- **Scheduling:** Rate-Monotonic (RMS) or Earliest-Deadline-First (EDF) for hard real-time tasks.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` instead of `|` to keep the markdown table valid. The cheat sheet below is the *one-page* summary a KTU student should commit to memory for this module.

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | CAN Frame max payload (Classical) | $N_{bytes} = 8$ | Bytes | 11/29-bit ID, CRC-15 |
| 2 | CAN-FD payload | $N_{bytes} \le 64$ | Bytes | Higher bit-rate in data phase |
| 3 | Bus utilisation (efficiency) | $U = \dfrac{T_{tx}}{T_{cycle}} = \dfrac{N_{bits}}{R_{bus}\,T_{cycle}}$ | dimensionless | $\le 0.7$ for CAN stability |
| 4 | Effective data rate | $R_{eff} = \dfrac{P\,f}{N_{bits}}$ | bps | $P$=payload bytes, $f$=frame/s |
| 5 | Worst-case latency (CAN) | $L_{max} = (N_{nodes} \cdot t_{bit}) + J$ | s | $J$ = jitter |
| 6 | End-to-end delay (multi-hop) | $D_{e2e} = \sum_{i} (T_{queue,i} + T_{proc,i} + T_{tx,i})$ | s | Used in timing analysis |
| 7 | Utilisation bound (Liu & Layland, RMS, n tasks) | $U \le n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ | — | Hard real-time schedulability |
| 8 | Wheel speed (sensor) | $v = 2\pi r\, f_{wheel}$ | m/s | $r$ = tyre radius |
| 9 | Vehicle deceleration demand | $a = \dfrac{v_f^2 - v_i^2}{2d}$ | m/s² | $d$ = stopping distance |
| 10 | Radar range (FMCW) | $R = \dfrac{c\,\Delta f}{2\,(df/dt)}$ | m | Beat-frequency method |
| 11 | LiDAR scan time | $t_{scan} = \dfrac{\theta_{FOV}}{\omega_{scan}}$ | s | $\omega_{scan}$ in rad/s |
| 12 | Bus arbitration (CAN, lower ID wins) | $ID_{winner} = \min(ID_1, ID_2, \dots, ID_n)$ | — | Non-destructive CSMA/CR |
| 13 | Memory size (Kalman filter) | $M = O(n^2)$ for state $n$ | bytes | For sensor fusion |
| 14 | ASIL risk classification | $ASIL = f(S, E, C)$ | A–D | Severity, Exposure, Controllability |
| 15 | CPU load budget (ECU) | $U_{CPU} \le 0.7$ (rule of thumb) | — | Leaves headroom for interrupts |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Latency bound (Row 5)** is critical in **brake-by-wire**: ISO 26262 ASIL-D demands $L_{max} \le 10$ ms from pedal sensor to brake actuator.
- **Bus utilisation (Row 3)** drives the choice between CAN and Ethernet: >70 % utilisation signals the need to upgrade the bus.
- **Scheduability test (Row 7)** is the foundation of AUTOSAR OS task design and a frequent KTU question.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every algebraic step is shown explicitly. No step is skipped. Each derivation is followed by a short *Conversion Logic* row in plain English.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — CAN Bus Maximum Frame Rate

### Problem
A CAN 2.0B bus runs at $R_{bus} = 500\text{ kbps}$. A single frame carries $P = 8$ bytes of data. Compute the *maximum number of frames per second* $f_{max}$ and the *effective throughput*.

### Step-by-step

**Step 1 — Identify frame structure on the wire**

A Classical CAN 2.0B frame has, in order: SOF (1 bit), Arbitration (12 bits for base + 1 IDE + 1 r0 = 29-bit extended), Control (6 bits: DLC etc.), Data ($8 \times 8 = 64$ bits), CRC (15 bits), ACK (2 bits), EOF (7 bits), IFS (3 bits).

$$
N_{overhead} = 1 + 32 + 6 + 15 + 2 + 7 + 3 = 66 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 2 — Data field size in bits**

$$
N_{data} = P \times 8 = 8 \times 8 = 64 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 3 — Total frame length on the wire**

$$
N_{total} = N_{overhead} + N_{data} = 66 + 64 = 130 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 4 — Time to transmit one frame**

$$
t_{frame} = \dfrac{N_{total}}{R_{bus}} = \dfrac{130}{500{,}000} = 2.6 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{s} = 260\ \mu\text{s}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** We divide total bits by the bit rate to get the wall-clock time per frame, because bit rate is "bits per second."

**Step 5 — Maximum frame rate (back-to-back, no inter-frame gap)**

$$
f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{t_{frame}} = \dfrac{1}{260 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 3846\ \text{frames/s}
$$

**Step 6 — Effective throughput (only the data bits carry payload)**

$$
R_{eff} = f_{max} \times N_{data} = 3846 \times 64 \approx 246{,}154\ \text{bps} \approx 246\ \text{kbps}
$$

**Step 7 — Bus efficiency**

$$
\eta_{bus} = \dfrac{R_{eff}}{R_{bus}} = \dfrac{246{,}154}{500{,}000} \approx 0.492 = 49.2\ \%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Even at maximum theoretical frame rate, only ~49 % of Classical CAN bandwidth carries application data. The rest is overhead. This is why CAN-FD and Ethernet are increasingly adopted.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Rate-Monotonic Schedulability (Liu & Layland)

### Problem
An ECU runs $n = 3$ periodic tasks. Compute the upper-bound utilisation for hard real-time schedulability under Rate-Monotonic Scheduling (RMS).

### Step-by-step

**Step 1 — General Liu–Layland bound**

$$
U_{bound}(n) = n\,\bigl(2^{1/n} - 1\bigr)
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $n = 3$**

$$
U_{bound}(3) = 3\,\bigl(2^{1/3} - 1\bigr)
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate $2^{1/3}$**

$$
2^{1/3} = \sqrt[3]{2} \approx 1.2599
$$

**Step 4 — Subtract 1**

$$
1.2599 - 1 = 0.2599
$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by $n$**

$$
U_{bound}(3) = 3 \times 0.2599 \approx 0.7798
$$

**Conversion Logic:** A utilisation below 0.7798 is *guaranteed* schedulable; above this, it *may* still be schedulable (Liu–Layland gives a sufficient, not necessary, condition). KTU students should remember the *asymptotic* limit: $U_{bound}(\infty) = \ln 2 \approx 0.693$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — End-to-End Latency on a Multi-Hop Gateway

### Problem
A signal traverses: Sensor → ECU-1 (CAN) → Gateway → ECU-2 (CAN-FD) → Actuator. Given:
- $T_{queue} = 0.4$ ms, $0.3$ ms, $0.5$ ms at the three hops
- $T_{proc} = 0.2$ ms, $0.15$ ms, $0.25$ ms
- $T_{tx} = 0.6$ ms, $0.4$ ms, $0.5$ ms

Compute the total end-to-end delay and check against an ASIL-D hard deadline of $D = 3$ ms.

### Step-by-step

**Step 1 — Sum queuing delays**

$$
T_{Q} = 0.4 + 0.3 + 0.5 = 1.2\ \text{ms}
$$

**Step 2 — Sum processing delays**

$$
T_{P} = 0.2 + 0.15 + 0.25 = 0.6\ \text{ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Sum transmission delays**

$$
T_{X} = 0.6 + 0.4 + 0.5 = 1.5\ \text{ms}
$$

**Step 4 — Total end-to-end delay**

$$
D_{e2e} = T_{Q} + T_{P} + T_{X} = 1.2 + 0.6 + 1.5 = 3.3\ \text{ms}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare with deadline**

$$
D_{e2e} = 3.3\ \text{ms} \;\gt\; D = 3.0\ \text{ms}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** The total *exceeds* the ASIL-D deadline by 0.3 ms — a timing violation. The engineer must either reduce $T_{queue}$ (raise priority), use CAN-FD on hop 1, or move the gateway function to the actuator ECU.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Trade-off Summary for KTU Exam

| Architecture | # of ECUs | Harness Mass | Cost | Scalability | Real-Time | KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Distributed | High (80–150) | High (~50 kg) | Low initial, high integration | Poor | Excellent | "List 2 disadvantages" |
| Domain-Centralised | Medium (5–10 DCs) | Medium | Balanced | Good | Good | "Compare with zonal" |
| Zonal + Central Compute | Low (1–4 HPCs) | Low (~15 kg) | High initial | Excellent | Excellent (with HPC) | "Future of E/E" |
| AUTOSAR Adaptive | — | — | High | Excellent | Soft-hard hybrid | "Software for ADAS" |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Modern Zonal Vehicle E/E Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Z1[Zone Controller Front Left]:::zone
    Z2[Zone Controller Front Right]:::zone
    Z3[Zone Controller Rear]:::zone
    Z4[Zone Controller Central]:::zone
    HPC[Central High Performance Compute SoC]:::hpc
    CLOUD[Cloud OTA Backend]:::cloud
    S1[Front Camera]:::sens
    S2[Front Radar 77 GHz]:::sens
    S3[Side Radar]:::sens
    S4[Ultrasonic Park Sensors]:::sens
    A1[Steer by Wire Actuator]:::act
    A2[Brake by Wire Actuator]:::act
    A3[Drive Motor Inverter]:::act
    A4[Body Lights and Mirrors]:::act
    ETH1[Automotive Ethernet 1 Gbps Backbone]:::bus

    S1 --> Z1
    S2 --> Z1
    S3 --> Z2
    S4 --> Z1
    Z1 -- CAN FD or Ethernet --> A1
    Z1 -- CAN FD --> A2
    A3 --> Z4
    A4 --> Z3
    Z1 --- ETH1
    Z2 --- ETH1
    Z3 --- ETH1
    Z4 --- ETH1
    ETH1 --> HPC
    HPC <-- 5G or WiFi --> CLOUD

    classDef zone fill:#ffd166,stroke:#222,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef hpc fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#222,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef cloud fill:#118ab2,stroke:#222,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff
    classDef sens fill:#ef476f,stroke:#222,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff
    classDef act fill:#8338ec,stroke:#222,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff
    classDef bus fill:#073b4c,stroke:#222,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- Coloured boxes map to the four roles defined in Section 1 (zone, hpc, sens, act, cloud, bus).
- The **1 Gbps Automotive Ethernet backbone** replaces dozens of point-to-point CAN wires.
- **Zonal controllers** are physically distributed; logic is centralised in HPC.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — AUTOSAR Layered Software Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ASW[Application Software Layer SWC1 SWC2 SWC3]:::asw
    RTE[Runtime Environment RTE]:::rte
    SVC[Services Layer OS COM DEM MEM]:::svc
    ECUAL[ECU Abstraction Layer Drivers ADC PWM CAN]:::ecu
    MCAL[Microcontroller Abstraction Layer MCAL]:::mcal
    HW[Hardware MCU Sensors Actuators]:::hw

    ASW --> RTE
    RTE --> SVC
    SVC --> ECUAL
    ECUAL --> MCAL
    MCAL --> HW

    classDef asw fill:#ffe66d,stroke:#222,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef rte fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#222,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff
    classDef svc fill:#4ecdc4,stroke:#222,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef ecu fill:#1a535c,stroke:#222,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff
    classDef mcal fill:#f7b801,stroke:#222,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef hw fill:#2d3047,stroke:#222,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff
```

**Reading the diagram:** A top-down flow — the application talks to the hardware only through well-defined RTE ports. The **RTE** is the only layer where a *Software Component* directly interacts with the lower software. Portability across hardware is achieved because the *MCAL* is the only hardware-specific layer.

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — V-Model of Automotive Software Development

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    REQ[Requirements Analysis] --> SYS[System Design]
    SYS --> SW[Software Design]
    SW --> IMPL[Code Implementation]
    IMPL --> SWUNIT[Software Unit Test]
    SWUNIT --> SWINT[Software Integration Test]
    SWINT --> SYSINT[System Integration Test]
    SYSINT --> ACC[Vehicle Acceptance Test]
    ACC --> VEH[In Vehicle Validation]
    VEH --> HWUNIT[Hardware Unit Test]
    HWUNIT --> HWINT[Hardware Integration Test]
    HWINT --> SYS
    HWINT --> SYSINT

    REQ -.-> ACC
    SW -.-> SWINT
    SWUNIT -.-> IMPL
    HWUNIT -.-> IMPL

    classDef left fill:#caf0f8,stroke:#222,color:#000
    classDef right fill:#ffd6a5,stroke:#222,color:#000
    classDef test fill:#bdb2ff,stroke:#222,color:#000
    class REQ,SYS,SW,IMPL left
    class ACC,VEH,HWUNIT,HWINT right
    class SWUNIT,SWINT,SYSINT test
```

**Reading the diagram:** The left leg is *decomposition* (high-level → code); the right leg is *integration & validation* (low-level → full-vehicle). The horizontal dotted lines are the **verification–validation links** required by ISO 26262.

---
