# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions are modelled on the **KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Evaluation (ESE)** pattern: Part A (2 × 3 = 6 marks) and Part B (1 × 14 marks with internal choice).

---

## Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**List and briefly explain the three dominant in-vehicle bus protocols used in modern passenger cars, with one typical application of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **CAN (Controller Area Network)** — Differential 2-wire bus, speed up to **500 kbps** (Classical) or **2 Mbps** (CAN-FD). Used for **powertrain and body control** (engine ECU, ABS, instrument cluster). Non-destructive CSMA/CR arbitration. (1 mark)
2. **LIN (Local Interconnect Network)** — Single-wire, master-slave, **20 kbps**. Used for **low-speed body electronics** like window lift, seat adjustment, mirror fold. Cost-optimised sub-bus of CAN. (1 mark)
3. **FlexRay** — Dual-channel, **10 Mbps**, time-triggered TDMA. Used for **chassis and x-by-wire** (steer-by-wire, brake-by-wire) where deterministic latency is mandatory. (1 mark)

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is AUTOSAR? List its three main software layers in correct order (top to bottom) and state the function of the RTE.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **AUTOSAR (AUTomotive Open System ARchitecture)** is an open, standardised software architecture for automotive ECUs that enables hardware-independent application development. (1 mark)
- **Three main layers (top → bottom):** *Application Software Layer (ASW)* → *Runtime Environment (RTE)* → *Basic Software (BSW)*. (1 mark)
- **RTE function:** The RTE is the central middleware that **virtualises inter-ECU communication** and **abstracts the BSW**, allowing software components to exchange data via standardised ports without knowing whether the receiver is on the same ECU or another. (1 mark)

---

## Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **hardware architecture of a typical modern electric vehicle (EV)**. Identify the major ECUs and the communication buses connecting them.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the **AUTOSAR Classic Platform layered architecture** with a diagram. Discuss how this architecture supports **hardware-independent software** development.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — EV Hardware Architecture (7 marks)**

> [!NOTE]
> Block diagram (1 mark), ECU list (3 marks), bus choice (2 marks), justification (1 mark)

**Block diagram (representative):**

```
[Battery Management BMS]   [Motor Control MCU]   [VCU]
            |                    |                |
            +------- CAN-FD -----+---- CAN-FD ---+
                                    |
                          [Gateway ECU]
                                    |
                          [Central HMI ECU]
                                    |
                            Ethernet 100BASE-T1
                                    |
                  [ADAS Domain Controller (HPC)]
                                    |
                Camera + Radar + LiDAR (sensor cluster)
```

**Major ECUs (3 marks):**

1. **VCU (Vehicle Control Unit)** — Master controller, torque management, mode selection.
2. **BMS (Battery Management System)** — Cell voltage/temperature monitoring, SoC/SoH estimation, balancing.
3. **MCU (Motor Control Unit)** — Three-phase inverter driving the e-machine, field-oriented control (FOC).
4. **Gateway ECU** — Routes CAN ↔ CAN-FD ↔ Ethernet, performs firewall, diagnostics.
5. **ADAS HPC** — Runs perception, planning, sensor-fusion on a multi-core SoC.
6. **HMI ECU** — Infotainment, cluster, OTA gateway.

**Communication buses (2 marks):**
- **CAN-FD** between VCU, BMS, MCU, gateway — high reliability, deterministic.
- **Automotive Ethernet 100/1000BASE-T1** between gateway, ADAS HPC, and HMI — high bandwidth for perception data.
- **LIN** for low-speed body items (lights, wipers).

**Justification (1 mark):** Ethernet is needed because raw camera data is ~3 Gbps; CAN cannot carry it. The gateway bridges legacy CAN to Ethernet, allowing gradual migration.

**Part (b) — AUTOSAR Architecture (7 marks)**

> [!NOTE]
> Layer list (2 marks), BSW breakdown (3 marks), RTE role (1 mark), hardware-independence explanation (1 mark)

**Layered structure (top → bottom):**

1. **ASW (Application Software Layer)** — Vehicle functions implemented as *Software Components (SWC)* with well-defined ports. Vehicle-agnostic.
2. **RTE (Runtime Environment)** — Middleware providing the *Virtual Function Bus (VFB)*; SWCs only see RTE APIs.
3. **BSW (Basic Software)** — Sub-divided into:
   - **Services:** OS, COM, DEM (Diagnostic Event Manager), MEM (Memory), NvM (Non-volatile Memory).
   - **ECU Abstraction (ECUAL):** MCAL-independent drivers for on-board peripherals (CAN controller, ADC, PWM).
   - **MCAL (Microcontroller Abstraction Layer):** Direct register-level drivers — *the only hardware-specific layer*.

**Hardware-Independence (1 mark):** A SWC is written once, then compiled against the *ECUAL + MCAL* of the chosen MCU. Swapping an Infineon Aurix for an NXP S32K only requires a new MCAL porting; the ASW and most of the BSW remain unchanged. This is the cornerstone of OEM–Tier-1 supply-chain decoupling.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Compare the **distributed, domain-centralised, and zonal E/E architectures** of modern vehicles. State two advantages and one disadvantage of the zonal approach.

**(b) [7 marks]** An **AUTOSAR ECU** runs 3 periodic tasks with computation times $C_1, C_2, C_3$ and periods $T_1, T_2, T_3$ (with $T_1 < T_2 < T_3$). Apply the **Rate-Monotonic Scheduling (RMS) bound** and verify if the task set is schedulable for the following data:

$$
(C_1, T_1) = (1, 4),\quad (C_2, T_2) = (2, 6),\quad (C_3, T_3) = (1, 10)
$$

All times are in milliseconds.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Architecture Comparison (7 marks)**

> [!NOTE]
> 3 marks for table-style comparison, 2 marks for zonal advantages, 1 mark for disadvantage, 1 mark for diagram mention

| Aspect | Distributed | Domain-Centralised | Zonal + Central Compute |
|---|---|---|---|
| **# ECUs** | 80–150 | 5–10 DCUs | 1–4 HPCs + ~4 zonal |
| **Wiring** | Long, complex | Shorter | Shortest, zonal bundles |
| **OTA updates** | Difficult | Possible per-domain | Easy (central OS) |
| **Cost** | Low unit cost | Moderate | High initial, low integration |
| **Best for** | Legacy vehicles | Current production | 2025+ EVs, ADAS |

**Zonal advantages (2 marks):**
1. **Reduced harness mass** — short intra-zone bundles instead of long domain wires (saves ~30 % mass).
2. **Easier OTA** — one or two central HPCs can update all features; no need to touch 100 ECUs.

**Zonal disadvantage (1 mark):**
- **Single point of failure risk** — if the central HPC fails, multiple zones can go dark. Requires redundancy (dual HPC).

**Diagrammatic credit (1 mark):** Students should be able to draw the zonal block diagram as in Section 4.1.

**Part (b) — RMS Scheduability Test (7 marks)**

> [!NOTE]
> Bound calculation (2 marks), utilisation calculation (2 marks), comparison and conclusion (2 marks), short note on RMS rule (1 mark)

**Step 1 — Compute individual utilisations**

$$
u_1 = \dfrac{C_1}{T_1} = \dfrac{1}{4} = 0.250
$$

$$
u_2 = \dfrac{C_2}{T_2} = \dfrac{2}{6} \approx 0.333
$$

$$
u_3 = \dfrac{C_3}{T_3} = \dfrac{1}{10} = 0.100
$$

**Step 2 — Total utilisation**

$$
U = u_1 + u_2 + u_3 = 0.250 + 0.333 + 0.100 = 0.683
$$

**Step 3 — Liu–Layland upper bound for $n = 3$** (from Section 3.2)

$$
U_{bound}(3) = 3(2^{1/3} - 1) \approx 0.7798
$$

**Step 4 — Compare**

$$
U = 0.683 \;\lt\; U_{bound}(3) = 0.7798
$$

**Conclusion (2 marks):** The task set **is hard-real-time schedulable** under RMS.

**RMS reminder (1 mark):** *Shorter period → higher priority* (here, Task 1 has the highest priority, Task 3 the lowest). The bound is *sufficient but not necessary* — values above the bound may still be schedulable.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing AUTOSAR Classic and Adaptive Platforms** — Classic = deeply embedded (OSEK-based), Adaptive = POSIX-based for HPC. Mixing them in a single answer is the #1 mistake.
> 2. **Forgetting to subtract overhead in CAN throughput** — Section 3.1 shows the correct 49 % figure. Students who claim "500 kbps" as effective throughput lose 2 marks.
> 3. **Wrong priority assignment in RMS** — Period and priority are *inversely* related; do not assign highest period to highest priority.
> 4. **Skipping the verification step in RMS** — computing $U_{bound}$ but not comparing with actual $U$ loses at least 1 mark.
> 5. **Diagrams without labels** — KTU examiners award marks for *labelled* arrows and *named* buses. A bare box earns zero.
> 6. **Ignoring ISO 26262 / ASIL** — A software-architecture answer without a sentence on functional safety is marked down.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Three E/E architectures:** Distributed → Domain → Zonal + Central. The future is zonal.
- ✅ **Bus protocols (in order of speed):** LIN (20 kbps) < CAN (500 kbps) < CAN-FD (2 Mbps) < FlexRay (10 Mbps) < Automotive Ethernet (100 Mbps–1 Gbps).
- ✅ **AUTOSAR layers (top → bottom):** ASW → RTE → BSW (Services → ECUAL → MCAL) → Hardware. RTE = middleware.
- ✅ **Classic vs Adaptive AUTOSAR:** Classic = deeply embedded, hard real-time (μC); Adaptive = POSIX-OS, high performance (HPC, ADAS).
- ✅ **CAN frame efficiency ≈ 49 %** at 500 kbps classical, 8-byte payload.
- ✅ **Liu–Layland RMS bound:** $U_{bound}(n) = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$; limit $U_{bound}(\infty) = \ln 2 \approx 0.693$.
- ✅ **End-to-end delay = Σ(queue + proc + tx)**; must be ≤ ASIL-D deadline (e.g., 10 ms for brake-by-wire).
- ✅ **ISO 26262 ASIL** = f(Severity, Exposure, Controllability) — A (low) to D (highest).
- ✅ **V-Model** is the industry standard for verification & validation of automotive software.
- ✅ **CPU load rule-of-thumb:** Keep $U_{CPU} \le 0.7$ to leave headroom for interrupts.
- ✅ **HPC (High-Performance Compute)** replaces many ECUs; runs on **SoCs** (e.g., NVIDIA Orin, Qualcomm Ride).
- ✅ **VFB (Virtual Functional Bus)** is the conceptual bus in AUTOSAR that allows SWCs to communicate regardless of which ECU they reside on.
- ✅ **Sensor suite for ADAS:** Camera + Radar + LiDAR + Ultrasonic + IMU — *sensor fusion* gives robust perception.
- ✅ **OTA (Over-The-Air) updates** require a gateway ECU with security (SecOC) and a reliable backend.
- ✅ **OBD-II port** provides standardised diagnostics using **UDS (Unified Diagnostic Services, ISO 14229)**.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Software and Hardware Architecture**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Safety and Cybersecurity Considerations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT865 - ADVANCED VEHICLE TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Safety and Cybersecurity Considerations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Safety in Advanced Vehicles — Formal Definition

In the context of modern automotive engineering, **Functional Safety** is formally defined as the absence of unreasonable risk due to hazards caused by malfunctioning behaviour of Electrical/Electronic (E/E) systems. The governing international standard is **ISO 26262** ("Road vehicles — Functional safety"), which is the automotive-specific adaptation of the broader IEC 61508 functional safety standard.

For KTU 2024 Scheme students, safety in Advanced Vehicle Technology encompasses **two intertwined pillars**:

1. **Safety** — Preventing physical harm to humans (drivers, passengers, pedestrians) arising from system failures.
2. **Cybersecurity** — Preventing malicious or unauthorised interference with vehicle E/E systems, which could subsequently induce safety hazards.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> *"Safety and Cybersecurity Considerations — Functional Safety (ISO 26262), ASIL classification, ADAS safety, Vehicle cybersecurity (ISO/SAE 21434), Attack vectors, Security countermeasures, OTA security, V2X security."*

## 1.2 Cybersecurity in Vehicles — Formal Definition

**Automotive Cybersecurity** is the discipline of protecting vehicle electronic systems, communication networks, software, and data from malicious attacks, unauthorised access, and tampering. The governing standard is **ISO/SAE 21434** ("Road vehicles — Cybersecurity engineering"), published in **2021**, which provides a structured framework to manage cybersecurity risks throughout the vehicle lifecycle (concept → design → production → operation → decommissioning).

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — Plain English

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Modern Car as a Rolling Smartphone**
>
> Imagine you are driving a **fortress on wheels** that is also a **Wi-Fi-connected smartphone**. The **fortress** part is Safety (crumple zones, airbags, ABS — protecting you in an accident). The **smartphone** part is where Cybersecurity comes in — your car has Bluetooth, Wi-Fi, cellular (4G/5G), GPS, and an OBD-II port. Just as a hacker can steal data from your phone, they can also try to **brake your car remotely**, **steal it via relay attacks**, or **inject malicious CAN messages** to disable the engine.
>
> In the past (1990s), cars were purely mechanical islands — almost impossible to attack remotely. Today, a Tesla or a modern Toyota is software-defined; it receives **OTA (Over-The-Air) updates**, talks to **V2X infrastructure**, and has **>100 ECUs** communicating over **CAN, LIN, Ethernet, FlexRay** buses. Every new "feature" (autonomy, connectivity, electrification) is a **new attack surface**.

## 1.4 Why This Topic is Exam-Critical

| Driver Trend | Safety Implication | Cyber Implication |
|---|---|---|
| ADAS / Autonomous Driving | Sensor/compute failures can be fatal | Sensor spoofing (e.g., LiDAR, camera) |
| Vehicle Electrification (EV) | High-voltage (400V/800V) battery hazards | Charging port attacks, BMS manipulation |
| V2X Connectivity | Communication latency in safety-critical decisions | Message injection, replay attacks |
| OTA Updates | Software bugs can brick the vehicle | Supply-chain attack on firmware |
| Shared Mobility / Fleet | Multi-driver exposure | Identity theft, fleet-wide compromise |

## 1.5 Key Metrics & Standards (Bolded for Emphasis)

- **ASIL** — Automotive Safety Integrity Level (**A, B, C, D** — D being most stringent).
- **FIT rate** — Failures In Time, measured as **1 failure per 10⁹ device-hours**.
- **PMHF** — Probabilistic Metric for Hardware Failures (target: **<10⁻⁸ /hr** for ASIL D).
- **CVSS** — Common Vulnerability Scoring System (**0.0 to 10.0**).
- **TTA (Time To Attack)** — Time taken by an attacker to compromise a target.
- **Cal (Cybersecurity Assurance Level)** — **CAL 1 to CAL 4**, defined in ISO/SAE 21434.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Safety Framework (ISO 26262) — Step-by-Step Logic

The ISO 26262 V-model is the backbone of vehicle functional safety. The process proceeds in **10 parts**:

### Step 1 — Item Definition
Define the system or function under analysis (e.g., "Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) subsystem"). State its **boundary**, **interfaces**, and **assumptions of use**.

### Step 2 — Hazard Analysis & Risk Assessment (HARA)
- Identify all **hazards** (e.g., unintended acceleration).
- Assess risk across three dimensions:
  - **Severity (S1–S3)** — S1 = Light injuries, S2 = Severe/life-threatening, S3 = Life-threatening/fatal.
  - **Exposure (E1–E4)** — E1 = Very low probability, E2 = Low, E3 = Medium, E4 = High.
  - **Controllability (C1–C3)** — C1 = Simply controllable, C2 = Normally controllable, C3 = Difficult to control/uncontrollable.

### Step 3 — ASIL Determination
The combination of Severity × Exposure × Controllability maps to an **ASIL level** (A, B, C, D, or QM — Quality Management only).

### Step 4 — Functional Safety Concept (FSC)
Define **safety goals**, derive **functional safety requirements (FSRs)**, and allocate them to system architecture.

### Step 5 — Technical Safety Concept (TSC)
Allocate FSRs to hardware/software. Define **hardware architectural metrics** and **software safety requirements**.

### Step 6 — Safety Validation
Prove by testing that the residual risk is acceptable.

## 2.2 ASIL Classification Table (KTU Must-Know)

| Severity ↓ / Exposure → | E1 | E2 | E3 | E4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **S1** | QM | QM | QM | A |
| **S2** | QM | QM | A | B |
| **S3** | QM | A | B | C |

*(C3 Controllability assumed; for C1 and C2, ASIL is reduced by one level)*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ASIL D** is the highest integrity level (e.g., airbag deployment, electronic stability control). It demands the strictest development process, the lowest permissible **PMHF**, and the highest diagnostic coverage.

## 2.3 Hardware Architectural Metrics — Formulas

For a system composed of multiple channels, ISO 26262 defines two architectural metrics:

### Metric 1 — Single-Point Fault Metric (SPFM)

$$
\text{SPFM} = 1 - \frac{\sum \lambda_{\text{single-point faults}}}{\sum \lambda_{\text{total}}}
$$

Where:
- $\lambda_{\text{single-point faults}}$ is the failure rate of single-point faults (no safety mechanism).
- $\lambda_{\text{total}}$ is the total failure rate of all elements.

**Target (ASIL D): SPFM $\geq 99\%$**.

### Metric 2 — Latent Fault Metric (LFM)

$$
\text{LFM} = 1 - \frac{\sum \lambda_{\text{latent faults}}}{\sum \lambda_{\text{total}}}
$$

**Target (ASIL D): LFM $\geq 90\%$**.

### Metric 3 — Probabilistic Metric for Hardware Failures (PMHF)

$$
\text{PMHF} = \sum_{i} \lambda_i \cdot \text{DC}_i \cdot t_{\text{mission}}
$$

A simplified single-point estimate for the item:

$$
\text{PMHF}_{\text{item}} \leq 10^{-8} \text{ /hr} \quad (\text{ASIL D})
$$

### Metric 4 — Diagnostic Coverage (DC)

$$
\text{DC} = \frac{\lambda_{\text{detected}}}{\lambda_{\text{detected}} + \lambda_{\text{undetected}}}
$$

> **Note on DC levels (per ISO 26262-5 Table D.2):**
> - DC $\geq 60\%$ → Low
> - DC $\geq 90\%$ → Medium
> - DC $\geq 99\%$ → High

## 2.4 Cybersecurity Engineering (ISO/SAE 21434) Framework

### Step 1 — Cybersecurity Management (CSM)
- Organisational cybersecurity policies, audit processes, governance.

### Step 2 — TARA — Threat Analysis & Risk Assessment
- **Asset identification** — What is valuable? (e.g., gateway ECU, brake-by-wire signal).
- **Threat identification** — STRIDE model (Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information disclosure, Denial of service, Elevation of privilege).
- **Risk = Impact × Attack Feasibility**.
- **Impact** = Severe / Major / Moderate / Negligible.
- **Attack Feasibility** = based on elapsed time, expertise, knowledge, window of opportunity, equipment.

### Step 3 — Cybersecurity Goals & Claims
Map each high-risk threat to a **Cybersecurity Goal (CSG)**.

### Step 4 — Cybersecurity Concept (CSC)
Define countermeasures: secure boot, message authentication, intrusion detection.

### Step 5 — Cybersecurity Validation
Penetration testing, fuzz testing, vehicle-level validation.

## 2.5 Cybersecurity Assurance Level (CAL) Determination

| Attack Feasibility ↓ / Impact → | Negligible | Moderate | Major | Severe |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Very Low | CAL 0 | CAL 1 | CAL 2 | CAL 3 |
| Low | CAL 0 | CAL 1 | CAL 2 | CAL 3 |
| Medium | CAL 1 | CAL 2 | CAL 3 | CAL 4 |
| High | CAL 2 | CAL 3 | CAL 4 | CAL 4 |
| **Very High** | CAL 3 | CAL 4 | CAL 4 | CAL 4 |

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet)

| Concept | Formula / Metric | Target / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Single-Point Fault Metric | $\text{SPFM} = 1 - \frac{\sum \lambda_{\text{SPF}}}{\sum \lambda_{\text{total}}}$ | $\geq 99\%$ (ASIL D) |
| Latent Fault Metric | $\text{LFM} = 1 - \frac{\sum \lambda_{\text{LF}}}{\sum \lambda_{\text{total}}}$ | $\geq 90\%$ (ASIL D) |
| PMHF | $\sum \lambda_i \cdot \text{DC}_i \cdot t_{\text{mission}}$ | $< 10^{-8}$ /hr (ASIL D) |
| Diagnostic Coverage | $\text{DC} = \frac{\lambda_{\text{det}}}{\lambda_{\text{det}} + \lambda_{\text{undet}}}$ | $\geq 99\%$ = High |
| Risk | $R = S \times E \times C$ | Mapped to ASIL A–D |
| Cyber Risk | $R_{\text{cyber}} = \text{Impact} \times \text{Feasibility}$ | Mapped to CAL 0–4 |
| FIT Rate | $\text{FIT} = \lambda \times 10^{9}$ | Failures per $10^9$ hours |
| MTTF (avg) | $\frac{1}{\lambda}$ | Hours |
| Availability | $A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | Dimensionless (0–1) |

## 2.7 Real-World Utility of These Frameworks

- **OEMs (e.g., Tata Motors, Mahindra, Toyota)** must comply with ISO 26262 to obtain type approval in markets like the EU, Japan, India (AIS standards aligned).
- **Tier-1 suppliers (Bosch, Continental, ZF)** must deliver components with documented ASIL ratings and PMHF values.
- **Cybersecurity regulation** is now mandated by **UNECE WP.29 R155** (Cyber Security Management System) and **R156** (Software Update Management System) — both required for type approval in the EU from **July 2022 onwards**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — ASIL Determination

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
A vehicle's **Lane Keeping Assist (LKA)** system can misbehave by steering the vehicle into oncoming traffic.

- **Severity:** Could be fatal (S3).
- **Exposure:** Operating on highways (E4 — High probability).
- **Controllability:** Driver cannot easily counteract an unexpected steering input (C3).

**Derivation (Step-by-Step):**

Step 1: Identify Severity.
The hazardous event (steering into oncoming traffic) is **life-threatening or fatal**. Hence, **Severity = S3**.

Step 2: Identify Exposure.
The vehicle is on a highway for a significant portion of driving time. Hence, **Exposure = E4**.

Step 3: Identify Controllability.
A sudden unexpected steering input at highway speed is **difficult or impossible** for the average driver to counteract in time. Hence, **Controllability = C3**.

Step 4: Apply ASIL mapping.
Using the ASIL matrix (C3 column):

$$
\text{ASIL}(S3, E4, C3) = C
$$

Wait — recall the rule: **ASIL is reduced by one level when Controllability is C1 or C2**. Since here C = C3, no reduction is applied.

**Final ASIL = C**.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — SPFM Calculation

**Problem Statement:**
An ECU has total failure rate $\lambda_{\text{total}} = 200$ FIT. Single-point faults contribute 2 FIT. What is the SPFM? Does it meet ASIL D?

**Derivation:**

Step 1: Recall the SPFM formula.

$$
\text{SPFM} = 1 - \frac{\sum \lambda_{\text{single-point faults}}}{\sum \lambda_{\text{total}}}
$$

Step 2: Substitute values.

$$
\text{SPFM} = 1 - \frac{2\ \text{FIT}}{200\ \text{FIT}}
$$

Step 3: Compute the ratio.

$$
\frac{2}{200} = 0.01
$$

Step 4: Subtract from 1.

$$
\text{SPFM} = 1 - 0.01 = 0.99
$$

Step 5: Convert to percentage.

$$
\text{SPFM} = 99\%
$$

Step 6: Compare to ASIL D target.
ASIL D requires **SPFM $\geq 99\%$**. Here, we have **exactly 99%**, which **meets** ASIL D (boundary inclusive).

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — PMHF Calculation

**Problem Statement:**
A brake-by-wire system has two independent channels. Each channel has $\lambda = 50$ FIT. Diagnostic coverage (DC) for each channel is 95%. Compute the PMHF for a mission time of 10,000 hours.

**Derivation:**

Step 1: Recall PMHF formula.

$$
\text{PMHF} = \sum_i \lambda_i \cdot \text{DC}_i \cdot t_{\text{mission}}
$$

Step 2: For each channel, $\lambda = 50$ FIT $= 50 \times 10^{-9}$ /hr.

Step 3: $\text{DC} = 0.95$.

Step 4: $t_{\text{mission}} = 10{,}000$ hr.

Step 5: Per-channel contribution.

$$
\text{PMHF}_1 = (50 \times 10^{-9}) \times 0.95 \times 10{,}000
$$

Step 6: Simplify.

$$
\text{PMHF}_1 = 50 \times 10^{-9} \times 0.95 \times 10^{4} = 50 \times 0.95 \times 10^{-5}
$$

Step 7: Compute.

$$
\text{PMHF}_1 = 47.5 \times 10^{-5} = 4.75 \times 10^{-4}
$$

Step 8: For two channels (assume failures are independent, summed).

$$
\text{PMHF}_{\text{total}} = 2 \times 4.75 \times 10^{-4} = 9.5 \times 10^{-4}
$$

Step 9: Compare to ASIL D target ($\leq 10^{-8}$ /hr).

$$
9.5 \times 10^{-4} \gg 10^{-8}
$$

**Conclusion: The system FAILS ASIL D by a wide margin.** Diagnostic coverage must be increased (or single-channel failure rate reduced) by orders of magnitude.

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Cybersecurity Risk (TARA) Determination

**Problem Statement:**
A malicious actor can inject false brake commands onto the in-vehicle CAN bus.

- **Impact if successful:** Fatal crash.
- **Attack feasibility:** Medium (requires CAN-OBD access + protocol knowledge + ECU reverse-engineering, estimated 1 month of effort).

**Derivation:**

Step 1: Map Impact.
"Fatal crash" → **Severe (S4)**.

Step 2: Map Attack Feasibility.
"Medium" (1 month, automotive protocol knowledge, $1000–$10k equipment) → **Medium**.

Step 3: Apply CAL matrix (Severe × Medium).
From the CAL table (Severe row, Medium column):

$$
\text{CAL} = 4
$$

Step 4: Interpret.
**CAL 4** is the highest cybersecurity assurance level, demanding the strictest countermeasure requirements: secure boot, message authentication (e.g., **SecOC** — Secure Onboard Communication per AUTOSAR), intrusion detection systems, and frequent penetration testing.

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing Safety Mechanisms

| Hazard Type | Recommended Safety Mechanism | Standard Reference |
|---|---|---|
| Random hardware failure | Redundancy + diagnostics | ISO 26262-5 |
| Systematic software fault | Diverse programming, code review | ISO 26262-6 |
| Communication error (CAN) | E2E protection, CRC, sequence counter | ISO 26262-6 |
| Message spoofing (Cyber) | MAC authentication (SecOC) | ISO/SAE 21434 |
| Firmware tampering | Secure boot + HSM (Hardware Security Module) | ISO/SAE 21434 |
| OTA malicious update | Code signing + rollback protection | UNECE R156 |
| V2X message spoofing | PKI + digital signatures (IEEE 1609.2) | IEEE / ETSI ITS |
| Key theft | Secure key storage in HSM | FIPS 140-2 |

## 3.6 Safety vs. Security Trade-off — Quantitative Comparison

| Dimension | Safety (ISO 26262) | Security (ISO/SAE 21434) |
|---|---|---|
| Trigger | Random/systematic faults | Intentional adversarial action |
| Probability model | Probabilistic (Poisson failure rate) | Risk-based, non-deterministic |
| Time horizon | Vehicle operational life | Entire lifecycle (incl. decommissioning) |
| Primary artefact | Safety case | Cybersecurity case |
| Risk metric | ASIL (A–D) | CAL (1–4) |
| Threat modelling | FMEA, FTA | TARA, STRIDE |
| Counterexample | Fault tree | Attack tree |
