# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]`
**Define ASIL. List the four ASIL levels as per ISO 26262. Which is the most stringent, and why?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) **Definition:** ASIL (Automotive Safety Integrity Level) is a risk classification scheme defined in ISO 26262 to specify the level of risk-reduction required to avoid an unreasonable residual risk.
- (1 mark) **Four levels:** ASIL A (lowest), ASIL B, ASIL C, ASIL D (highest). QM (Quality Management) is used when no safety requirement exists.
- (1 mark) **Most stringent:** ASIL D. It requires the strictest development process, the lowest permissible failure rate, the highest diagnostic coverage, and the highest level of verification/validation.

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]`
**What is the difference between functional safety and cybersecurity in vehicles? Mention one standard for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) **Functional Safety:** Deals with hazards arising from **unintended/malfunctioning behaviour** of E/E systems (random/systematic faults). Governed by **ISO 26262**.
- (1 mark) **Cybersecurity:** Deals with hazards arising from **intentional malicious attacks** on vehicle systems. Governed by **ISO/SAE 21434** (or UNECE WP.29 R155).
- (1 mark) **Key difference:** Safety is fault-driven and probabilistic; cybersecurity is threat-driven and adversarial.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Option 1): `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **ISO 26262 hazard analysis and risk assessment (HARA)** process in detail, with reference to the three risk parameters — Severity, Exposure, and Controllability. Derive the **ASIL matrix** and determine the ASIL for a vehicle **Electronic Stability Control (ESC)** system that fails to apply brake pressure during a skid (assumed S3, E4, C3).

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. (1 mark) **HARA Definition:** HARA is a systematic method to identify and categorise hazardous events of an item, and to formulate safety goals.
2. (1 mark) **Severity (S):** S0 (no injuries), S1 (light/moderate injuries), S2 (severe/life-threatening, survival probable), S3 (life-threatening/fatal, survival uncertain).
3. (1 mark) **Exposure (E):** E0 (incredibly unlikely), E1 (very low probability), E2 (low), E3 (medium), E4 (high probability).
4. (1 mark) **Controllability (C):** C0 (controllable in general), C1 (simply controllable), C2 (normally controllable, ≥ 90% of drivers), C3 (difficult to control/uncontrollable).
5. (1 mark) **ASIL Determination Logic:** Risk = Severity × Exposure × Controllability. Higher values map to more stringent ASIL.
6. (1 mark) **Calculation for ESC:** Given S3, E4, C3. From the ASIL matrix, this combination maps to **ASIL D** (assuming C3 ⇒ no reduction).
7. (1 mark) **Interpretation:** ESC failure is critical; ASIL D demands redundancy, fault detection within 100 ms, FTTI (Fault Tolerant Time Interval) compliance, and PMHF $< 10^{-8}$ /hr.

**(b)** Describe the **ISO/SAE 21434 cybersecurity engineering** workflow. With a suitable example, explain how the **TARA (Threat Analysis and Risk Assessment)** process identifies threats and assigns **Cybersecurity Assurance Levels (CALs)**.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. (1 mark) **ISO/SAE 21434 Workflow Overview:** CSM → TARA → Cybersecurity Goals → Cybersecurity Requirements → Cybersecurity Architecture → Implementation → Validation → Production.
2. (1 mark) **TARA Steps:** Asset identification → Threat scenario identification → Impact rating → Attack feasibility rating → Risk value determination → Cybersecurity goal derivation.
3. (1 mark) **Asset Example:** The brake-by-wire CAN message (ID 0x123, 8 bytes).
4. (1 mark) **Threat Scenario:** An attacker with physical access injects brake commands at high frequency to cause wheel lockup.
5. (1 mark) **Impact Rating:** Wheel lockup at highway speed → Severe (potential fatality).
6. (1 mark) **Attack Feasibility:** Medium — requires OBD access, CAN protocol knowledge, ~1 month of effort.
7. (1 mark) **CAL Assignment:** Severe × Medium = **CAL 4**. Countermeasure: Implement **SecOC (Secure Onboard Communication)** with MAC, use a hardware security module for key storage, add intrusion detection on CAN bus.

---

### Question B (Option 2): `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **layered defence-in-depth architecture** for connected vehicle cybersecurity. Discuss the role of **Hardware Security Modules (HSM)** and **Secure Boot** in establishing a root of trust.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. (1 mark) **Definition of Defence-in-Depth:** A layered security strategy where multiple independent security controls protect overlapping layers, so the failure of one layer does not compromise the system.
2. (1 mark) **Layer 1 — Perimeter:** Firewalls on the TCU, intrusion prevention on IP-based interfaces (cellular, Wi-Fi, Bluetooth), rate limiting.
3. (1 mark) **Layer 2 — Network Segmentation:** Central gateway isolates powertrain CAN, chassis CAN, body CAN from each other; OBD-II port locked behind authentication.
4. (1 mark) **Layer 3 — Secure Onboard Communication:** SecOC per AUTOSAR adds a MAC to each CAN/Ethernet frame, ensuring authenticity and integrity.
5. (1 mark) **Layer 4 — ECU Hardening — Secure Boot:** At every ECU power-on, boot ROM verifies the digital signature of firmware using a public key stored in immutable memory. Unsigned/modified firmware is rejected.
6. (1 mark) **Layer 5 — HSM (Hardware Security Module):** Tamper-resistant chip that stores cryptographic keys, performs hardware-accelerated AES/SHA/RSA, and provides a true random number generator (TRNG). It anchors the **root of trust**.
7. (1 mark) **Layer 6 — IDS + Safety Island:** In-vehicle IDS monitors CAN bus for anomalies (e.g., message frequency, plausibility); the safety island (ASIL-D MCU with watchdog) can bring the vehicle to a safe state.

**(b)** Compute the **Single-Point Fault Metric (SPFM)** and the **PMHF** for a steer-by-wire system with two channels (Channel A: $\lambda_A = 30$ FIT, DC = 95%; Channel B: $\lambda_B = 40$ FIT, DC = 90%). Mission time = 8000 hours. Compare with ASIL D targets.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. (1 mark) **Total failure rate:**

$$
\lambda_{\text{total}} = \lambda_A + \lambda_B = 30 + 40 = 70\ \text{FIT} = 70 \times 10^{-9}\ /\text{hr}
$$

2. (1 mark) **Single-point (uncovered) failure rate per channel:**

$$
\lambda_{\text{SPF,A}} = \lambda_A (1 - \text{DC}_A) = 30 \times 10^{-9} \times 0.05 = 1.5 \times 10^{-9}\ /\text{hr}
$$

$$
\lambda_{\text{SPF,B}} = \lambda_B (1 - \text{DC}_B) = 40 \times 10^{-9} \times 0.10 = 4.0 \times 10^{-9}\ /\text{hr}
$$

3. (1 mark) **Total single-point failure rate:**

$$
\sum \lambda_{\text{SPF}} = 1.5 \times 10^{-9} + 4.0 \times 10^{-9} = 5.5 \times 10^{-9}\ /\text{hr}
$$

4. (1 mark) **SPFM calculation:**

$$
\text{SPFM} = 1 - \frac{5.5 \times 10^{-9}}{70 \times 10^{-9}} = 1 - 0.0786 = 0.9214 = 92.14\%
$$

5. (1 mark) **PMHF calculation:**

$$
\text{PMHF} = (\lambda_A \cdot \text{DC}_A + \lambda_B \cdot \text{DC}_B) \cdot t_{\text{mission}}
$$

$$
= [(30 \times 10^{-9} \times 0.95) + (40 \times 10^{-9} \times 0.90)] \times 8000
$$

$$
= (28.5 + 36) \times 10^{-9} \times 8000
$$

$$
= 64.5 \times 10^{-9} \times 8000 = 5.16 \times 10^{-4}\ /\text{hr}
$$

6. (1 mark) **Comparison with ASIL D:**
   - SPFM target: $\geq 99\%$. Achieved: $92.14\%$. **FAILS.**
   - PMHF target: $\leq 10^{-8}$ /hr. Achieved: $5.16 \times 10^{-4}$ /hr. **FAILS by ~5 orders of magnitude.**

7. (1 mark) **Conclusion:** The current design does not meet ASIL D. To comply, **diagnostic coverage must be raised to $\geq 99\%$** for both channels, OR a third redundant channel must be added with a watchdog voter.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in PEAUT865 Module 3:**
> 1. **Confusing ASIL with SIL** — ASIL is automotive-specific (ISO 26262); SIL is the generic IEC 61508 term. They are NOT identical mapping.
> 2. **Forgetting the C1/C2 ASIL reduction rule** — When Controllability is C1 or C2, the ASIL is reduced by one level. Many students miss this in exam questions, costing 1 mark.
> 3. **Unit errors in PMHF** — FIT must be converted to /hr ($1\ \text{FIT} = 10^{-9}\ /\text{hr}$). Direct multiplication without conversion gives a wrong answer by $10^9$.
> 4. **Mixing Safety and Security in TARA** — TARA is a cybersecurity activity, not a safety activity. Do not cite ISO 26262 when answering ISO/SAE 21434 questions.
> 5. **Forgetting UNECE WP.29 R155/R156** — Indian OEMs exporting to the EU must comply. Examiners often give bonus credit for mentioning these.
> 6. **Not labelling Mermaid diagrams or block diagrams** — A diagram without labels cannot earn full marks. Always annotate every block.
> 7. **Missing the "v-model" in safety lifecycle** — Many students list HARA steps but omit that the process is V-shaped (concept → implementation → validation).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Functional Safety** = absence of unreasonable risk due to **faults** (ISO 26262).
- ✅ **Cybersecurity** = protection against **threats/attacks** (ISO/SAE 21434).
- ✅ **ASIL A < B < C < D** in strictness. **QM** = no safety requirement.
- ✅ **SPFM $\geq 99\%$, LFM $\geq 90\%$, PMHF $\leq 10^{-8}$ /hr** for **ASIL D**.
- ✅ **ASIL reduction by 1** when Controllability is C1 or C2.
- ✅ **CAL 0 to CAL 4** — analogous levels for cybersecurity (CAL 4 = most stringent).
- ✅ **TARA = Threat Analysis & Risk Assessment** — ISO/SAE 21434's HARA-equivalent.
- ✅ **UNECE WP.29 R155 (Cyber)** and **R156 (OTA)** — mandatory for EU type approval.
- ✅ **SecOC** = Secure Onboard Communication (AUTOSAR) — adds MAC to CAN/Ethernet frames.
- ✅ **HSM** = Hardware Security Module — stores keys, anchors root of trust.
- ✅ **Secure Boot** verifies firmware signature at every ECU power-on.
- ✅ **V2X security** uses **IEEE 1609.2** PKI (digital signatures on BSM messages).
- ✅ **OTA updates** must be **code-signed, encrypted in transit, and rollback-protected**.
- ✅ **OBD-II port** is the #1 physical attack vector — must be **locked behind gateway authentication**.
- ✅ **CAN bus** is broadcast and unauthenticated — a prime target for **message injection attacks**.
- ✅ **ADAS safety-critical ECUs** must run on **ASIL-D microcontrollers** with **lockstep cores**.
- ✅ **Defence-in-depth** = layered security (perimeter → network → SecOC → HSM → IDS → safety island).
- ✅ **FMEA** = Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (safety); **Attack Tree** = cybersecurity counterpart.
- ✅ **Cyber Risk** = Impact × Attack Feasibility; **Safety Risk** = S × E × C.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Leading Companies and Their Innovations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAUT865 - ADVANCED VEHICLE TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Leading Companies and Their Innovations # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Advanced Vehicle Technology (PEAUT865 – Module 3)**, the topic **"Leading Companies and Their Innovations"** refers to the systematic study of major global automotive Original Equipment Manufacturers (OEMs), Tier-1 suppliers, and technology disruptors that are redefining the modern automotive value chain. It encompasses their strategic innovations in: - **Electrification** (Battery Electric Vehicles – BEVs, Plug-in Hybrids – PHEVs, Fuel Cell Electric Vehicles – FCEVs) - **Autonomy** (Advanced Driver Assistance Systems – ADAS, Level 2 to Level 5 autonomy) - **Connectivity** (V2X, OTA updates, IoT integration) - **Alternative Powertrains** (Hydrogen, Biofuels, Range Extenders) - **Sustainable Manufacturing** (Circular economy, gigacasting, solid-state batteries) > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > *"Leading Companies and Their Innovations" is the analytical study of industry pioneers (e.g., Tesla, Toyota, BYD, Hyundai, Volkswagen Group, Rivian, Bosch, CATL, NVIDIA) and the disruptive engineering, strategic, and business-model innovations they deploy to shape next-generation mobility.* --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine the global automotive industry as a massive **"innovation Olympics"** where every major company is a competing nation. Each nation has a unique "playing style": - **Tesla** is like the **maverick athlete** who redefined the rules by skipping hybrid steps and going straight to fully electric, building a charging empire (Supercharger network – over **50,000+** global stalls). - **Toyota** is the **veteran strategist** — the master of hybrids (Prius, launched **1997**) and a pioneer in hydrogen fuel cell vehicles (Mirai). - **BYD** is the **vertically integrated champion** — making its own batteries, motors, and even chips, achieving massive scale at low cost. - **Rivian / Lucid** are the **niche premium specialists** — focusing on adventure EVs and ultra-luxury performance respectively. - **Bosch & Continental (Tier-1 suppliers)** are the **coaches behind the scenes** — supplying the ADAS sensors and ECUs to nearly every OEM. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this matters for KTU exams:** > Examiners frequently ask comparative questions (e.g., *"Compare Tesla and Toyota's EV strategies"* or *"Discuss BYD's Blade Battery innovation"*). You must remember **company name → flagship innovation → year** triplets for scoring full marks. --- ## 1.3 Industry Benchmarks & Physical Constants | Metric | Benchmark | Significance | |---|---|---| | Tesla Supercharger Network | **~50,000+** stalls globally | Largest fast-charging network | | Toyota Prius Launch | **1997** | First mass-produced hybrid | | BYD Blade Battery | **2020** | LFP chemistry, puncture-proof | | Lucid Air Dream Edition | **EPA range ~837 km** | Longest-range EV (as of 2024) | | Tesla Model S Plaid | **1,020 hp, 0–100 km/h in ~2.1 s** | Performance benchmark | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Highlight:** The automotive sector contributes approximately **7–8% of global GDP** and is undergoing the largest transformation since Henry Ford's assembly line (**1913**). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Sheet ## 2.1 Categorical Breakdown of Leading Companies ### A. Pure-EV Disruptors - **Tesla, Inc.** — Vertical integration, Gigafactories, FSD (Full Self-Driving) software stack. - **Rivian** — Electric adventure vehicles (R1T truck, R1S SUV), Amazon EDV partnership. - **Lucid Motors** — Ultra-premium efficiency (Lucid Air, **~837 km** EPA range). - **NIO, XPeng, BYD** — Chinese EV powerhouses. ### B. Legacy OEMs Pivoting to Electric - **Volkswagen Group** — "ID." series, **MEB & PPE platforms**. - **Hyundai-Kia** — E-GMP platform (**800 V** architecture), IONIQ series. - **BMW** — Neue Klasse platform (post-2025). - **Mercedes-Benz** — EQ series, **EQXX** concept with **>1,200 km** single-charge range. ### C. Hybrid & Fuel-Cell Leaders - **Toyota** — Prius, Mirai (hydrogen FCEV). - **Honda** — Clarity Fuel Cell, hybrid synergy drive. ### D. Tier-1 Suppliers & Tech Backbone - **CATL, BYD, LG Energy Solution, Panasonic** — Battery cell manufacturers. - **Bosch, Continental, ZF, Magna** — ADAS, ECU, sensors. - **NVIDIA, Mobileye, Qualcomm** — Autonomous compute platforms (e.g., **NVIDIA DRIVE Thor**, **Mobileye EyeQ6**). --- ## 2.2 Strategic Innovation Pillars — Logic Steps 1. **Powertrain Innovation** — Transition from ICE → Hybrid → BEV → FCEV. 2. **Battery Innovation** — Energy density (Wh/kg), charging speed (C-rate), cycle life. 3. **Software-Defined Vehicle (SDV)** — OTA updates, centralized compute (zonal architecture). 4. **Autonomy Stack** — Sensor fusion (Camera + Radar + LiDAR), AI inference chips. 5. **Manufacturing Innovation** — Gigacasting (single-piece underbody), dry electrode coating. 6. **Charging Ecosystem** — Fast charging (**350 kW+**), bidirectional V2G (Vehicle-to-Grid). > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Relevance:** Each innovation pillar directly maps to KTU Course Outcomes (COs): *CO3 – Analyze emerging automotive technologies*, *CO4 – Evaluate sustainability & business impact*. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula / Data Sheet | Concept | Formula / Standard | Units / Value | |---|---|---| | Energy stored in battery | $E = V \times Q$ | Wh (Watt-hour) | | Specific energy | $\rho = E / m$ | Wh/kg | | Battery C-rate | $I = C \times Q_{rated}$ | A (Amperes) | | Vehicle range | $R = (E_{bat} \times \eta) / E_{100}$ | km | | Charging time | $t_{ch} = Q / I_{ch}$ | hours | | Tesla Supercharger V3 power | $P$ | **250 kW** peak | | BYD Blade Cell chemistry | — | **LFP** (Lithium Iron Phosphate) | | Toyota Mirai H₂ tank pressure | $P$ | **70 MPa** (700 bar) | | CATL Qilin battery | — | **255 Wh/kg**, **13C** discharge | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Note for Students:** Never confuse Tesla's "4680 cell" with "18650/21700" predecessors. The numbers denote **diameter × length in mm** (46 mm × 80 mm), directly affecting energy density and manufacturing yield. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Battery Management Systems (BMS):** Innovation by CATL and Tesla enables second-life battery reuse in grid storage. - **Autonomy Compute:** NVIDIA DRIVE Thor delivers **2,000 TOPS** (Tera Operations Per Second), powering L4 autonomy. - **Hydrogen Mobility:** Toyota Mirai's **70 MPa** tanks store **~5 kg H₂**, delivering **~650 km** range. - **Gigacasting:** Tesla's Giga Press (**6,000–9,000 tonnes**) replaces **~70 parts** with a single casting. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Case Derivations ## 3.1 Case Study A: Tesla — Vertical Integration & 4680 Cell Economics ### Problem Context Compute the **volumetric energy density** improvement of Tesla's 4680 cell over the older 21700 cell, and estimate the cost savings per kWh. ### Given Data | Parameter | 21700 Cell | 4680 Cell | |---|---|---| | Diameter | 21 mm | 46 mm | | Length | 70 mm | 80 mm | | Capacity | ~4.8 Ah | ~26 Ah | | Nominal Voltage | 3.7 V | 3.7 V | --- ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Calculate the energy per cell (E in Wh)** For the 21700 cell: $$E_{21700} = V \times Q = 3.7 \times 4.8 = 17.76 \text{ Wh}$$ For the 4680 cell: $$E_{4680} = V \times Q = 3.7 \times 26 = 96.20 \text{ Wh}$$ **Step 2 — Calculate cell volume (V_cell in cm³)** For 21700: $$V_{cell,21700} = \pi \times \left(\frac{2.1}{2}\right)^2 \times 7.0 \approx 24.24 \text{ cm}^3$$ For 4680: $$V_{cell,4680} = \pi \times \left(\frac{4.6}{2}\right)^2 \times 8.0 \approx 132.95 \text{ cm}^3$$ **Step 3 — Volumetric energy density (ρ_v in Wh/L)** For 21700: $$\rho_{v,21700} = \frac{E}{V_{cell}} \times 1000 = \frac{17.76}{24.24} \times 1000 \approx 733 \text{ Wh/L}$$ For 4680: $$\rho_{v,4680} = \frac{96.20}{132.95} \times 1000 \approx 724 \text{ Wh/L}$$ > **Observation:** Volumetric energy density is roughly **similar**, but the **manufacturing cost** drops by **~14%** because one 4680 cell replaces **~5× 21700 cells**, reducing internal connections, welds, and casing overhead. **Step 4 — Cost-per-kWh projection** If 21700 cell cost = **\$100/kWh**, then 4680 cell cost ≈ $$C_{4680} = 100 \times (1 - 0.14) = \$86 \text{ per kWh}$$ **Engineering Conclusion:** Tesla's 4680 + structural battery pack reduces pack-level cost toward the **\$60/kWh** "magic number" — the threshold at which BEVs achieve **price parity** with ICE vehicles. --- ## 3.2 Case Study B: BYD Blade Battery — Safety & LFP Chemistry ### Analytical Logic Chain 1. **Chemistry Choice:** LFP (LiFePO₄) instead of NMC (Nickel-Manganese-Cobalt) — eliminates thermal runaway risks above **~250 °C**. 2. **Cell-to-Pack (CTP) Design:** Removes intermediate module packaging, improving volume utilization by **~50%**. 3. **Nail Penetration Test:** BYD's Blade Battery withstands penetration without fire or smoke — a benchmark in safety. ### Energy Density Comparison $$\rho_{LFP} \approx 160 \text{ Wh/kg}, \quad \rho_{NMC} \approx 250 \text{ Wh/kg}$$ ### Trade-off Interpretation $$\text{Safety} \uparrow \uparrow, \quad \text{Energy Density} \downarrow$$ BYD compensates with **mass-efficient pack design** and **lower cost (~\$80/kWh)**. --- ## 3.3 Case Study C: Toyota Mirai — Hydrogen FCEV Efficiency ### Given Data - H₂ tank pressure: **70 MPa** - H₂ stored: **~5.6 kg** - Fuel cell efficiency: **~50–60%** ### Range Calculation **Step 1 — Energy content of H₂** $$E_{H_2} = m \times LHV = 5.6 \times 33.33 \approx 186.7 \text{ kWh (chemical)}$$ **Step 2 — Usable electrical energy (with FC efficiency η = 0.55)** $$E_{elec} = 186.7 \times 0.55 \approx 102.7 \text{ kWh}$$ **Step 3 — Vehicle range (assuming consumption = 0.15 kWh/km)** $$R = \frac{E_{elec}}{E_{km}} = \frac{102.7}{0.15} \approx 685 \text{ km}$$ > **Outcome:** Matches Toyota's claimed **~650 km** range, validating the model. **Real-world engineering insight:** FCEVs excel in **heavy-duty transport** (trucks, buses) where fast refueling (**~5 min**) beats BEV charging time. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Company vs. Innovation | Company | Flagship Innovation | Year | Key Engineering Metric | |---|---|---|---| | Tesla | Model S + Supercharger | 2012 / 2012 | **265 mi (EPA)** range | | Tesla | 4680 Structural Pack | 2022 | **5× energy, 14% cost cut** | | Toyota | Prius (Hybrid) | **1997** | **~22 km/L** | | Toyota | Mirai (FCEV) | 2014 | **~650 km range, 70 MPa** | | BYD | Blade Battery | 2020 | **LFP, nail-test safe** | | Hyundai | E-GMP 800 V | 2021 | **18 min → 80% charge** | | Volkswagen | MEB Platform | 2020 | **ID.3, ID.4 scale** | | Lucid | Air Dream Edition | 2021 | **~837 km (EPA)** | | Rivian | R1T Quad-Motor | 2021 | **829 hp, tank turn** | | CATL | Qilin / Shenxing | 2023 | **10 min → 400 km** | | NVIDIA | DRIVE Thor | 2024 | **2,000 TOPS** | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Innovation Ecosystem Flow Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR node1[Raw Materials
Li, Ni, Co, Graphite] --> node2[Battery Cell Makers
CATL, BYD, LG, Panasonic] node2 --> node3[OEMs
Tesla, Toyota, VW, Hyundai] nodeA[Tier-1 Suppliers
Bosch, ZF, Magna] --> node3 nodeB[Compute & AI
NVIDIA, Mobileye, Qualcomm] --> node3 nodeC[Charging Infra
Supercharger, IONITY, NIO Swap] --> node3 node3 --> node4[End User Vehicle
BEV / PHEV / FCEV] node4 --> node5[Second-Life Battery
Grid Storage] node4 --> node6[Recycling Loop
Li, Ni Recovery] node5 --> node1 node6 --> node1 ``` ## 4.2 Tesla Vertical Integration Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD cellA[4680 Battery Cell
Gigafactory Berlin, Texas] --> packA[Structural Battery Pack] packA --> motorA[PMSM / In-House Motor] motorA --> ctrlA[Inverter & Drive Unit] ctrlA --> autobrn[Autopilot / FSD
HW4 + Dojo] autobrn --> superA[Supercharger Network
250 kW V3, 350 kW V4] superA --> userA[Customer OTA Updates] userA -.feedback loop.-> autobrn ``` ## 4.3 BYD Multi-Energy Platform Strategy ```mermaid flowchart LR stratA[BYD Multi-Energy Strategy] --> dm[Dm-i Platform
PHEV] stratA --> ev[Pure EV Platform
Han, Seal, Dolphin] stratA --> blade[Blade Battery
LFP CTP] stratA --> semi[Semi-Truck BEV
Class 8] stratA --> chip[In-House SiC Chip
IGBT 5.0] blade --> ev blade --> semi chip --> ev chip --> dm ``` ## 4.4 Global Autonomy Compute Stack ```mermaid flowchart LR cam[Camera Array
8 MP - 12 MP] --> fuse1[Sensor Fusion ECU] rad[77 GHz Radar] --> fuse1 lid[LiDAR
128 - 300 channels] --> fuse1 fuse1 --> compute[Compute Platform] compute --> nvidia[NVIDIA DRIVE Thor
2000 TOPS] compute --> mob[Mobileye EyeQ6
144 TOPS] compute --> qual[Qualcomm Ride
Snapdragon 8775] nvidia --> act[Path Planning & Actuation] mob --> act qual --> act ``` ## 4.5 Company Innovation Timeline (Block Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart LR y1997[1997
Toyota Prius
First Mass Hybrid] y2008[2008
Tesla Roadster
First Premium EV] y2010[2010
Nissan Leaf
Mass BEV] y2012[2012
Tesla Model S
+ Supercharger] y2014[2014
Toyota Mirai
FCEV Launch] y2020[2020
BYD Blade Battery
VW ID.3 MEB] y2021[2021
Hyundai E-GMP 800V
Lucid Air] y2022[2022
Tesla 4680 Pack
Rivian R1T] y2023[2023
CATL Qilin
Mercedes EQXX 1200km] y2024[2024
NVIDIA Drive Thor
BYD Seagull] y1997 --> y2008 --> y2010 --> y2012 --> y2014 --> y2020 --> y2021 --> y2022 --> y2023 --> y2024 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **List any three major innovations introduced by Tesla in the EV domain.** **Model Answer:** 1. **Vertical Integration** — Tesla manufactures its own battery cells (**4680**), motors, inverters, and software, reducing dependency on suppliers. 2. **Supercharger Network** — Global fast-charging infrastructure exceeding **50,000+ stalls** delivering up to **250 kW (V3)** and **350 kW (V4)**. 3. **Full Self-Driving (FSD) Software** — Vision-based autonomy stack using **HW4** hardware and in-house **Dojo AI training supercomputer**. --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] **Explain the significance of Toyota's Mirai as a hydrogen fuel cell vehicle.** **Model Answer:** The **Toyota Mirai** (launched 2014) is the world's first mass-produced **Fuel Cell Electric Vehicle (FCEV)**. It stores **~5 kg of hydrogen** at **70 MPa (700 bar)**, generates electricity via a **PEM fuel cell stack**, and emits only water vapor. With a range of **~650 km** and refueling time of **~5 minutes**, Mirai represents Toyota's commitment to a **multi-pathway carbon-neutral strategy** beyond battery EVs. --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Internal Choice:** Answer **ONE** of the following. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** Discuss in detail the innovations brought by **BYD** in the EV industry. Highlight the **Blade Battery** technology and its engineering advantages. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Compare the **Vertical Integration strategy of Tesla** with the **Multi-Platform approach of Volkswagen Group**. *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution — Part (a): BYD Innovations **Step 1 — Company Background** Build Your Dreams (**BYD**), founded **1995** by Wang Chuanfu, is the world's largest NEV (New Energy Vehicle) manufacturer by volume (**3+ million units in 2023**). **Step 2 — Blade Battery Innovation** - **Chemistry:** Lithium Iron Phosphate (LFP) — eliminates cobalt/nickel, lowering cost. - **Form Factor:** Long, thin cell (**~960 mm × 90 mm × 13.5 mm**) resembling a "blade." - **CTP (Cell-to-Pack):** Volume utilization improved from **~40% → 62%**. - **Safety:** Passes nail-penetration test without fire at **>250 °C** thermal stability. **Step 3 — Other BYD Innovations** - **DM-i Super Hybrid System:** Plug-in hybrid delivering **~1,200 km** combined range. - **In-house SiC (Silicon Carbide) Chips:** Reduces inverter losses by **~5%**. - **Vertical Integration:** Mines lithium, refines cathodes, makes cells, packs, motors, ECUs, and vehicles — all in-house. **Step 4 — Engineering Conclusion** BYD demonstrates that **safety + scale + vertical integration** can deliver affordable EVs at **~$10,000** price points, undercutting Western rivals. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b): Tesla vs. VW Strategy | Parameter | Tesla | Volkswagen Group | |---|---|---| | **Strategy** | Vertical Integration | Modular Multi-Platform (MQB, MEB, PPE, SSP) | | **Battery** | In-house 4680 cells | Sourced from CATL, LG, Northvolt + PowerCo | | **Software** | Proprietary FSD / Dojo | CARIAD software unit (under restructuring) | | **Production** | Gigafactories (Berlin, Texas, Shanghai) | Wolfsburg, Zwickau, Emden, Chattanooga | | **Cost Target** | \$60/kWh cell | \$100/kWh pack-level by 2025 | | **Strengths** | Speed, OTA, branding | Scale, dealer network, engineering heritage | | **Weaknesses** | Quality control, recalls | Slow software rollout, bureaucracy | **Conclusion:** Tesla wins on **software and speed**, while VW leverages **scale and platform reuse** — two opposing but valid paradigms. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice* **(a)** Explain **Hyundai-Kia's E-GMP 800 V architecture**. What are its advantages over conventional **400 V** systems? *(7 marks)* **(b)** Discuss the role of **Tier-1 suppliers (Bosch, Continental, CATL, NVIDIA)** in accelerating advanced vehicle technology. *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution — Part (a): E-GMP 800 V Architecture **Step 1 — Background** The **Electric-Global Modular Platform (E-GMP)** underpins Hyundai IONIQ 5, IONIQ 6, Kia EV6, and Genesis GV60. **Step 2 — 800 V Advantages** - **Faster Charging:** Supports **350 kW DC fast charging** (IONIQ 5: **10–80% in 18 min**). - **Thinner Cables:** Higher voltage → lower current → **reduced copper usage by ~30%**. - **Lower Heat Losses:** $P_{loss} = I^2 R$, so halving current reduces losses by **75%**. - **Better Performance:** Faster motor response, higher sustained track speeds. **Step 3 — V2L (Vehicle-to-Load) Innovation** Bidirectional power output enables the EV to power appliances (**~3.6 kW**), even during blackouts. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b): Tier-1 Supplier Ecosystem **1. CATL (Battery Cell Leader)** — **37%** global market share; innovations include **Qilin (255 Wh/kg)** and **M3P (LMFP)** chemistries. **2. Bosch (Diversified Tech Giant)** — Supplies **electric axles, e-axle drives, ADAS sensors, and SiC inverters** to virtually every European OEM. **3. Continental** — Pioneers in **brake-by-wire, surround-view cameras**, and **zonal E/E architectures**. **4. NVIDIA** — **DRIVE Thor** compute platform (**2,000 TOPS**) is the brain behind next-gen L4 autonomy for **Mercedes, Volvo, Hyundai**. **Conclusion:** Tier-1 suppliers act as the **innovation backbone**, enabling OEMs to focus on **integration, branding, and customer experience** rather than reinventing core technologies. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Don't confuse Tesla's "4680" with "21700" or "18650"** — students frequently write wrong dimensions. Always state **diameter × length in mm**. > 2. **Toyota Prius is from 1997**, not 2000 — year matters! > 3. **Tesla did NOT invent the EV** — it commercialized the **modern premium EV** with the 2008 Roadster and 2012 Model S. > 4. **BYD Blade is LFP, not NMC** — chemistry is the key differentiator. > 5. **E-GMP is Hyundai-Kia**, not just Hyundai — Kia EV6, Genesis also use it. > 6. Avoid generic statements like *"Tesla makes good cars"* — examiners want **specific innovations, years, and numbers**. --- ## Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist - **Tesla:** 4680 cell (46×80 mm), FSD, Supercharger V3 (250 kW) / V4 (350 kW), Giga Press. - **Toyota:** Prius (1997), Mirai FCEV, **70 MPa** H₂ storage, multi-pathway carbon neutrality. - **BYD:** Blade Battery (LFP), CTP, DM-i hybrid, vertical integration, world's largest NEV maker (2023). - **Hyundai-Kia:** E-GMP 800 V platform, V2L, IONIQ 5/6, EV6. - **VW Group:** MEB → PPE → SSP platforms, ID. series, CARIAD software unit. - **Lucid:** ~837 km EPA range — current EV range record holder. - **Rivian:** R1T quad-motor, tank turn, Amazon EDV partnership. - **Mercedes-Benz:** EQXX concept (**>1,200 km**), Vision EQXX solar roof. - **Tier-1 Suppliers:** CATL (battery), Bosch/Continental (ADAS), NVIDIA (compute), Mobileye (vision). - **Key Battery Metrics:** Wh/kg, Wh/L, C-rate, cycle life, **\$/kWh**. - **Key Dates:** 1997 (Prius), 2008 (Roadster), 2012 (Model S + Supercharger), 2014 (Mirai), 2020 (Blade Battery), 2021 (E-GMP), 2024 (DRIVE Thor). - **Key Numbers:** 50,000+ Superchargers, 837 km (Lucid), 70 MPa (Mirai), 800 V (E-GMP), 2,000 TOPS (Thor). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU Tip:** For any "Leading Company" question, structure your answer as: > **(i) Background → (ii) Flagship Innovation → (iii) Engineering Metrics → (iv) Industry Impact**. This 4-step template guarantees **full marks** under the 2024 OBE rubric.

topics.title: Using Data Analytics to Forecast Vehicle Maintenance Needs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAUT865 - ADVANCED VEHICLE TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Using Data Analytics to Forecast Vehicle Maintenance Needs ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Data Analytics for Vehicle Maintenance Forecasting** is the systematic application of statistical, machine-learning, and signal-processing techniques on heterogeneous vehicular telemetry — collected through the **OBD-II port, CAN bus, IoT sensors, GPS modules, and fleet management systems** — to estimate component health, predict failure probability, and prescribe optimal maintenance interventions **before** a breakdown occurs. In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, this falls under the umbrella of **Prognostics and Health Management (PHM)** — a discipline that combines real-time condition monitoring with historical data mining to transition maintenance philosophy from *reactive* (fix-when-broken) to *predictive* (fix-before-failure). > [!NOTE] > **Prognostics and Health Management (PHM):** An engineering discipline that focuses on predicting the future state of a system (Remaining Useful Life) using current and historical operational data, thereby enabling Condition-Based Maintenance (CBM). ### 1.2 The Four Pillars of Maintenance Strategy The maintenance landscape evolves along a maturity axis. Each step represents a higher data-analytic capability: | # | Strategy | Trigger | Data Analytics Used | Cost Impact | |---|----------|---------|---------------------|-------------| | 1 | **Reactive (Run-to-Failure)** | Breakdown occurs | None | **Highest** (towing, downtime) | | 2 | **Preventive (Scheduled)** | Calendar / mileage | Basic descriptive stats | Medium | | 3 | **Predictive (Condition-Based)** | Sensor threshold breach | Diagnostic + predictive analytics | **Low** | | 4 | **Prescriptive (Cognitive)** | Forecasted failure mode | Prescriptive + reinforcement learning | **Lowest** | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Vehicle Fitness Tracker" Imagine strapping a **Fitbit** to a human body. It records heart rate, sleep cycles, step counts, and SpO₂. When abnormal patterns emerge (e.g., resting heart rate spiking for three consecutive nights), the app warns: *"You might be getting sick — see a doctor."* You don't wait until the heart attack. **A vehicle today does exactly the same:** - The **ECU (Engine Control Unit)** is the brain. - The **sensors** (oxygen, knock, temperature, wheel-speed) are the "wearable bands." - The **CAN bus** is the bloodstream carrying data. - The **cloud-based analytics engine** is the doctor's diagnostic AI. - The **alert ("Service Due in 800 km")** is the prescription. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Insight:** A modern connected car generates between **25 GB and 500 GB of data per hour** (Intel, 2024 estimate). The challenge is not *collecting* this data — it is *extracting actionable failure forecasts* from it. ### 1.4 The Four Analytical Layers The KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly classifies analytics into four ascending cognitive tiers. Memorize this hierarchy — it is a frequent 3-mark question. 1. **Descriptive Analytics** — *What happened?* (dashboards, MPG trends) 2. **Diagnostic Analytics** — *Why did it happen?* (root-cause via fault codes) 3. **Predictive Analytics** — *What will happen?* (RUL estimation, failure probability) 4. **Prescriptive Analytics** — *What should we do?* (optimal service scheduling) --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Predictive Maintenance (PdM) Workflow — Six Logical Steps 1. **Data Acquisition** — Telemetry harvested from OBD-II, GPS, accelerometers, battery management system (BMS), tire-pressure monitoring system (TPMS). 2. **Data Pre-processing** — Outlier removal (z-score / IQR), missing-value imputation (mean, KNN), noise filtering (Kalman / moving average). 3. **Feature Engineering** — Time-domain (mean, RMS, kurtosis), frequency-domain (FFT peaks), and statistical (rolling correlation) features extracted. 4. **Model Training** — Supervised (regression, SVM), unsupervised (K-means anomaly detection), or deep (LSTM, Autoencoder) models fitted on labelled failure history. 5. **Prediction & RUL Estimation** — Output is the Remaining Useful Life in km/days and the failure probability over the next service window. 6. **Decision & Action** — A threshold-based policy routes the alert to a workshop, schedules a part order, or triggers an OTA firmware update. ### 2.2 Why This Matters — The 'Why' Behind Each Step - **Why feature engineering?** Raw CAN frames are temporally correlated and noisy. A 1-D vibration signal carries information only when summarised into RMS, crest factor, or spectral kurtosis. - **Why probabilistic and not deterministic?** Failure is a stochastic process — identical vehicles in identical conditions fail at different mileages. Probability captures this irreducible randomness. - **Why continuous retraining?** Vehicle components age, driving styles evolve, and road conditions shift. Concept drift demands periodic model refresh. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet The following table is the **single most important reference** for solving numerical problems on this topic in the KTU University Exam. | # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Variables | Units | Boundary / Note | |---|---------|----------------------|-----------|-------|-----------------| | 1 | **Reliability Function** | $R(t) = P(T > t)$ | $T$ = time-to-failure r.v. | dimensionless | $R(0) = 1$, $R(\infty) = 0$ | | 2 | **Failure (Hazard) Rate** | $\lambda(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)} = -\dfrac{R'(t)}{R(t)}$ | $f(t)$ = PDF of failure | failures / hour | Weibull $\lambda(t) = \dfrac{\beta}{\eta}\!\left(\!\dfrac{t}{\eta}\!\right)^{\beta-1}$ | | 3 | **Weibull CDF** | $F(t) = 1 - e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$ | $\beta$ = shape, $\eta$ = scale | dimensionless | $\beta < 1$: infant mortality; $\beta = 1$: random; $\beta > 1$: wear-out | | 4 | **MTBF / MTTF** | $\text{MTBF} = \displaystyle\int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t)\,dt$ | first moment of $f(t)$ | hours | MTBF = MTTF + MTTR (repairable) | | 5 | **Availability** | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | MTTR = Mean Time To Repair | dimensionless | Fleet availability target: $A > 0.95$ | | 6 | **Remaining Useful Life (RUL)** | $\text{RUL}(t) = T - t \,\big\vert\, T > t$ | $T$ = random failure time | hours or km | Conditional expectation $E[\text{RUL}\vert\,\text{data}]$ | | 7 | **Anomaly Score (z-norm)** | $z_i = \dfrac{x_i - \mu}{\sigma}$ | $\mu, \sigma$ from training | dimensionless | $\vert z_i \vert > 3 \Rightarrow$ anomaly | | 8 | **Confidence Interval on $\hat{p}$** | $\hat{p} \pm z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{\dfrac{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})}{n}}$ | $\hat{p}$ = failure proportion | dimensionless | 95 % CI: $z = 1.96$ | | 9 | **Laplace's Rule of Succession** | $P(\text{fail next}) = \dfrac{s+1}{n+2}$ | $s$ = successes, $n$ = trials | dimensionless | Bayesian prior of uniform | | 10 | **Exponential Decay (Wear Model)** | $w(t) = w_0 \, e^{-k t}$ | $w_0$ = initial property, $k$ = decay const. | variable | Common for battery SOH | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Units Vigilance:** When MTBF is given in hours and mileage in km, convert using average fleet speed: $\text{km} = \text{hours} \times \bar{v}_{\text{fleet}}$. A frequent KTU board pitfall is mixing hours with km without conversion. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Commercial Fleet Operators** (e.g., Uber, Ola, FedEx) save **12 – 18 %** on maintenance OPEX by adopting PdM over preventive schedules (McKinsey, 2023 industry benchmark). - **OEM Recalls**: Tesla and Toyota use over-the-air analytics to detect battery thermal runaway precursors weeks in advance, avoiding large-scale recalls. - **Insurance Telematics**: Usage-based insurance uses the same diagnostic + predictive stack to compute risk premiums from accelerometer and GPS data. - **EV Battery Health**: State-of-Health (SOH) of a lithium-ion pack is computed via incremental capacity analysis (ICA) on charging curves — a classic prescriptive analytics use-case. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Reliability from the Weibull Distribution The Weibull distribution is the **workhorse** of automotive reliability engineering because its shape parameter $\beta$ elegantly captures the three classical failure regimes (infant mortality, random, wear-out). Below is a complete, exam-ready derivation of the reliability function. **Step 1 — Start from the Weibull PDF.** The Weibull probability density function for time-to-failure $T$ is given by: $$ f(t) = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta - 1} e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}, \quad t \geq 0 $$ where $\beta > 0$ is the shape parameter and $\eta > 0$ is the scale (characteristic life) parameter. **Step 2 — Express the CDF by integrating the PDF.** The Cumulative Distribution Function $F(t) = P(T \leq t)$ is obtained by integration: $$ F(t) = \int_{0}^{t} f(\tau)\, d\tau $$ **Step 3 — Substitute and simplify.** Let $u = (\tau / \eta)^{\beta}$, so $du = \frac{\beta}{\eta}(\tau/\eta)^{\beta - 1}\, d\tau$. Then: $$ F(t) = \int_{0}^{(t/\eta)^{\beta}} e^{-u}\, du = 1 - e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}} $$ **Step 4 — Apply the fundamental reliability identity.** Reliability is the complement of the CDF: $$ R(t) = 1 - F(t) = e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}} $$ **Step 5 — Derive the instantaneous hazard (failure) rate.** $$ \lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = \frac{\dfrac{\beta}{\eta}\!\left(\!\dfrac{t}{\eta}\!\right)^{\beta - 1} e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}}{e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}} = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\!\left(\!\dfrac{t}{\eta}\!\right)^{\beta - 1} $$ **Step 6 — Derive the MTBF.** For Weibull, the first moment (mean time to failure) is: $$ \text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t)\, dt = \eta \,\Gamma\!\left(1 + \tfrac{1}{\beta}\right) $$ where $\Gamma(\cdot)$ is the Gamma function. > [!NOTE] > **Sanity check:** Setting $\beta = 1$ reduces Weibull to the exponential distribution with $\text{MTTF} = \eta$, since $\Gamma(2) = 1! = 1$. This corresponds to the "random-failure" regime of constant hazard. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — RUL via Conditional Expectation The Remaining Useful Life at current operating time $t$ is defined as the expected residual life given survival up to $t$: $$ \text{RUL}(t) = E[T - t \,\big\vert\, T > t] $$ **Step 1 — Substitute the definition of conditional expectation.** $$ \text{RUL}(t) = \frac{1}{R(t)} \int_{t}^{\infty} (\tau - t)\, f(\tau)\, d\tau $$ **Step 2 — Make the substitution $\tau = t + x$ where $x$ is the residual life.** $$ \text{RUL}(t) = \frac{1}{R(t)} \int_{0}^{\infty} x \cdot f(t + x)\, dx $$ **Step 3 — For a Weibull-distributed $T$, evaluate the integral.** After standard manipulation (omitted here for brevity but available in advanced texts), one obtains the closed form: $$ \text{RUL}(t) = \eta \, e^{(t/\eta)^{\beta}} \int_{(t/\eta)^{\beta}}^{\infty} u^{1/\beta - 1} e^{-u}\, du $$ **Step 4 — Numerical recipe for the board exam.** Substitute the fleet-estimated parameters (e.g., $\beta = 2.3$, $\eta = 80{,}000$ km) and evaluate the incomplete gamma integral using a calculator or Simpson's rule with four panels. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Exponential Wear Model for EV Battery State-of-Health The state of health $\text{SOH}(t)$ of a lithium-ion cell decays approximately exponentially: $$ \text{SOH}(t) = e^{-k t} $$ **Step 1 — Convert the calendar life to cycles.** Given an average depth-of-discharge $\text{DoD} = 80\,\%$ and an equivalent full-cycle life $N_{\text{EFC}} = 2000$ cycles, the decay constant $k$ is obtained by requiring $\text{SOH}(N_{\text{EFC}}) = 0.8$ (end-of-life threshold). **Step 2 — Solve for $k$.** $$ 0.8 = e^{-k \cdot 2000} \;\Longrightarrow\; k = -\frac{\ln 0.8}{2000} $$ **Step 3 — Numerical evaluation.** $$ k = -\frac{-0.2231}{2000} = 1.1157 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{cycle}^{-1} $$ **Step 4 — Predict SOH at cycle $t = 1200$.** $$ \text{SOH}(1200) = e^{-1.1157 \times 10^{-4} \times 1200} = e^{-0.1339} \approx 0.8747 $$ So the battery retains **87.47 %** of its nominal capacity after 1200 equivalent full cycles. The RUL in cycles is: $$ \text{RUL} = \frac{\ln(1 / 0.8)}{k} = \frac{0.2231}{1.1157 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 2000 \;\text{cycles} $$ confirming the original EFC rating. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-world refinement:** Battery degradation is *not* purely exponential; it follows a superposition of calendar aging (linear) and cyclic aging (power-law). The simple exponential is the **board-exam shortcut**; production code uses a two-factor model. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing PdM Algorithms | Algorithm | Type | Strength | Weakness | Best PdM Use-Case | |-----------|------|----------|----------|-------------------| | **Linear Regression** | Statistical | Interpretable, fast | Misses non-linearity | Trend extrapolation | | **Random Forest** | ML ensemble | Handles mixed features | Opaque, heavy | Fault classification | | **SVM (RBF kernel)** | ML | Strong on small data | Kernel tuning | Vibration anomaly detection | | **LSTM / GRU** | Deep learning | Captures temporal deps | Needs lots of data | RUL on time-series telemetry | | **Autoencoder** | Unsupervised DL | Anomaly detection w/o labels | False positives | Zero-shot fault discovery | | **Hidden Markov Model** | Probabilistic | Sequence modelling | State assumptions | Gearbox wear stages | | **Bayesian Network** | Probabilistic | Uncertainty quantification | Prior elicitation | Brake-disc RUL with priors | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 End-to-End Data Analytics Pipeline for Vehicle Maintenance Forecasting The figure below is a **block-level functional architecture** showing how raw CAN-bus telemetry is transformed into a prescriptive maintenance alert. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Vehicle Sensors
O2 Knock Temp Vibration GPS] --> B[OBD II Port and CAN Bus Gateway] B --> C[Edge ECU
Signal Conditioning] C --> D[Telematics Control Unit
4G or 5G Uplink] D --> E[(Cloud Data Lake
Raw and Curated)] E --> F[Pre Processing
Cleaning Imputation Normalization] F --> G[Feature Engineering
RMS Kurtosis FFT Spectral] G --> H{Prediction Engine} H --> H1[Descriptive Layer
Dashboards and KPIs] H --> H2[Diagnostic Layer
Root Cause and Fault Codes] H --> H3[Predictive Layer
RUL and Failure Probability] H --> H4[Prescriptive Layer
Optimal Service Schedule] H3 --> I[Threshold and Policy Engine] H4 --> I H2 --> I H1 --> I I --> J[Workshop Alert and OTA Patch] I --> K[Fleet Manager Dashboard] I --> L[Customer Mobile App Notification] ``` **How to read this diagram (for the exam):** Trace the data flow from physical sensors (left) to human-facing decisions (right). The *prediction engine* is a quad-layered AI service; the *threshold and policy engine* arbitrates between conflicting alerts (e.g., a brake-pad warning should override an oil-life warning for safety reasons). --- ### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Failure Regime Recognition via Weibull Shape Parameter ```mermaid flowchart LR P[Collect Time to Failure Data] --> Q[Fit Weibull MLE
Estimate Beta and Eta] Q --> R{Beta Value} R --> R1[Beta less than 1
Infant Mortality] R --> R2[Beta equals 1
Random Failures] R --> R3[Beta greater than 1
Wear Out Phase] R1 --> S1[Action Burn In Screening] R2 --> S2[Action Redundancy and Derating] R3 --> S3[Action Preventive Replacement before Eta] ``` **Reading guide:** This block flow encapsulates the diagnostic logic of the Weibull shape parameter $\beta$. For an exam question that gives a histogram of failure times, the student must identify the dominant $\beta$ regime and recommend the corresponding engineering action. --- ### 4.3 Functional Architecture — Anomaly Detection Sub-system ```mermaid flowchart TD M[Telemetry Stream Window t] --> N[Compute Z Score per Feature] N --> O{Absolute Z greater than 3} O --> P1[Flag as Point Anomaly] O --> P2[Mark Window as Normal] P1 --> Q[Aggregate Anomalies over 7 Day Window] Q --> R{Anomaly Density Threshold Crossed} R --> S1[Trigger Predictive Maintenance Workflow] R --> S2[Continue Passive Monitoring] ``` This sub-system corresponds to the **diagnostic-analytics layer** described in Section 2. It is intentionally lightweight so that the **edge ECU** can run it in real time before uplinking to the cloud. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]** **Q1.** Differentiate between *preventive*, *predictive*, and *prescriptive* maintenance strategies. Give one vehicle subsystem example for each. **Model Answer (for 3 marks):** | Strategy | Trigger | Vehicle Example | |----------|---------|-----------------| | Preventive | Fixed interval (every 10 000 km) | Engine oil change | | Predictive | Real-time sensor anomaly (e.g., coolant temp > 105 °C) | Radiator fan failure forecast | | Prescriptive | Optimisation output from RL / cost model | Optimal brake-pad replacement that minimises total fleet downtime | The student should explicitly mention that predictive uses **condition-monitoring data**, while prescriptive goes a step further to **recommend the action** that minimises a cost function. --- > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]** **Q2.** List any **four** data sources on a modern connected vehicle that feed the maintenance analytics pipeline. For each, name one feature that can be extracted for failure forecasting. **Model Answer (for 3 marks):** 1. **OBD-II / CAN bus** → engine RPM, coolant temperature, MAF sensor reading → combustion anomaly detection. 2. **GPS module** → speed profile, idle time → driver-behaviour scoring and clutch-wear estimation. 3. **TPMS sensors** → per-wheel pressure and temperature → slow-leak detection. 4. **Battery Management System (BMS)** → cell voltage, SoC, temperature → EV battery State-of-Health. *(Mentioning any four with feature-extraction linkage earns full 3 marks.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model Question]** #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a **data-analytics-driven predictive maintenance system** for a connected vehicle. List and justify the choice of any **three** condition-monitoring sensors. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A fleet of delivery vans uses brake pads whose time-to-failure follows a **Weibull distribution** with shape parameter $\beta = 2.1$ and scale parameter $\eta = 65{,}000$ km.     (i) Compute the reliability at $t = 40{,}000$ km.     (ii) Compute the failure rate $\lambda(t)$ at the same mileage.     (iii) Estimate the MTTF.     (iv) The fleet operates 250 days/year at 80 km/day. What is the expected number of brake-pad failures per year? **(7 marks)** --- **(a) Model Answer:** The architecture is best represented by the **block diagram of Section 4.1** (re-drawn in the answer sheet for marks). The four blocks the student must label: 1. **Data Acquisition Layer** — sensors, CAN bus, OBD-II gateway. 2. **Edge Processing** — local filtering, thresholding. 3. **Cloud Analytics** — feature engineering, ML model inference, RUL estimator. 4. **Decision / Actuation** — workshop alert, OTA patch, mobile notification. **Three condition-monitoring sensors (1 mark each — 3 marks total):** - **Knock sensor** → detects pre-ignition; early indicator of low-octane fuel use or carbon-deposit buildup in cylinders. - **Wheel-speed sensor + accelerometer** → enables ABS fault detection and brake-disc wear estimation through vibration spectral analysis. - **Coolant temperature + oil-pressure sensor** → jointly detect head-gasket failure precursors. *(Any other three sensors with correct engineering justification are acceptable.)* The remaining **1 mark** is for the diagram neatness, arrow directions, and labelled data flow. --- **(b) Model Answer:** **(i) Reliability at $t = 40{,}000$ km:** $$ R(40{,}000) = e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}} = e^{-(40000/65000)^{2.1}} $$ First compute the ratio: $$ \frac{t}{\eta} = \frac{40{,}000}{65{,}000} = 0.6154 $$ Then raise to the power 2.1: $$ (0.6154)^{2.1} = e^{2.1 \ln(0.6154)} = e^{2.1 \times (-0.4855)} = e^{-1.0196} = 0.3609 $$ Therefore: $$ R(40{,}000) = e^{-0.3609} \approx 0.6970 \quad \text{or} \quad 69.70\,\% $$ **(ii) Failure rate at $t = 40{,}000$ km:** $$ \lambda(t) = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta - 1} $$ Using $(t/\eta)^{\beta - 1} = (0.6154)^{1.1} = e^{1.1 \ln(0.6154)} = e^{-0.5340} = 0.5864$: $$ \lambda(40{,}000) = \frac{2.1}{65{,}000} \times 0.5864 = \frac{1.2314}{65{,}000} \approx 1.895 \times 10^{-5} \;\text{failures/km} $$ **(iii) MTTF:** $$ \text{MTTF} = \eta \,\Gamma\!\left(1 + \tfrac{1}{\beta}\right) = 65{,}000 \times \Gamma(1 + 1/2.1) $$ Compute the gamma argument: $1 + 1/2.1 = 1.4762$. Using $\Gamma(1.4762) \approx 0.8859$: $$ \text{MTTF} = 65{,}000 \times 0.8859 \approx 57{,}583 \;\text{km} $$ **(iv) Expected annual failures:** Annual mileage per van = $250 \times 80 = 20{,}000$ km. Cumulative failure probability by 20 000 km: $$ F(20{,}000) = 1 - e^{-(20000/65000)^{2.1}} = 1 - e^{-(0.3077)^{2.1}} $$ $(0.3077)^{2.1} = e^{2.1 \ln(0.3077)} = e^{2.1 \times (-1.1787)} = e^{-2.4753} = 0.0840$. $$ F(20{,}000) = 1 - e^{-0.0840} = 1 - 0.9194 = 0.0806 $$ If the fleet has $N$ vans, expected failures per year = $0.0806 \times N$. For a **fleet of 50 vans**: $$ \text{Expected failures} = 0.0806 \times 50 \approx 4.03 \;\text{failures/year} $$ So roughly **4 brake-pad replacements per year** in a 50-van fleet. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Internal alternative — any student may opt for this)* **(a)** Define the **Remaining Useful Life (RUL)** of a vehicle component. Show mathematically how RUL can be estimated from the Weibull reliability function. State one limitation of purely statistical RUL estimation. **(7 marks)** **(b)** The State-of-Health of an EV battery pack decays as $\text{SOH}(t) = e^{-k t}$ where $t$ is in equivalent full cycles. The pack reaches end-of-life at $\text{SOH} = 0.8$ after 1800 cycles.     (i) Find the decay constant $k$.     (ii) Predict the SOH at $t = 1000$ cycles.     (iii) Estimate the RUL (in cycles) when the current SOH reads 0.88.     (iv) If the vehicle averages 1.2 full charge-discharge cycles per day, convert this RUL into remaining years. **(7 marks)** --- **(a) Model Answer:** **Definition (2 marks):** RUL is the expected residual operating time (or mileage) of a component, conditioned on the fact that it has survived up to the present observation time $t$. Formally: $$ \text{RUL}(t) = E[T - t \,\vert\, T > t] $$ **Mathematical estimation from Weibull (3 marks):** Using $R(t) = e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$, the conditional survival function for the residual life $x = T - t$ is: $$ P(T - t > x \,\vert\, T > t) = \frac{R(t + x)}{R(t)} = e^{-[(t + x)/\eta]^{\beta} + (t/\eta)^{\beta}} $$ The mean residual life is therefore: $$ \text{RUL}(t) = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{R(t + x)}{R(t)}\, dx $$ For Weibull with $\beta > 1$, the integral can be expressed via the incomplete gamma function (closed-form given in Section 3.2 of these notes). **Limitation (2 marks):** Pure Weibull estimation is **population-level** — it averages over the entire fleet and ignores the specific wear signature of the individual vehicle (driving style, climate, terrain). For per-vehicle RUL, data-driven models (LSTM, Bayesian filters) must be fused with the Weibull prior — a technique called **particle-filter-based hybrid prognostics**. --- **(b) Model Answer:** **(i) Decay constant $k$:** Using $\text{SOH}(1800) = 0.8 = e^{-k \cdot 1800}$: $$ k = -\frac{\ln(0.8)}{1800} = \frac{0.22314}{1800} = 1.2397 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{cycle}^{-1} $$ **(ii) SOH at $t = 1000$ cycles:** $$ \text{SOH}(1000) = e^{-1.2397 \times 10^{-4} \times 1000} = e^{-0.1240} \approx 0.8834 $$ So the battery retains **88.34 %** of nominal capacity after 1000 cycles. **(iii) RUL at $\text{SOH} = 0.88$:** Solve $0.88 = e^{-k t_{\text{current}}}$: $$ t_{\text{current}} = -\frac{\ln(0.88)}{k} = \frac{0.12783}{1.2397 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 1031 \;\text{cycles} $$ End-of-life occurs at $t_{\text{EOL}} = 1800$ cycles, so: $$ \text{RUL} = 1800 - 1031 \approx 769 \;\text{cycles} $$ **(iv) Convert to years:** Daily cycle rate = 1.2 cycles/day, so cycles per year = $1.2 \times 365 = 438$ cycles/year. $$ \text{RUL in years} = \frac{769}{438} \approx 1.76 \;\text{years} $$ The driver has roughly **1 year and 9 months** before the battery reaches its 80 % end-of-life threshold. --- ### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Zones for This Topic** > > 1. **Unit Inconsistency (–2 marks):** Mixing hours and km without converting. Always state the conversion factor (e.g., "fleet average 60 km/h") in your solution. > 2. **Forgetting $R(0) = 1$ (–1 mark):** Reliability at zero time must equal 1. Weibull satisfies this automatically, but if a student writes $R(t) = 1 - e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$, they have inverted the formula — instant loss. > 3. **Confusing MTBF with MTTF (–1 mark):** MTBF applies to **repairable** systems; MTTF to **non-repairable** components. Brake pads = replaceable → use MTBF. Engine block = typically non-replaceable → use MTTF. > 4. **Skipping the Diagram (–2 to –3 marks on 14-mark Q):** In Question A(a), a missing block diagram forfeits the architecture mark. > 5. **Bayesian / ML Over-reach:** If the question asks for *statistical* RUL, do **not** quote LSTM equations. Stick to Weibull. Conversely, if it asks for *data-driven* RUL, do not quote only Weibull — credit is given for sensor-fusion logic. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Maintenance Hierarchy (ascending):** Reactive → Preventive → Predictive → Prescriptive. - **Analytics Hierarchy (ascending):** Descriptive → Diagnostic → Predictive → Prescriptive. - **Weibull is King:** Always the default reliability model in KTU board questions. Memorise $R(t)$, $f(t)$, $\lambda(t)$, and the MTTF formula. - **Shape Parameter $\beta$ Regimes:**   • $\beta < 1$ — infant mortality → burn-in screening.   • $\beta = 1$ — random failures (exponential).   • $\beta > 1$ — wear-out → preventive replacement. - **Three Core Reliability Identities (must be on your fingertips):**   1. $R(t) + F(t) = 1$   2. $\lambda(t) = f(t) / R(t)$   3. $\text{RUL}(t) = E[T - t \mid T > t]$ - **Fleet Availability Target:** Aim for $A > 0.95$; calculate via $A = \text{MTBF} / (\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR})$. - **Data Sources to Quote:** OBD-II, CAN bus, GPS, BMS, TPMS, accelerometers, microphones (NVH-based anomaly detection). - **EV Battery End-of-Life Threshold:** Universally **80 % of nominal capacity** ($SOH = 0.80$). - **Cost-Benefit:** PdM saves **12 – 18 %** in maintenance OPEX vs scheduled programs. - **Algorithm Shortlist:** Linear Regression → Random Forest → SVM → LSTM → Autoencoder → HMM → Bayesian Network. Pick the simplest model that solves the problem (Occam's Razor). - **Diagram Must-Have:** Sensor → CAN bus → Edge ECU → Telematics → Cloud → Pre-processing → Feature Engineering → ML Model → Decision Engine → Alert. ---

topics.title: Condition-Based Monitoring: AI for Monitoring Vehicle Health in Real-Time
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT865 - ADVANCED VEHICLE TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Condition-Based Monitoring: AI for Monitoring Vehicle Health in Real-Time

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Condition-Based Monitoring (CBM)** is a real-time, sensor-driven maintenance paradigm in which the actual operating condition of a vehicle's sub-system (engine, transmission, brakes, battery, suspension, etc.) is continuously observed, measured, and analysed using **Artificial Intelligence (AI)** and **Machine Learning (ML)** algorithms. Maintenance action is triggered *only* when the monitored parameters indicate degradation or an impending failure, rather than at fixed time intervals (preventive) or after a breakdown has occurred (reactive).

The modern formulation extends classical vibration-based condition monitoring to a **cyber-physical framework** where:

- Data acquisition happens through **on-board sensors** (accelerometers, current sensors, thermistors, pressure transducers, OBD-II ports).
- Inference happens either **on the edge** (ECU/AI accelerator) or in the **cloud** (telematics backend).
- Decisions are communicated to the driver (HMI) or directly to the actuator layer (prognostic control).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Vehicle as a Patient"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Car is a Patient in an ICU:**
> Imagine your car is a patient lying in an intensive care unit. Instead of a doctor visiting once a year for a "check-up" (preventive maintenance) or only being rushed to the ER after a heart attack (reactive maintenance), the patient is **wired with sensors 24/7** — heart rate, blood pressure, oxygen, temperature. An AI doctor watches every reading in real time and says, *"Your left brake caliper is showing abnormal thermal signature; failure predicted in 320 km — schedule service."* That is Condition-Based Monitoring.

## 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **CBM** | Condition-Based Monitoring |
| **PHM** | Prognostics & Health Management |
| **RUL** | Remaining Useful Life |
| **OBD** | On-Board Diagnostics (standardised SAE J1962 port) |
| **CAN Bus** | Controller Area Network (ISO 11898) |
| **ECU** | Electronic Control Unit |
| **Anomaly Score** | A scalar value quantifying deviation from "healthy" baseline |
| **Edge Inference** | Running the AI model locally on the vehicle's compute unit |
| **Digital Twin** | A live, virtual replica of the physical asset used for simulation & prediction |
| **TTF** | Time-To-Failure |

## 1.4 Why CBM Matters — The "Why" in KTU Context

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners expect students to articulate **why CBM has become the de-facto standard** in modern automotive engineering. The three drivers are:
> 1. **Safety** — Prevents catastrophic in-operation failures (brake fade, battery thermal runaway, steering loss).
> 2. **Economy** — Eliminates *over-maintenance* (worn parts replaced early) and *under-maintenance* (costly catastrophic failures). Industry data: CBM reduces maintenance cost by **20–30%** and downtime by **70–75%**.
> 3. **Emissions & Efficiency** — A poorly tuned engine can emit **up to 3× the regulated NOx**. Real-time monitoring keeps the after-treatment system within optimal operating window.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The CBM Operational Pipeline (Six Logical Steps)

> [!NOTE]
> The CBM pipeline is a chain of six transformations. **Master this — KTU regularly frames 7-mark questions around it.**

1. **Data Acquisition Layer** — Sensors (vibration, acoustic emission, temperature, current, voltage, oil-quality, GPS, OBD-II) sample at fixed rates (typically **1 kHz to 50 kHz** for vibration, **10 Hz** for temperature).
2. **Signal Pre-processing** — Anti-aliasing filtering, decimation, normalisation. The Nyquist criterion: $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$.
3. **Feature Extraction** — Statistical (RMS, kurtosis, skewness), spectral (FFT peaks, band power), or time-frequency (STFT, wavelet packet energy) features.
4. **AI/ML Inference** — The feature vector $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is fed into a trained model that outputs an **anomaly score** $S$ or a **class label** $y \in \{healthy, degraded, faulty\}$.
5. **Decision & Thresholding** — If $S > S_{th}$, an alert is raised. The threshold $S_{th}$ is typically chosen from a Receiver-Operating-Characteristic (ROC) curve to meet a target false-alarm rate $\alpha$ (often $\alpha = 0.01$).
6. **Prognostics (RUL Prediction)** — For degrading systems, predict the time/cycle/distance to failure: $RUL(t) = T_{fail} - t$.

## 2.2 The Maintenance Strategy Spectrum

| Strategy | Trigger | Cost | Risk |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Reactive (Run-to-Failure)** | Component breaks | Low upfront, very high downtime | Highest safety risk |
| **Preventive (Scheduled)** | Time / mileage interval | Medium | Wastes life, can miss random failures |
| **Predictive / CBM** | Real-time condition | Higher upfront, **lowest** lifecycle | Lowest risk if data is reliable |
| **Prescriptive** | CBM + optimisation | Highest | Specifies *what action* to take |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All equations below are **KTU 2024 Scheme examinable**. Memorise the units and boundary conditions.

| # | Equation | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\displaystyle a = \omega^{2} \cdot r$ | Vibration acceleration from rotating mass (e.g., wheel imbalance) |
| 2 | $\displaystyle f_{res} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$ | Natural frequency of a sprung / unsprung mass |
| 3 | $\displaystyle f_{s} \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Nyquist sampling criterion |
| 4 | $\displaystyle RMS = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}x_{i}^{2}}$ | Root-mean-square — proxy for vibration energy |
| 5 | $\displaystyle \text{Kurtosis} = \frac{\frac{1}{N}\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^{4}}{\left(\frac{1}{N}\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^{2}\right)^{2}}$ | Sensitive to impulsive faults (bearing spalls) |
| 6 | $\displaystyle \text{Crest Factor} = \frac{\vert x_{peak} \vert}{RMS}$ | Increases sharply with incipient bearing defects |
| 7 | $\displaystyle P(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{d/2}\vert\Sigma\vert^{1/2}}\exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x}-\mu)^{T}\Sigma^{-1}(\mathbf{x}-\mu)\right)$ | Gaussian anomaly model — Mahalanobis distance |
| 8 | $\displaystyle RUL(t) = T_{fail} - t$ | Remaining Useful Life definition |
| 9 | $\displaystyle h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{1 - F(t)}$ | Hazard rate (Cox / Weibull proportional hazards) |
| 10 | $\displaystyle \text{SOH}_{bat}(t) = \frac{C_{max}(t)}{C_{nominal}}$ | State-of-Health of an EV battery (0 to 100 %) |
| 11 | $\displaystyle \eta_{DCDC} = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = 1 - \frac{P_{loss}}{P_{in}}$ | Real-time efficiency monitor for power electronics |
| 12 | $\displaystyle \mathcal{L}_{CE} = -\sum_{i=1}^{C}y_i \log(\hat{y}_i)$ | Cross-entropy loss for classification CNN/RNN models |
| 13 | $\displaystyle \text{ReLU}(x) = \max(0,x)$ | Activation in deep networks |
| 14 | $\displaystyle \text{Confusion Matrix Accuracy} = \frac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN}$ | Performance metric for fault classifier |

> [!WARNING]
> When writing $RUL(t)$ or $P(\mathbf{x})$ on the answer sheet, **always** specify units (hours, km, cycles) and state the assumption (e.g., "assuming Weibull-distributed failure times with shape parameter $k$").

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **EV Battery Packs** — Continuous State-of-Health ($\text{SOH}$) estimation using recurrent neural networks on current/voltage/temperature time-series. Avoids the cost and risk of periodic capacity tests.
- **Brake-by-Wire Systems** — Acoustic emission sensors detect pad-wear and rotor-disc cracks *before* they reach the regulatory minimum thickness.
- **ICE Engines** — Oil-quality sensors + vibration analysis on the crankshaft detect bearing wear 200–400 hours before catastrophic seizure.
- **Tyre Pressure / TPMS** — Already mandatory in many jurisdictions; direct sensors report pressure every **~60 s** via RF to the ECU.
- **Connected Fleets** — Heavy commercial vehicles (trucks, buses) stream CAN-bus data to a telematics cloud, where ML models predict failures 5–10 days in advance, slashing fleet downtime.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The derivations below are **fully worked out** with no skipped algebraic steps, in line with KTU 2024 Scheme valuation expectations. Each step is followed by a `[Logic]` annotation explaining the engineering reasoning.

## 3.1 Derivation — Vibration Energy (RMS) of a Wheel Imbalance

A wheel of mass $m_w$ rotating at angular velocity $\omega$ has an unbalance mass $m_u$ at radius $r$. The centripetal force on the suspension generates an acceleration that the accelerometer registers.

**Step 1.** Centripetal force from the unbalanced mass:

$$F_c = m_u \, \omega^{2} \, r$$

[Logic] — Newton’s second law for circular motion; $F_c$ points radially inward.

**Step 2.** Acceleration transmitted to the sprung mass (assuming rigid coupling for simplicity):

$$a = \frac{F_c}{m_u} = \omega^{2} \, r$$

[Logic] — Dividing by the unbalanced mass gives the rotating-frame acceleration.

**Step 3.** The accelerometer samples this sinusoid $x(t) = A \sin(\omega t)$ where $A = \omega^{2} r$.

**Step 4.** RMS value over one full period $T = 2\pi/\omega$:

$$\begin{aligned}
RMS &= \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} x^{2}(t)\,dt} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} A^{2}\sin^{2}(\omega t)\,dt} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{A^{2}}{2T}\int_{0}^{T}\bigl(1-\cos(2\omega t)\bigr)\,dt} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{A^{2}}{2T}\Bigl[t - \frac{\sin(2\omega t)}{2\omega}\Bigr]_{0}^{T}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{A^{2}}{2T}\cdot T} = \frac{A}{\sqrt{2}}
\end{aligned}$$

[Logic] — Used the identity $\sin^{2}\theta = \frac{1-\cos(2\theta)}{2}$; the cosine term integrates to zero over a full period.

**Step 5.** Substitute $A = \omega^{2}r$:

$$RMS = \frac{\omega^{2} r}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\,\omega^{2}\,r$$

[Logic] — Final form. Note that RMS scales with the **square of the rotational speed**, so a wheel imbalance fault becomes dramatically more severe at highway speeds.

**Step 6. Diagnostic Rule** — If $RMS$ at a given $\omega$ exceeds the baseline by a factor $k$ (typically $k \geq 1.5$ for road wheels, $k \geq 2.0$ for engine bearings), the system flags an anomaly.

## 3.2 Derivation — Bearing Fault Frequency (BPFO)

A localised defect on the outer race of a rolling-element bearing strikes once per shaft revolution. With $N$ balls, ball diameter $d$, pitch diameter $D$, and contact angle $\phi$:

**Step 1.** The geometric relationship for outer-race fault frequency:

$$f_{BPFO} = \frac{N}{2}\,f_{shaft}\,\left(1 - \frac{d}{D}\cos\phi\right)$$

[Logic] — Each ball passes the defect zone once per revolution; the factor $(1 - d\cos\phi/D)$ accounts for the rolling geometry.

**Step 2.** Numerical example: $N = 12$, $f_{shaft} = 50\,\text{Hz}$, $d = 10\,\text{mm}$, $D = 60\,\text{mm}$, $\phi = 0$:

$$\begin{aligned}
f_{BPFO} &= \frac{12}{2}\cdot 50 \cdot \left(1 - \frac{10}{60}\cos 0\right) \\
&= 6 \cdot 50 \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1}{6}\right) \\
&= 300 \cdot \frac{5}{6} \\
&= 250 \;\text{Hz}
\end{aligned}$$

[Logic] — The AI model is trained to look for an energy spike at 250 Hz and its harmonics $(500, 750, \ldots)$ in the FFT spectrum. If the **envelope spectrum** of the vibration signal shows a peak at $f_{BPFO}$, an outer-race defect is confirmed.

## 3.3 Derivation — Anomaly Score using Mahalanobis Distance

Suppose a healthy vehicle produces a feature vector $\mathbf{x} = [x_1, x_2]^{T}$ drawn from a multivariate Gaussian with mean $\boldsymbol{\mu}$ and covariance $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$. The Mahalanobis distance:

**Step 1.** Definition:

$$D_{M}(\mathbf{x}) = \sqrt{(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})^{T}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})}$$

[Logic] — Standardises the deviation by the covariance, making it scale- and correlation-invariant.

**Step 2.** For a healthy operating region, $D_M$ follows a $\chi^{2}$ distribution with $d$ degrees of freedom (here $d=2$).

**Step 3.** Threshold for a 99 % confidence level: $D_{M,th} = \sqrt{\chi^{2}_{2,\,0.99}} = \sqrt{9.21} \approx 3.035$.

**Step 4.** Decision rule:

$$\text{State} = \begin{cases} \text{Healthy}, & D_{M}(\mathbf{x}) \le D_{M,th} \\ \text{Anomalous}, & D_{M}(\mathbf{x}) > D_{M,th} \end{cases}$$

[Logic] — The "anomaly score" $S$ is the Mahalanobis distance itself; the threshold is set by the desired false-alarm rate $\alpha$.

## 3.4 Derivation — EV Battery State-of-Health (Coulomb-Counting Method)

A simple yet important CBM metric. The maximum releasable capacity of a Li-ion cell degrades over charge-discharge cycles.

**Step 1.** Measure the actual charge extracted during a full discharge from $V_{max}$ to $V_{min}$:

$$Q_{actual} = \int_{0}^{t_{end}} I(t)\,dt$$

[Logic] — Coulomb-counting integrates the current.

**Step 2.** Compare to the rated nominal capacity $Q_{nom}$:

$$SOH(t) = \frac{Q_{actual}(t)}{Q_{nominal}} \times 100\%$$

**Step 3.** End-of-life criterion (industry standard): $SOH \le 80\%$.

**Step 4.** Worked example — a pack rated $Q_{nominal} = 60\,\text{Ah}$ delivers only $Q_{actual} = 52\,\text{Ah}$ after 3 years:

$$SOH = \frac{52}{60}\times 100\% = 86.7\%$$

The AI system would predict $RUL$ to reach $80\%$ using an exponential decay fit: $SOH(t) = 100 \cdot e^{-\lambda t}$, solving for $t$ when $SOH=80$ gives $t_{EOL} = -\ln(0.8)/\lambda$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> All diagrams below are **Mermaid block-level functional architectures**. They map sensor → edge AI → cloud → driver, exactly as the KTU 2024 scheme expects students to draw in the exam.

## 4.1 High-Level CBM System Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[PHYSICAL VEHICLE] --> B[SENSOR LAYER]
    B --> C[ECU GATEWAY]
    C --> D[EDGE AI PROCESSOR]

    D --> F[CLOUD TELEMATICS]

    J --> A
```

| Node | Function |
|---|---|
| A | Physical Vehicle | The actual car, truck, or two-wheeler |
| B | Sensor Layer | Accelerometers, thermistors, current shunts, OBD-II, oil-quality, microphones |
| C | ECU Gateway | CAN/LIN bus aggregation, message filtering |
| D | Edge AI Processor | Runs inference on-device (TPU/NPU) |
| E | Alert and HMI | Dashboard warning, mobile app notification |
| F | Cloud Telematics | Long-term storage, fleet-level analytics |
| G | Fleet Manager Dashboard | Web UI for commercial operators |
| H | Digital Twin | Virtual replica used for what-if simulation |
| I | Prognostic Decisions | RUL predictions and recommended actions |
| J | Actuator Layer | Engine, brake, steering, battery management |

## 4.2 AI Inference Pipeline (Inside the Edge Processor)

```mermaid
    S2 --> S3[NORMALISATION]

```
