<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Differentiate between preventive maintenance, predictive maintenance, and condition-based monitoring. Mention one application for each in the automotive context.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Preventive maintenance** is performed at fixed time or mileage intervals regardless of the actual component condition — e.g., engine-oil replacement every 10 000 km. *(1 mark)*
2. **Predictive maintenance** anticipates the time of failure using historical trends and statistical models and schedules intervention just before predicted failure — e.g., fleet-level truck brake-pad replacement scheduled from regression on pad-wear data. *(1 mark)*
3. **Condition-based monitoring (CBM)** continuously measures real-time parameters via sensors and triggers action only when the parameter crosses a defined threshold — e.g., TPMS triggering a low-pressure warning when tyre pressure drops below 30 psi. *(1 mark)*

### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**List any three sensors used in condition-based monitoring of vehicles and state the parameter each one measures.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Accelerometer (piezoelectric / MEMS)** — measures vibration acceleration in g (gravitational) units; used for bearing, engine, and chassis diagnostics. *(1 mark)*
2. **Thermistor / RTD** — measures temperature in °C; used for battery thermal management, brake-disc heat, and coolant monitoring. *(1 mark)*
3. **Current shunt / Hall-effect sensor** — measures DC current in amperes; used for battery State-of-Health and motor-controller health. *(1 mark)*

*(Acceptable alternatives: oil-quality sensor, acoustic-emission microphone, GPS/IMU, OBD-II, wheel-speed sensor.)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Following the KTU 2024 ESE pattern, **answer ANY ONE** of the two full questions below. Each carries 14 marks split across sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of an AI-based real-time vehicle health-monitoring system**. Describe the function of each block in detail. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the **Mahalanobis-distance-based anomaly detection rule** for a 2-D feature vector and explain how the threshold is selected from a $\chi^{2}$ distribution for a 1 % false-alarm rate. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks

The architecture (drawn from Section 4.1) consists of **five main blocks** (1 mark each, plus 2 marks for description):

1. **Sensor Layer** — Multi-modal sensors (accelerometer, thermistor, current sensor, oil-quality sensor, OBD-II). Function: convert physical phenomena into electrical signals.
2. **Data Acquisition / ECU Gateway** — Samples signals at $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$, applies anti-aliasing, packetises on the CAN/LIN bus, time-stamps.
3. **Edge AI Processor (On-board)** — Runs pre-trained ML models (CNN, LSTM, Autoencoder) for low-latency inference. Performs feature extraction, anomaly scoring, and RUL prediction.
4. **Cloud Telematics Backend** — Aggregates long-term data, retrains models, hosts the **Digital Twin**, and provides fleet-level dashboards.
5. **Actuator / HMI Layer** — Displays warnings, modulates engine mapping, limits torque, contacts emergency services, or schedules service.

**Conclusion (extra 1 mark):** The system is *closed-loop* — actuator responses feed back into the physical vehicle, completing the cyber-physical loop.

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Define the 2-D feature vector $\mathbf{x} = [x_1, x_2]^{T}$ extracted from the vibration signal (e.g., RMS and kurtosis).

**Step 2 (2 marks):** During a healthy baseline run, collect $N$ samples and compute the mean $\boldsymbol{\mu}$ and covariance $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$.

$$\boldsymbol{\mu} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\mathbf{x}_i, \qquad \boldsymbol{\Sigma} = \frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(\mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu})(\mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu})^{T}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Define Mahalanobis distance and explain its meaning.

$$D_{M}(\mathbf{x}) = \sqrt{(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})^{T}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})}$$

It is dimensionless, correlation-aware, and scale-invariant.

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Under the null hypothesis (healthy), $D_M^{2} \sim \chi^{2}_{d}$ with $d=2$ degrees of freedom. For $\alpha = 0.01$, the critical value is $\chi^{2}_{2,\,0.99} = 9.21$, so the threshold is:

$$D_{M,\,th} = \sqrt{9.21} \approx 3.035$$

**Decision rule:** Raise an alert if $D_{M}(\mathbf{x}) > 3.035$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> - *Do not* confuse **Euclidean** distance with **Mahalanobis** distance. Mahalanobis accounts for correlation between features.
> - Always specify the **degrees of freedom** of the $\chi^2$ distribution; it equals the **dimension** of the feature vector, not the number of samples.
> - State the **false-alarm rate** explicitly. A solution that computes a threshold without justifying it loses 1–2 marks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**(a)** What is the **State-of-Health (SOH)** of an EV battery? Derive the **coulomb-counting formula** for SOH estimation. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A wheel of a four-wheeler has an unbalance mass of **50 g** at a radius of **30 cm** while rotating at **120 km/h**. Compute the **vibration acceleration** and the **RMS value** experienced at the wheel hub. Use the value to comment on the diagnostic threshold for road-wheel imbalance. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 (1 mark): Definition.**
State-of-Health ($SOH$) is the ratio of the *currently deliverable maximum capacity* of a battery to its *rated nominal capacity*, expressed as a percentage.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Define the symbols.

- $Q_{nominal}$ — rated capacity in Ah.
- $Q_{actual}(t)$ — charge extracted during a full discharge at time $t$.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Derive from the definition of capacity as the integral of current over a full discharge.

$$Q_{actual}(t) = \int_{0}^{t_{end}} I(\tau)\,d\tau$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Substituting into the ratio:

$$SOH(t) = \frac{Q_{actual}(t)}{Q_{nominal}}\times 100\%$$

**End-of-life criterion:** $SOH \le 80\%$ (industry standard for automotive Li-ion packs).

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks

**Given:** $m_u = 0.05\,\text{kg}$, $r = 0.30\,\text{m}$, $v = 120\,\text{km/h}$.

**Step 1 (1 mark): Convert to angular velocity.**

$$v = 120 \times \frac{1000}{3600} = 33.33\,\text{m/s}$$

Assuming standard wheel rolling radius $R_w = 0.30\,\text{m}$ (equal to unbalance radius for simplicity in a textbook problem):

$$\omega = \frac{v}{R_w} = \frac{33.33}{0.30} = 111.1\,\text{rad/s}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark): Convert to frequency and RPM for the wheel hub.**

$$f = \frac{\omega}{2\pi} = \frac{111.1}{2\pi} \approx 17.68\,\text{Hz} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; n \approx 1061\,\text{rpm}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks): Acceleration from unbalance.**

$$a = \omega^{2}\,r = (111.1)^{2}\times 0.30 = 3703\,\text{m/s}^{2} = 377.6\,g$$

(where $g = 9.81\,\text{m/s}^{2}$).

**Step 4 (1 mark): RMS value of the sinusoidal acceleration.**

$$a_{RMS} = \frac{a}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{3703}{1.414} \approx 2619\,\text{m/s}^{2} \approx 267\,g$$

**Step 5 (2 marks): Diagnostic comment.**
A road-wheel imbalance of only 50 g at 120 km/h generates a peak acceleration of **~378 g** at the hub. Such a level is easily measurable by a MEMS accelerometer (typical range $\pm 16\,g$ to $\pm 200\,g$). KTU / industry practice sets a **diagnostic threshold of 1.5× to 2× the healthy baseline RMS**; in this case, an accelerometer mounted on the knuckle will reliably detect the imbalance well before the driver feels a vibration at the steering wheel (which typically requires $a > 1$–$2\,g$ at the seat-track reference point).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> - *Unit conversion errors* (km/h → m/s) are the single most common mistake. Always show the factor $1000/3600$.
> - Confusing **peak** with **RMS** acceleration. The question explicitly asks for both.
> - Forgetting to specify the **rolling radius** — a numerical answer with no assumption is penalised.
> - Failing to convert $a$ into **g** before comparing to sensor range.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> Final-minute revision pointer list. Read **twice** before entering the exam hall.

- [x] **CBM = Real-time + sensor-driven + AI-decided** maintenance. Always mention all three attributes.
- [x] Know the **six-block CBM pipeline**: Sensors → Acquisition → Pre-processing → Feature Extraction → AI Inference → Decision/Prognostics.
- [x] Master the **vibration formulas**: $a = \omega^2 r$, $RMS = A/\sqrt{2}$, $f_{res} = (1/2\pi)\sqrt{k/m}$.
- [x] Remember the **Nyquist criterion** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ and the role of anti-aliasing filters.
- [x] **Bearing fault frequencies** $f_{BPFO}$, $f_{BPFI}$, $f_{BSF}$, $f_{FTF}$ — only $f_{BPFO}$ was derived here; the examiner can ask for any.
- [x] **Kurtosis** is the most sensitive scalar feature for impulsive (spall-type) bearing faults.
- [x] **Mahalanobis distance** > Euclidean distance for anomaly detection; threshold comes from $\chi^2$.
- [x] For **EV batteries**: $SOH = Q_{actual}/Q_{nominal}$; **EOL = 80%**.
- [x] For **RUL prediction**: $RUL(t) = T_{fail} - t$; methods include Weibull, Cox PH, LSTM, Particle Filter.
- [x] **ML model choice**: CNN for images/signals, RNN/LSTM/Transformer for time-series, Autoencoder/Isolation-Forest for unlabelled anomaly detection.
- [x] The **Digital Twin** mirrors the physical vehicle in software and is used for what-if simulations.
- [x] Cite **standards** when relevant — ISO 26262 (functional safety), SAE J1939 (CAN for heavy vehicles), SAE J1962 (OBD-II connector).
- [x] Always end an answer with a **one-line conclusion** or **engineering significance** sentence — this is the easiest free mark.
- [x] In numericals, **state every assumption** (e.g., "assume wheel rolling radius = 0.30 m", "assume contact angle $\phi = 0$").

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: AI for Diagnostic Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT865 - ADVANCED VEHICLE TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** AI for Diagnostic Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **AI for Diagnostic Systems (Automotive Context)**
> An **AI-Driven Diagnostic System** in a vehicle is an intelligent computational framework that integrates **on-board diagnostics (OBD) data**, **multi-sensor telemetry**, **machine learning (ML)**, **deep learning (DL)**, and **knowledge-based expert systems** to automatically detect, classify, predict, and isolate faults in vehicle sub-systems (powertrain, battery, brakes, ADAS, etc.) with minimal human intervention.

According to the **KTU 2024 PEAUT865** syllabus framework, AI for diagnostics bridges three core domains:

1. **Data Acquisition Layer** — CAN bus, OBD-II, IoT edge sensors.
2. **Intelligence Layer** — ML classifiers, deep neural networks, fuzzy logic, Bayesian inference.
3. **Decision Layer** — Prognostics, remaining-useful-life (RUL) estimation, repair advisories.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Vehicle Doctor Inside"

Imagine a car is a **human body**. Just like a doctor uses:
- A **stethoscope** (sensor),
- **Blood reports** (OBD data),
- **Pattern memory** from thousands of past patients (training dataset),

the AI diagnostic system listens to the vehicle's "heartbeat" (vibration, current, temperature signals), compares it against millions of stored patterns, and tells you *exactly* what's wrong — often *before the check-engine light even turns on*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Distinction:**
> - **Fault Detection** → *Is something wrong?* (Binary classification)
> - **Fault Diagnosis** → *What is wrong and where?* (Multi-class classification / localization)
> - **Fault Prognosis** → *How long until failure?* (Time-series regression / RUL estimation)

## 1.3 Core AI Techniques Used in Vehicle Diagnostics

| Technique | Diagnostic Role | Typical Vehicle Use-Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Artificial Neural Networks (ANN)** | Pattern recognition in sensor data | Engine misfire detection |
| **Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN)** | Image/spectrogram fault classification | Brake disc wear from camera images |
| **Recurrent Neural Networks (RNN / LSTM)** | Time-series fault evolution | Battery State-of-Health (SOH) tracking |
| **Support Vector Machines (SVM)** | High-dimensional fault classification | EV motor insulation fault |
| **Random Forest / XGBoost** | Structured DTC code classification | OBD-II DTC mapping |
| **Autoencoders** | Anomaly detection | Detecting unknown cyber-attacks on CAN bus |
| **Fuzzy Logic + Expert Systems** | Rule-based reasoning | Emission control diagnostics |
| **Bayesian Networks** | Uncertainty quantification | Sensor fusion for ADAS fault tolerance |

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following **standardized automotive diagnostic metrics** are critical for KTU numerical/analytical questions:

- **OBD-II Standard Sampling Rate:** $\mathbf{100\,\text{Hz}}$ for fast CAN signals
- **CAN Bus Data Rate:** $\mathbf{500\,\text{kbps}}$ (high-speed CAN) / $\mathbf{125\,\text{kbps}}$ (low-speed CAN)
- **Battery SOH Threshold for Replacement:** $\mathbf{80\%}$ (industry standard, IEEE 1188)
- **Diagnostic Trouble Code (DTC) Format:** $\mathbf{P}$ (Powertrain), $\mathbf{C}$ (Chassis), $\mathbf{B}$ (Body), $\mathbf{U}$ (Network)
- **Standard SAE J1939 Fault Severity Levels:** $\mathbf{0\text{–}7}$ (0 = highest severity)
- **Typical ML Training Dataset Size:** $\mathbf{\geq 10{,}000}$ labeled samples per fault class
- **Target Diagnostic Accuracy:** $\mathbf{\geq 95\%}$ for production-grade systems
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of an AI Diagnostic Pipeline

The standard **5-stage AI diagnostic architecture** adopted across modern vehicles:

### Stage 1 — Data Acquisition
- Sensors: temperature, pressure, current, voltage, vibration, acoustic, IMU.
- Communication: CAN, LIN, FlexRay, Ethernet (100BASE-T1).
- Storage: circular buffer, edge memory (typically $\mathbf{2\text{–}8\,\text{GB}}$).

### Stage 2 — Pre-processing
- Noise filtering: **Kalman filter**, **moving average**, **wavelet denoising**.
- Normalization: Min-Max scaling to $[0,1]$:
  $$x_{\text{norm}} = \frac{x - x_{\min}}{x_{\max} - x_{\min}}$$

### Stage 3 — Feature Extraction
- **Time-domain:** Mean, RMS, kurtosis, skewness, crest factor.
- **Frequency-domain:** FFT peaks, spectral entropy.
- **Time-frequency:** Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT), Continuous Wavelet Transform (CWT).

### Stage 4 — AI Inference Engine
- Classification model outputs fault label + confidence score.
- Regression model outputs **RUL** (Remaining Useful Life).

### Stage 5 — Decision Output & HMI
- Driver alert, service scheduling, OTA firmware update trigger.

## 2.2 Why AI Over Traditional Rule-Based Diagnostics?

| Aspect | Rule-Based (Legacy) | AI-Based (Modern) |
|---|---|---|
| **Handles Unknown Faults** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (anomaly detection) |
| **Adapts Over Time** | ❌ Static thresholds | ✅ Online learning |
| **Sensor Noise Robustness** | ⚠ Moderate | ✅ High |
| **Multi-fault Isolation** | ❌ Sequential, slow | ✅ Parallel, real-time |
| **Computational Demand** | ✅ Low (ECU-friendly) | ⚠ Higher (needs ECU + cloud) |
| **Interpretability** | ✅ Fully transparent | ⚠ Black-box (XAI needed) |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Purpose / Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $x_{\text{norm}} = \dfrac{x - x_{\min}}{x_{\max} - x_{\min}}$ | Min-Max normalization for ML input features |
| 2 | $\text{RMS} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}x_i^2}$ | Vibration signal energy indicator for bearing/gear fault |
| 3 | $\text{SOH} = \dfrac{C_{\text{actual}}}{C_{\text{rated}}} \times 100\%$ | State-of-Health metric for EV battery |
| 4 | $\text{RUL} = T_{\text{failure}} - T_{\text{current}}$ | Remaining Useful Life (prognostics) |
| 5 | $\text{Accuracy} = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Standard classifier performance |
| 6 | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}$ | Balanced metric for imbalanced fault datasets |
| 7 | $\sigma(x) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-x}}$ | Sigmoid activation (binary fault output) |
| 8 | $\text{Loss}_{\text{CE}} = -\sum_{i} y_i \log(\hat{y}_i)$ | Cross-entropy loss for multi-class fault classification |
| 9 | $\hat{y} = W \cdot x + b$ | Linear neuron forward pass |
| 10 | $w_{t+1} = w_t - \eta \nabla L(w_t)$ | Gradient descent weight update ($\eta$ = learning rate) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Boundary Values for KTU Problems:**
> - **Battery SOH End-of-Life:** $\mathbf{80\%}$ (IEEE 1188 standard)
> - **OBD-II DTC Severity-1 (Emission-Critical):** Code P0xxx family
> - **Typical Inference Latency for Real-Time Diagnostics:** $\mathbf{\leq 10\,\text{ms}}$
> - **CAN Frame Standard ID Length:** $\mathbf{11\,\text{bits}}$ (extended: $\mathbf{29\,\text{bits}}$)

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

AI diagnostic systems are **not academic novelties** — they directly enable:

- **Tesla's Over-the-Air (OTA) Predictive Maintenance:** ML models flag battery module degradation 2–4 weeks before failure.
- **BMW's Condition Based Service (CBS):** Uses sensor ML to compute exact oil-change intervals.
- **Bosch's eCALL / EDR Systems:** Crash diagnostics combined with AI triage.
- **NASCAR & F1 Telemetry:** Real-time AI fault prediction across $\mathbf{300+}$ sensors at $\mathbf{1\,\text{kHz}}$ per channel.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Battery State-of-Health (SOH) Estimation using LSTM

### Problem Setup
A KTU numerical problem typically states:
*"Given the rated capacity of an EV battery pack as $\mathbf{75\,\text{Ah}}$, after 800 charge-discharge cycles the measured capacity is $\mathbf{69\,\text{Ah}}$. Compute the SOH and determine whether the battery requires replacement as per IEEE 1188. An LSTM model predicts RUL = 320 cycles. Compute the projected end-of-life cycle number."*

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the relevant formulas.**

We need two relations:
- **State-of-Health (SOH):**
  $$\text{SOH} = \frac{C_{\text{actual}}}{C_{\text{rated}}} \times 100\%$$
- **Remaining Useful Life (RUL):**
  $$\text{RUL} = N_{\text{EOL}} - N_{\text{current}}$$

**Step 2 — Extract given numerical values.**

We are told:
- $C_{\text{rated}} = 75\,\text{Ah}$
- $C_{\text{actual}} = 69\,\text{Ah}$
- $N_{\text{current}} = 800\,\text{cycles}$
- $\text{RUL}_{\text{LSTM}} = 320\,\text{cycles}$

**Step 3 — Compute SOH.**

$$\text{SOH} = \frac{69}{75} \times 100\%$$

$$\text{SOH} = 0.92 \times 100\%$$

$$\boxed{\text{SOH} = 92\%}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the IEEE 1188 replacement criterion.**

The IEEE 1188 standard mandates battery replacement when:
$$\text{SOH} \leq 80\%$$

Since $92\% > 80\%$, the battery is **healthy and does not yet require replacement**.

**Step 5 — Compute the projected end-of-life cycle number.**

$$N_{\text{EOL}} = N_{\text{current}} + \text{RUL}_{\text{LSTM}}$$

$$N_{\text{EOL}} = 800 + 320$$

$$\boxed{N_{\text{EOL}} = 1120\,\text{cycles}}$$

**Step 6 — Engineering interpretation.**

At the current degradation rate, the battery will reach the **80% SOH threshold** approximately at cycle 1120, after which the Battery Management System (BMS) must issue a replacement advisory.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Confusion Matrix for a Multi-Class Fault Classifier

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most frequently asked analytical structure** in KTU ESE questions on AI diagnostics.

For a **3-class classifier** (Normal / Bearing Fault / Misalignment Fault), suppose the test set has 100 samples and the model's predictions yield:

| Actual \ Predicted | Normal (P) | Bearing (B) | Misalignment (M) | **Total** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Normal (P)** | 38 | 2 | 0 | 40 |
| **Bearing (B)** | 1 | 27 | 2 | 30 |
| **Misalignment (M)** | 1 | 1 | 28 | 30 |
| **Total** | 40 | 30 | 30 | 100 |

### Step 1 — Compute Class-wise Accuracy (Recall)

$$\text{Recall}_{P} = \frac{TP_P}{40} = \frac{38}{40} = 0.95$$

$$\text{Recall}_{B} = \frac{27}{30} = 0.90$$

$$\text{Recall}_{M} = \frac{28}{30} \approx 0.933$$

### Step 2 — Compute Overall Accuracy

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP_P + TP_B + TP_M}{N_{\text{total}}}$$

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{38 + 27 + 28}{100} = \frac{93}{100}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Accuracy} = 93\%}$$

### Step 3 — Compute Macro-Averaged $F_1$ Score

For each class, compute Precision and $F_1$:

$$\text{Precision}_P = \frac{38}{40} = 0.95$$

$$\text{Precision}_B = \frac{27}{30} = 0.90$$

$$\text{Precision}_M = \frac{28}{30} \approx 0.933$$

$$F_{1,P} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.95 \cdot 0.95}{0.95 + 0.95} = 0.95$$

$$F_{1,B} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.90 \cdot 0.90}{0.90 + 0.90} = 0.90$$

$$F_{1,M} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.933 \cdot 0.933}{0.933 + 0.933} \approx 0.933$$

$$\boxed{F_{1,\text{macro}} = \frac{0.95 + 0.90 + 0.933}{3} \approx 0.928}$$

### Step 4 — Engineering Verdict

Since accuracy $\geq 95\%$ is the production benchmark and $F_1 \geq 0.90$ is the safety threshold, this classifier is **acceptable for deployment** in non-safety-critical fault monitoring, but the **Bearing class recall (90%)** should be improved using SMOTE oversampling before safety-critical deployment.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Vibration RMS for Bearing Fault Threshold

**Step 1 — RMS formula for discrete vibration signal:**

$$V_{\text{RMS}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}v_i^2}$$

**Step 2 — Fault threshold (ISO 10816 standard for vehicle drivetrain):**

$$V_{\text{RMS}} > 4.5\,\text{mm/s} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Bearing degradation alert}$$

**Step 3 — Engineering logic:**
- If $V_{\text{RMS}} \leq 2.8\,\text{mm/s}$ → Healthy zone.
- If $2.8 < V_{\text{RMS}} \leq 4.5\,\text{mm/s}$ → Acceptable but monitor.
- If $V_{\text{RMS}} > 4.5\,\text{mm/s}$ → AI triggers service alert.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End AI Diagnostic System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vehicle Sensors] --> B[CAN Bus Data Acquisition]
    B --> C[Edge Pre-processing Unit]
    C --> D[Feature Extraction Module]
    D --> E{AI Inference Engine}
    E -->|Classify| F[Fault Type Classifier]
    E -->|Regression| G[RUL Predictor LSTM]
    E -->|Anomaly| H[Autoencoder Detector]
    F --> I[Decision Fusion Layer]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Driver HMI Alert]
    I --> K[Cloud Telemetry Upload]
    I --> L[OTA Service Scheduler]
    K --> M[Fleet Management Dashboard]
```

## 4.2 Deep Learning Model Hierarchy for Vehicle Faults

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Raw Sensor Stream] --> A2[FFT / Wavelet Transform]
    A2 --> A3[Spectrogram Image]
    A3 --> A4[CNN Feature Extractor]
    A4 --> A5[Fault Class Output]
    A6[Time-Series Sequence] --> A7[LSTM Network]
    A7 --> A8[RUL in Cycles]
    A9[CAN Frame Log] --> A10[Random Forest]
    A10 --> A11[DTC Code Prediction]
```

## 4.3 Decision Flow for Predictive Maintenance Trigger

```mermaid

    S1[Sensor Reading In] --> S2{RMS exceeds threshold}
    S2 -->|No| S3[Continue Normal Operation]

    S5 -->|Yes| S6[Trigger Driver Alert]

```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define an AI-based vehicle diagnostic system. List any two advantages over conventional rule-based diagnostics.**

**Model Answer:**

An AI-based vehicle diagnostic system is an intelligent computational framework that uses **machine learning algorithms** (such as neural networks, SVM, random forest) and **sensor data** from the vehicle's CAN bus to automatically detect, classify, and predict faults in mechanical, electrical, and electronic sub-systems.

**Advantages over rule-based systems:**
1. It can detect **previously unseen / unknown faults** using anomaly detection (e.g., autoencoders), which rule-based threshold methods cannot.
2. It **adapts over time** through online/transfer learning, whereas rule-based systems require manual threshold re-tuning.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition + 2 for advantages)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is a Diagnostic Trouble Code (DTC)? Explain the P0xxx family with one example.**

**Model Answer:**

A **DTC (Diagnostic Trouble Code)** is a standardized 5-character alphanumeric code defined by the **SAE J2012 / ISO 15031-6** standard that uniquely identifies a specific vehicle fault reported by the On-Board Diagnostic (OBD-II) system.

**Structure breakdown:**
- **1st character** — System category:
  - **P** = Powertrain (engine, transmission)
  - **C** = Chassis (brakes, steering, suspension)
  - **B** = Body (airbags, lighting, HVAC)
  - **U** = Network/Communication (CAN, LIN)
- **2nd character** — Code type (0 = Generic SAE, 1 = Manufacturer-specific).
- **3rd digit** — Subsystem.
- **4th & 5th digits** — Specific fault number.

**P0xxx Example:** `P0301` → **Cylinder 1 Misfire Detected** (a generic SAE powertrain fault).

*(3 marks — 2 for explanation + 1 for example)*

---

## Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

#### (a) **[7 Marks]** Explain the complete architecture of an AI-driven vehicle diagnostic system. With the help of a block diagram, describe the role of each layer from sensor acquisition to driver alert.

**Model Answer:**

The AI diagnostic architecture is typically organized into **five functional layers**:

**Layer 1 — Sensor & Data Acquisition Layer:**
This layer comprises physical sensors (temperature, pressure, vibration, current, voltage, acoustic, IMU) connected to ECUs via the **CAN bus** (typically $\mathbf{500\,\text{kbps}}$ for powertrain). The **OBD-II port** allows external diagnostic tools to retrieve standardized parameters like engine RPM, coolant temperature, MAF, and DTCs.

**Layer 2 — Pre-processing Layer:**
Raw signals contain noise from electromagnetic interference and sensor drift. Techniques used include:
- **Kalman filtering** for state estimation under noise.
- **Moving average / median filters** for spike removal.
- **Min-Max normalization** to bring features to the $[0,1]$ range for stable neural network training.

**Layer 3 — Feature Extraction Layer:**
Engineered features are extracted in three domains:
- **Time-domain:** Mean, RMS, kurtosis, crest factor.
- **Frequency-domain:** FFT peaks (e.g., bearing race frequencies: BPFO, BPFI).
- **Time-frequency domain:** STFT and CWT spectrograms (input to CNNs).

**Layer 4 — AI Inference Layer:**
Three parallel models operate:
1. **CNN** for image/spectrogram fault classification.
2. **LSTM** for time-series RUL prediction.
3. **Autoencoder** for unsupervised anomaly detection on novel/unknown faults.

**Layer 5 — Decision & Output Layer:**
The fused decision drives the **Human-Machine Interface (HMI)** — dashboard warning, infotainment notification — and triggers **OTA diagnostics upload** to the manufacturer's cloud fleet management system.

*(Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 for the visual schematic.)*

#### (b) **[7 Marks]** With respect to an EV battery, compute the State-of-Health (SOH) given: rated capacity = 60 Ah, measured capacity = 54 Ah. An LSTM model predicts the RUL as 450 cycles at the current cycle count of 600. Determine if replacement is needed as per IEEE 1188, and compute the projected end-of-life cycle number.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — SOH calculation:**
$$\text{SOH} = \frac{C_{\text{actual}}}{C_{\text{rated}}} \times 100\% = \frac{54}{60} \times 100\% = 90\%$$

**Step 2 — IEEE 1188 compliance check:**
The standard mandates replacement when $\text{SOH} \leq 80\%$. Since $90\% > 80\%$, **the battery does not yet require replacement** but is approaching the warning zone.

**Step 3 — Projected end-of-life cycle:**
$$N_{\text{EOL}} = N_{\text{current}} + \text{RUL} = 600 + 450 = 1050\,\text{cycles}$$

**Step 4 — Engineering recommendation:**
The Battery Management System (BMS) should display a **"monitor closely" advisory** and re-evaluate SOH every 50 cycles. Replacement scheduling should be initiated when SOH crosses the 85% soft-warning threshold to allow driver planning.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

#### (a) **[7 Marks]** Compare and contrast the following AI techniques used for vehicle fault diagnosis: (i) Artificial Neural Networks (ANN), (ii) Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN), (iii) Recurrent Neural Networks / LSTM. Provide one automotive use-case for each.

**Model Answer:**

| Technique | Architecture Strength | Vehicle Diagnostic Use-Case |
|---|---|---|
| **ANN (Feed-forward)** | Universal function approximator; handles structured tabular data well. | **Use-case:** Mapping OBD-II DTC codes + sensor readings to root-cause fault labels (multi-class classification). |
| **CNN** | Automatic spatial feature extraction via convolutional filters; excels on 2D inputs like images or spectrograms. | **Use-case:** Classifying brake disc wear severity from a **dashboard-mounted camera image** using a pretrained ResNet/VGG backbone. Also used on **vibration spectrograms** for bearing fault identification. |
| **RNN / LSTM** | Captures temporal dependencies and long-range patterns in sequential data through gated memory cells. | **Use-case:** Predicting **battery SOH trajectory** from a sequence of charge-discharge voltage/current cycles; the LSTM learns the temporal degradation pattern to forecast RUL. |

**Key Contrast:**
- ANN ignores **spatial** and **temporal** structure — best for flat feature vectors.
- CNN exploits **spatial locality** — best for image-like inputs.
- LSTM exploits **temporal dynamics** — best for time-series signals.

**Combined Diagnostic Stack (Industry Practice):** Modern vehicle AI stacks use a **CNN-LSTM hybrid**, where a CNN extracts spatial features from spectrograms, and the LSTM models their temporal evolution — yielding superior accuracy over either model alone.

#### (b) **[7 Marks]** A diagnostic confusion matrix for a 4-class fault classifier (Normal, Brake Fault, Sensor Drift, CAN Bus Error) is given below. Compute: (i) Overall accuracy, (ii) Per-class precision, (iii) Macro-averaged $F_1$ score. Comment on the model's production-readiness.

| Actual \ Predicted | Normal | Brake | Drift | CAN |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Normal** | 42 | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| **Brake** | 2 | 28 | 0 | 0 |
| **Drift** | 1 | 1 | 26 | 2 |
| **CAN** | 0 | 0 | 2 | 33 |

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Overall accuracy:**
$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{42 + 28 + 26 + 33}{45 + 30 + 30 + 35} = \frac{129}{140} \approx 0.9214$$

$$\boxed{\text{Accuracy} \approx 92.14\%}$$

**Step 2 — Per-class precision (Precision = TP / Predicted Total):**

$$\text{Precision}_{\text{Normal}} = \frac{42}{45} \approx 0.9333$$

$$\text{Precision}_{\text{Brake}} = \frac{28}{30} \approx 0.9333$$

$$\text{Precision}_{\text{Drift}} = \frac{26}{29} \approx 0.8966$$

$$\text{Precision}_{\text{CAN}} = \frac{33}{36} \approx 0.9167$$

**Step 3 — Per-class recall (Recall = TP / Actual Total):**

$$\text{Recall}_{\text{Normal}} = \frac{42}{45} \approx 0.9333$$

$$\text{Recall}_{\text{Brake}} = \frac{28}{30} \approx 0.9333$$

$$\text{Recall}_{\text{Drift}} = \frac{26}{30} \approx 0.8667$$

$$\text{Recall}_{\text{CAN}} = \frac{33}{35} \approx 0.9429$$

**Step 4 — Macro-averaged $F_1$:**

$$F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}$$

$$F_{1,\text{Normal}} \approx 0.9333$$

$$F_{1,\text{Brake}} \approx 0.9333$$

$$F_{1,\text{Drift}} \approx 0.8814$$

$$F_{1,\text{CAN}} \approx 0.9296$$

$$F_{1,\text{macro}} = \frac{0.9333 + 0.9333 + 0.8814 + 0.9296}{4} \approx 0.9194$$

$$\boxed{F_{1,\text{macro}} \approx 0.919}$$

**Step 5 — Production-readiness comment:**
- Accuracy $92.14\% < 95\%$ production benchmark.
- $F_{1,\text{macro}} \approx 0.92 < 0.95$ safety threshold.
- The **Drift class** is the weakest (Recall = 86.67%) due to confusion with Normal and CAN.
- **Recommendation:** Apply **SMOTE oversampling** for the Drift class, and consider **focal loss** to penalize misclassification of minority faults. Re-validate before deployment in any safety-critical context.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Typically Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the formula denominator:** Many students compute Precision as TP alone, missing the predicted-total denominator. Always write the full formula before substituting.
> 2. **Confusing Accuracy with $F_1$:** Accuracy can be misleading on imbalanced fault datasets (where Normal samples dominate). Always compute $F_1$ for safety-critical diagnostics.
> 3. **Ignoring IEEE 1188 threshold:** In SOH problems, students often just compute the percentage but **forget to compare with the 80% replacement threshold**, which carries 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Not mentioning units in final answers:** Always state `cycles`, `%`, `mm/s`, `Ah` — KTU examiners deduct for unit omissions.
> 5. **Skipping the engineering interpretation:** After a numerical answer, KTU expects a **1-line engineering recommendation** (e.g., "battery needs monitoring" or "classifier is acceptable for non-safety deployment").

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for "AI for Diagnostic Systems"**

- **Definition:** AI diagnostics = ML + DL + expert systems + sensor data → automatic fault detection/diagnosis/prognosis.
- **5-Layer Architecture:** Sensor → Pre-processing → Feature Extraction → AI Inference → Decision/HMI.
- **Three Diagnostic Tasks:**
  - **Detection** (binary, is there a fault?)
  - **Diagnosis** (multi-class, which fault?)
  - **Prognosis** (regression, how long until failure?)
- **DTC Format:** First letter = P (Powertrain), C (Chassis), B (Body), U (Network).
- **Battery SOH Formula:** $\text{SOH} = \frac{C_{\text{actual}}}{C_{\text{rated}}} \times 100\%$.
- **IEEE 1188 Replacement Threshold:** SOH $\leq \mathbf{80\%}$.
- **RUL Computation:** $\text{RUL} = N_{\text{EOL}} - N_{\text{current}}$.
- **Key Models:**
  - **CNN** → spectrogram/image fault classification.
  - **LSTM** → time-series SOH/RUL prediction.
  - **Autoencoder** → anomaly detection for unknown faults.
  - **Random Forest / XGBoost** → DTC code classification.
- **Critical Metrics:**
  - **Accuracy** $\geq \mathbf{95\%}$ for production.
  - **$F_1$ Score** $\geq \mathbf{0.95}$ for safety-critical deployment.
  - **Inference Latency** $\leq \mathbf{10\,\text{ms}}$.
- **CAN Bus Speed:** $\mathbf{500\,\text{kbps}}$ (high-speed CAN).
- **Vibration Threshold (ISO 10816):** $V_{\text{RMS}} > \mathbf{4.5\,\text{mm/s}}$ → bearing degradation alert.
- **Industry Examples to Quote in Answers:**
  - Tesla OTA predictive battery maintenance.
  - BMW Condition Based Service (CBS).
  - Bosch eCALL / F1 real-time telemetry.
- **Common Pitfalls:**
  - Accuracy on imbalanced data is misleading.
  - SOH problems always need threshold comparison.
  - Always state units and engineering interpretation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Onboard Diagnostics: AI-Enhanced Diagnostics for Identifying and Resolving Issues
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT865 - ADVANCED VEHICLE TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Onboard Diagnostics: AI-Enhanced Diagnostics for Identifying and Resolving Issues

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Onboard Diagnostics (OBD)** refers to a vehicle's self-diagnostic and reporting capability, standardized primarily through the **OBD-II (SAE J1979 / ISO 15031)** protocol, which continuously monitors the performance of major engine and emissions control subsystems through a network of **Electronic Control Units (ECUs)**, **sensors**, and **actuators**. The system stores **Diagnostic Trouble Codes (DTCs)** when malfunctions are detected and exposes them through a standardized communication interface for external scan tools.

**AI-Enhanced Onboard Diagnostics** extends this conventional framework by integrating **machine learning (ML)**, **deep learning (DL)**, **digital twins**, and **edge-AI inference engines** directly into the vehicle's electronic architecture. The objective is to:

1. **Predict** component degradation *before* a hard fault triggers a DTC.
2. **Classify** the root-cause of a fault from multi-sensor signatures.
3. **Recommend** or autonomously execute corrective actions.
4. **Explain** the diagnosis (Explainable AI – XAI) to technicians and drivers.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** context, this topic sits at the intersection of automotive electronics, embedded systems, and intelligent mobility — linking **Connected Vehicle (CV)** concepts, **Autonomous Driving (AD)** stacks, and **IoT-enabled prognostics**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Car as a "Patient with a Smart Health Watch"**

Imagine a human body equipped with a continuous health-monitoring smartwatch:

| Human Health Watch | Vehicle Onboard Diagnostics |
|---|---|
| Heart-rate sensor | Crankshaft position sensor |
| Blood-oxygen sensor | Oxygen (Lambda) sensor |
| Doctor visits (annual) | OBD-II periodic readiness check |
| ECG report at hospital | Snapshot data via scan tool |
| AI predicts heart-attack risk 6 hrs early | ML predicts turbocharger failure 500 km early |
| Doctor prescribes lifestyle change | ECU adjusts fuel map / torque limit |

**Even better analogy:** A traditional OBD system is like a smoke detector — it only screams *after* the fire has started. **AI-Enhanced OBD is like a thermal camera with a fire-prediction AI** — it notices the wiring getting warm *hours* before ignition, and proactively alerts the driver and the cloud.

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics & Standard Constants

The following metrics govern AI-enhanced diagnostics and must be committed to memory for KTU examinations:

- **OBD-II Connector Standard:** SAE J1962 (16-pin DLC — Data Link Connector).
- **Communication Protocols:** ISO 9141-2, ISO 14230-4 (KWP2000), **ISO 15765 (CAN)**, SAE J1939 (heavy-duty).
- **DTC Format:** 5-character alphanumeric — e.g., **P0301** (P = Powertrain, 0 = Generic/SAE, 3 = Misfire, 01 = Cylinder 1).
- **ML Inference Latency Target:** **<10 ms** for in-loop control, **<100 ms** for diagnostic alerts.
- **Model Footprint Constraint:** **<512 KB** for embedded edge-AI deployment (AUTOSAR Classic).
- **Diagnostic Coverage:** **>90%** of emission-related faults must be detectable per **OBD-II regulatory mandate (CARB/EPA)**.
- **Memory:** Freeze-frame data retention for last fault event.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of a Conventional OBD-II System

A traditional OBD-II system operates on a **closed-loop monitoring principle** with five functional blocks:

1. **Sensor Layer (Input)** — Temperature, pressure, O₂, NOx, MAF, MAP, crankshaft, camshaft, knock, wheel-speed sensors.
2. **ECU Layer (Processing)** — Powertrain Control Module (PCM), Transmission Control Module (TCM), Body Control Module (BCM), Battery Management System (BMS).
3. **Actuator Layer (Output)** — Fuel injectors, ignition coils, EGR valve, throttle body, VVT solenoid.
4. **Diagnostic Executive (Software)** — Runs on the ECU; executes *monitor routines* on each subsystem.
5. **Communication Layer (Reporting)** — CAN bus → OBD-II DLC → Scan tool / Telematics Control Unit (TCU).

### Operational Flow (Why & How)

> **Why does each step exist?**
> Emissions regulations require that *any fault* causing tailpipe emissions to exceed **1.5× the standard** must be detected and the driver alerted within a defined drive cycle. This forces a hierarchical "monitor → detect → store → report" pipeline.

- **Step 1 — Continuous Sampling:** Sensors are sampled at rates from **1 Hz** (coolant temperature) to **20 kHz** (knock sensor).
- **Step 2 — Signal Conditioning:** Analog signals are filtered, linearized, and digitized via the ECU's ADC.
- **Step 3 — Monitor Routines:** Periodic software routines (e.g., catalyst efficiency monitor, evaporative system monitor, Misfire Detection Monitor) compare live data against calibrated thresholds.
- **Step 4 — Fault Confirmation:** To prevent false positives, a fault must be detected in **two consecutive drive cycles** before the MIL (Malfunction Indicator Lamp) is lit, per OBD-II regulations.
- **Step 5 — DTC Storage + Freeze Frame:** The 5-character DTC plus a snapshot of sensor values at the moment of fault is written to non-volatile memory.
- **Step 6 — External Reporting:** Upon a scan-tool request via **PID (Parameter ID) service $01**, the DTC list is streamed over CAN at 500 kbps.

## 2.2 The AI Enhancement — Where Machine Learning Fits

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Conventional OBD is **reactive** (rule-based, threshold-triggered). AI-enhanced OBD is **proactive** (data-driven, pattern-recognition-based, predictive).

The AI layer is integrated as an **overlay** that does *not* replace the regulatory monitors but augments them with three additional capabilities:

### (A) Anomaly Detection (Unsupervised Learning)
- Algorithms: **Isolation Forest, One-Class SVM, Autoencoders, LSTM-AE**.
- Purpose: Detect *unknown* fault modes that have no predefined DTC.
- Training data: Normal operation telemetry from thousands of vehicles (fleet learning).

### (B) Fault Classification (Supervised Learning)
- Algorithms: **Random Forest, XGBoost, 1-D CNN, Transformer encoder**.
- Purpose: Given a multi-sensor signature, predict the most probable root cause (e.g., "mass airflow sensor drift" vs "intake manifold leak").
- Output: Confidence score + ranked fault list.

### (C) Remaining Useful Life (RUL) Prediction (Regression / Sequence Models)
- Algorithms: **LSTM, GRU, Temporal Convolutional Networks (TCN), Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINN)**.
- Purpose: Estimate *how many km/cycles remain* before a component fails.
- Output: Probability curve of failure vs. time.

### (D) Explainable AI (XAI) Layer
- Algorithms: **SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations), LIME, Attention Maps**.
- Purpose: Provide human-readable justification for the AI's diagnosis — critical for warranty claim adjudication and technician trust.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are essential for solving KTU numerical / analytical questions on this topic.

| # | Formula / Expression | Description | Units / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **DTC = (S, C, X, DD)** | S = System (P/B/C/U), C = Code Type (0/1/2/3), X = Subsystem, DD = Specific Fault | 5-char alphanumeric |
| 2 | $\text{Coverage} = \dfrac{N_{\text{detected}}}{N_{\text{injected}}} \times 100\%$ | Diagnostic coverage ratio | **>90%** target |
| 3 | $\text{FalseAlarmRate} = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | ML diagnostic reliability metric | **<5%** target |
| 4 | $L_{\text{BCE}} = -\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\left[y_i \log(\hat{y_i}) + (1-y_i)\log(1-\hat{y_i})\right]$ | Binary cross-entropy loss for fault classifier | dimensionless |
| 5 | $L_{\text{MSE}} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(y_i - \hat{y_i})^2$ | MSE for RUL regression | cycles / km |
| 6 | $\text{ReconError} = \vert x - \hat{x}\vert_2^2$ | Autoencoder anomaly score | sensor units² |
| 7 | $T_{\text{inference}} = \dfrac{N_{\text{ops}}}{f_{\text{clock}} \times N_{\text{cores}}}$ | Edge-AI latency | **<10 ms** in-loop |
| 8 | $\text{Threshold}_{\text{MIL}} = 1.5 \times \text{EmissionStandard}$ | OBD-II emission threshold multiplier | CARB / EPA |
| 9 | $P(\text{fault} \mid \mathbf{x}) = \text{softmax}(\mathbf{W}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b})$ | Probabilistic fault prediction | $0 \le P \le 1$ |
| 10 | $\text{RUL}(t) = \int_t^{\infty} p(\tau) \, d\tau$ | Expected remaining useful life | km / hours |

> **Notation Note:** Use `\vert` (not `|`) inside table cells to avoid breaking markdown parsing, e.g., $\vert x - \hat{x}\vert$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Fleet Management:** Predictive maintenance reduces breakdown incidents by up to **70%** and lowers total maintenance cost by **25–30%**.
- **Emissions Compliance:** EU Regulation (EC) 715/2007 and India's **BS-VI Phase 2** require OBD-II monitoring; AI augmentation helps meet stricter future Euro 7 / BS-VII thresholds.
- **Insurance (UBI — Usage-Based Insurance):** Telematics + AI diagnostics feed risk-scoring models.
- **OTA (Over-The-Air) Updates:** AI models can be remotely refined using fleet-level federated learning without sending raw vehicle data to the cloud.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Diagnostic Coverage & MIL Illumination Logic

The Malfunction Indicator Lamp (MIL) must illuminate only after a fault is **confirmed** to avoid nuisance trips. The confirmation logic per OBD-II is:

> **A fault is confirmed when it is detected in two consecutive drive cycles OR detected once in the current cycle if a previously confirmed fault of the same type exists in pending memory.**

Let:
- $F_k$ = Boolean flag = 1 if fault detected in drive cycle $k$, else 0.
- $C_k$ = Boolean flag = 1 if fault is *confirmed* after drive cycle $k$.

**Step 1: Define the pending counter $P_k$**

$$P_k = \begin{cases} P_{k-1} + F_k & \text{if } P_{k-1} < 2 \\ 2 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 2: Define the confirmed flag $C_k$**

$$C_k = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } P_k = 2 \text{ OR } (C_{k-1} = 1 \text{ AND } F_k = 1) \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 3: MIL state**

$$\text{MIL}_k = \text{OR}(C_k, \text{MIL}_{k-1})$$

**Step 4: Total diagnostic coverage over N drive cycles**

$$\text{Coverage} = \frac{\sum_{k=1}^{N} C_k}{\sum_{k=1}^{N} \text{FaultInjected}_k} \times 100\%$$

This formulation is what KTU examiners expect when asking "explain MIL illumination logic with mathematical formulation."

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Autoencoder Anomaly Score for OBD Sensors

An autoencoder compresses a multi-sensor vector $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ into a latent code $\mathbf{z} \in \mathbb{R}^m$ ($m \ll d$) and reconstructs $\hat{\mathbf{x}}$. Trained only on *healthy* data, it produces low reconstruction error for normal operation and high error for anomalous (faulty) operation.

**Step 1 — Encoder mapping**

$$\mathbf{z} = \sigma_e(\mathbf{W}_e \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}_e)$$

where $\sigma_e$ = ReLU activation, $\mathbf{W}_e \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times d}$, $\mathbf{b}_e \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$.

**Step 2 — Decoder mapping**

$$\hat{\mathbf{x}} = \sigma_d(\mathbf{W}_d \mathbf{z} + \mathbf{b}_d)$$

where $\sigma_d$ = sigmoid (for normalized sensor inputs), $\mathbf{W}_d \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times m}$, $\mathbf{b}_d \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$.

**Step 3 — Per-sample reconstruction error (anomaly score)**

$$s(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{d} (x_i - \hat{x_i})^2 = \vert\vert \mathbf{x} - \hat{\mathbf{x}} \vert\vert_2^2$$

**Step 4 — Threshold decision rule**

$$\text{FaultFlag} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } s(\mathbf{x}) > \tau \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

The threshold $\tau$ is calibrated from the 99th percentile of healthy-data scores: $\tau = Q_{0.99}(s_{\text{healthy}})$.

**Step 5 — Model training objective (minimize reconstruction MSE)**

$$L_{\text{AE}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \vert\vert \mathbf{x}^{(j)} - \hat{\mathbf{x}}^{(j)} \vert\vert_2^2 + \lambda \vert\vert \mathbf{W}_e \vert\vert_F^2$$

The last term is L2 regularization (weight decay) to prevent overfitting, with $\lambda \approx 10^{-4}$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — RUL Prediction Using LSTM

For a sensor time-series $\mathbf{X}_{1:t} = \{\mathbf{x}_1, \mathbf{x}_2, \ldots, \mathbf{x}_t\}$, an LSTM cell maintains a hidden state $\mathbf{h}_t$ and cell state $\mathbf{c}_t$ updated by gating mechanisms.

**Step 1 — Forget gate**

$$\mathbf{f}_t = \sigma(\mathbf{W}_f [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_f)$$

**Step 2 — Input gate**

$$\mathbf{i}_t = \sigma(\mathbf{W}_i [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_i)$$

**Step 3 — Candidate cell state**

$$\tilde{\mathbf{c}}_t = \tanh(\mathbf{W}_c [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_c)$$

**Step 4 — Cell state update**

$$\mathbf{c}_t = \mathbf{f}_t \odot \mathbf{c}_{t-1} + \mathbf{i}_t \odot \tilde{\mathbf{c}}_t$$

**Step 5 — Output gate and hidden state**

$$\mathbf{o}_t = \sigma(\mathbf{W}_o [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_o)$$

$$\mathbf{h}_t = \mathbf{o}_t \odot \tanh(\mathbf{c}_t)$$

**Step 6 — RUL regression head**

$$\hat{y}_t = \mathbf{w}_y^{\top} \mathbf{h}_t + b_y$$

where $\hat{y}_t$ is the predicted RUL (in cycles or km). Training minimizes the Huber loss:

$$L_{\text{Huber}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{j=1}^{N} \begin{cases} 0.5 (y^{(j)} - \hat{y}^{(j)})^2 & \text{if } \vert y^{(j)} - \hat{y}^{(j)} \vert \le \delta \\ \delta \cdot (\vert y^{(j)} - \hat{y}^{(j)} \vert - 0.5\delta) & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

with $\delta = 1.0$ being the typical choice. Huber loss is preferred over MSE for RUL because it is **robust to outliers** (sudden noisy spikes in sensor data).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Diagnostic Paradigms

| Dimension | Rule-Based OBD-II | AI-Enhanced OBD |
|---|---|---|
| Fault Coverage | Known faults only (~30–50% of real failures) | Known + unknown faults (>85%) |
| Detection Mode | Reactive (after threshold breach) | Predictive (before threshold breach) |
| Latency | **<1 ms** | **5–50 ms** (edge-AI) |
| Memory Footprint | KB scale (lookup tables) | **100 KB – 10 MB** (quantized models) |
| False Positive Rate | Moderate (1–3%) | Low with good training (<1%) |
| Explainability | High (DTC text lookup) | Moderate (requires XAI layer) |
| Regulatory Status | Mandatory (BS-VI/Euro 6) | Optional augmentation |
| Updateability | ECU reflash required | OTA model updates possible |
| Power Draw | Negligible | 0.5–3 W on AI accelerator |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of AI-Enhanced OBD

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **end-to-end data flow** from physical sensors through AI inference to driver/cloud feedback. It is intentionally a *functional architecture* (not a wiring diagram) to remain readable in a Mermaid renderer.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Physical Vehicle Sensors\nMAP MAF TPS O2 NOx CrankCam Knock CoolantOIL] --> B[Sensor Acquisition Layer\nADC Sampling 1 Hz to 20 kHz]
    B --> C[CAN Bus Network\n500 kbps ISO 15765]
    C --> D[Powertrain ECU\nPCM TCM BCM BMS]
    D --> E[Conventional OBD-II Engine\nThreshold Monitors and DTC Library]
    E --> F{DTC Triggered?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Freeze Frame Storage\nNon Volatile Memory]
    F -- No --> H[Continue Monitoring]
    G --> I[OBD II DLC Port\nSAE J1962 16 Pin]
    I --> J[External Scan Tool or Telematics]
    D --> K[Edge AI Inference Engine\nNPU DSP FPGA]
    K --> L[Feature Extraction Buffer\nSliding Window 256 Samples]
    L --> M[Anomaly Detector\nAutoencoder One Class SVM]
    M --> N{Anomaly Score above Tau?}
    N -- Yes --> O[Fault Classifier\n1D CNN or XGBoost]
    N -- No --> P[RUL Predictor\nLSTM TCN PINN]
    O --> Q[Root Cause Confidence Score\n0 to 1]
    P --> R[Predicted Remaining Useful Life\nkm or cycles]
    Q --> S[Explainable AI Module\nSHAP Attention Map]
    R --> S
    S --> T[HMI Display\nDriver Dashboard]
    S --> U[Cloud Telematics TCU\nFleet Learning OTA]
    S --> V[Service Workshop Alert\nOBD App Push Notification]
```

## 4.2 Sequence Flow — Predictive Fault Detection Cycle

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant V as Vehicle
    participant E as ECU
    participant AI as Edge AI Engine
    participant C as Cloud
    participant D as Driver
    V->>E: Stream sensor data at 100 Hz
    E->>AI: Push 256-sample window
    AI->>AI: Run autoencoder reconstruction
    AI->>AI: Compute anomaly score s
    alt s exceeds threshold tau
        AI->>AI: Run fault classifier
        AI->>C: Upload telemetry + classification
        AI->>D: Display warning on dashboard
        AI->>E: Request MIL illumination if confirmed
        C->>C: Federated learning aggregation
        C-->>AI: Push updated model weights via OTA
    else s below threshold
        AI->>AI: Run RUL predictor
        AI->>D: Update predictive maintenance timer
    end
```

## 4.3 Data-Flow Hierarchy (Layered View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Layer 1 Physical\nSensors Actuators Wiring] --> L2[Layer 2 Signal\nADC Filtering Linearization]
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3 ECU Compute\nConventional OBD Engine]
    L2 --> L4[Layer 4 AI Edge\nAnomaly Detection Classification RUL]
    L3 --> L5[Layer 5 Decision Fusion\nRule plus ML Hybrid Engine]
    L4 --> L5
    L5 --> L6[Layer 6 Communication\nCAN Ethernet 5G V2X]
    L5 --> L7[Layer 7 HMI and Cloud\nDashboard App Fleet Server]
```

## 4.4 Component Interaction — DTC + AI Fusion Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Vehicle Ignition ON]) --> Init[Initialize ECU and AI Engine]
    Init --> Mon[Run Conventional OBD Monitors]
    Init --> AISense[AI Engine Begins Streaming Inference]
    Mon --> Reg{DTC within Regulatory Scope?}
    Reg -- Yes --> MIL[MIL Illumination per OBD-II Rule]
    Reg -- No --> AISense
    AISense --> ML{Anomaly Score High?}
    ML -- Yes --> Class[Run Classifier for Root Cause]
    ML -- No --> RUL[Update RUL Estimate]
    Class --> Fuse[Decision Fusion Module]
    RUL --> Fuse
    MIL --> Fuse
    Fuse --> Action{Action Level}
    Action -- Inform --> Info[Soft Warning to Driver]
    Action -- Warn --> Warn[Solid MIL plus App Notification]
    Action -- Protect --> Protect[Derate Torque plus Limp Home Mode]
    Fuse --> Log[Log to Non Volatile Memory and Cloud]
```
